topics.title: Introduction to UAV Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to UAV Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)**, commonly referred to as a *drone*, is a powered, aerial vehicle that does not carry a human operator, uses aerodynamic forces to provide vehicle lift, can fly autonomously or be remotely piloted, and can carry a payload. It is the aerial component of an **Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS)**, which integrates the air vehicle, a ground control station (GCS), a communication data link, and the human operator (when applicable).

The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) prefers the term **Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS)** to emphasize that a licensed pilot remains legally responsible for the flight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Terminology Lockdown**
> UAV = Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (the flying machine itself)
> UAS = Unmanned Aircraft System (UAV + GCS + Data Link + Operator)
> RPAS = Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (ICAO terminology)
> Drone = Popular/colloquial term (NOT preferred in technical answers)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Pet Bird"

Imagine a **trained falcon** used for hunting centuries ago. The bird flies, it has wings (airframe), muscles (propulsion), eyes (sensors), and a brain (autopilot). Yet, the *falconer* on the ground decides where to fly, when to dive, and what to catch. The communication between the falconer and the bird is invisible (hand signals, whistles).

A UAV is exactly this — a **mechanical falcon**:
- **Wings/Rotors** → Airframe
- **Muscles/Battery** → Propulsion system
- **Eyes/Camera** → Sensor payload
- **Brain/Computer** → Autopilot & flight controller
- **Whistle/Radio** → Communication data link
- **Falconer** → Ground Control Station (GCS)

The only difference: the mechanical falcon can be programmed to make its own decisions using AI (autonomous flight).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Quantities Every Aerial Robotics Engineer Must Memorise**
>
> - Standard sea-level air density: $\rho_{SL} = \mathbf{1.225 \ kg/m^3}$
> - Speed of sound at sea level: $a_{SL} = \mathbf{340.29 \ m/s}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.80665 \ m/s^2}$
> - Earth’s standard atmosphere: **ISA (International Standard Atmosphere)**
> - Operational ceiling tiers (ICAO classification): **400 ft / 120 m** for civil micro-drones
> - Maximum takeoff weight for nano-class UAVs: **< 250 g** (DGCA India, No-Permission-No-License)
> - Endurance benchmark for fixed-wing UAVs: **2–40 hours** (depending on class)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of a UAV

A UAV is decomposed into **five primary subsystems**, each with a clear engineering function:

1. **Airframe** — Structural skeleton (fuselage, wings, rotor booms). Materials range from EPP foam (micro) to carbon-fibre composites (tactical).
2. **Propulsion Subsystem** — Generates thrust. Components: motor (BLDC), propeller, ESC (Electronic Speed Controller), and energy source (Li-Po / Li-ion / fuel cell / solar).
3. **Avionics / Flight Control System (FCS)** — The “brain.” Contains IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit: gyroscope + accelerometer), GPS, magnetometer, barometer, and the autopilot processor (e.g., Pixhawk, ArduPilot).
4. **Payload** — Mission-specific equipment: EO/IR camera, LiDAR, multispectral sensor, parcel, or sprayer.
5. **Communication Data Link** — Telemetry & command radio (e.g., 2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz, 900 MHz, or LTE/5G).

## 2.2 Operational "Why" Behind Each Subsystem

- **Why an IMU?** Because GPS updates only at **1–10 Hz**, but a quadcopter can crash in **< 100 ms**. The IMU samples angular rates at **> 1 kHz** to keep it stable between GPS corrections.
- **Why BLDC motors?** They offer the highest **power-to-weight ratio (> 5 kW/kg)** among commercial electric motors and have no brush wear.
- **Why a separate data link?** Because C2 (Command & Control) and payload data require different bandwidths, latencies, and encryption.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|--------|----------|----------------------|------|-----------------|
| $W$ | Aircraft weight (Takeoff weight) | $W = m \cdot g$ | N | $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$ |
| $T/W$ | Thrust-to-Weight Ratio | $T/W = \frac{F_T}{W}$ | dimensionless | $> 1$ for vertical lift |
| $P/W$ | Power Loading | $P/W = \frac{P_{avail}}{W}$ | W/N | Lower is better |
| $W/P$ | Wing Loading | $W/S = \frac{W}{S_{wing}}$ | N/m² | $S_{wing}$ = wing area |
| $b$ | Wingspan | — | m | For fixed-wing |
| $E$ | Endurance (Breguet for propeller) | $E = \frac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$ | s | $c_T$ = specific fuel consumption |
| $R$ | Range (Breguet) | $R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$ | m | $V$ = true airspeed |
| $\rho$ | Air density (ISA) | $\rho = \rho_0 \left(1 - \frac{L h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{RL}-1}$ | kg/m³ | $L = 0.0065 \ K/m$ |
| $a$ | Speed of sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| $\tau$ | Battery endurance (electric) | $\tau = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover}}$ | s | $E_{bat}$ in joules |
| $R_{comm}$ | Max communication range | $R_{comm} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,min}}}$ | m | Friis transmission equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pro-Tip:** In Breguet equations, the factor $\frac{L}{D}$ (lift-to-drag ratio) is the single most important aerodynamic efficiency metric. **Higher L/D = longer range AND longer endurance** for propeller-driven UAVs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture**: Precision spraying UAVs use the $W/P$ ratio to determine optimal nozzle flow rates.
- **Defence**: Endurance formula drives the design of MALE (Medium-Altitude Long-Endurance) UAVs such as the **MQ-9 Reaper** (27-hour endurance).
- **Logistics**: Wing loading $W/S$ determines whether a parcel-delivery drone can land on a roof without structural collapse.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Breguet Range Equation for a Fixed-Wing UAV

The classical Breguet range equation is the foundation of all UAV mission-planning software (e.g., QGroundControl, Mission Planner).

**Starting Assumption (Step 1):** A propeller-driven UAV flies at constant altitude, constant true airspeed $V$, constant lift coefficient $C_L$, and constant $L/D$.

**Step 2 — Specific Fuel Consumption Definition:**

The specific fuel consumption $c_T$ is defined as fuel mass-flow rate per unit thrust:

$$
c_T = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{F_T} \quad \text{[units: 1/s]}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Fuel Flow in terms of Drag:**

Since the aircraft is in steady level flight, thrust equals drag: $F_T = D$.

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = c_T \cdot D
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Aerodynamic Drag Identity:**

Drag is $D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_D$. Substituting:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = c_T \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_D
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Lift-to-Drag Ratio:**

Use $C_D = \frac{C_L}{L/D}$ and the steady-level lift equation $W = L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_L$.

Dividing thrust by weight:

$$
\frac{F_T}{W} = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{1}{L/D}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the Differential Equation of Range:**

For an infinitesimal distance element $dR = V \, dt$, and infinitesimal mass loss $dW = -g \, dm_{fuel}$:

$$
\frac{dR}{dW} = \frac{V \, dt}{dW} = \frac{V}{g \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}} = \frac{V}{g \cdot c_T \cdot F_T} = \frac{V}{g \cdot c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D}
$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from Initial to Final Weight:**

$$
R = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \frac{V}{g \cdot c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \, \frac{dW}{W}
$$

Because $V$, $c_T$, and $L/D$ are constant by assumption:

$$
R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) \quad \text{[metres]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Breguet Range Equation (Propeller UAV)**
> 
> $$\boxed{R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)}$$
> 
> This is the form examiners expect in KTU University Exams.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electric Multirotor Endurance (Hover Case)

For a **quadcopter / multirotor**, there is no forward flight; thrust must equal weight.

**Step 1:** Required hover power (momentum theory):

$$
P_{hover} = \frac{W^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}
$$

where $A_{rotor} = \pi R^2_{prop}$ is the propeller disk area.

**Step 2:** Battery energy:

$$
E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat} \cdot 3600 \quad \text{[Joules]}
$$

where $Q_{bat}$ is the capacity in Amp-hours.

**Step 3:** Useful available energy (after derating for depth-of-discharge $\eta_{DoD}$ and motor/ESC efficiency $\eta_{sys}$):

$$
E_{useful} = E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DoD} \cdot \eta_{sys}
$$

**Step 4:** Hover endurance:

$$
\tau = \frac{E_{useful}}{P_{hover}} = \frac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DoD} \cdot \eta_{sys} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}{W^{3/2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Endurance scales **inversely** with $W^{3/2}$. This is why doubling the payload of a delivery drone reduces endurance by a factor of $\approx 2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$ — a critical KTU design takeaway.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thrust-to-Weight Ratio for Hover

A multirotor in hover must produce a total thrust equal to its weight. Each motor produces thrust $F_{motor}$. For an $n$-rotor configuration:

$$
n \cdot F_{motor} = W
$$

Adding a **thrust margin** $\mu$ (typically $\mu = 1.5$ to $2.0$ for agile manoeuvres):

$$
\frac{T}{W} = \frac{n \cdot F_{motor,max}}{W} = \mu
$$

Solving for the minimum thrust per motor:

$$
F_{motor,min} = \frac{\mu \cdot W}{n}
$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):**
A hexacopter ($n = 6$) weighs $W = 18$ N. The required thrust margin is $\mu = 1.8$ for acrobatic flight.

$$
F_{motor,min} = \frac{1.8 \times 18}{6} = \mathbf{5.4 \ N \ per \ motor}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Complete UAS

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the five subsystems interact during a real flight mission.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GCS[GROUND CONTROL STATION]
        opA[Human Operator]
        swA[Mission Planner Software]
        joyA[Joystick or Tablet]
    end

    subgraph DL[DATA LINK C2 BAND 2.4 GHZ]
        antA[Ground Antenna]
    end

    subgraph AV[UAV AIR VEHICLE]
        afA[Airframe Composite]
        prA[Propulsion BLDC Motor plus ESC plus Propeller]
        avA[Avionics Pixhawk FC plus IMU plus GPS]
        plA[Payload EO Camera plus LiDAR]
        anA[Airborne Antenna]
    end

    opA -- command intent --> swA
    swA -- flight plan --> joyA
    joyA -- encoded RC --> antA
    antA -. radio uplink .-> anA
    anA -- RC demodulated --> avA
    avA -- PWM throttle --> prA
    prA -- thrust --> afA
    avA -- gimbal control --> plA
    plA -- video stream --> anA
    anA -. downlink telemetry plus video .-> antA
    antA -- data to operator --> swA
```

## 4.2 UAV Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[UAV Classification]
    rootA --> fwA[Fixed Wing]
    rootA --> rwA[Rotary Wing]
    rootA --> hyA[Hybrid VTOL]

    fwA --> fw1A[Conventional e.g. Predator]
    fwA --> fw2A[Flying Wing e.g. B 2 Spirit derivative]

    rwA --> rw1A[Single Rotor Helicopter]
    rwA --> rw2A[Multirotor Tricopter Quadcopter Hexacopter Octocopter]

    hyA --> hy1A[Tilt Rotor e.g. V 22 Osprey class]
    hyA --> hy2A[Tail Sitter e.g. QuadPlane]

    rw2A --> rw2subA[Mission Mapping Survey Delivery]
    fw1A --> fw1subA[Long Endurance ISR Recon]
    hy1A --> hy1subA[Medical Logistics Heavy Lift]
```

## 4.3 Guidance-Navigation-Control (GNC) Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmdA[Commanded Trajectory from GCS]
    navA[Navigation Module GPS plus INS]
    fbkA[Sensor Feedback IMU plus Barometer]
    errA[Error Computation Subtractor]
    ctlA[Controller PID or LQR]
    actA[Actuator Motors and Servos]
    dynA[UAV Dynamics 6 DOF Rigid Body]
    outA[Measured State Position plus Velocity plus Attitude]

    cmdA --> errA
    navA --> errA
    fbkA --> navA
    errA -- delta error --> ctlA
    ctlA -- control input --> actA
    actA -- forces and moments --> dynA
    dynA --> outA
    outA --> navA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the GNC Diagram:** The outer loop runs at **low frequency** (GPS, ~10 Hz) for navigation. The inner loop runs at **high frequency** (IMU, ~1 kHz) for stability. This is the *dual-loop* architecture used by ArduPilot and PX4 autopilot stacks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Define a UAV. Differentiate between UAV, UAS, and RPAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle)* is a powered aerial vehicle that does not carry a human operator, uses aerodynamic lift, and can fly autonomously or be remotely controlled. It is the airborne component only. A *UAS (Unmanned Aircraft System)* is the complete operational system comprising the UAV, a Ground Control Station (GCS), the data link, and the human crew. *RPAS (Remotely Piloted Aircraft System)* is the ICAO-preferred term, explicitly emphasising that a licensed pilot remains in command via remote control.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**List any six civil applications of UAVs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Precision agriculture (spraying, crop-health monitoring)
2. Aerial photography and cinematography
3. Disaster management and search-and-rescue
4. Package delivery (logistics)
5. Power-line and pipeline inspection
6. Mapping, surveying, and 3-D photogrammetry

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning**
> Common reasons students lose marks in this topic:
> 1. **Confusing UAV with UAS** — Examiners award 0 marks if the distinction is missing.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the Breguet derivation — every numerical answer must carry the unit (m, s, N).
> 3. **Skipping assumptions** — Always state "constant altitude, constant $V$, constant $L/D$" before deriving the range equation.
> 4. **Writing "drone" in formal answers** — Use *UAV* / *RPAS* in technical writing.
> 5. **Not classifying UAV by weight class** — A complete answer must mention MTOW-based categories.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**(a) Classify UAVs based on (i) wing type, (ii) maximum take-off weight (MTOW), and (iii) operational altitude. Give one example for each class. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Wing Type — 3 marks**

| Class | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| Fixed-wing | Rigid wing generating lift by forward motion | IAI Heron (MALE) |
| Rotary-wing | Rotors generate lift; capable of hover | DJI Phantom (quadcopter) |
| Hybrid VTOL | Combines vertical take-off with fixed-wing cruise | Wingcopter 198, Amazon Prime Air |

**(ii) Classification by MTOW — 2 marks**

- **Micro / Nano UAV:** $< 250$ g — e.g., DJI Mini
- **Mini UAV:** $250$ g – $25$ kg — e.g., RQ-11 Raven
- **Tactical UAV:** $25$ kg – $150$ kg — e.g., RQ-7 Shadow
- **MALE / HALE:** $150$ kg – $1500$ kg — e.g., MQ-9 Reaper
- **Combat UAV:** $> 1500$ kg — e.g., X-47B

**(iii) Classification by Operational Altitude — 2 marks**

- **Low-Altitude:** Up to 1 km AGL — Survey drones
- **Medium-Altitude:** 1–10 km — Tactical UAVs
- **High-Altitude:** > 10 km — HALE UAVs like Global Hawk (up to 18 km)

**(b) Derive the Breguet range equation for a propeller-driven fixed-wing UAV and explain the role of $L/D$ on mission range. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — State assumptions: constant altitude, constant true airspeed $V$, constant $C_L$, constant $L/D$, fuel mass is a small fraction of total mass.

Step 2 — Define specific fuel consumption: $c_T = \dot{m}_{fuel} / F_T$.

Step 3 — In steady level flight, $F_T = D = W/(L/D)$.

Step 4 — Express infinitesimal range: $dR = V \, dt = V \cdot dm_{fuel} / (c_T \cdot F_T)$.

Step 5 — Convert fuel-mass to weight: $dW = -g \, dm_{fuel}$.

Step 6 — Integrate from $W_i$ to $W_f$:

$$
R = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)
$$

Step 7 — Interpret $L/D$: Higher $L/D$ means less thrust is needed for the same weight, hence less fuel burnt per kilometre. Aerodynamic refinement (high aspect-ratio wing, laminar airfoil) directly extends mission range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numericals to Practise:** Substitute $V = 60$ m/s, $c_T = 0.0001$ /s, $L/D = 18$, $W_i = 500$ N, $W_f = 450$ N, and compute $R$ to get $\approx 597$ km.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a UAV. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A UAV consists of **five interacting subsystems**:

1. **Airframe** — Provides structural shape and houses all components. Material selection depends on mission; foam for micro-UAVs, carbon-fibre composites for tactical.

2. **Propulsion Subsystem** — Comprises the energy source (battery / fuel), power converter (ESC), motor (BLDC), and thrust-producing device (propeller / rotor). For hover, total thrust must equal weight times the safety margin.

3. **Avionics / Flight Control System** — The autopilot board (e.g., Pixhawk) running flight firmware (PX4 / ArduPilot). It executes sensor fusion (EKF) and runs the control loops.

4. **Payload** — Mission-specific (camera, LiDAR, sprayer). Often gimbal-mounted for stabilisation.

5. **Communication Data Link** — Bidirectional radio for telemetry and command. Frequency bands: 433 MHz (long range), 2.4 GHz (mid-range), 5.8 GHz (video downlink).

```
[ Block diagram description for exam ]

  GCS <----> Data Link <----> [ Airframe + Avionics ]
                                      |
                              [ Propulsion ]
                                      |
                                  [ Payload ]
```

(Examiners award 1 mark for a labelled block diagram, 6 marks for crisp subsystem explanations with one example each.)

**(b) A quadcopter weighing 20 N hovers at sea level. Each motor can produce 6 N of thrust. Calculate (i) thrust-to-weight ratio, (ii) whether it can hover, (iii) the maximum additional payload it can carry while maintaining a thrust margin of 1.5. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Thrust-to-Weight Ratio:**

$$
\frac{T}{W} = \frac{4 \times 6}{20} = \frac{24}{20} = \mathbf{1.2}
$$

**(ii) Hover Feasibility:**

Since $T/W = 1.2 > 1.0$, the quadcopter **can hover** (with a 20 % margin).

**(iii) Maximum Additional Payload:**

Required total thrust with margin $= 1.5 \times W_{total}$.

$$
4 \times 6 = 1.5 \times (20 + W_{payload})
$$

$$
24 = 30 + 1.5 \cdot W_{payload}
$$

$$
W_{payload} = \frac{24 - 30}{1.5} = \frac{-6}{1.5} = \mathbf{-4 \ N}
$$

A **negative** payload means the quadcopter is already under-powered for the required margin. The student should conclude that the motors must be upgraded, or the weight must be reduced by **4 N** ($\approx 408$ g) to meet the 1.5 margin requirement.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **UAV = flying machine; UAS = system; RPAS = ICAO term** — do not interchange.
- Standard sea-level air density is **$\rho_{SL} = 1.225 \ kg/m^3$** — used in every aerodynamic problem.
- **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio $T/W > 1$** is mandatory for vertical-lift UAVs (multirotors).
- **Breguet Range Equation** (fixed-wing, propeller): $R = \dfrac{V}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$.
- **Breguet Endurance Equation** (fixed-wing, propeller): $E = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$.
- **Hover Endurance** of a multirotor scales **inversely** with $W^{3/2}$.
- Five UAV subsystems: **Airframe, Propulsion, Avionics, Payload, Data Link**.
- Classifications by **wing type** (fixed/rotary/hybrid), **MTOW** (nano/mini/tactical/MALE/HALE), **altitude** (low/medium/high).
- Civil regulations (DGCA India): Nano UAVs $< 250$ g are **exempt** from licence; micro UAVs $250$ g – $2$ kg need **NPNT (No Permission No Take-off)** compliance.
- Always **state assumptions** before deriving Breguet equations — examiners mark step-by-step.
- **Common pitfall:** writing "drone" in formal answers costs marks; use **UAV** or **RPAS**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes of UAV Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classes of UAV Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — CLASSES OF UAV SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎓 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV):** A powered, aerial vehicle that does not carry a human operator, uses aerodynamic forces to provide vehicle lift, can fly autonomously or be remotely piloted, can be expendable or recoverable, and can carry a payload. UAVs may be classified into multiple distinct classes based on **mass, altitude, endurance, wing configuration, mission profile, and operational domain**.

A **UAV system** is more than just the airframe — it comprises the **Air Vehicle (AV)**, **Ground Control Station (GCS)**, **Data Link (DL)**, **Payload**, and **Launch & Recovery Equipment (LRE)**. The *class* of a UAV is determined by aggregating its performance envelope and design constraints.

---

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Tree of Drones"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of UAV classes like automobile segments:** Just as road vehicles are classified into *two-wheelers, hatchbacks, sedans, SUVs, trucks, and heavy-duty haulers* based on weight, range, and payload, UAVs are similarly partitioned. A *micro-UAV* is like a bicycle (very close-range, lightweight, indoor-capable), while a *HALE UAV* resembles a long-haul jumbo jet (high altitude, 24+ hour endurance, multi-ton payload). Each class serves a unique mission — reconnaissance, combat, surveying, or delivery — just as each automobile type serves a specific transport need.

---

### 🔑 Key Parameters Used for Classification

1. **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW)** — measured in **kg**.
2. **Service Ceiling** — operational altitude in **m / ft**.
3. **Endurance** — flight duration in **minutes / hours**.
4. **Range** — operational radius in **km**.
5. **Wing / Lift Configuration** — fixed, rotary, flapping, or hybrid.
6. **Mission Profile** — civil, commercial, military, scientific.
7. **Level of Autonomy** — remotely piloted, semi-autonomous, fully autonomous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Reminder:** Weight classes below are based on the widely accepted NATO STANAG 4670 & Indian DRDO classification ranges. Always cite the source framework when answering exam questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 📐 Classification Frameworks Explained

Different bodies (NATO, US DoD, Indian MHA, FAA, EASA) classify UAVs slightly differently. The most board-relevant framework is the **mass-based + altitude-based** dual matrix.

#### **A. Classification by Mass (Weight Class)**

| Weight Class | Mass Range | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Nano / Micro** | < 250 g | Crazyflie, Black Hornet |
| **Mini / Small** | 250 g – 5 kg | DJI Mavic, Parrot Anafi |
| **Light Tactical** | 5 kg – 25 kg | RQ-11B Raven |
| **Medium Tactical** | 25 kg – 150 kg | RQ-7 Shadow |
| **Medium / Heavy** | 150 kg – 600 kg | MQ-1 Predator |
| **Heavy / MALE** | 600 kg – 2,000 kg | MQ-9 Reaper |
| **Strategic (HALE)** | > 2,000 kg | RQ-4 Global Hawk |

#### **B. Classification by Altitude & Endurance**

- **LASE** — Low Altitude Short Endurance (< 1,500 m, < 2 h)
- **LALE** — Low Altitude Long Endurance (< 3,000 m, 2–24 h)
- **MALE** — Medium Altitude Long Endurance (3,000–9,000 m, 24+ h)
- **HALE** — High Altitude Long Endurance (> 9,000 m, 24–48 h)
- **UCAV** — Unmanned Combat Aerial Vehicle (variable)

---

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Breguet Endurance | $E = \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{b}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)$ | Endurance of propeller-driven UAV; $L/D$ = lift-to-drag ratio, $\eta_{prop}$ = prop efficiency, $c_b$ = specific fuel consumption, $W_i$ and $W_f$ = initial and final weights |
| 2 | Breguet Range | $R = \dfrac{V}{c_{b}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)$ | Range of propeller-driven fixed-wing UAV |
| 3 | Hover Power (Multirotor) | $P_{hover} = \dfrac{(m g)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}$ | Power required to hover a rotary-wing UAV; $\rho$ = air density, $A_{rotor}$ = rotor disc area |
| 4 | Disc Loading | $\dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{m g}{A_{rotor}}$ | Affects hover efficiency & stability |
| 5 | Endurance (Multirotor, simple) | $E = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{hover}}$ | Battery-limited endurance; $C_{bat}$ in Ah, $V_{bat}$ in V |
| 6 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V c}{\mu}$ | Low Re → laminar; critical for micro-UAV aerodynamic design |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S}$ | High AR → efficient long-endurance fixed-wing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Boards:**
> - $AR < 5$ → Low-endurance agile UAV.
> - $AR > 12$ → Long-endurance surveillance platform.
> - $Re < 10^{5}$ → Micro-UAV regime (laminar / viscous dominated).
> - $Re > 10^{6}$ → Conventional aerodynamic regime.

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surveying & Mapping:** Fixed-wing MALE UAVs like the **Thrust UAV** carry LiDAR + RGB cameras.
- **Defence:** HALE platforms like the **RQ-4 Global Hawk** deliver 24+ hour ISR coverage.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Mini multirotors perform variable-rate spraying using real-time NDVI.
- **Disaster Response:** LASE quadcopters deploy inside collapsed buildings (GPS-denied environments).
- **Logistics:** Heavy-lift multirotors (> 25 kg class) handle last-mile delivery in urban zones.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivation 1: Breguet Endurance Equation (Propeller-Driven Fixed-Wing UAV)

The **Breguet endurance equation** is foundational for sizing fuel reserves in fixed-wing UAVs — a **high-yield KTU derivation**.

**Starting premise:** For steady, level, unaccelerated cruise flight, lift equals weight:

$$L = W = mg$$

Drag equals thrust, and thrust-power relation is:

$$T \cdot V = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{prop}}, \quad \text{so} \quad P = T V \eta_{prop}$$

Since $T = D$, we have:

$$P = D V \eta_{prop} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{3} S C_{D}$$

The rate of fuel mass consumption is governed by specific fuel consumption $c_b$:

$$\dot{m}_{f} = c_{b} \cdot P$$

The rate of change of total aircraft weight is:

$$\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -\dot{m}_{f} g = -c_{b} P g$$

Substituting the power equation:

$$\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -c_{b} g \cdot \dfrac{V D}{\eta_{prop}}$$

For cruise, $L = W$, so $V = \dfrac{L}{W} \cdot \dfrac{W}{D} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho S C_{L}^{1/2}}$ — however, the classical Breguet approach expresses **endurance** as:

$$E = \int dt = \int_{W_{f}}^{W_{i}} \dfrac{-dW}{c_{b} g \cdot \dfrac{V D}{\eta_{prop}}}$$

Using $V D / W = V (D/L)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{b}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \int_{W_{f}}^{W_{i}} \dfrac{-dW}{W} \\
  &= \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{b}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Final KTU Board Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,E = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{b}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Endurance increases linearly with $L/D$ and propeller efficiency, and logarithmically with fuel-fraction ratio.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 2: Hover Power of a Multirotor (Ideal Disk Theory)

For an ideal hovering rotor in steady conditions, the power required equals the rate of increase of kinetic energy in the slipstream.

Mass flow through rotor disk:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_{rotor} v_{i}$$

where $v_i$ = induced velocity at the disk.

Thrust equals weight:

$$T = W = mg = 2 \dot{m} v_{i} = 2 \rho A_{rotor} v_{i}^{2}$$

Solve for induced velocity:

$$v_{i} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}$$

Power required (ideal):

$$P_{hover} = T \cdot v_{i} = W v_{i} = W \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}$$

Substituting $W = mg$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{hover} &= mg \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{mg}{2 \rho A_{rotor}}} \\
          &= \dfrac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Final KTU Board Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,P_{hover} = \dfrac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Quadrotor hover power scales with $m^{3/2}$ — doubling mass increases hover power by ~2.83×, which is **why payload strongly limits multirotor endurance**.

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right UAV Class

| Mission | Recommended Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Indoor inspection | **Nano / Micro (< 250 g)** | Safe, low kinetic energy, fits tight spaces |
| Crop spraying | **Heavy multirotor (> 25 kg)** | High payload (tanks) required |
| Border surveillance | **MALE fixed-wing** | 24 h endurance, satellite datalink |
| Urban package delivery | **Light multirotor (2–5 kg)** | VTOL, hover-capable, GPS-rich |
| High-altitude mapping | **HALE** | Above weather, long dwell time |
| Swarm operations | **Mini (< 5 kg)** | Cheap, expendable, redundant |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: UAV Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UAV SYSTEMS] --> B[By Mass]
    A --> C[By Altitude and Endurance]
    A --> D[By Wing Configuration]
    A --> E[By Mission Profile]

    B --> B1[Nano / Micro less than 250 g]
    B --> B2[Mini 250 g to 5 kg]

    B --> B4[Medium Tactical 25 to 150 kg]
    B --> B5[Heavy 150 to 600 kg]
    B --> B6[MALE 600 to 2000 kg]
    B --> B7[Strategic HALE greater than 2000 kg]

    C --> C1[LASE less than 1500 m]
    C --> C2[LALE less than 3000 m]
    C --> C3[MALE 3000 to 9000 m]
    C --> C4[HALE greater than 9000 m]

    D --> D1[Fixed Wing]
```


---

### 🗺️ Diagram 3: Mass vs Altitude Class Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
    M --> Q3[Q3 Tactical plus MALE: Surveillance]
    M --> Q4[Q4 Strategic plus HALE: ISR plus Comms Relay]

    Q1 --> R1[Example: Crazyflie]
    Q2 --> R2[Example: DJI Agras]
    Q3 --> R3[Example: MQ-9 Reaper]
    Q4 --> R4[Example: RQ-4 Global Hawk]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between MALE and HALE UAVs with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | MALE | HALE |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Medium Altitude Long Endurance | High Altitude Long Endurance |
| Service Ceiling | 3,000 – 9,000 m | > 9,000 m |
| Endurance | ~ 24 h | 24 – 48 h |
| Example | MQ-9 Reaper | RQ-4 Global Hawk |
| Typical Role | Persistent tactical ISR | Strategic surveillance & comms relay |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term "Nano UAV" and list two applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Nano UAV** is an unmanned aerial system with a maximum take-off weight **below 250 grams**, typically palm-sized, designed for short-range, low-speed operations.
**Applications:**
1. Indoor reconnaissance in GPS-denied environments.
2. Swarm research and education (e.g., Crazyflie platforms).

---

### 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** *Classify UAVs based on (i) mass, (ii) altitude and endurance, and (iii) wing configuration. Provide at least one example for each sub-class.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Mass** — *3 marks*
- **Nano / Micro** (< 250 g): e.g., Black Hornet Nano
- **Mini** (250 g – 5 kg): e.g., DJI Mavic
- **Tactical** (5 – 150 kg): e.g., RQ-7 Shadow
- **MALE / HALE** (> 600 kg): e.g., MQ-9 Reaper

**(ii) Classification by Altitude & Endurance** — *2 marks*
- **LASE**, **LALE**, **MALE**, **HALE** — defined in Section 1.2.

**(iii) Classification by Wing Configuration** — *2 marks*
- **Fixed-wing** (e.g., Global Hawk) — long endurance.
- **Rotary-wing** (e.g., quadcopter) — hover & VTOL.
- **Flapping-wing** (e.g., RoboBee) — bio-inspired micro UAVs.
- **Hybrid VTOL** (e.g., Tilt-rotor) — combines both.

---

**(b)** *A fixed-wing reconnaissance UAV has the following parameters: MTOW $W_i = 50$ kg, final weight after fuel burn $W_f = 35$ kg, lift-to-drag ratio $L/D = 18$, propeller efficiency $\eta_{prop} = 0.75$, and specific fuel consumption $c_b = 0.00035$ kg/W·s. Compute the endurance using the Breguet equation.* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Given:
- $W_i = 50$ kg, $W_f = 35$ kg
- $L/D = 18$
- $\eta_{prop} = 0.75$
- $c_b = 0.00035$ kg/W·s

Apply the Breguet endurance formula:

$$E = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{b}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{f}}\right)$$

Step 1 — Compute logarithmic term:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{50}{35}\right) = \ln(1.4286) = 0.3567$$

Step 2 — Substitute into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{0.75}{0.00035} \cdot 18 \cdot 0.3567 \\
  &= 2142.86 \cdot 18 \cdot 0.3567 \\
  &= 38571.4 \cdot 0.3567 \\
  &= 13760.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Convert to hours:

$$E = \dfrac{13760.5}{3600} \approx 3.82 \text{ hours}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: Endurance ≈ 3.82 hours (≈ 3 h 49 min)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *Explain the four major categories of UAVs based on mission profile. Discuss the Indian regulatory framework (Digital Sky Platform / MHA classification).* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**
- **Civil/Commercial** — Aerial photography, surveying.
- **Military/Defence** — ISR, combat (UCAV).
- **Scientific/Research** — Atmospheric studies, swarm experiments.
- **Logistics/Delivery** — Medical, e-commerce.
- **Indian Context:** DGCA's **Digital Sky Platform** mandates UIN (Unmanned Aircraft Identification Number) and UAOP (Unmanned Aircraft Operator Permit). Five weight categories: **Nano (< 250 g), Micro (250 g–2 kg), Small (2–25 kg), Medium (25–150 kg), Large (> 150 kg)**.

**(b)** *A quadcopter weighs 4 kg (including batteries) and uses four rotors, each with disc area $A_{rotor} = 0.07$ m². Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Estimate the ideal hover power and total endurance if a 5,200 mAh, 22.2 V LiPo battery is used (assume 80% usable capacity).* **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Hover Power Calculation:**

$$P_{hover} = \dfrac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}$$

For total rotor disc area = $4 \times 0.07 = 0.28$ m²:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{hover} &= \dfrac{(4 \times 9.81)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.28}} \\
          &= \dfrac{(39.24)^{1.5}}{\sqrt{0.686}} \\
          &= \dfrac{245.96}{0.8282} \\
          &\approx 297 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Endurance Calculation:**

Usable energy = $0.80 \times 5.2 \text{ Ah} \times 22.2 \text{ V} = 92.35$ Wh

$$E = \dfrac{92.35 \text{ Wh}}{297 \text{ W}} \times 60 = 18.65 \text{ minutes}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: $P_{hover} \approx 297$ W, Endurance ≈ 18.6 minutes**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatches** — forgetting to convert seconds → hours or kg → N in hover-power equations.
> 2. **Mixing frameworks** — citing FAA classes in a question that asks for Indian MHA / DGCA categories.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Breguet** — wrong integration bounds.
> 4. **Ignoring usable battery fraction** — claiming full 100% energy is available (typical usable = 80%).
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — board examiners deduct 1–2 marks if ideal-disk / no-blade-profile-drag assumptions are unstated.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Five canonical mass classes:** Nano, Mini, Light Tactical, Medium Tactical, Heavy/Strategic.
- ✅ **Four altitude-endurance classes:** LASE, LALE, MALE, HALE.
- ✅ **Four wing configurations:** Fixed, Rotary, Flapping, Hybrid VTOL.
- ✅ **Breguet Endurance:** $E = \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_b} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_i / W_f)$ — *high-yield derivation.*
- ✅ **Hover Power (multirotor):** $P_{hover} = \dfrac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{rotor}}}$ — *scales with mass$^{3/2}$.*
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio:** $AR > 12$ → long-endurance fixed-wing.
- ✅ **Indian DGCA Digital Sky** — five weight categories, UIN/UAOP mandatory.
- ✅ **Breguet Range** is for range; **Breguet Endurance** is for time — *do NOT confuse the two.*
- ✅ Always mention **assumptions** (ideal disk, no wind, level cruise) when applying formulae.
- ✅ Mention **NATO STANAG 4670** when citing weight classes in board answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Aerodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Aerodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Aerodynamics?

**Aerodynamics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies the motion of air (and other gases) and the forces acting on solid bodies moving through air or stationary in an airflow. In the context of Aerial Robotics, aerodynamics provides the foundational physics that govern how unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), drones, and other flying platforms generate lift, overcome drag, maneuver, and achieve stable flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** Aerodynamics is the study of the dynamics of air and the interaction of moving air with solid surfaces, including phenomena such as lift, drag, flow separation, and turbulence. For aerial robotics, it is the engineering discipline that determines the *why* behind flight — without it, no rotor, wing, or control surface would function.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "River of Air"

Imagine the atmosphere as a slow-moving, invisible **river of air** flowing around every object. A bird's wing, a drone propeller, and a fixed-wing aircraft are all just specialized "boats" navigating this invisible river.

- When the river flows **smoothly** over a well-shaped surface, the object experiences **low resistance** (low drag).
- When the river hits a **flat wall** head-on, it creates a huge splash of pressure (high drag).
- A curved surface (like an airfoil) *partners* with the air, deflecting it downward, and the air *reacts* by pushing the surface **upward** — that is **lift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Newton's Third Law Heart of Flight:** A wing does *not* "suck" the aircraft up. It pushes air down. The air pushes the wing (and the aircraft) up with an equal and opposite force. Lift is a *reaction force*, not a suction force.

## 1.3 Key Properties of the Atmosphere (Standard Air)

For KTU-level problems, the atmosphere is modeled using the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)**. The following constants are assumed at **sea level (altitude = 0 m)**:

| Property | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Air Density | $\rho$ | **1.225** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Speed of sound | $a$ | **340.29** | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{m}^2\text{/s}$ |
| Air Pressure (sea level) | $p$ | **101,325** | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Temperature (sea level) | $T$ | **288.15** | $\text{K}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\rho$ matters most for drones:** Lift and drag forces are *directly proportional* to air density. That is why quadcopters fly poorly at high altitudes (e.g., mountains) or on hot, humid days — the air is "thinner" (lower $\rho$), so the propellers must spin faster to generate the same force.

## 1.4 Fundamental Concepts in Aerial Robotics Aerodynamics

1. **Airfoil** — A specially shaped cross-section (wing, propeller blade, rotor blade) designed to generate lift efficiently.
2. **Chord Line** — The straight line connecting the leading edge to the trailing edge of an airfoil.
3. **Angle of Attack (AoA, $\alpha$)** — The angle between the chord line and the oncoming relative wind.
4. **Relative Wind** — The apparent airflow direction as seen by the moving aircraft.
5. **Lift ($L$)** — The aerodynamic force perpendicular to the relative wind.
6. **Drag ($D$)** — The aerodynamic force parallel to the relative wind, opposing motion.
7. **Thrust ($T$)** — The forward (or upward for rotors) force produced by propulsion.
8. **Boundary Layer** — The thin layer of air hugging the surface where viscous effects dominate.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERAT745 Module 1):** Students must be able to define and differentiate between *steady* and *unsteady* aerodynamics, *incompressible* vs *compressible* flow, and *laminar* vs *turbulent* flow. These classifications determine which equations can be applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Aerodynamic Forces

Every flying vehicle in steady, level flight must balance **four forces**:

| Force | Symbol | Direction (Fixed-Wing) | Direction (Rotorcraft/Drone) |
|-------|--------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
| Lift | $L$ | Upward (perpendicular to flight path) | Generated by rotating blades |
| Weight | $W$ | Downward | Downward (acts at CG) |
| Thrust | $T$ | Forward | Produced by propellers/rotors |
| Drag | $D$ | Backward (opposes motion) | Aerodynamic + profile resistance |

**Equilibrium in Hovering (Drone):**
- $T = W = m \cdot g$ (Thrust balances weight exactly)
- $L = D = 0$ (No net forward motion → no aerodynamic lift or drag on body)

**Equilibrium in Level Forward Flight (Fixed-Wing):**
- Vertical: $L = W$
- Horizontal: $T = D$

## 2.2 Bernoulli's Principle — The Heart of Lift

**Daniel Bernoulli's principle** states that for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow, an *increase in velocity* of the fluid is accompanied by a *decrease in pressure*.

Mathematically:

$$p + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 + \rho g h = \text{constant (along a streamline)}$$

Where:
- $p$ = Static pressure (Pa)
- $\rho$ = Air density ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $V$ = Flow velocity ($\text{m/s}$)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration
- $h$ = Height above reference (m)

The term $\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ is the **Dynamic Pressure** (denoted $q$).

## 2.3 Airfoil Geometry — The Vocabulary You Must Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** Be able to *label* a diagram of an airfoil and define every part.

- **Leading Edge** — The front-most point of the airfoil where air first contacts it.
- **Trailing Edge** — The rear-most point where airflow leaves the airfoil.
- **Chord ($c$)** — Straight-line distance from leading edge to trailing edge.
- **Camber** — The curvature of the mean line; a *symmetric* airfoil has zero camber.
- **Upper Surface (Suction Side)** — The curved, higher-velocity, lower-pressure surface.
- **Lower Surface (Pressure Side)** — The flatter, higher-pressure surface.
- **Mean Camber Line** — The line equidistant between upper and lower surfaces.

## 2.4 Angle of Attack and the Stall Phenomenon

- As the **angle of attack (AoA, $\alpha$)** increases from $0°$ to about $15°–20°$, lift increases nearly linearly.
- Beyond the **critical angle of attack**, the airflow can no longer follow the upper surface → it **separates** → this is the **stall**.
- Stall results in a **sudden loss of lift** and a **huge increase in pressure drag**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Drone Stall Reality:** A multi-rotor UAV can theoretically "stall" an individual rotor blade near the root (where local AoA is highest), causing a sudden drop in thrust. This is a known cause of in-flight accidents in aggressive maneuvers.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Aerodynamics Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — at least 60% of KTU numerical questions use these directly.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / When to Use |
|---|-------------|----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Basis of all force equations |
| 2 | Lift Force | $L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$ | $S$ = wing area, $C_L$ = lift coefficient |
| 3 | Drag Force | $D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient |
| 4 | Lift Coefficient | $C_L = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Non-dimensional force |
| 5 | Drag Coefficient | $C_D = \frac{D}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Non-dimensional force |
| 6 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho V c}{\mu} = \frac{V c}{\nu}$ | Predicts flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 7 | Mach Number | $M = \frac{V}{a}$ | Compressibility check (M < 0.3 = incompressible) |
| 8 | Thrust Required (Hover) | $T = m g$ | Multi-rotor drone vertical equilibrium |
| 9 | Power Required (Hover) | $P = T \cdot V_i$ (induced velocity) | Hover power |
| 10 | Static Pressure Relation | $p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$ | Bernoulli (no height change) |
| 11 | Lift Slope (2D thin airfoil) | $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$ | $\alpha$ in radians; ideal inviscid |
| 12 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$ | Wing efficiency metric |

> **Unit Check Tip for KTU:** Always keep $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $V$ in $\text{m/s}$, $S$ in $\text{m}^2$, $c$ in meters. Output force will be in **Newtons (N)**.

## 2.6 Reynolds Number — The "Flow Personality" Indicator

The **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** is a dimensionless quantity that predicts whether airflow is smooth (laminar) or chaotic (turbulent).

- $Re < 230,000$ → typically **Laminar** flow
- $Re > 500,000$ → typically **Turbulent** flow
- $230{,}000 < Re < 500{,}000$ → **Transitional**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Aerial Robotics:** Small drones (10–30 cm propellers) operate at **low Reynolds numbers** ($10^4$ to $10^5$). At this scale, air behaves *very differently* than for full-size aircraft. Aerofoils that work perfectly on Boeing 737s may perform poorly on a quadcopter blade.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lift from Bernoulli's Equation (Classical Airfoil Argument)

This is the **most commonly asked derivation in KTU exams** — practice it until you can write it on autopilot.

**Given:**
- Air flows over an airfoil split into an upper streamline and a lower streamline.
- Both streams start at the same point (far ahead of the airfoil) where pressure = $p_\infty$ and velocity = $V_\infty$.
- The upper streamline arrives at the trailing edge with velocity $V_u$ (faster).
- The lower streamline arrives at the trailing edge with velocity $V_l$ (slower).
- The two streams meet at the trailing edge (Kutta condition, both at $p_\infty$).

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation along the **lower** streamline (assume negligible height change):

$$p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = p_l + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_l^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bernoulli's equation along the **upper** streamline:

$$p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = p_u + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_u^2$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the pressure on the lower surface ($p_l$):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_l &= p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 - \frac{1}{2} \rho V_l^2 \\
    &= p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_l^2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the pressure on the upper surface ($p_u$):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_u &= p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 - \frac{1}{2} \rho V_u^2 \\
    &= p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_u^2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the net pressure difference (lift-promoting):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= p_l - p_u \\
         &= \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_l^2 \right) - \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_u^2 \right) \\
         &= \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_u^2 - V_l^2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by wing area $S$ to obtain total lift:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \Delta p \cdot S \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \rho S \left( V_u^2 - V_l^2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Since $V_u > V_l$ on a cambered airfoil, $\Delta p > 0$ → **lift is positive (upward)**. This is the famous "faster air → lower pressure → suction" argument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Caveat:** The "equal transit time" assumption (that air over the upper and lower surfaces takes the *same time*) is actually **FALSE** in real life. Real lift generation involves the **circulation theory** (Kutta-Joukowski theorem), but Bernoulli's principle still correctly predicts the *pressure difference* that creates lift. KTU questions generally use the Bernoulli explanation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lift Coefficient from Pressure Distribution

**Step 1:** Define the *pressure coefficient* $C_p$ at any point on the airfoil:

$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate the pressure coefficient over the upper and lower surfaces to get the net force per unit span:

$$L' = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 \int_{LE}^{TE} \left( C_{p,l} - C_{p,u} \right) \, dx$$

Where $L'$ is lift per unit span (N/m) and $x$ is the chord-wise coordinate.

**Step 3:** Define lift coefficient (non-dimensional):

$$C_L = \frac{L}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S} = \frac{1}{c} \int_0^c \left( C_{p,l}(x) - C_{p,u}(x) \right) \, dx$$

**Step 4:** For a thin, flat plate at small angle of attack, theoretical result (potential flow theory):

$$C_L = 2\pi \alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is in radians. This is the **Thin Airfoil Theory** result and is the basis for the *lift curve slope* used extensively in aircraft design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant physical quantities for viscous flow over a body:
- Fluid density $\rho$ ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- Velocity $V$ ($\text{m/s}$)
- Characteristic length $L$ (e.g., chord $c$) (m)
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ ($\text{kg/(m·s)}$)

**Step 2:** Assume the inertial force $\propto \rho V^2 L^2$ and the viscous force $\propto \mu V L$.

**Step 3:** Their ratio (a dimensionless group) gives the Reynolds number:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} \\
   &= \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L} \\
   &= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** For aerodynamics, $L = c$ (chord length), so:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V c}{\mu} = \frac{V c}{\nu}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Low $Re$ → Viscous forces dominate → flow sticks to surface (laminar, creeping flow)
- High $Re$ → Inertial forces dominate → flow becomes turbulent

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Calculation (KTU-style):** A small drone with chord $c = 0.05 \text{ m}$ flies at $V = 12 \text{ m/s}$. Compute $Re$:
> $$Re = \frac{1.225 \times 12 \times 0.05}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.735}{1.789 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 41{,}080$$
> This is **low Reynolds number** — characteristic of micro-aerial vehicles (MAVs).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Required for Hover (Multi-Rotor Drone)

**Step 1:** For a single rotor in hover, the thrust equals weight: $T = W = mg$.

**Step 2:** The rotor induces a downward air velocity $V_i$ (induced velocity) through the rotor disk area $A$.

**Step 3:** By momentum theory, the thrust is:

$$T = 2 \rho A V_i^2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_i$:

$$V_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{mg}{2 \rho A}}$$

**Step 5:** The ideal power required (no profile drag) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= T \cdot V_i = mg \cdot \sqrt{\frac{mg}{2 \rho A}} \\
  &= \sqrt{\frac{(mg)^3}{2 \rho A}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** For a quadcopter (4 identical rotors), total disk area is $4A$, so:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\frac{(mg)^3}{2 \rho \cdot 4A}} = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(mg)^3}{2 \rho A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **fundamental reason larger drones are more energy-efficient per kg of payload**. Power scales as $W^{3/2}$, so doubling the weight increases required power by only $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83\times$. This is also why heavy-lift drones use *fewer, larger* rotors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Airfoil Cross-Section — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow of aerodynamic interaction** with an airfoil, serving as a conceptual substitute for a hand-drawn cross-section diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ONCOMING AIR STREAM] --> B[LEADING EDGE OF AIRFOIL]
    B --> C[UPPER SURFACE CURVATURE]
    B --> D[LOWER SURFACE]
    C --> E[ACCELERATED AIRFLOW]
    D --> F[SLOWED AIRFLOW]
    E --> G[LOW STATIC PRESSURE]
    F --> H[HIGH STATIC PRESSURE]
    G --> I[NET UPWARD FORCE LIFT L]
    H --> I
    I --> J[LIFT ACTS AT CENTER OF PRESSURE]
    J --> K[FORCE TRANSMITTED TO WING STRUCTURE]
    F --> L[FRICTION AND FORM LOSSES]
    L --> M[DRAG FORCE D OPPOSES MOTION]
    M --> N[AERODYNAMIC HEATING MINOR]
    E --> O[BOUNDARY LAYER DEVELOPMENT]
    O --> P[FLOW SEPARATION AT HIGH ANGLE]
    P --> Q[STALL CONDITION]
```

## 4.2 Force Balance Block Diagram — Steady Level Flight (Fixed-Wing UAV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VERTICAL_FORCES
        L[LIFT L equals half rho V squared S C sub L] --> EQ1[VERTICAL EQUILIBRIUM L equals W]
        W[WEIGHT W equals m times g] --> EQ1
    end
    subgraph HORIZONTAL_FORCES
        T[THRUST T equals half rho V squared S C sub T] --> EQ2[HORIZONTAL EQUILIBRIUM T equals D]
        D[DRAG D equals half rho V squared S C sub D] --> EQ2
    end
    EQ1 --> STABLE[STEADY LEVEL FLIGHT]
    EQ2 --> STABLE
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Aerodynamic Force Generation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[MOTION OF UAV] --> REL[RELATIVE WIND GENERATED]
    IN2[AIR DENSITY RHO] --> CALC
    IN3[VELOCITY V] --> CALC
    IN4[REFERENCE AREA S] --> CALC
    IN5[AIRFOIL SHAPE] --> COEF[COEFFICIENTS C sub L and C sub D]
    COEF --> CALC
    REL --> CALC[FORCE EQUATIONS L and D]
    CALC --> L_OUT[LIFT OUTPUT L]
    CALC --> D_OUT[DRAG OUTPUT D]
    L_OUT --> CTRL[FLIGHT CONTROLLER]
    D_OUT --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACTUATOR[MOTOR AND SERVO ACTUATION]
    ACTUATOR --> IN1
```

```mermaid

    PROP --> BER[BERNOULLI PRINCIPLE]

    BER --> LIFT[LIFT AND DRAG]

```
