# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a)** Angle of Attack
**(b)** Reynolds Number
**(c)** Boundary Layer

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Angle of Attack (AoA, $\alpha$):** It is the angle formed between the **chord line** of an airfoil and the **direction of the relative wind** (oncoming airflow). For example, when a commercial aircraft tilts its nose upward before takeoff, the angle of attack of its wings increases, thereby generating more lift.

**(b) Reynolds Number ($Re$):** It is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. Mathematically, $Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$. For example, $Re \approx 10^5$ for a small drone propeller but $Re \approx 10^7$ for a commercial airliner wing.

**(c) Boundary Layer:** It is the thin layer of fluid (air) immediately adjacent to a solid surface in which the fluid velocity changes from zero (at the surface, due to the no-slip condition) to the free-stream velocity. For example, the air within a few millimeters of a drone's propeller blade is in the boundary layer.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**State Bernoulli's principle and write its mathematical form for an incompressible, inviscid fluid along a horizontal streamline.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Bernoulli's principle states that for a steady, incompressible, and inviscid flow, the sum of static pressure, dynamic pressure, and hydrostatic pressure remains constant along a streamline.

**Mathematical Form (Horizontal Streamline, $h$ = constant):**

$$p + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = \text{constant}$$

Or equivalently:

$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2$$

**Significance:** This principle forms the basis for explaining lift generation over an airfoil — the higher velocity on the upper surface corresponds to lower static pressure, and vice versa, creating a net upward force.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Full Question]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of an airfoil and derive the expression for lift force using **Bernoulli's principle**. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small fixed-wing UAV weighing **12 kg** is flying at a steady horizontal velocity of **18 m/s** at sea level. The wing area is **0.6 m²** and the lift coefficient is **0.45**. Determine:
  (i) The lift force generated.
  (ii) Whether the UAV is in **vertical equilibrium** (i.e., does lift balance weight?).  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Airfoil Working and Derivation:**

**Diagram (describe):** A typical airfoil cross-section showing the leading edge, trailing edge, chord line, upper (curved) surface, lower (flatter) surface, and relative wind direction with angle of attack $\alpha$.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, and inviscid flow.
2. Air density $\rho$ = constant.
3. Negligible height change between stagnation point and trailing edge.
4. Air streams on upper and lower surfaces meet at the trailing edge (Kutta condition).

**Derivation:**

Let the free-stream conditions be $p_\infty$ and $V_\infty$.

Applying Bernoulli's equation to the **upper surface** (velocity $V_u$):

$$p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = p_u + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_u^2$$

Solving for $p_u$:

$$p_u = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_u^2 \right)$$

Applying Bernoulli's equation to the **lower surface** (velocity $V_l$):

$$p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = p_l + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_l^2$$

Solving for $p_l$:

$$p_l = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_l^2 \right)$$

**Net pressure difference:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= p_l - p_u \\
         &= \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_l^2 \right) - \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_\infty^2 - V_u^2 \right) \\
         &= \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( V_u^2 - V_l^2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since the upper surface is more curved, $V_u > V_l$, hence $\Delta p > 0$ (lift is upward).

**Total Lift (multiplying by wing area $S$):**

$$\boxed{L = \frac{1}{2} \rho S \left( V_u^2 - V_l^2 \right)}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 12 \text{ kg}$
- Velocity $V = 18 \text{ m/s}$
- Wing area $S = 0.6 \text{ m}^2$
- Lift coefficient $C_L = 0.45$
- Sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Lift Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (18)^2 \times 0.6 \times 0.45 \\
  &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 324 \times 0.6 \times 0.45 \\
  &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 324 \times 0.27 \\
  &= 53.59 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step sub-calculation:**
- $\frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 = 0.6125$
- $0.6125 \times 324 = 198.45$
- $198.45 \times 0.6 = 119.07$
- $119.07 \times 0.45 = 53.58 \text{ N}$

$$\boxed{L \approx 53.58 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Check Vertical Equilibrium:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= m \cdot g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N} \\
L &= 53.58 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $L \ll W$ ($53.58 \text{ N} \ll 117.72 \text{ N}$), the UAV is **NOT in vertical equilibrium**. The lift is less than the weight, meaning the aircraft will **descend** at this speed and AoA. To maintain level flight, the UAV must either increase its speed, increase its angle of attack (to raise $C_L$), or both.

$$\boxed{\text{Lift} \neq \text{Weight} \implies \text{UAV is descending, NOT in level flight.}}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Alternative]`
**(a)** Define **Reynolds number**. Explain its significance in low-speed aerodynamics and derive it using dimensional analysis. Discuss the typical range of $Re$ for micro aerial vehicles (MAVs) versus full-scale aircraft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **multi-rotor drone** of total mass **4 kg** hovers in still air. The four rotors together have a total disk area of **0.08 m²**. Using the **momentum theory of rotors**:
  (i) Calculate the **induced velocity** $V_i$ of the air below the rotor.
  (ii) Calculate the **ideal power required** to hover.  
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Reynolds Number — Definition, Derivation, Significance:**

**Definition:** The Reynolds number $Re$ is a dimensionless parameter representing the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

where $\rho$ is density, $V$ is velocity, $L$ is characteristic length (chord $c$ for airfoils), $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity.

**Dimensional Analysis Derivation:**

Let $Re = \rho^a V^b L^c \mu^d$. For dimensional consistency:

$$[M^0 L^0 T^0] = \left( \frac{M}{L^3} \right)^a \left( \frac{L}{T} \right)^b (L)^c \left( \frac{M}{L T} \right)^d$$

Comparing powers:
- $M$: $a + d = 0 \implies d = -a$
- $T$: $-b - d = 0 \implies d = -b$
- $L$: $-3a + b + c - d = 0$

Setting $a = 1$: $d = -1$, $b = 1$, $c = 1 - (-3) - 1 + 1 \cdot \text{adjustment}$. Solving fully: $c = 1$. Therefore:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Significance:**
- $Re < 230,000$ → **Laminar** (smooth) flow
- $Re > 500,000$ → **Turbulent** (chaotic) flow
- For MAVs (5–25 cm blades), $Re \approx 10^4$ to $10^5$ (low Reynolds regime)
- For full-scale aircraft, $Re \approx 10^6$ to $10^7$ (high Reynolds regime)

**Engineering Implication:** At low $Re$, viscous effects dominate, and standard airfoils behave inefficiently. MAVs often need *custom low-$Re$ airfoils* with sharp leading edges and high camber.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 4 \text{ kg}$
- $A = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Induced Velocity:**

Thrust required in hover: $T = mg = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ N}$

From momentum theory:

$$V_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_i &= \sqrt{\frac{39.24}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.08}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\frac{39.24}{0.196}} \\
    &= \sqrt{200.20} \\
    &= 14.15 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_i \approx 14.15 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Ideal Hover Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= T \cdot V_i = mg \cdot V_i \\
  &= 39.24 \times 14.15 \\
  &= 555.25 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 555.25 \text{ W} \approx 0.555 \text{ kW}}$$

**Verification (alternative formula):**

$$P = \sqrt{\frac{(mg)^3}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{(39.24)^3}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.08}} = \sqrt{\frac{60{,}415}{0.196}} = \sqrt{308{,}240} \approx 555.2 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Units Trap:** Students frequently forget to convert $V$ from km/h to m/s. If $V = 72 \text{ km/h}$, the correct value is $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$ — examiners *will* deduct marks.
> 2. **Sign Errors in Bernoulli:** Mixing up $p_u$ and $p_l$ is a common 2-mark loss. Always state *clearly* that upper surface has higher velocity → lower pressure.
> 3. **Reynolds Number Units:** Forgetting $\mu$ in the denominator is a recurring error. Write the full expression every time.
> 4. **Stall Misconception:** Some students write "stall = no lift." This is wrong — stall is *loss of attached flow*, not zero lift. Partial lift still exists below $C_{L,\max}$.
> 5. **Incompressible Assumption:** Bernoulli is valid for $M < 0.3$. KTU does not ask supersonic questions in Module 1, but mentioning this assumption *earns you bonus marks*.
> 6. **Forgetting Square on Velocity:** The dynamic pressure has $V^2$, not $V$. This is the #1 source of numerical errors in lift/drag problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Four Forces of Flight:** Lift ($L$), Weight ($W$), Thrust ($T$), Drag ($D$) — must be balanced in steady level flight: $L = W$, $T = D$.
- ✅ **Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal):** $p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$.
- ✅ **Dynamic Pressure:** $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — the cornerstone of all force formulas.
- ✅ **Lift Formula:** $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$. **Memorize the order: $\rho \rightarrow V^2 \rightarrow S \rightarrow C_L$.**
- ✅ **Drag Formula:** $D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \frac{\rho V c}{\mu} = \frac{V c}{\nu}$. Low $Re$ for MAVs, high $Re$ for aircraft.
- ✅ **Mach Number:** $M = V/a$. Incompressible flow valid for $M < 0.3$.
- ✅ **Airfoil Parts:** Leading edge, trailing edge, chord, camber, upper/lower surfaces — must be able to label a diagram.
- ✅ **Stall:** Sudden lift loss at critical AoA due to flow separation, NOT a complete loss of lift.
- ✅ **Hovering Drone:** $T = mg$, $P = mg \cdot V_i$, $V_i = \sqrt{\frac{mg}{2\rho A}}$.
- ✅ **ISA Sea-Level Values:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $a = 340.29 \text{ m/s}$.
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^2/S$ — higher $AR$ → lower induced drag (glider-like).
- ✅ **Thin Airfoil Theory:** $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$ (for small $\alpha$ in radians, flat plate).
- ✅ **Newton's 3rd Law Basis:** Lift is a *reaction* to the wing deflecting air downward — not suction.
- ✅ **Standard Atmosphere Assumptions:** Air is a perfect gas, hydrostatic equilibrium, no moisture — used universally in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Stability in Aerial Robotics?

In the context of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), **stability** is defined as the inherent tendency of an aircraft to return to its equilibrium state (trim condition) after being subjected to a small disturbance, without any continuous input from the pilot or autopilot system. In mathematical terms, a UAV is considered stable if all bounded input disturbances produce bounded output responses (BIBO stability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus):** *"Stability is the property of an aerial system to develop restoring forces or moments when displaced from an equilibrium state, and to eventually damp out oscillations and return to the original trim condition."*

Stability is broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Static Stability** – The initial tendency of the vehicle to return to equilibrium after a disturbance. Think of it as the *first push back*.
2. **Dynamic Stability** – The overall time-domain behavior of the vehicle after a disturbance, including how the oscillations decay over time (damped, sustained, or divergent).

## 1.2 What is Control in Aerial Robotics?

**Control** in aerial robotics refers to the active manipulation of the UAV's control surfaces (aileron, elevator, rudder) and/or thrust vectoring mechanisms to achieve a desired flight state. The control system continuously measures the actual state of the vehicle, compares it with the desired reference state, computes an error signal, and generates corrective commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A stable UAV *naturally* wants to return to trim. An *unstable* but *controllable* UAV can be kept flying only through continuous autopilot corrections (e.g., a quadrotor in hover).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Broomstick vs. The Pendulum

Imagine balancing a **broomstick upside-down on the tip of your finger**. This is analogous to a quadrotor in hover. It is *inherently unstable* and requires constant, continuous corrections by your hand (the autopilot) to remain upright.

Now imagine a **pendulum hanging from a string**. Push it slightly and it swings back to rest. This is a *statically and dynamically stable* system — equivalent to a fixed-wing aircraft in cruise.

> [!TIP]
> A **rock throwing competition analogy**: A stable rocket (one with fins) will naturally straighten out mid-air (like a badminton shuttlecock). An unstable rocket will tumble uncontrollably. Control is the *active guidance* that can still steer an unstable vehicle along a desired trajectory.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following table summarizes critical metrics used in UAV stability analysis:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard sea-level air density | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | dimensionless (0 to 1 for stable) |
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n$ | rad/s |
| Time constant | $\tau$ | seconds |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Stability in Fixed-Wing UAVs

A fixed-wing aircraft's stability is analyzed along three independent axes:

### 2.1.1 Longitudinal Stability (Pitch Axis)
- Pertains to motion in the **vertical plane** (pitching moment).
- Governed by the **horizontal tailplane** and the position of the center of gravity (CG) relative to the **neutral point (NP)**.
- The static margin, defined as $\text{SM} = \frac{\text{NP} - \text{CG}}{\text{MAC}}$, is a key parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** For a conventionally stable aircraft, the **CG must lie ahead of the Neutral Point**. A positive static margin (typically **5%–15%** of MAC) is preferred for safe operation.

### 2.1.2 Lateral Stability (Roll Axis)
- Perturbs motion about the **longitudinal (roll) axis**.
- Achieved primarily through **dihedral effect** (upward inclination of wings).
- A positive dihedral angle provides restoring roll moments.

### 2.1.3 Directional Stability (Yaw Axis)
- Perturbs motion about the **vertical (yaw) axis**.
- Provided by the **vertical fin / rudder**.
- Weathercock stability ensures the nose aligns with the relative airflow.

## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Control Architecture

Every UAV autopilot implements a **Negative Feedback Control Loop**. The fundamental block diagram representation is:

**Reference Input → Comparator (Error = r − y) → Controller → Actuator → Plant (UAV) → Sensor → Output**

For a multirotor, the plant is a multi-input multi-output (MIMO) system, but each axis (roll, pitch, yaw, altitude) is often decoupled into a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) loop for control design.

## 2.3 The PID Controller – Workhorse of Aerial Robotics

The Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller is the most widely deployed control law in UAV autopilots due to its simplicity and robustness.

The control output is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (handles present error → response speed)
- $K_i$ = Integral gain (handles past error → eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = Derivative gain (handles future error → improves damping)
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = tracking error

## 2.4 Transfer Function Representation

Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions), the PID controller in the **s-domain** becomes:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A pure derivative term $K_d s$ is non-causal and amplifies sensor noise. In practice, a **filtered derivative** is used: $\dfrac{K_d s}{\tau_f s + 1}$, where $\tau_f$ is typically **0.01 to 0.05 seconds**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Engineering Utility | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Static Margin | $\text{SM} = (\text{NP} - \text{CG}) / \text{MAC}$ | Ensures pitch stability | dimensionless |
| Damping Ratio (2nd order) | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | Determines overshoot / decay | dimensionless |
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Determines response speed | rad/s |
| Settling Time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx 4 / (\zeta \omega_n)$ | KTU favorite exam formula | seconds |
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$ | Core control law | varies |
| PID Transfer Function | $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | s-domain design | varies |
| Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s) C(s)}{1 + G(s) C(s)}$ | Stability & tracking | dimensionless |
| Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s) C(s) = 0$ | Routh-Hurwitz, Root Locus | polynomial in s |
| Routh-Hurwitz Condition (2nd) | $a_0, a_1, a_2 > 0$ | Necessary stability condition | coefficient signs |
| Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = 1 / (1 + K_p)$ | Tuning criterion | dimensionless |
| Rise Time | $t_r \approx 1.8 / \omega_n$ | Response speed metric | seconds |
| Percent Overshoot | $\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Damping metric | percent |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts above are not merely academic — they are the foundation of:
- **Pixhawk / ArduPilot** firmware running on thousands of commercial drones.
- **NASA's X-57 Maxwell** electric experimental aircraft.
- **Aircraft certification** (FAA Part 23, EASA CS-23) where static margin, damping, and control authority must be rigorously demonstrated.
- **Autonomous swarms**, where each agent executes local PID loops while sharing state with neighbors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Characteristic Equation of a Closed-Loop UAV Pitch Loop

Consider a simplified **pitch dynamics model** of a fixed-wing UAV. The open-loop plant transfer function (from elevator deflection $\delta_e$ to pitch rate $q$) is approximated as:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_a}{s \, (s + a)}$$

Where $K_a$ is the actuator-aerodynamic gain and $a$ is the pitch rate damping coefficient (a positive real number for a stable open-loop plant).

Now we close the loop with a **PD controller** (proportional + derivative), which is the standard choice for the inner loop of a UAV autopilot:

$$C(s) = K_p + K_d \, s$$

### Step 1: Form the Open-Loop Transfer Function

We compute the product of the plant and the controller in the forward path:

$$L(s) = G(s) \, C(s) = \frac{K_a \, (K_p + K_d s)}{s \, (s + a)}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Negative Feedback Formula

For a standard unity negative feedback system, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$$

### Step 3: Combine Numerator and Denominator

Substituting the expression for $L(s)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K_a (K_p + K_d s)}{s(s+a)}}{1 + \dfrac{K_a (K_p + K_d s)}{s(s+a)}}$$

To clear the inner fraction, multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+a)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_a (K_p + K_d s)}{s(s+a) + K_a (K_p + K_d s)}$$

### Step 4: Expand the Denominator

The denominator is the **characteristic equation** of the closed loop. Let us expand it explicitly:

$$s(s+a) + K_a (K_p + K_d s) = s^2 + a s + K_a K_p + K_a K_d s$$

### Step 5: Collect Like Powers of s

Rearranging in descending order of $s$:

$$s^2 + (a + K_a K_d) s + K_a K_p = 0$$

### Step 6: Compare with the Standard Second-Order Form

The standard second-order characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

By **direct coefficient matching**, we obtain:

$$\omega_n^2 = K_a K_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{K_a K_p}$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = a + K_a K_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{a + K_a K_d}{2 \sqrt{K_a K_p}}$$

### Step 7: Engineering Interpretation of the Result

- The **proportional gain $K_p$** directly controls the natural frequency $\omega_n$. Increasing $K_p$ makes the system respond faster.
- The **derivative gain $K_d$** increases the effective damping $\zeta$. Increasing $K_d$ reduces overshoot and oscillations.
- For a critically damped response, we set $\zeta = 1$, which yields a specific $K_d$ for a given $K_p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Increasing $K_p$ alone speeds up the response but also *reduces* damping (since $\omega_n$ grows in the denominator of $\zeta$ faster than the numerator). This is why a **PD** (not just P) controller is essential for UAV inner loops.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Stability Criteria Comparison

| Criterion | Type | Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Routh-Hurwitz | Algebraic | Determine stability range of a gain | **Very High** |
| Root Locus | Graphical | Visualize pole trajectories as gain varies | **High** |
| Bode Plot | Frequency | Phase margin / Gain margin analysis | **High** |
| Nyquist | Frequency | Systems with time delays | Medium |
| Lyapunov | Nonlinear | Quadrotor attitude stability proofs | Medium |
| Lyapunov Linearization | Combined | Hover stability of multirotor | **High** |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Routh-Hurwitz Application

**Problem:** Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system with characteristic equation below is stable:

$$s^3 + 5 s^2 + 10 s + K = 0$$

### Step 1: Verify the Necessary Condition

For a third-order polynomial $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ to be stable, **all coefficients must be strictly positive**. Here, $1, 5, 10$ are positive, so the necessary condition is satisfied for $K > 0$.

### Step 2: Construct the Routh Array

The Routh array is constructed as follows:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $10$ |
| $s^2$ | $5$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $b_1$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $c_1$ | |

### Step 3: Compute the $s^1$ Row Entry

$$b_1 = \frac{(5)(10) - (1)(K)}{5} = \frac{50 - K}{5}$$

### Step 4: Compute the $s^0$ Row Entry

$$c_1 = \frac{(b_1)(K) - (5)(0)}{b_1} = K$$

### Step 5: Apply the Stability Condition

For stability, the **first column** must have all strictly positive entries:

- $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $5 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $b_1 > 0 \Rightarrow 50 - K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 50$
- $c_1 > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$

### Step 6: Final Answer

$$\boxed{0 < K < 50}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When $K = 50$, the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillations). When $K > 50$, the system is **unstable** (right-half-plane poles appear). Always state the critical $K$ value explicitly in your answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: UAV Closed-Loop Pitch Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the architecture of a typical UAV pitch control loop. Note that all node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels contain raw uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REF PITCH COMMAND] --> B[COMPARATOR SUM]
    B --> C[PID CONTROLLER KP KI KD]
    C --> D[ELEVATOR SERVO ACTUATOR]
    D --> E[UAV PITCH DYNAMICS PLANT G OF S]
    E --> F[PITCH RATE GYRO SENSOR]
    F --> G[FEEDBACK SIGNAL Y]
    G --> B
    H[WIND DISTURBANCE] -.-> E
    I[NOISE] -.-> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f0e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1f0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The reference pitch (A) is summed with the negative feedback from the gyro (F) at the comparator (B). The error passes through the PID controller (C), drives the elevator actuator (D), which deflects the elevator of the UAV (E). The aircraft's pitch rate is sensed by the gyro (F) and fed back. External disturbances (H) enter the plant, and sensor noise (I) corrupts the feedback path.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Quadrotor Cascaded Control

Quadrotors use a **cascaded (nested) control structure**. The outer loop commands attitude, and the inner loop commands body rates.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O1[OUTER LOOP POSITION CONTROLLER] --> O2[OUTER LOOP VELOCITY CONTROLLER]
    O2 --> O3[OUTER LOOP ATTITUDE CONTROLLER]
    O3 --> I1[INNER LOOP RATE CONTROLLER PID]
    I1 --> I2[MOTOR MIXER ALLOCATION]
    I2 --> I3[BLDC MOTORS AND ESCS]
    I3 --> I4[QUADROTOR PLANT DYNAMICS]
    I4 --> I5[IMU SENSOR FUSION]
    I5 --> I1
    I4 --> I6[GPS AND VISION SENSORS]
    I6 --> O1
    style O1 fill:#e1f0ff
    style O2 fill:#e1f0ff
    style O3 fill:#e1f0ff
    style I1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style I2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style I3 fill:#f0ffe1
    style I4 fill:#f0e1ff
    style I5 fill:#fff4e1
    style I6 fill:#fff4e1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Relevance:** The cascaded architecture is a frequent short-answer question. You must explicitly state that the **inner loop must run at a higher rate (e.g., 1 kHz)** than the outer loop (e.g., 100 Hz) for the time-scale separation principle to hold.

## 4.3 Stability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START WITH STATE SPACE OR TRANSFER FUNCTION] --> S2[DERIVE CHARACTERISTIC EQUATION]
    S2 --> S3[APPLY ROUTH HURWITZ FOR ALGEBRAIC RANGE]
    S2 --> S4[PLOT ROOT LOCUS FOR POLE TRAJECTORY]
    S2 --> S5[PLOT BODE FOR PHASE AND GAIN MARGIN]
    S3 --> S6[CONCLUDE STABLE UNSTABLE MARGINALLY STABLE]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[DESIGN COMPENSATOR IF NEEDED]
    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S2 fill:#fff4e1
    style S3 fill:#e1ffe1
    style S4 fill:#ffe1f0
    style S5 fill:#f0e1ff
    style S6 fill:#ffe1e1
    style S7 fill:#e1ffe1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Static vs. Dynamic Stability
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **static stability** and **dynamic stability** of an aerial vehicle. Give one example of an aircraft configuration that is statically stable but dynamically unstable.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Static stability** is the *initial* tendency of an aircraft to return to its equilibrium state after a small disturbance. (1 mark)
- **Dynamic stability** describes the *time-domain behavior* of the aircraft over the entire transient response, including whether oscillations decay, grow, or are sustained. (1 mark)
- **Example:** A high-tail aircraft (such as a transport jet) may be statically stable (initial restoring moment exists) but if the damping in pitch is insufficient, the resulting phugoid or short-period oscillations may *grow* with time, making it **dynamically unstable** despite being statically stable. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: PID Controller Action
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q2.** What are the three actions of a PID controller, and what aspect of the error does each one act upon?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Proportional (P) action:** $K_p e(t)$ — acts on the *present* error. Increases the speed of response and reduces rise time. (1 mark)
- **Integral (I) action:** $K_i \int e(t) dt$ — acts on the *past* accumulated error. Eliminates steady-state (offset) error. (1 mark)
- **Derivative (D) action:** $K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ — acts on the *rate of change* (future trend) of the error. Improves damping and reduces overshoot. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module Internal Choice

---

### Question Choice A: Cascade Control Architecture
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

> **Q3A.** (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascaded control architecture** used in a quadrotor UAV autopilot. Clearly identify the outer loop and inner loop and justify the choice of controller for each. (7 marks)
>
> (b) The pitch dynamics of a fixed-wing UAV is modeled as $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. A PD controller $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$ is used. Determine the values of $K_p$ and $K_d$ such that the closed-loop system has a natural frequency $\omega_n = 5$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

1. A quadrotor is **underactuated** (4 actuators for 6 DOF) and **inherently unstable** in hover, hence requires active control on all axes. (1 mark)
2. The **cascaded control** approach decomposes the control problem into nested loops, with the **outer loop running slower** and the **inner loop running faster**. (1 mark)
3. **Outer loop:** Position (X, Y, Z) → Velocity → Attitude. Typically uses a **P or PI** controller because position dynamics are slow and integral action removes steady-state position errors. (1 mark)
4. **Inner loop:** Body rates (roll rate, pitch rate, yaw rate). Typically uses a **PD** controller because rate dynamics are fast and derivative action provides critical damping. (1 mark)
5. The inner loop must run at **at least 4× to 10×** the rate of the outer loop to satisfy the **time-scale separation assumption**. (1 mark)
6. The **sensor fusion** (typically Extended Kalman Filter) combines IMU and GPS data to provide clean state estimates to each loop. (1 mark)
7. A labeled block diagram (such as the one in Section 4.2) should be drawn. (1 mark)

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, $C(s) = K_p + K_d s$.

**Step 1:** Form the open-loop transfer function $L(s) = G(s) C(s)$:

$$L(s) = \frac{10 (K_p + K_d s)}{s(s+2)} = \frac{10 K_d s + 10 K_p}{s^2 + 2s}$$

**Step 2:** Form the closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + L(s) = 0$:

$$s^2 + 2s + 10 K_d s + 10 K_p = 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$s^2 + (2 + 10 K_d) s + 10 K_p = 0$$

**Step 4:** Match with the standard form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$\omega_n^2 = 10 K_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25 = 10 K_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = 2.5$$

**Step 5:** Apply the damping condition:

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = 2 + 10 K_d$$

Substituting $\zeta = 0.7$ and $\omega_n = 5$:

$$2(0.7)(5) = 2 + 10 K_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad 7 = 2 + 10 K_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10 K_d = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = 0.5$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{K_p = 2.5, \quad K_d = 0.5}$ (1 mark for both)

---

### Question Choice B: Stability Analysis Using Routh-Hurwitz
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q3B.** (a) Explain the **Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion** with its two essential conditions. Apply the criterion to determine the range of $K$ for which the system with characteristic equation $s^4 + 2 s^3 + 3 s^2 + 4 s + K = 0$ is stable. (7 marks)
>
> (b) For a UAV roll-control system, the open-loop transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+3)(s+6)}$. Using Routh-Hurwitz, determine the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** determines stability by examining the signs of the first column of the Routh array. The two conditions are:

1. **Necessary Condition:** All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be strictly positive (no zero or negative coefficients). (1 mark)
2. **Sufficient Condition:** All entries in the **first column** of the Routh array must be strictly positive. (1 mark)

**Application to $s^4 + 2 s^3 + 3 s^2 + 4 s + K = 0$:**

Routh Array construction:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $3$ | $K$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 4}{2} = 1$ | $\dfrac{2 K - 0}{2} = K$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{1 \cdot 4 - 2 K}{1} = 4 - 2K$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

(3 marks for array)

**Stability Conditions:**
- $4 - 2K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 2$
- $K > 0$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{0 < K < 2}$ (1 mark for stating range)

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$s(s+3)(s+6) + K = 0$$

**Step 2:** Expand the cubic term:

$$(s^2 + 3s)(s+6) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 3s^2 + 18s = s^3 + 9s^2 + 18s$$

**Step 3:** Full characteristic equation:

$$s^3 + 9 s^2 + 18 s + K = 0$$

**Step 4:** Construct the Routh array (1 mark for each correctly computed row):

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $18$ |
| $s^2$ | $9$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{9 \cdot 18 - 1 \cdot K}{9} = \dfrac{162 - K}{9}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

**Step 5:** Apply first-column positivity (2 marks):

- $\dfrac{162 - K}{9} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 162$
- $K > 0$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{0 < K < 162}$ (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Coefficient expansion errors:** Students frequently drop the $K_d$ term when forming the characteristic equation. Always write out *every* term explicitly.
> 2. **Array arithmetic mistakes:** The $s^1$ row formula is the most error-prone. Re-derive it from the general formula $\dfrac{(a)(c) - (b)(d)}{a}$ where $a,b$ are the row above and $c,d$ are two rows above.
> 3. **Forgetting the marginal stability boundary:** When $K$ equals the critical value (e.g., $K = 2$ in Q3B(a)), state explicitly that the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation at the imaginary axis crossing).
> 4. **No units / no justification in Part (a):** For descriptive answers, you must include the *justification* (e.g., "PD is used in the inner loop *because* it provides damping"). Mere mention of "PD is used" earns only 0.5 of 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Stability ≠ Controllability:** A system can be unstable but still controllable (e.g., a rocket). Stability refers to natural behavior; control is *active* intervention.
- [ ] **CG must be forward of NP** for positive static margin in fixed-wing aircraft.
- [ ] **Static margin range:** Typically **5%–15%** of MAC for safe operation.
- [ ] **PID formulas:** $K_p$ acts on present error, $K_i$ acts on past (accumulated) error, $K_d$ acts on future trend (rate of change).
- [ ] **Filtered derivative:** Practical implementation uses $\dfrac{K_d s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ with $\tau_f \in [0.01, 0.05]$ s.
- [ ] **Standard 2nd order parameters:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{mk})$.
- [ ] **Settling time formula (2%):** $t_s \approx 4 / (\zeta \omega_n)$ — high-yield KTU formula.
- [ ] **Percent overshoot formula:** $\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- [ ] **Cascaded control:** Outer loop (position) slow with PI; inner loop (rates) fast with PD.
- [ ] **Time-scale separation:** Inner loop must be **at least 4×–10× faster** than outer loop.
- [ ] **Routh-Hurwitz:** All first-column entries must be positive; coefficients must be positive.
- [ ] **Marginal stability:** Occurs when a row becomes exactly zero — sustained oscillation at a critical gain.
- [ ] **Quadrotor:** Inherently unstable in hover; requires continuous autopilot intervention.
- [ ] **Wind disturbance & sensor noise:** Always shown as dashed arrows into the plant and sensor in control block diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launch and Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Launch and Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and Aerial Robotics, **Launch and Recovery** refers to the two critical phase-of-flight operations that bound every UAV mission. The **Launch (Take-off)** phase encompasses the mechanisms, energy sources, and control strategies used to transition the air vehicle from a ground, sea, or air-borne rest state into a sustained, controlled, winged or thrust-borne flight regime. The **Recovery (Landing)** phase encompasses the inverse process — dissipating the vehicle's kinetic and potential energy and bringing it back to a safe rest state without damage to the airframe, payload, or recovery infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Extract):**
> "Launch and recovery systems define the non-aerodynamic, ground-based, or mechanical interfaces that enable a UAV to commence and terminate flight. For fixed-wing UAVs without thrust-to-weight ratio sufficient for conventional take-off, alternate launch (catapult, hand-launch, pneumatic rail) and recovery (parachute, net, deep-stall) schemes are mandatory design drivers."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

Imagine you are a child launching a paper airplane from a balcony. You have two choices:
- **Launch**: You can either gently toss it by hand (no runway, low energy) or run and throw it with full arm swing (more energy, faster airspeed).
- **Recovery**: The plane must either glide to a soft grass patch, snag a clothesline (net), or come down with a makeshift leaf-parachute.

UAVs face the *exact same trade-off* — but with **hundreds of kilograms of airframe**, **multi-lakh-rupee payloads**, and **zero onboard pilot** to recover from a mistake. Therefore, the launch system is the **energy injector** that gives the UAV its initial flying speed, and the recovery system is the **energy extractor + safing system** that brings it back alive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** A UAV is only as mission-capable as its launch/recovery (L/R) system. Many high-endurance, long-range UAVs (e.g., surveillance drones) spend **less than 5%** of mission time in launch/recovery, yet **over 60% of total mission loss** historically occurs in these two phases.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Exam-Critical

Per KTU 2024 Scheme outcome mapping, this topic directly satisfies:
- **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental design drivers of UAV subsystems.*
- The L/R system is one of the **first three design constraints** fixed in any UAV conceptual design (along with mission radius and endurance).

**Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for Recall):**

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Launch Kinetic Energy | **10 – 500 kJ** | Energy the L/R system must supply |
| Launch Acceleration | **2 – 15 g** | Structural load on airframe |
| Recovery Sink Rate | **3 – 7 m/s** | Impact load driver |
| Parachute Opening Shock | **< 10 g** | Payload survival limit |
| Net Engagement Window | **2 – 5 m** | Precision-recovery tolerance |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Launch Methods

The choice of launch method is dictated by three primary constraints:
1. **Wing loading** $(W/S)$ — high wing loading demands more launch energy.
2. **Thrust-to-weight ratio** $(T/W)$ — if $T/W < 0.4$, conventional take-off becomes impractical.
3. **Operational footprint** — shipboard, desert, forest, or urban deployments.

### A. Hand-Launch
The operator provides launch energy by physically throwing the UAV.

**Why it works:** Airframe is light (< 5 kg), wing loading is very low, and a human arm can deliver $\approx 30$ J of kinetic energy in a $5$ m/s release.

**How it works:** The UAV has a *handgrip* moulded into the upper fuselage. The pilot throws it forward and slightly upward; a small electric motor then ramps up to maintain flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Hand-launch is feasible **only** when $W \le 5$ kg and $W/S \le 30$ N/m². Above this, the human cannot supply enough release energy without injury.

### B. Catapult / Rail Launch
A mechanical or pneumatic accelerator imparts translational velocity to the UAV along a rigid rail.

**Why it works:** Converts stored elastic/pneumatic potential energy into linear kinetic energy of the airframe.

**How it works:**
- UAV is clamped to a shuttle on a rail.
- Actuator (pneumatic cylinder, bungee cord, or hydraulic ram) accelerates the shuttle to the required **release velocity** $(V_R)$.
- At $V_R$, the UAV has sufficient airspeed for the wing to generate $L = W$.
- Shuttle is then decelerated by a *spike arrestor* or friction damper.

### C. Runway / Conventional Take-off
The UAV accelerates on a prepared surface under its own thrust until $L \ge W$.

**Why it works:** Most aerodynamically efficient and energy-conservative. The runway provides the *ground-roll distance* $s_g$ to reach $V_{LOF}$ (lift-off speed).

**How it works:**
- Engine spool-up → rolling acceleration $a = (T - D - \mu W) / m$.
- At $V_{LOF}$, pilot (or autopilot) pulls back on the elevator → rotation → climb-out.

### D. Pneumatic / Zero-Length Launch
High-pressure air (typically $N_2$ or air at $200$–$300$ bar) drives a piston that launches the rail.

### E. Vertical Take-Off (VTOL)
Multirotor, tilt-rotor, or tailsitter configurations achieve take-off using pure thrust.

## 2.2 Classification of Recovery Methods

### A. Runway / Conventional Landing
Standard three-stage landing: *approach → flare → ground roll*.

**Final approach speed:**
$$V_{app} = 1.3 \, V_{stall}$$

This **30% margin** ensures that any wind gust or pilot error does not stall the wing on short final.

### B. Parachute Recovery
A canopy deployed at altitude (or after engine failure) creates a large drag area to slow descent.

**Why it works:** A hemispherical canopy of diameter $D_c$ creates a drag force that, at equilibrium, equals weight.

**How it works:**
1. **Deployment phase** — bag is ejected, parachute inflates rapidly (causing *opening shock*).
2. **Steady descent phase** — terminal velocity is reached when $D_{parachute} = W$.
3. **Impact phase** — vehicle strikes ground at sink rate $V_s$, dissipating remaining kinetic energy.

### C. Net Recovery
A ground-based or shipboard suspended net arrests a low-altitude approaching UAV. Used widely in **STUAS / tactical UAVs** (e.g., Israeli Aerostar, US MQ-8 Fire Scout derivatives).

### D. Deep Stall Landing
The UAV intentionally enters a fully stalled configuration at altitude. With flaps deflected and the CG well aft, the aircraft settles at a very high angle of attack with low vertical speed.

### E. Belly / Skid Landing
UAV slides on reinforced skids or ventral fuselage at low horizontal speed.

### F. Vertical Landing (VTOL)
Reverse of vertical take-off — controlled descent onto a designated pad.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every KTU-testable equation for Launch & Recovery. No pipe symbol `|` is used; instead `\vert` is employed to keep markdown table integrity intact.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{launch} = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_R^{2}$ | $m$ = mass, $V_R$ = release velocity | $\text{J}$ | Energy required from catapult |
| 2 | $V_R = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$ | margin over stall | $\text{m/s}$ | Minimum launch rail exit speed |
| 3 | $V_{stall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}}}$ | $W$ weight, $\rho$ density, $S$ wing area, $C_{L_{max}}$ max lift coeff. | $\text{m/s}$ | Determines minimum launch speed |
| 4 | $T/W \ge 0.4$ (conventional) | thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless | Eligibility for runway take-off |
| 5 | $a_{catapult} = \dfrac{V_R^{2}}{2 s_{rail}}$ | $s_{rail}$ = rail length | $\text{m/s}^{2}$ | Peak acceleration on rail |
| 6 | $F_{catapult} = m (a + g \sin\theta) + D$ | $D$ drag, $\theta$ rail tilt | $\text{N}$ | Required catapult thrust force |
| 7 | $D_{parachute} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^{2} \, C_D \, A_c$ | $C_D$ canopy drag coeff., $A_c$ canopy area | $\text{N}$ | Parachute drag at terminal velocity |
| 8 | $V_{sink} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, C_D \, A_c}}$ | steady-state descent | $\text{m/s}$ | Parachute terminal sink rate |
| 9 | $E_{impact} = m g h_{deploy} + \tfrac{1}{2} m V_s^{2}$ | $h_{deploy}$ deploy altitude | $\text{J}$ | Total impact energy on landing |
| 10 | $\text{Opening Shock} = \rho \, V_d^{2} \, A_c \, C_D / 2$ | $V_d$ deployment velocity | $\text{N}$ | Parachute inflation peak load |
| 11 | $V_{app} = 1.3 \, V_{stall}$ | approach speed | $\text{m/s}$ | Standard final approach |
| 12 | $s_{ground\,roll} = 1.69 \, \dfrac{W^{2}}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_L \, T}$ | empirical ground roll | $\text{m}$ | Runway length required |
| 13 | $W/S \le 30$ N/m² (hand-launch) | wing loading | $\text{N/m}^{2}$ | Hand-launch feasibility |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Military ISR UAVs** (Searcher, Heron): pneumatic catapult + parachute/skid recovery — no runway dependency.
- **Combat UAVs** (Predator, Reaper): conventional runway — high $T/W$, long endurance.
- **Shipboard UAVs** (ScanEagle): pneumatic launcher + Skyhook net recovery — works in $40$+ knot deck winds.
- **Commercial Drones** (delivery, surveying): VTOL — flexible, no infrastructure.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Catapult Launch Velocity

**Problem Statement:** Derive the minimum velocity $V_R$ at which a UAV must exit a launch rail in order to maintain straight, controlled flight immediately after release.

### Step 1: Identify the lift-generation requirement at release

For the wing to support the airframe weight at the instant of release, the lift force must equal or exceed weight:

$$L \ge W$$

### Step 2: Substitute the standard lift equation

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V^{2} \, S \, C_L$$

Setting $L = W$ and solving for $V$ at the minimum (i.e., at $C_L = C_{L_{max}}$):

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V_R^{2} \, S \, C_{L_{max}} = W$$

### Step 3: Isolate the release velocity

$$\begin{aligned}
V_R^{2} &= \dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}} \\
V_R &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Apply the standard 20% safety margin (KTU convention)

To guard against gusts, control lags, and angle-of-attack transients, the *operational* release velocity is $1.2 \times$ the theoretical minimum:

$$V_{R, \, \text{op}} = 1.2 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the 1.2 factor:** $1.2^{2} = 1.44$, i.e., a 44% lift surplus over weight. This corresponds to the FAR 23 / CS-VLA certification margin for single-engine light aircraft under a 20-knot gust encounter.

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

Given:
- $W = 50$ N (≈ 5 kg UAV)
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea-level standard)
- $S = 0.5$ m²
- $C_{L_{max}} = 1.4$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{R, \text{min}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50}{1.225 \times 0.5 \times 1.4}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{0.8575}} \\
&= \sqrt{116.62} \\
&= 10.8 \text{ m/s} \\
V_{R, \text{op}} &= 1.2 \times 10.8 = 12.96 \text{ m/s} \approx 13 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

This 13 m/s is the rail exit speed the catapult must deliver.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rail Length for a Given Acceleration Limit

**Problem Statement:** Determine the minimum rail length $s_{rail}$ such that the UAV reaches $V_{R,op}$ without exceeding a peak acceleration of $a_{max}$.

### Step 1: Apply the kinematic equation of uniformly accelerated motion

Starting from rest ($V_0 = 0$), the relationship between velocity, acceleration, and distance is:

$$V^{2} = V_0^{2} + 2 \, a \, s$$

### Step 2: Substitute $V_0 = 0$ and $V = V_{R,op}$

$$V_{R,op}^{2} = 2 \, a_{max} \, s_{rail}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $s_{rail}$

$$\begin{aligned}
s_{rail} &= \dfrac{V_{R,op}^{2}}{2 \, a_{max}} \\
&= \dfrac{(1.2)^{2} \cdot 2W}{2 \, a_{max} \, \rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.44 \, W}{a_{max} \, \rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Using the same UAV, and setting $a_{max} = 5 g = 49.05$ m/s² (structural limit for a composite airframe):

$$s_{rail} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 50}{49.05 \times 1.225 \times 0.5 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{72}{42.04} = 1.71 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** A 1.7-meter rail is sufficient.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parachute Terminal Sink Velocity

**Problem Statement:** Derive the steady-state (terminal) sink velocity of a parachute-recovered UAV.

### Step 1: Force balance at terminal velocity

At terminal velocity, the net vertical force is zero, i.e., drag equals weight:

$$D_{parachute} = W$$

### Step 2: Expand the drag equation

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, V_s^{2} \, C_D \, A_c = W$$

where $A_c$ is the canopy projected area and $C_D$ is the effective drag coefficient of the inflated canopy ($\approx 0.8$ for a round canopy, $\approx 1.4$ for a parafoil).

### Step 3: Solve for sink rate $V_s$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s^{2} &= \dfrac{2W}{\rho \, C_D \, A_c} \\
V_s &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, C_D \, A_c}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

For the 50 N UAV with a hemispherical canopy of $D_c = 2$ m (so $A_c = \pi r^{2} = 3.14$ m²) and $C_D = 0.8$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50}{1.225 \times 0.8 \times 3.14}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{3.078}} \\
&= \sqrt{32.49} \\
&= 5.70 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

This is well within the **3–7 m/s safe impact envelope** for fragile payloads.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Ground-Roll Distance for Conventional Take-off

**Problem Statement:** Derive an empirical expression for the ground roll distance $s_g$ of a propeller-driven UAV taking off from a hard runway.

### Step 1: Newton's second law on the runway

The net accelerating force is thrust minus drag minus rolling friction:

$$F_{net} = T - D - \mu \, W = m \, \dfrac{dV}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Use the chain rule to convert from $dt$ to $ds$

$$m V \, \dfrac{dV}{ds} = T - D - \mu W$$

### Step 3: Recognise that the dominant term in the average is thrust

For a propeller-driven UAV, drag and friction are much smaller than thrust during ground roll. Assuming constant $T$:

$$s_g = \dfrac{V_{LOF}^{2}}{2 \, (a_{avg})}$$

### Step 4: Standard empirical form (Ktü/Anderson)

A more accurate empirical form, derived by integrating the full equation and substituting $V_{LOF} = 1.2 V_{stall}$:

$$s_g = 1.69 \, \dfrac{W^{2}}{g \, \rho \, S \, C_{L_{LOF}} \, T}$$

where $C_{L_{LOF}}$ is the lift coefficient at lift-off (typically $\approx 0.5 \, C_{L_{max}}$).

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Given $W = 5000$ N, $T = 2500$ N, $S = 12$ m², $C_{L_{LOF}} = 0.7$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_g &= 1.69 \times \dfrac{(5000)^{2}}{9.81 \times 1.225 \times 12 \times 0.7 \times 2500} \\
&= 1.69 \times \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{7}}{2.578 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= 1.69 \times 96.97 \\
&= 163.9 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A 164-meter runway is required — illustrating why tactical UAVs prefer catapult launch.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Selection Matrix (KTU Design Question)

| Launch Method | Max Mass | Energy Source | Footprint | Complexity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand-launch | $\le 5$ kg | Human arm | Zero | **Very Low** | ₹ |
| Catapult (pneumatic) | 5–500 kg | Compressed air | **Small** | Medium | ₹₹ |
| Catapult (bungee) | 5–50 kg | Elastic cord | Small | Low | ₹ |
| Runway | 50–5000+ kg | Own engine | **Large** | Low | ₹ |
| VTOL (multirotor) | 0.1–50 kg | Battery | Pad only | **High (control)** | ₹₹₹ |
| Zero-length | 500–5000 kg | Rocket booster | Zero | High | ₹₹₹₹ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between hand-launch and pneumatic catapult launch methods for a fixed-wing UAV. Under what conditions is each method preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hand-launch is a **zero-infrastructure** technique in which the operator physically imparts the launch energy by throwing the UAV (typically with a handgrip). It is preferred for **micro/mini-UAVs** with $W \le 5$ kg and wing loading $W/S \le 30$ N/m², where a human arm can deliver $\approx 30$ J of release kinetic energy.

Pneumatic catapult launch, by contrast, uses **high-pressure air (200–300 bar)** driving a piston to accelerate a shuttle on a rigid rail, delivering $V_R = 12$–$30$ m/s reliably. It is preferred for **tactical UAVs in the 5–500 kg class** operating from unprepared fields, ship decks, or forward bases without runways.

**(1 mark) Definition, (1 mark) Condition, (1 mark) Application.**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List three commonly used UAV recovery methods. State one advantage and one disadvantage of parachute recovery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three common recovery methods are:
1. **Conventional runway landing** (1 mark)
2. **Parachute recovery** (1 mark)
3. **Net/skid/belly landing** (1 mark — any one)

**Parachute recovery:**
- *Advantage:* Independent of runway infrastructure; can be deployed after engine failure, providing an emergency-safing role (1 mark).
- *Disadvantage:* Precision is poor — landing point dispersion is typically 50–200 m, making it unsuitable for confined urban or shipboard operations (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students answer Q2 with **only one** advantage and **no** disadvantage, or list a "disadvantage" that is actually an advantage. Make sure to present a balanced trade-off.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
*`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the major launch methods used for fixed-wing UAVs. Discuss the operating envelope (mass, wing loading, infrastructure) of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small UAV has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 8$ kg, Weight $W = 78.4$ N
- Wing area $S = 0.6$ m²
- $C_{L_{max}} = 1.5$
- Sea-level density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Maximum allowable launch acceleration = 4 g

Compute the **(i)** minimum release velocity, **(ii)** operational release velocity (with 20% margin), and **(iii)** minimum catapult rail length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) [7 Marks] — Classification of Launch Methods**

**1. Hand-launch (1 mark):** Operator imparts initial kinetic energy. Used for micro-UAVs ($m \le 5$ kg, $W/S \le 30$ N/m²). Pros: zero infrastructure. Cons: limited energy transfer.

**2. Bungee/catapult launch (1 mark):** Elastic cord stores strain energy, then rapidly releases to pull the UAV along a rail. Used for tactical UAVs ($m = 5$–$50$ kg). Pros: low cost, portable. Cons: variable energy depending on cord stretch.

**3. Pneumatic catapult (1 mark):** Compressed air drives a piston; very repeatable launch profile. Used for tactical and MALE UAVs ($m = 5$–$500$ kg). Pros: high controllability, shipboard-compatible. Cons: needs compressor logistics.

**4. Runway / conventional take-off (1 mark):** UAV accelerates on a hard surface under its own thrust; $T/W \ge 0.4$ required. Used for medium and large UAVs (e.g., Heron, Predator). Pros: energy-efficient, no launch system mass. Cons: 100+ m runway needed.

**5. VTOL (1 mark):** Multirotor, tilt-rotor, or tailsitter configuration; uses pure thrust. Used for confined-area operations. Pros: no infrastructure. Cons: complexity, battery-limited endurance.

**6. Zero-length launch (1 mark):** Rocket booster or jettisonable trolley; military application only. Pros: no runway. Cons: extreme accelerations (8–12 g), single-use booster.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Selection is governed by mass, wing loading, $T/W$, and available infrastructure.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical**

**(i) Minimum release velocity (2 marks):**

$$V_{R,min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L_{max}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 78.4}{1.225 \times 0.6 \times 1.5}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{156.8}{1.1025}} = \sqrt{142.2} = 11.92 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Operational release velocity (2 marks):**

$$V_{R,op} = 1.2 \times 11.92 = 14.31 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Minimum rail length (3 marks):**

Using $a_{max} = 4g = 39.24$ m/s²:

$$s_{rail} = \dfrac{V_{R,op}^{2}}{2 \, a_{max}} = \dfrac{(14.31)^{2}}{2 \times 39.24} = \dfrac{204.78}{78.48} = 2.61 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark for unit clarity):** $V_{R,min} = 11.92$ m/s, $V_{R,op} = 14.31$ m/s, $s_{rail} \ge 2.61$ m.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
*`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of parachute recovery for a UAV. Derive the expression for the terminal sink velocity. List two design trade-offs in canopy selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UAV weighing $200$ N is to be recovered by a single round parachute. The canopy diameter is $3$ m and the effective drag coefficient is $0.8$. Sea-level air density is $1.225$ kg/m³. Compute the **(i)** terminal sink velocity and **(ii)** the steady-state drag force (which equals the weight — verify). If the canopy is replaced with a parafoil of $C_D = 1.4$ and identical area, what is the **new sink velocity**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Working principle (2 marks):** A parachute is a deployable drag device. Upon deployment, the canopy inflates, creating a large frontal area that generates aerodynamic drag. At terminal velocity, drag equals weight, and the vehicle descends at a constant, low vertical speed.

**Sequence (2 marks):** 
1. Bay door opens
2. Pilot chute ejects main bag
3. Main canopy inflates (opening shock)
4. Steady descent at $V_s$
5. Impact with energy-absorbing skid/airbag

**Derivation of terminal sink velocity (2 marks):**

Force balance: $D = W$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^{2} C_D A_c = W \implies V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho C_D A_c}}$$

**Two design trade-offs (1 mark):**
- *Larger canopy* → lower sink rate (safer) but more opening shock and greater deployment mass.
- *Higher $C_D$ (parafoil)* → lower sink rate but can be unstable in wind.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

Given: $W = 200$ N, $D_c = 3$ m $\Rightarrow A_c = \pi (1.5)^{2} = 7.07$ m², $C_D = 0.8$.

**(i) Terminal sink velocity (3 marks):**

$$V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 200}{1.225 \times 0.8 \times 7.07}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400}{6.93}} = \sqrt{57.72} = 7.60 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Steady drag (2 marks):**

$$D = W = 200 \text{ N} \quad \text{(verified by definition of terminal velocity)}$$

**With parafoil ($C_D = 1.4$, same area) (2 marks):**

$$V_{s,new} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400}{1.225 \times 1.4 \times 7.07}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400}{12.13}} = \sqrt{32.98} = 5.74 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** The parafoil reduces sink rate from 7.60 m/s to 5.74 m/s, a 24% improvement in survivability for fragile payloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.2 / 1.3 safety margin** on $V_{stall}$ when computing $V_R$ or $V_{app}$. Always state the margin explicitly.
> 2. **Using $W$ (weight) instead of $m$ (mass)** in the kinetic energy equation $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$. This is the single most common dimensional error.
> 3. **Neglecting drag on the rail** when computing catapult force. The correct expression is $F = m(a + g \sin\theta) + D$, not simply $F = ma$.
> 4. **In parachute problems, mixing up $C_D$** of the round canopy (0.8) and parafoil (1.4). Always state which is being assumed.
> 5. **Not converting $g$ to 9.81 m/s²** when needed in $a_{max} = ng$ expressions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] **Launch energy** is purely kinetic: $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_R^{2}$.
- [ ] **Release velocity** is *always* $1.2 \, V_{stall}$ for safety; approach speed is $1.3 \, V_{stall}$.
- [ ] **Hand-launch** is feasible **only** when $W/S \le 30$ N/m² and $m \le 5$ kg.
- [ ] **Catapult force** must overcome **inertia + drag + gravity component**: $F = m a + D + mg\sin\theta$.
- [ ] **Rail length** comes from kinematics: $s = V^{2}/(2a)$.
- [ ] **Parachute sink velocity** is $V_s = \sqrt{2W / (\rho C_D A_c)}$ — directly from $D = W$.
- [ ] **Round canopy** has $C_D \approx 0.8$; **parafoil** has $C_D \approx 1.4$ (more efficient but directional).
- [ ] **Opening shock** must be kept **below 10 g** to protect the payload.
- [ ] **Conventional take-off** requires $T/W \ge 0.4$ and adequate ground-roll distance.
- [ ] **Net recovery** is the precision option for shipboard / confined-area operations.
- [ ] **Deep stall** is an energy-management technique, not a separate "system."
- [ ] **VTOL** removes launch/recovery infrastructure but adds control complexity and mass.
- [ ] **L/R phases** account for **5% of mission time** but **> 60% of mission losses** — design accordingly.
- [ ] **In every KTU numerical**, always state: (i) the formula, (ii) substitution, (iii) result with units, and (iv) a one-line physical interpretation.
- [ ] **Final design check:** always compute $W/S$ and $T/W$ before choosing a launch method — these two non-dimensional numbers decide *everything*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Anatomy of Quadcopter - Power Train and Propellers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hardware Anatomy of Quadcopter - Power Train and Propellers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Power Train** of a quadcopter is the integrated electromechanical energy conversion and transmission subsystem responsible for converting stored electrochemical energy into controlled aerodynamic thrust. It comprises four primary hardware elements arranged in a strict series topology: $$\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{ESC} \rightarrow \text{BLDC Motor} \rightarrow \text{Propeller} \rightarrow \text{Aerodynamic Thrust}$$ Each element is a matched impedance stage; a mismatch anywhere in the chain results in either catastrophic in-flight failure (brownout, desync) or grossly inefficient operation (excess heat, reduced endurance). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The power train is treated as a *system of coupled subsystems* where the Battery acts as the energy reservoir, the ESC as the commutator-controller, the BLDC motor as the electromechanical transducer, and the propeller as the aerodynamic actuator. The propeller is the *only* component that physically interacts with the surrounding fluid (air). ### 1.2 Component-Level Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) Imagine the quadcopter power train as a **water pump system used in agriculture irrigation**: | Quadcopter Component | Water Pump Analogy | Function | |---|---|---| | **LiPo Battery** | The overhead water tank | Stores potential energy (electrical charge) | | **Power Wires (XT60)** | The main pipeline | Transmits energy with minimal loss | | **ESC (Electronic Speed Controller)** | The motorized valve | Regulates the rate of flow (PWM switching) | | **BLDC Motor** | The pump impeller driven by an electric motor | Converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy | | **Propeller** | The spinning rotor that sprays water | Converts rotational energy into directed force (thrust) | | **Frame & Airframe** | The pipe housing | Provides structural mounting and routing | If the **tank is too small** (low capacity battery), the pump runs dry quickly (short flight time). If the **valve opens too fast** (high PWM duty), the impeller demands more water than the pipe can deliver (voltage sag / brownout). If the **impeller is mismatched** to the rotor (wrong motor KV for propeller), water sprays everywhere with no useful pressure (wasted energy as heat). The propeller is the *nozzle* — its geometry dictates how efficiently the rotational effort becomes useful force. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **standard metrics** must be memorized for KTU examinations: > [!NOTE] > **Universal Physical Constants Used in Propeller/Motor Analysis:** > - **Air density at sea level (ISA):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - **Kinematic viscosity of air:** $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ > - **Speed of sound at sea level:** $a = 340.3 \text{ m/s}$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard Propeller/Motor Metrics:** > - **Diameter ($D$):** in **inches** (e.g., 5", 10", 13", 15") > - **Pitch ($P$):** in **inches** (e.g., 4.5", 5.1") > - **KV rating:** in **RPM per Volt** (e.g., 920 KV, 2400 KV) > - **C-rating:** dimensionless multiplier (e.g., 25C, 75C) > - **Cell count (S):** integer number of LiPo cells in series > - **Nominal cell voltage:** **3.7 V**; **Full charge:** **4.2 V**; **Storage:** **3.8 V** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown #### 2.1.1 The LiPo (Lithium Polymer) Battery — Energy Reservoir A LiPo battery is composed of multiple cells in series (denoted **S**). Each cell has a nominal voltage of **3.7 V** and a maximum safe discharge voltage of **4.2 V**. The two dominant performance parameters are: **1. Capacity ($Q$, measured in mAh):** The total charge the battery can deliver. A 5000 mAh battery can theoretically supply 5 A for 1 hour, or 50 A for 6 minutes. **2. C-rating ($C$):** The maximum safe continuous discharge rate as a multiple of capacity. For a 5000 mAh (5 Ah) battery rated 25C: $$I_{\max} = C \times \text{Capacity(Ah)} = 25 \times 5 = 125 \text{ A}$$ **Why does this matter?** A quadcopter in aggressive flight can draw 25–30 A per motor. For a quadcopter drawing ~100 A peak, a 25C/5000 mAh pack is just barely sufficient. Under-sized C-rating causes **voltage sag**, which can trigger brownout. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The C-rating × Capacity formula is a **direct 3-mark formula** in Part A. Always show units explicitly: mAh → Ah conversion is **mandatory**. #### 2.1.2 The ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) — Commutator & Power Modulator The ESC performs three critical functions: 1. **DC → 3-phase AC inversion** for the BLDC motor. 2. **PWM throttle command interpretation** from the flight controller. 3. **Battery elimination (BEC)** for low-power FC supply (in some models). The ESC switches at high frequency (typically **8–32 kHz**, with protocols like **DShot600** reaching 600 kbaud). The **throttle command** is encoded as a digital/analog PWM signal; the ESC drives the three motor phases with the correct commutation sequence based on rotor position feedback (sensorless back-EMF or sensored Hall effect). **Key ESC parameter:** Continuous current rating (e.g., 30 A ESC = can handle 30 A continuous per motor). #### 2.1.3 The BLDC (Brushless DC) Motor — Electromechanical Transducer The KV rating defines the motor's no-load speed per applied volt. The governing equations are: $$n_{\text{RPM}} = KV \times V_{\text{supplied}}$$ $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times KV \times V}{60} \text{ rad/s}$$ A **low KV motor (e.g., 920 KV)** paired with a **large propeller (e.g., 13"×5.5")** on a **6S (22.2 V)** battery is typical for long-endurance cinematography quads. A **high KV motor (e.g., 2400 KV)** paired with a **small propeller (e.g., 5"×4.3")** on a **4S (14.8 V)** battery is typical for racing/freestyle quads. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *direction* of the relationship. **Higher KV → Higher RPM for a given voltage → Requires smaller, lighter propellers** (because high-KV motors lack torque to swing large props). **Lower KV → Higher torque → Can swing larger propellers** (more thrust per motor). #### 2.1.4 The Propeller — Aerodynamic Actuator A propeller is a twisted airfoil. It converts rotational mechanical work into a pressure differential across its disk area. The two key geometric parameters are: - **Diameter ($D$):** Twice the tip radius. Defines the swept disk area: $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ - **Pitch ($P$):** The theoretical axial distance the propeller would advance per revolution in a solid medium. A propeller labeled **"1045"** means **D = 10 inches** and **P = 4.5 inches**. The **Pitch-to-Diameter ratio (P/D)** characterizes propeller behavior: - **Low P/D (~0.3):** "Crawler" — high torque, low top speed (good for heavy lift). - **High P/D (~1.0+):** "Racer" — low torque, high top speed. ### 2.2 Aerodynamic Theory of the Propeller (Momentum Theory) The propeller is analyzed as an **actuator disk** that imparts momentum to a stream tube of air. The **momentum theory** yields two foundational relations: **Thrust produced by a propeller:** $$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$ **Power consumed by a propeller:** $$P = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$$ Where: - $C_T$ = Thrust coefficient (dimensionless, empirically determined) - $C_P$ = Power coefficient (dimensionless) - $\rho$ = Air density (1.225 kg/m³ at sea level) - $n$ = Rotational speed in revolutions per second (Hz, **not RPM**) - $D$ = Propeller diameter in meters - $T$ = Thrust in Newtons - $P$ = Power in Watts > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Point:** Notice the exponents — thrust scales with $n^2$ and $D^4$, while power scales with $n^5$ and $D^5$. This is why **doubling propeller diameter yields 16× thrust but 32× power** — a dominant design constraint. ### 2.3 The Figure of Merit (Propeller Efficiency) The **Figure of Merit (FM)** is the ratio of *ideal power* (momentum-theory minimum) to *actual shaft power* delivered to the propeller: $$FM = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A} \cdot P_{\text{shaft}}}$$ Typical well-designed propellers achieve $FM \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ at their design point. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Motor no-load RPM | $n = KV \cdot V$ | RPM | $V$ is supplied voltage | | 2 | Motor angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot KV \cdot V}{60}$ | rad/s | Divide RPM by 9.55 | | 3 | Max battery current | $I_{\max} = C \cdot Q_{Ah}$ | A | $Q$ must be in Ah, not mAh | | 4 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Wh | Multiply by 1000 for mWh | | 5 | Propeller disk area | $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | Convert inches → meters: $\times 0.0254$ | | 6 | Thrust | $T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ | N | $n$ in **rev/s**, $D$ in **meters** | | 7 | Power | $P = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$ | W | $n$ in **rev/s**, $D$ in **meters** | | 8 | Tip speed | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot n \cdot D$ | m/s | Watch for transonic limit (~0.7 Mach) | | 9 | Tip Mach number | $M_{tip} = \frac{\pi \cdot n \cdot D}{a}$ | dimensionless | $a = 340.3$ m/s | | 10 | Figure of Merit | $FM = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{actual}}}$ | dimensionless | $0 < FM \leq 1$ | | 11 | Hover throttle | $T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g$ | N | Sum of 4 motor thrusts | | 12 | Per-motor thrust | $T_m = \frac{m \cdot g}{4}$ | N | For symmetric quadcopter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This formula sheet is the **daily working tool** of a drone design engineer at companies like DJI, Parrot, or Skydio. Before any prototype is built, the propeller–motor–battery matching is solved on paper using exactly these equations to estimate hover time, max payload, and current draw. ### 2.5 Propeller Material & Geometry Engineering Modern quadcopter propellers are manufactured from: - **Polycarbonate (PC):** Flexible, durable, lower efficiency. Used in training quads. - **Glass-filled nylon:** Stiffer, slightly higher efficiency. Used in mid-tier racing. - **Carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP):** Stiffest, highest efficiency, brittle on impact. Used in professional racing and cinematography. The number of blades (2, 3, 4, 5) affects efficiency and thrust: - **2-blade:** Most efficient (lowest induced drag), least thrust. - **3-blade:** Balanced choice (most common in 5" racing). - **4–5 blade:** Higher thrust per diameter, lower efficiency, used for heavy lift. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Motor RPM from KV Rating and Battery Voltage **Problem Context:** A 920 KV brushless motor is connected to a fully charged 6S LiPo battery. Compute the no-load motor speed in RPM, rad/s, and the corresponding tip speed for a 13-inch propeller. **Step 1 — Determine the supplied voltage:** A 6S LiPo battery at full charge has $6 \times 4.2 = 25.2$ V. The nominal voltage is $6 \times 3.7 = 22.2$ V. We use the **fully charged value** for worst-case RPM: $$V = 6 \times 4.2 = 25.2 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the KV equation:** $$n_{RPM} = KV \times V = 920 \times 25.2$$ $$n_{RPM} = 23184 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 3 — Convert RPM to rad/s:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n_{RPM}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 23184}{60}$$ $$\omega = 2427.6 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Convert propeller diameter from inches to meters:** $$D = 13 \times 0.0254 = 0.3302 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the tip speed:** $$v_{tip} = \pi \cdot n \cdot D = \pi \times \left(\frac{23184}{60}\right) \times 0.3302$$ $$v_{tip} = 3.1416 \times 386.4 \times 0.3302$$ $$v_{tip} = 400.9 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the tip Mach number:** $$M_{tip} = \frac{v_{tip}}{a} = \frac{400.9}{340.3} = 1.178$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Result:** The tip Mach number exceeds 1.0! This means the propeller is operating in the **transonic regime** at full throttle, where shock-wave losses drastically reduce efficiency and produce the characteristic high-pitched whistle. For sustained flight, the motor KV or prop diameter must be reduced. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thrust and Power Calculation for a Hovering Quadcopter **Problem Context:** A quadcopter of total mass $m = 1.6$ kg is in hover. The propellers are 8-inch diameter with $C_T = 0.10$ and $C_P = 0.045$. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. The motor spins at $n = 6000$ RPM. Verify that the thrust is sufficient for hover and compute the power per motor. **Step 1 — Compute the total hover thrust required:** $$T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g = 1.6 \times 9.81 = 15.696 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the per-motor thrust required (symmetric quad):** $$T_m = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{4} = \frac{15.696}{4} = 3.924 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Convert propeller diameter to meters:** $$D = 8 \times 0.0254 = 0.2032 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Convert RPM to rev/s (Hz):** $$n = \frac{6000}{60} = 100 \text{ rev/s}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the per-motor thrust from the propeller equation:** $$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$ $$T = 0.10 \times 1.225 \times (100)^2 \times (0.2032)^4$$ $$T = 0.10 \times 1.225 \times 10000 \times 0.001703$$ $$T = 2.085 \text{ N per motor}$$ **Step 6 — Compare to required thrust:** $$T_{\text{per\_motor}} = 2.085 \text{ N} \quad < \quad T_{\text{required}} = 3.924 \text{ N}$$ **Conclusion:** The thrust is **insufficient** for hover. The motor must spin faster, or a larger propeller must be used. **Step 7 — Required rotational speed for hover:** $$n_{\text{required}} = \sqrt{\frac{T_m}{C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}$$ $$n_{\text{required}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.924}{0.10 \times 1.225 \times 0.001703}}$$ $$n_{\text{required}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.924}{2.086 \times 10^{-4}}}$$ $$n_{\text{required}} = \sqrt{18811} = 137.2 \text{ rev/s} = 8228 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 8 — Compute the power at this rotational speed:** $$P = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$$ $$P = 0.045 \times 1.225 \times (137.2)^5 \times (0.2032)^5$$ $$P = 0.045 \times 1.225 \times 4.86 \times 10^{10} \times 3.47 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$P = 0.045 \times 1.225 \times 1.687 \times 10^{7}$$ $$P \approx 930.0 \text{ W per motor}$$ **Step 9 — Total hover power:** $$P_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 930 = 3720 \text{ W} = 3.72 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 10 — Estimate flight time with a 6S 5000 mAh battery:** Battery energy: $$E = V \times Q = 22.2 \times 5 = 111 \text{ Wh}$$ Assuming 80% powertrain efficiency: $$t_{\text{hover}} = \frac{0.8 \times 111}{3.72} = 23.9 \text{ minutes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** The 5th-power dependence of power on $n$ is the reason why *hovering at high altitude* (thin air → motor must spin faster) is **drastically more energy expensive** than at sea level. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Motor–Propeller Matching Decision Table The following matrix summarizes the **engineering trade-offs** that KTU examiners expect you to reason about: | Design Goal | Motor KV | Prop Size | Battery S | Why? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Long endurance (mapping, surveying) | **Low** (300–900 KV) | **Large** (13"–22") | 6S–12S | Low KV + large prop = high torque, high efficiency, low current | | Heavy-lift delivery | **Very low** (100–400 KV) | **Very large** (18"–30") | 6S–14S | Maximum thrust per motor; current spread across large prop disk | | Racing / freestyle | **High** (2300–2700 KV) | **Small** (3"–5") | 4S–6S | High RPM = fast response, aggressive thrust-to-weight ratio | | Cinematic filming | **Medium** (800–1300 KV) | **Medium** (7"–10") | 6S | Balanced thrust, smooth response, low vibration | ### 3.4 Complete Step-by-Step Solution Template (For KTU 14-Mark Problems) When KTU asks a design question, follow this **5-step template**: 1. **State the hover thrust requirement:** $T_m = mg/4$ 2. **Select propeller size** (use design tables or empirical $C_T$). 3. **Solve for required $n$** from the thrust equation. 4. **Compute required power** from the power equation. 5. **Verify battery current** $I = P/V$ is within battery C-rating. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Train Signal & Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BATTERY
LiPo Pack
e.g. 6S 5000mAh 25C] -->|DC 22.2V
Up to 125A| B[POWER DISTRIBUTION BOARD
PDB / XT60 Harness] B --> C1[ESC Motor 1
30A Continuous] B --> C2[ESC Motor 2
30A Continuous] B --> C3[ESC Motor 3
30A Continuous] B --> C4[ESC Motor 4
30A Continuous] B -->|5V BEC| D[FLIGHT CONTROLLER
Receives PWM/DShot] D -->|Throttle Command| C1 D -->|Throttle Command| C2 D -->|Throttle Command| C3 D -->|Throttle Command| C4 C1 --> E1[BLDC Motor 1
e.g. 920 KV] C2 --> E2[BLDC Motor 2
e.g. 920 KV] C3 --> E3[BLDC Motor 3
e.g. 920 KV] C4 --> E4[BLDC Motor 4
e.g. 920 KV] E1 --> F1[Propeller 1
e.g. 13x5.5 inch] E2 --> F2[Propeller 2
e.g. 13x5.5 inch] E3 --> F3[Propeller 3
e.g. 13x5.5 inch] E4 --> F4[Propeller 4
e.g. 13x5.5 inch] F1 --> G[Aerodynamic Thrust
Net Vertical Force] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The energy flows along the **outer path** (Battery → ESC → Motor → Propeller), while the *control* signals flow along the **inner path** (Flight Controller → ESC).
