The PDB is a passive bus that fans out battery power to all four ESCs in parallel. ### 4.2 Propeller Geometry Anatomy (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB P[PROPELLER ANATOMY] --> P1[Hub / Center Mount
5mm or 6mm bore] P --> P2[Blade Root
High chord, low pitch] P --> P3[Blade Mid-section
Twist transition] P --> P4[Blade Tip
Low chord, high pitch] P --> P5[Leading Edge
Cuts air] P --> P6[Trailing Edge
Pressure side boundary] P --> P7[Pitch Angle
beta = arctan P / pi D] ``` ### 4.3 Functional State Diagram: Power Train Operating States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> SpinningUp: Throttle PWM > 5% SpinningUp --> Hover: Throttle achieves mg/4 per motor Hover --> Maneuvering: Throttle differential per motor Maneuvering --> Hover: Differential returns to zero Hover --> Descent: Throttle PWM reduces Descent --> Idle: Throttle PWM to 0% SpinningUp --> Brownout: Voltage sag below ESC cutoff Hover --> Brownout: Excessive current draw Brownout --> [*]: Battery replacement required ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** *Define the term "KV rating" of a brushless DC motor used in quadcopters. A quadcopter uses 2400 KV motors with a 4S LiPo battery. Compute the no-load motor speed in RPM.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **KV rating** of a BLDC motor is defined as the **no-load rotational speed (in RPM) produced per volt of applied DC voltage**, assuming no external load on the shaft. It is a constant of the motor's electromagnetic design and is inversely related to the motor's torque constant. **Computation:** For a fully charged 4S battery: $$V = 4 \times 4.2 = 16.8 \text{ V}$$ $$n = KV \times V = 2400 \times 16.8 = 40320 \text{ RPM}$$ **Marks Distribution:** - Definition: 1 mark - Voltage calculation: 1 mark - RPM calculation: 1 mark --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** *What is the significance of the "C-rating" of a LiPo battery? A 4S 2200 mAh 50C LiPo battery is used in a micro quadcopter. Calculate the maximum safe continuous discharge current.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **C-rating** of a LiPo battery is a dimensionless multiplier that specifies the **maximum safe continuous discharge current** the battery can deliver without damage, expressed as a multiple of its nominal capacity. It is a critical safety and performance parameter that prevents the battery from overheating, swelling, or catching fire under load. **Computation:** $$Q = 2200 \text{ mAh} = 2.2 \text{ Ah}$$ $$I_{\max} = C \times Q_{Ah} = 50 \times 2.2 = 110 \text{ A}$$ **Marks Distribution:** - Definition of C-rating: 1.5 marks - Calculation: 1.5 marks --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]** A quadcopter has the following specifications: - Total mass (including battery): $m = 2.0$ kg - Propeller diameter: $D = 10$ inches - Propeller pitch: $P = 4.5$ inches - Thrust coefficient: $C_T = 0.105$ - Power coefficient: $C_P = 0.048$ - Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - Motor KV: 920 KV - Battery: 6S LiPo, 5000 mAh, 25C **Answer the following:** **(a)** Compute the rotational speed required for hover and the corresponding no-load supply voltage the motor demands. (7 marks) **(b)** Compute the power consumed per motor and the total current drawn from the battery. Estimate the hover flight time, assuming 80% overall powertrain efficiency. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Rotational Speed for Hover and Supply Voltage (7 Marks)** *Step 1 — Total hover thrust required:* $$T_{\text{total}} = m \cdot g = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$$ *Step 2 — Per-motor thrust:* $$T_m = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{4} = \frac{19.62}{4} = 4.905 \text{ N}$$ *Step 3 — Convert propeller diameter to meters:* $$D = 10 \times 0.0254 = 0.254 \text{ m}$$ *Step 4 — Solve the thrust equation for $n$:* $$T_m = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \implies n = \sqrt{\frac{T_m}{C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}$$ *Step 5 — Compute the denominator:* $$C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 = 0.105 \times 1.225 \times (0.254)^4 = 0.105 \times 1.225 \times 4.160 \times 10^{-3} = 5.35 \times 10^{-4}$$ *Step 6 — Compute $n$:* $$n = \sqrt{\frac{4.905}{5.35 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{9168.2} = 95.75 \text{ rev/s}$$ *Step 7 — Convert to RPM:* $$n_{RPM} = 95.75 \times 60 = 5745 \text{ RPM}$$ *Step 8 — Compute the supply voltage from the KV equation:* $$V = \frac{n_{RPM}}{KV} = \frac{5745}{920} = 6.245 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** The 6S battery supplies 22.2 V nominally, but the motor uses only ~6.25 V equivalent under load due to the back-EMF that opposes the supply. The ESC commutates the phases using PWM, effectively lowering the average applied voltage to ~6.25 V at hover. **(b) Power, Current, and Flight Time (7 Marks)** *Step 1 — Power per motor from the power equation:* $$P_m = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$$ $$P_m = 0.048 \times 1.225 \times (95.75)^5 \times (0.254)^5$$ $$P_m = 0.048 \times 1.225 \times 8.05 \times 10^9 \times 1.054 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$P_m = 0.048 \times 1.225 \times 8.488 \times 10^6$$ $$P_m = 499.0 \text{ W per motor}$$ *Step 2 — Total electrical power required at the battery:* $$P_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 499.0 = 1996 \text{ W}$$ *Step 3 — Battery current:* $$I = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{battery}}} = \frac{1996}{22.2} = 89.9 \text{ A}$$ *Step 4 — Verify against battery C-rating:* $$I_{\max} = 25 \times 5 = 125 \text{ A}$$ Since $89.9 \text{ A} < 125 \text{ A}$, the battery is **safe** for hover. *Step 5 — Battery energy:* $$E = V \times Q = 22.2 \times 5 = 111 \text{ Wh}$$ *Step 6 — Flight time with 80% efficiency:* $$t = \frac{0.8 \times 111}{1996} = 0.0445 \text{ hours} = 2.67 \text{ minutes}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** (inches → meters) is the most common error — it propagates to all subsequent values and costs **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Confusing RPM with rev/s** in the $n^2$ and $n^5$ terms: the equation demands **rev/s**, NOT RPM. This single error changes the answer by a factor of 3600. > 3. **Omitting the mAh → Ah conversion** in the C-rating problem: $5000 \text{ mAh} \neq 5000 \text{ Ah}$. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**. > 4. **Showing the substitution step** explicitly: the examiner needs to see the numbers plugged in, not just the final result. > 5. **Hover throttle assumption:** students often forget that $T_m = mg/4$ assumes a symmetric quadcopter in perfect hover; if the problem states a tilt angle or payload offset, the per-motor thrust is NOT equal. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the power train architecture of a quadcopter. List the functions of the ESC and the BLDC motor. (7 marks) **(b)** A 13-inch (diameter) propeller is mounted on a 920 KV motor supplied by a 6S LiPo battery. Compute: (i) the no-load motor speed in RPM, (ii) the tip speed of the propeller, and (iii) the tip Mach number. State whether the propeller is operating in a subsonic or transonic regime. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Power Train Architecture and Component Functions (7 Marks)** *Block Diagram (3 marks):* ``` [Battery (6S LiPo)] | [PDB / Power Harness] | ----------------- | | | | [ESC][ESC][ESC][ESC] <-- One per motor | | | | [M1] [M2] [M3] [M4] <-- BLDC motors | | | | [P1] [P2] [P3] [P4] <-- Propellers ``` *Function of ESC (2 marks):* - Converts DC battery voltage into three-phase AC waveforms to drive the BLDC motor. - Interprets throttle commands (PWM, Oneshot, DShot) from the flight controller and modulates the duty cycle. - Provides low-voltage BEC output (5 V or 12 V) to power the flight controller and radio receiver. - Includes protective features: low-voltage cutoff, thermal shutdown, motor desync protection. *Function of BLDC Motor (2 marks):* - Converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy. - The KV rating relates applied voltage to rotational speed: $n_{RPM} = KV \times V$. - The motor's torque constant and current draw determine the thrust the propeller can produce. - In a quadcopter, four BLDC motors are arranged in alternating CW/CCW pairs to cancel net yaw torque. **(b) Propeller Tip Speed and Mach Number (7 Marks)** *Step 1 — Compute the no-load RPM (2 marks):* $$V = 6 \times 4.2 = 25.2 \text{ V}$$ $$n = KV \times V = 920 \times 25.2 = 23184 \text{ RPM}$$ *Step 2 — Convert to rev/s:* $$n = \frac{23184}{60} = 386.4 \text{ rev/s}$$ *Step 3 — Convert diameter to meters (1 mark):* $$D = 13 \times 0.0254 = 0.3302 \text{ m}$$ *Step 4 — Compute tip speed (2 marks):* $$v_{tip} = \pi \cdot n \cdot D = \pi \times 386.4 \times 0.3302$$ $$v_{tip} = 3.1416 \times 127.61 = 400.9 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 5 — Compute tip Mach number (2 marks):* $$M_{tip} = \frac{v_{tip}}{a} = \frac{400.9}{340.3} = 1.178$$ *Step 6 — Conclusion:* Since $M_{tip} = 1.178 > 0.85$, the propeller is operating in the **transonic regime** at full throttle. This is undesirable because: - Shock-wave formation on the blade reduces aerodynamic efficiency. - Noise levels increase significantly (the characteristic "screaming" of racing drones). - Blade tips experience additional drag and heating. **Recommendation:** Use a lower-KV motor (e.g., 700–800 KV) or a smaller propeller (e.g., 11-inch) to keep the tip Mach below 0.7 for efficient, quiet operation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 Power Train & Propellers — Last-Minute Revision Checklist** - ✅ The power train flow is **Battery → ESC → BLDC Motor → Propeller** (memorize this exact order). - ✅ A LiPo cell has nominal voltage **3.7 V**, full charge **4.2 V**, storage **3.8 V**. Cell count $S$ is in series, not parallel. - ✅ C-rating × Capacity (in Ah) = **maximum safe current in Amperes**. Always convert mAh → Ah first. - ✅ BLDC motor: $n_{RPM} = KV \times V$ (no-load). Lower KV = higher torque, suited for large propellers. Higher KV = lower torque, suited for small propellers. - ✅ Propeller label "D × P" gives **diameter and pitch in inches** (e.g., 1045 = 10 inch diameter, 4.5 inch pitch). - ✅ Thrust equation: $T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ with $n$ in **rev/s** and $D$ in **meters**. - ✅ Power equation: $P = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^5 \cdot D^5$ — the $n^5$ dependence is the key reason for high-altitude inefficiency. - ✅ Hover thrust per motor: $T_m = mg / 4$ for a symmetric quadcopter. - ✅ Tip speed: $v_{tip} = \pi n D$. Tip Mach must stay below **0.7–0.85** for efficient, subsonic operation. - ✅ Disk area: $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. The propeller disk is the only surface interacting with air. - ✅ Figure of Merit $FM$ approaches 1.0 for an ideal propeller; well-designed props reach 0.6–0.8. - ✅ ESC provides DC→3-phase AC inversion, throttle command interpretation, and BEC output. - ✅ 2-blade props are most efficient; 3-blade is balanced; 4–5 blade gives high thrust at lower efficiency. - ✅ **Always convert units before computing** (inches → meters, mAh → Ah, RPM → rev/s). These unit conversions are worth 2–3 marks each in KTU 14-mark problems. - ✅ Carbon fiber props are stiff and efficient but brittle; polycarbonate props are flexible and crash-resistant but less efficient. - ✅ The four BLDC motors spin in alternating **CW/CCW** pairs to cancel net yaw torque on the airframe. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** In every numerical answer, **state the governing equation first**, then **substitute values with units**, then **compute the final number with correct significant figures**. This three-step structure is exactly what KTU examiners are trained to award partial credit for.

topics.title: Motors, Total Lift, Electronic Speed Controllers, Flight Battery, Radio Transmitter and Receiver, Flight Controller, Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motors, Total Lift, Electronic Speed Controllers, Flight Battery, Radio Transmitter and Receiver, Flight Controller, Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motors in Aerial Robotics

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A motor, in the context of aerial robotics, is a rotary electromagnetic actuator that converts electrical energy from the flight battery into mechanical rotational energy (torque × angular velocity) used to spin the propellers, thereby generating the aerodynamic thrust required for flight. In multirotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors** are the de-facto industry standard due to their high power-to-weight ratio, high efficiency (**>85%**), and low maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand the difference between Brushed and Brushless DC motors, the meaning of the **KV rating**, and how motor specifications couple to propeller selection.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a drone motor as the "heart" of a quadcopter — the same way your heart pumps blood, the motor pumps air downwards using its propeller. When you push a syringe's plunger down, air rushes out the bottom and pushes the syringe up. A propeller does exactly this to air: it accelerates a large mass of air downwards at a moderate speed, and by Newton's Third Law, the drone gets pushed upwards. The BLDC motor is essentially the "muscle" that drives this propeller at thousands of revolutions per minute.

**Key Motor Terminology:**

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **KV Rating** | Motor RPM per volt applied (no-load) | **RPM/V** |
| **Torque (τ)** | Rotational force produced by the motor | **N·m** |
| **Thrust (T)** | Linear force generated by propeller rotation | **N (Newtons)** |
| **Power (P)** | Rate of doing work: $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | **W (Watts)** |
| **Current (I)** | Electrical current draw under load | **A (Amps)** |
| **Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of mechanical output to electrical input | **%** |

---

## 1.2 Total Lift

**Formal Definition:** *Total Lift* is the cumulative upward aerodynamic force generated by **all** the propellers/rotors of the UAV acting in parallel. For a multirotor with $n$ identical rotors, total lift is simply the sum of individual rotor thrusts.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine four people each blowing air into a single balloon from four different directions. The balloon stays up because the total upward push equals the weight of the balloon. In a quadcopter, each of the four motors is one of these "blowers." Total lift = sum of all four upward pushes.

---

## 1.3 Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)

**Formal Definition:** An **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** is a dedicated solid-state power electronic circuit that regulates the rotational speed of a BLDC motor by commutating its three-phase windings in a precise sequence and controlling the power delivered. The ESC receives a low-power command signal (typically a **PWM servo pulse of 1000–2000 μs at 50 Hz**) from the flight controller and switches the high-current battery voltage to the motor phases using MOSFETs.

**Conceptual Analogy:** The ESC is the "translator" between the flight controller (which speaks a quiet, polite language of millisecond pulses) and the motor (which needs raw, high-current electrical punches). Without the ESC, the flight controller cannot directly handle the high currents (often **30–60 A**) that motors demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** Modern UAV ESCs use protocols like **DShot, OneShot, or Multishot** (digital) instead of legacy analog PWM for lower latency and higher resolution.

---

## 1.4 Flight Battery (LiPo)

**Formal Definition:** The *Flight Battery* is the onboard electrochemical energy storage that supplies high discharge current to the ESCs and motors. In modern UAVs, **Lithium Polymer (LiPo)** batteries are universal because of their exceptional energy density (**150–250 Wh/kg**), high C-rating discharge capability, and ability to deliver hundreds of amps in compact form factors.

**Conceptual Analogy:** The battery is the "fuel tank" of the drone. A higher C-rating means a wider fuel pipe — it can deliver more "fuel" (current) per second without overheating.

**Critical LiPo Parameters:**

- **Cell Count (S):** A 4S LiPo = 4 cells in series; nominal voltage = **3.7 V × cells** = 14.8 V for 4S.
- **Capacity:** Measured in **mAh (milliampere-hours)**.
- **C-Rating:** Maximum safe continuous discharge rate; Max current = Capacity × C-rating.

---

## 1.5 Radio Transmitter and Receiver (TX/RX)

**Formal Definition:** The **Radio Transmitter (TX)** is the handheld pilot-side controller that converts stick/switch inputs into a modulated radio-frequency (RF) signal. The **Radio Receiver (RX)** mounted on the UAV demodulates this signal and outputs the decoded channel data (throttle, roll, pitch, yaw, auxiliary switches) to the flight controller.

**Conceptual Analogy:** The TX is your "voice" and the RX is the drone's "ears." You speak (transmit) your commands, and the drone hears (receives) them. The 2.4 GHz band is the "language" they share because it is license-free worldwide and offers good range/penetration.

---

## 1.6 Flight Controller (FC)

**Formal Definition:** The **Flight Controller** is the central embedded computer of the UAV, typically built around a 32-bit microcontroller (e.g., **STM32F405, STM32F722, F765**). It runs real-time firmware (e.g., **PX4, Ardupilot, Betaflight, INAV**) that fuses sensor data, executes flight control algorithms (PID), and generates motor commands at high frequency (**up to 8 kHz loop rate**).

**Conceptual Analogy:** The flight controller is the "brain" and "central nervous system" of the drone. It reads signals from the sensors (eyes, ears, balance organs), compares them to the pilot's desired state, calculates the necessary correction, and sends motor commands — all thousands of times per second.

---

## 1.7 Sensors

**Formal Definition:** A *sensor* in aerial robotics is any physical transducer that measures a flight-relevant quantity and converts it into an electrical signal interpretable by the flight controller. The Minimum Sensor Suite (MSS) for stable multirotor flight includes an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** containing a 3-axis accelerometer and 3-axis gyroscope; more advanced systems add magnetometer, barometer, GPS, optical flow, and LiDAR.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Sensors are the drone's "senses." Without sensors, the flight controller would be "blind" — it would not know which way is up, how fast it is rotating, or where it is in the world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The **IMU (Accelerometer + Gyroscope)** is the *non-negotiable* minimum sensor for any stabilized multirotor. All other sensors are augmentations that enable higher-level autonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLDC Motor — Operating Principle and Equations

A BLDC motor is a synchronous machine: its rotor (permanent magnets) rotates at the same electrical frequency as the stator's rotating magnetic field. Speed is therefore set by the **electrical commutation frequency** produced by the ESC.

**KV Rating Equation:**

$$KV = \frac{\text{No-load RPM}}{V_{applied}}$$

**Torque Constant and Back-EMF:**

$$K_t = \frac{1}{K_v} \quad \text{(in SI units, when } K_v \text{ in rad/s/V)}$$

**Motor Power Equations:**

$$P_{electrical} = V \cdot I$$

$$P_{mechanical} = \tau \cdot \omega = 2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{n}{60}$$

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{mechanical}}{P_{electrical}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The KV rating tells you the *speed* of the motor. A high KV motor (e.g., **2400 KV**) spins fast with low torque per amp — suited for small, aggressive multirotors. A low KV motor (e.g., **400 KV**) spins slowly but produces high torque — suited for large propellers on heavy-lift platforms.

---

## 2.2 Propeller Aerodynamics and Thrust Generation

A propeller accelerates a mass of air downwards. The thrust produced by a single propeller follows **BEMT (Blade Element Momentum Theory)**, approximated by the thrust coefficient formulation:

$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

Where:
- $T$ = Thrust in **Newtons**
- $C_T$ = Thrust coefficient (dimensionless, typically **0.08 – 0.12** for small UAV props)
- $\rho$ = Air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, ISA
- $n$ = Rotational speed in **revolutions per second (Hz)**
- $D$ = Propeller diameter in **meters**

**Power Absorbed by Propeller:**

$$P_{prop} = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot n^3 \cdot D^5$$

Where $C_P$ is the power coefficient (typically **0.04 – 0.06**).

---

## 2.3 Total Lift of a Multirotor

For a symmetric multirotor with $N$ identical rotors spinning at the same speed $n$:

$$T_{total} = N \cdot T_{rotor} = N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

**Hover Condition (Fundamental Equation of Multirotor Flight):**

$$T_{total} = m_{total} \cdot g$$

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n_0^2 \cdot D^4 = m_{total} \cdot g$$

Solving for the hover RPM $n_0$:

$$n_0 = \sqrt{\frac{m_{total} \cdot g}{N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}$$

**Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR):**

$$TWR = \frac{T_{total,max}}{m_{total} \cdot g}$$

- **TWR > 1.0** → Multirotor can climb vertically.
- **TWR ≈ 2.0** → Standard for a sport multirotor.
- **TWR > 3.0** → Required for aggressive acrobatic flight.
- **TWR < 1.0** → Cannot take off.

---

## 2.4 Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) Signal Theory

The legacy analog ESC command signal is a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) servo pulse**:

$$f_{PWM} = 50 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{period} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Pulse Width Mapping:**

$$t_{pulse} = 1000 \,\mu s \;\longleftrightarrow\; 0\% \text{ throttle (motor off)}$$

$$t_{pulse} = 1500 \,\mu s \;\longleftrightarrow\; 50\% \text{ throttle (mid)}$$

$$t_{pulse} = 2000 \,\mu s \;\longleftrightarrow\; 100\% \text{ throttle (full)}$$

**Modern Digital Protocols (DShot):**

- **DShot150:** 150 kbaud, 16 bits/frame
- **DShot600:** 600 kbaud, very low latency
- **DShot1200:** 1.2 Mbaud, racing applications

These encode throttle command directly as a digital number, eliminating PPM jitter.

---

## 2.5 Flight Battery — Energy and Discharge Equations

**Nominal Voltage:**

$$V_{nom} = N_{cells} \times 3.7 \text{ V}$$

**Fully Charged Voltage:** $V_{full} = N_{cells} \times 4.20 \text{ V}$

**Cutoff Voltage:** $V_{cut} = N_{cells} \times 3.30 \text{ V}$ (storage) or $3.50 \text{ V}$ (flight cutoff for safety).

**Maximum Continuous Discharge Current:**

$$I_{max} = \text{Capacity (Ah)} \times C_{rating}$$

**Energy Stored:**

$$E_{battery} = V_{nom} \times \text{Capacity (Ah)} \quad \text{(Wh)}$$

**Flight Time Estimation (Multirotor):**

$$t_{flight} = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)} \times V_{nom}}{P_{hover} \times 1000} \times 60 \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

---

## 2.6 Radio Link — Range and Link Budget

The **Friis Transmission Equation** for free-space path loss:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Transmitted power
- $G_t, G_r$ = Antenna gains
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength
- $R$ = Range

**Practical UAV Radio Bands:**

- **2.4 GHz** — Control link (RC), short range, high bandwidth.
- **433 MHz / 900 MHz** — Long-range telemetry.
- **5.8 GHz** — Video downlink (FPV).

---

## 2.7 Flight Controller — Control Loop and Sensor Fusion

The FC runs a **cascaded PID control loop** with sensor fusion. For multirotor attitude control, the standard pipeline is:

1. **Sensor Sampling (IMU):** $\omega_{gyro}$, $a_{accel}$ at 1–8 kHz.
2. **Complementary Filter / EKF:** Fuses gyro and accelerometer → estimates roll, pitch, yaw.
3. **Outer Loop (Angle P):** Compares desired angle to estimated angle → produces desired angular rate.
4. **Inner Loop (Rate PID):** Compares desired rate to gyro rate → produces motor command.
5. **Mixer:** Converts (roll, pitch, yaw, throttle) into individual motor speeds.

**Complementary Filter (1D, conceptual):**

$$\hat{\theta} = \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta} + \omega_{gyro} \cdot \Delta t) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}$$

Where $\alpha$ is typically **0.95–0.98**, weighting the gyro heavily (short-term accurate) and the accelerometer lightly (long-term stable).

---

## 2.8 Sensor Specifications

| Sensor | Measures | Range (typical UAV) | Noise Density |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MEMS Gyroscope (e.g., MPU-6000)** | Angular rate | $\pm$2000 °/s | **0.005 °/s/√Hz** |
| **MEMS Accelerometer** | Linear acceleration | $\pm$16 g | **400 μg/√Hz** |
| **Magnetometer (e.g., HMC5883L)** | Magnetic field | $\pm$8 Gauss | — |
| **Barometer (e.g., BMP280)** | Pressure → altitude | 300–1100 hPa | **0.12 hPa RMS** |
| **GPS (u-blox NEO-M8N)** | Position, velocity | 50 channels | **2.5 m CEP** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Sensor fusion in modern flight controllers uses an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** with 24 states (or 22 in PX4) to combine IMU + GPS + baro + mag into a robust state estimate. This is the foundation of waypoint navigation, position hold, and return-to-home (RTH) autonomy.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$ | Single-rotor thrust | **N** |
| 2 | $T_{total} = N \cdot T$ | Total multirotor lift | **N** |
| 3 | $T_{total} = m g$ | Hover condition | **N** |
| 4 | $n_0 = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{N C_T \rho D^4}}$ | Hover RPM per motor | **rev/s** |
| 5 | $TWR = \frac{T_{max}}{m g}$ | Thrust-to-weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P = C_P \rho n^3 D^5$ | Propeller absorbed power | **W** |
| 7 | $P = \tau \omega$ | Mechanical power | **W** |
| 8 | $KV = \text{RPM}/V$ | Motor speed constant | **RPM/V** |
| 9 | $V_{nom} = 3.7 \cdot S$ | LiPo nominal voltage | **V** |
| 10 | $I_{max} = C \cdot \text{Cap}$ | Max discharge current | **A** |
| 11 | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Battery energy | **Wh** |
| 12 | $f = 1/T$ | PWM frequency relation | **Hz** |
| 13 | $PL_{dB} = 20 \log\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$ | Free-space path loss | **dB** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hover RPM from First Principles (Total Lift Balance)

**Given:**
- Quadcopter, $N = 4$ identical rotors
- Total mass $m_{total} = 1.5$ kg
- Propeller diameter $D = 0.25$ m (10 inch)
- Thrust coefficient $C_T = 0.10$ (assumed constant)
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Find:** Hover rotational speed $n_0$ in revolutions per second and per minute.

**Step 1 — Write the thrust equation for one rotor:**

$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $N$ for total lift of the multirotor:**

$$T_{total} = N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$

**Step 3 — Apply the hover condition (lift = weight):**

$$N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n_0^2 \cdot D^4 = m_{total} \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $n_0^2$:**

$$n_0^2 = \frac{m_{total} \cdot g}{N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$n_0^2 = \frac{1.5 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.10 \times 1.225 \times (0.25)^4}$$

Calculate the denominator step by step:

$$(0.25)^4 = 0.00390625 \text{ m}^4$$

$$4 \times 0.10 \times 1.225 \times 0.00390625 = 4 \times 0.10 \times 0.004784 = 0.001913 \text{ (approx.)}$$

$$n_0^2 = \frac{14.715}{0.001913} = 7692.0 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root to get $n_0$ in rev/s:**

$$n_0 = \sqrt{7692.0} = 87.7 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to RPM:**

$$n_{RPM} = n_0 \times 60 = 87.7 \times 60 = 5262 \text{ RPM per motor}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A 1.5 kg quadcopter with 10-inch props needs ~5260 RPM per motor to hover. If motor KV is 920 KV on a 4S (14.8 V) LiPo, max RPM = 920 × 14.8 = 13,616 RPM — comfortably above hover, giving the drone a healthy thrust margin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flight Time from Battery and Hover Power

**Given:**
- 4S LiPo, capacity $Q = 5000$ mAh = 5.0 Ah
- Nominal voltage $V_{nom} = 14.8$ V
- Hover power $P_{hover} = 200$ W (entire system)
- Battery usable fraction: 80 % (never fully discharge LiPo)

**Find:** Estimated flight time in minutes.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in battery:**

$$E_{total} = V_{nom} \times Q = 14.8 \times 5.0 = 74.0 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 2 — Usable energy (80% depth of discharge):**

$$E_{usable} = 0.80 \times 74.0 = 59.2 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Flight time in hours:**

$$t_{flight} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{hover}} = \frac{59.2}{200} = 0.296 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes:**

$$t_{flight} = 0.296 \times 60 = 17.76 \text{ min} \approx 17.8 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using mAh method:**

$$t = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)} \times V_{nom} \times \eta_{DoD}}{P_{hover} \times 1000} \times 60$$

$$t = \frac{5000 \times 14.8 \times 0.80}{200 \times 1000} \times 60 = 17.76 \text{ min} \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Real flight time is typically **70–80%** of the calculated value because (a) throttle is not always at hover, (b) wind adds drag, and (c) voltage sag at high C-rates reduces effective capacity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Discharge Current Verification

**Given:** 4S 5000 mAh 100C LiPo

**Step 1 — Convert capacity to Ah:**

$$Q = 5000 \text{ mAh} = 5.0 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by C-rating:**

$$I_{max} = Q \times C = 5.0 \times 100 = 500 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with motor current draw (assume 30 A peak per motor):**

$$I_{peak,total} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Margin check:**

$$\frac{I_{max}}{I_{peak,total}} = \frac{500}{120} = 4.17$$

The battery can deliver **4.17×** the peak demand — safe and conservative.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Component Function vs Failure Mode

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Input → Output | Common Failure Mode | Effect on Flight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BLDC Motor** | Convert electrical to mechanical | $V, I$ → $\tau, \omega$ | Bearing wear, magnet degauss | Reduced thrust, vibration |
| **Propeller** | Convert torque to thrust | $\omega$ → $T$ | Crack, imbalance | Vibration, crash |
| **ESC** | Commutate and regulate motor | PWM → 3-phase AC | MOSFET burnout | Motor stops |
| **LiPo Battery** | Store and deliver energy | Chemical → $V, I$ | Puncture, over-discharge | Fire, power loss |
| **TX / RX** | Command link | Stick → RF → PWM | RF interference, low battery | Loss of control |
| **Flight Controller** | Stabilize, navigate | Sensors → motor PWM | Firmware bug, sensor failure | Crash |
| **IMU** | Measure orientation rates | Inertia → digital | Bias drift, shock damage | Oscillation, divergence |
| **GPS** | Absolute position | Sat signals → lat/lon | Multipath, jamming | Position drift |
| **Barometer** | Altitude | Pressure → altitude | Light sensitivity errors | Altitude oscillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **every** subsystem has at least one failure mode. This is why redundancy (dual IMUs, dual GPS, dual FC) is used in safety-critical applications like medical delivery or military UAVs — a single point of failure can mean loss of an expensive aircraft or, worse, harm to people.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture of a Multirotor UAV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pilot[PILOT] -->|Stick inputs| tx[RADIO TRANSMITTER 2.4 GHz]
    tx -->|RF command| rx[RADIO RECEIVER mounted on UAV]
    rx -->|PWM or SBUS| fc[FLIGHT CONTROLLER STM32]

    fc -->|PWM or DShot| esc1[ESC Motor 1]
    fc -->|PWM or DShot| esc2[ESC Motor 2]
    fc -->|PWM or DShot| esc3[ESC Motor 3]
    fc -->|PWM or DShot| esc4[ESC Motor 4]

    batt[LIPO BATTERY 4S 14.8 V] -->|DC bus| pdb[POWER DISTRIBUTION BOARD]
    pdb -->|high current| esc1
    pdb -->|high current| esc2
    pdb -->|high current| esc3
    pdb -->|high current| esc4
    pdb -->|regulated 5 V| fc
    pdb -->|regulated 5 V| rx

    esc1 -->|3 phase AC| m1[BLDC MOTOR 1]
    esc2 -->|3 phase AC| m2[BLDC MOTOR 2]
    esc3 -->|3 phase AC| m3[BLDC MOTOR 3]
    esc4 -->|3 phase AC| m4[BLDC MOTOR 4]

    m1 --> p1[PROPELLER CW 10 inch]
    m2 --> p2[PROPELLER CCW 10 inch]
    m3 --> p3[PROPELLER CW 10 inch]
    m4 --> p4[PROPELLER CCW 10 inch]

    p1 -->|thrust| air[AIRFRAME mass 1.5 kg]
    p2 -->|thrust| air
    p3 -->|thrust| air
    p4 -->|thrust| air

    imu[IMU accel plus gyro] -->|SPI or I2C| fc
    mag[MAGNETOMETER] -->|I2C| fc
    baro[BAROMETER] -->|I2C| fc
    gps[GPS MODULE] -->|UART| fc
    sonar[SONAR or OPTICAL FLOW] -->|UART| fc

    fc -.->|telemetry 433 MHz| gcs[GROUND CONTROL STATION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = electrical / data flow in normal operation. The dashed arrow from FC to GCS is the optional **telemetry downlink** for monitoring during flight. Notice the *closed control loop*: pilot → FC → ESC → motor → propeller → airframe motion → IMU → FC (back to FC).

---

## 4.2 Flight Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[IMU raw data] --> b[Sensor Fusion EKF]
    c[GPS data] --> b
    d[BARO data] --> b
    e[MAG data] --> b
    b --> f[State Estimate position, velocity, attitude]
    f --> g[Outer Loop Angle P]
    h[Pilot Desired Angle] --> g
    g --> i[Inner Loop Rate PID]
    f --> i
    i --> j[MIXER]
    k[Throttle command] --> j
    j --> l[Motor 1 PWM]
    j --> m[Motor 2 PWM]
    j --> n[Motor 3 PWM]
    j --> o[Motor 4 PWM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Sensor fusion produces a *single, consistent state estimate*. The outer loop (angle) is slow (~250 Hz), the inner loop (rate) is fast (~4 kHz). This is the **cascaded PID** architecture used in PX4, Ardupilot, and Betaflight.

---

## 4.3 ESC Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[Command Input PWM or DShot] --> decoder[Signal Decoder]
    decoder --> mcu[Microcontroller]
    mcu -->|gate drive| hs1[High-side FET Phase A]
    mcu -->|gate drive| hs2[High-side FET Phase B]
    mcu -->|gate drive| hs3[High-side FET Phase C]
    mcu -->|gate drive| ls1[Low-side FET Phase A]
    mcu -->|gate drive| ls2[Low-side FET Phase B]
    mcu -->|gate drive| ls3[Low-side FET Phase C]
    hs1 --> motor[BLDC Motor 3 phase windings]
    hs2 --> motor
    hs3 --> motor
    ls1 --> motor
    ls2 --> motor
    ls3 --> motor
    bemf[BEMF sensing] --> mcu
    vbat[Battery voltage monitor] --> mcu
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The BEMF (Back Electromotive Force) feedback allows the ESC microcontroller to determine rotor position sensorlessly. Modern ESCs use **field-oriented control (FOC)** for smoother commutation and higher efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **KV rating** of a BLDC motor. A motor rated **920 KV** is powered by a **4S LiPo** (nominal 14.8 V). Compute the no-load RPM.

**Model Answer:**

> **KV rating** of a BLDC motor is defined as the motor's rotational speed in revolutions per minute (RPM) per volt of applied DC voltage, under no-load conditions.

**Calculation:**

$$RPM_{no\text{-}load} = KV \times V_{nominal} = 920 \times 14.8 = 13{,}616 \text{ RPM}$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **three** sensors mandatory for stable multirotor flight and state the physical quantity each measures.

**Model Answer:**

1. **3-axis MEMS Gyroscope** — measures angular velocity (°/s) around the body axes.
2. **3-axis MEMS Accelerometer** — measures linear acceleration (m/s²) including gravity vector.
3. **Barometer** — measures atmospheric pressure, from which altitude is derived.
*(Alternative: Magnetometer measures Earth's magnetic field for heading.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the function of an **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** in a multirotor UAV. Discuss the standard PWM command protocol and mention **two advantages** of DShot over analog PWM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadcopter has the following specifications: $N = 4$ rotors, propeller diameter $D = 0.254$ m, $C_T = 0.10$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, total mass $m = 2.0$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate:
  (i) Hover rotational speed in rev/s and RPM per motor.
  (ii) Total thrust at hover.
  (iii) Maximum thrust per motor if each motor can spin up to **6500 RPM** without exceeding safe limits. Comment on the **TWR** of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution A(a):**

> The **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** is a power-electronic interface between the flight controller and the BLDC motor. Its block diagram includes: PWM/DShot input → microcontroller → gate drivers → 3-phase MOSFET bridge (high-side and low-side) → BLDC motor.

**Standard PWM Protocol:**
- Frequency: **50 Hz**, period = 20 ms
- Pulse width: **1000 μs** = 0% throttle, **2000 μs** = 100% throttle, **1500 μs** = 50% throttle.

**Two advantages of DShot over analog PWM:**

1. **No jitter from PPM decoding** — DShot is a fully digital UART-based protocol, so the command value is not corrupted by noise or timing imprecision in the PPM frame.
2. **Bidirectional telemetry** — DShot allows the ESC to send back RPM, current, and temperature to the FC, enabling advanced safety features (e.g., motor stall detection, dynamic thrust estimation).

---

**Solution A(b):**

**(i) Hover rotational speed:**

$$n_0 = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{N C_T \rho D^4}}$$

$$n_0^2 = \frac{2.0 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.10 \times 1.225 \times (0.254)^4}$$

$$(0.254)^4 = 0.004166 \text{ m}^4$$

$$n_0^2 = \frac{19.62}{4 \times 0.10 \times 1.225 \times 0.004166} = \frac{19.62}{0.002041} = 9612$$

$$n_0 = 98.04 \text{ rev/s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n_{RPM} = 98.04 \times 60 = 5882 \text{ RPM per motor}$$

**(ii) Total thrust at hover:**

$$T_{total} = m g = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Maximum thrust at 6500 RPM:**

$$n_{max} = \frac{6500}{60} = 108.33 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$T_{max,total} = N \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n_{max}^2 \cdot D^4$$

$$= 4 \times 0.10 \times 1.225 \times (108.33)^2 \times 0.004166$$

$$= 0.49 \times 11{,}735 \times 0.004166 = 23.96 \text{ N (approx.)}$$

Wait — recalc more carefully:

$$(108.33)^2 = 11{,}735.4$$

$$0.49 \times 11{,}735.4 = 5750.3$$

$$5750.3 \times 0.004166 = 23.96 \text{ N}$$

**Comment on TWR:**

$$TWR = \frac{T_{max,total}}{m g} = \frac{23.96}{19.62} = 1.22$$

**TWR = 1.22** → the quadcopter can take off and climb, but with **only 22% thrust margin**. It will be sluggish in maneuvers and unable to fly aggressively. A **sport quad typically needs TWR > 2.0**, so this system is underpowered.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a BLDC motor** used in multirotors. State the relationship between **KV rating, torque constant $K_t$**, and derive the **motor power equation** $P = \tau \omega$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drone uses a **6S 10000 mAh 25C LiPo battery**. Calculate:
  (i) Nominal and fully charged battery voltage.
  (ii) Maximum safe continuous discharge current.
  (iii) Total energy stored in Wh and kJ.
  (iv) If the drone hovers at a power of **400 W**, estimate the **flight time** assuming 80% depth of discharge. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution B(a):**

> A **BLDC (Brushless DC) motor** uses permanent magnets on the rotor and electronically commutated windings on the stator. The ESC energizes the stator phases in a precisely timed sequence (using either Hall sensors or sensorless BEMF detection), creating a rotating magnetic field. The rotor's permanent magnets lock onto this field and rotate synchronously.

**KV and Torque Constant:**

$$KV \;\;[\text{RPM/V}] \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \omega_0 = KV \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} \;\;[\text{rad/s/V}]$$

The torque constant is the reciprocal in SI units:

$$K_t = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{60}{2\pi \cdot KV_{RPM/V}} \;\;[\text{N·m/A}]$$

**Derivation of Motor Power:**

Power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft, work done in one revolution = torque × angle:

$$W_{1\,rev} = \tau \cdot 2\pi$$

If the shaft rotates at $n$ revolutions per second, work per second is:

$$P = W_{1\,rev} \times n = 2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot n = \tau \cdot \omega$$

Where $\omega = 2\pi n$ is the angular velocity in **rad/s**. Thus:

$$\boxed{P = \tau \cdot \omega}$$

**For a given motor**, electrical input power is $P_{elec} = V I$, and the mechanical output is $P_{mech} = \tau \omega$. Efficiency is $\eta = P_{mech}/P_{elec}$.

---

**Solution B(b):**

**(i) Nominal and fully charged voltage:**

- 6S LiPo: $N_{cells} = 6$
- Nominal: $V_{nom} = 6 \times 3.7 = 22.2$ V
- Fully charged: $V_{full} = 6 \times 4.20 = 25.2$ V

**(ii) Maximum continuous discharge current:**

$$I_{max} = C \times Q = 25 \times 10{,}000 \text{ mAh} = 25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Total energy stored:**

$$E = V_{nom} \times Q = 22.2 \text{ V} \times 10 \text{ Ah} = 222 \text{ Wh}$$

$$E = 222 \times 3600 = 799{,}200 \text{ J} = 799.2 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iv) Flight time at 400 W with 80% DoD:**

$$E_{usable} = 0.80 \times 222 = 177.6 \text{ Wh}$$

$$t = \frac{177.6}{400} = 0.444 \text{ h} = 26.64 \text{ min}$$

**Flight time ≈ 26.6 minutes** (theoretical, hover only; expect ~20 min in real flight).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $g$ in hover condition** — many students write $T = m$ instead of $T = m g$. Always include gravitational acceleration; weight is in Newtons, not kg.
> 2. **Confusing thrust coefficient** $C_T$ with power coefficient $C_P$ — they are different dimensionless numbers. The thrust exponent on $n$ is **2**; the power exponent on $n$ is **3**.
> 3. **Confusing nominal, full-charge, and cutoff voltages** of LiPo. KTU exam questions often specify 4.2 V/cell or 3.7 V/cell — read the wording carefully.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors** between RPM and rev/s: $1 \text{ rev/s} = 60 \text{ RPM}$. Many KTU students lose 1–2 marks per question on this alone.
> 5. **Battery capacity is in mAh**, but the formula needs Ah: divide by 1000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **BLDC motor** = synchronous machine; commutation by ESC; characterized by **KV (RPM/V)**.
- ✅ **KV × Voltage = max no-load RPM** — quick sanity check for any motor/battery combo.
- ✅ **Single-rotor thrust:** $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$ — quadratic in RPM, fourth-power in diameter.
- ✅ **Hover condition:** $T_{total} = m g$ — this is the most important equation in multirotor dynamics.
- ✅ **TWR > 1.0** to fly, **TWR > 2.0** for sport, **TWR > 3.0** for acrobatics.
- ✅ **ESC** receives PWM (50 Hz, 1000–2000 μs) or DShot; switches battery voltage across 3-phase MOSFET bridge.
- ✅ **LiPo:** $V_{nom} = 3.7 \times S$; full = 4.2 V/cell; cutoff = 3.3–3.5 V/cell; max current = C × Ah.
- ✅ **TX/RX** at 2.4 GHz for control; range governed by **Friis equation** (free-space path loss $\propto 1/R^2$).
- ✅ **Flight controller** runs at 1–8 kHz; cascaded PID; **outer (angle) loop slow, inner (rate) loop fast**.
- ✅ **IMU** = 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer = *minimum sensor suite*; supplemented by mag, baro, GPS, optical flow.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion** (complementary filter or EKF) is required because no single sensor is sufficient: gyros drift, accelerometers are noisy in vibration, GPS is too slow.
- ✅ **Flight time** $\approx$ (Capacity × $V_{nom}$ × DoD) / $P_{hover}$ → typical multirotor flight is 15–30 minutes.
- ✅ **Closed control loop:** Pilot → TX → RX → FC → ESC → Motor → Prop → Airframe → IMU → FC (continuous).
- ✅ For KTU exams: **always include units**, show every algebraic step, and explicitly state assumptions (e.g., $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camera Assembling for Quadcopter - Connectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Camera Assembling for Quadcopter - Connectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Aerial Robotics** and specifically the **PERAT745 – Aerial Robotics (KTU 2024 Scheme)** syllabus, **Camera Assembling for Quadcopter – Connectors** refers to the systematic electromechanical integration of onboard imaging payloads (FPV cameras, action cameras, gimbals, video transmitters, and telemetry cameras) onto an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) using standardized interconnect systems. A **connector** in this domain is defined as a **pair of mating electro-mechanical interfaces** (male plug and female receptacle) that establish electrical power distribution, data signalling, and mechanical fixation between the camera module and the flight stack (Flight Controller, ESC, VTX, BEC, and battery bus).

Per the KTU Module-2 framework on **Drone Payloads & Assembly**, connectors are classified under three functional categories:

1. **Power Connectors** — deliver regulated DC voltage to the camera and VTX.
2. **Data/Video Connectors** — transmit analog video, digital video, or control telemetry.
3. **Mechanical/Mounting Connectors** — physically attach the camera to the frame or gimbal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Aerial Robotics):** Camera mounting, connector selection, and signal routing are core hands-on assembly competencies. Connector mismatch is the **#1 cause of in-flight camera failure** in prototype UAVs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a quadcopter's camera system like a **hospital patient monitoring setup**:
- The **camera** is the *patient* (the source of data).
- The **flight controller** is the *doctor's monitor*.
- The **VTX (Video Transmitter)** is the *wireless broadcasting antenna*.
- The **connectors** are the *IV lines and ECG cables* that deliver power (oxygen) and relay data (heartbeat).

If the IV line is too thin (undersized connector rated for less current), the patient "starves" (camera browns out mid-flight). If the cable is too long and noisy, the heartbeat (video signal) gets distorted. The right connector for the right job is, therefore, a **life-support decision** for a quadcopter.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and standards recur in KTU numericals on this topic:

- **Nominal LiPo cell voltage:** **3.7 V** (1S)
- **Full-charge LiPo voltage:** **4.2 V** (per cell)
- **Standard FPV camera supply:** **5 V DC** (regulated via BEC)
- **HD camera supply:** **7.4 V – 12.6 V** (2S–3S direct)
- **Signal impedance (analog video):** **75 Ω** (standard for CVBS)
- **Operating temperature of UAV-grade connectors:** **−20 °C to +85 °C**
- **MIPI CSI-2 data rate:** up to **2.5 Gbps per lane** (modern HD cameras)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the relationship **P = V × I** in the context of selecting a power connector with sufficient current rating. A standard FPV camera (e.g., 600 TVL analog) draws **~120 mA**, while an HD action camera (e.g., GoPro-class) may draw **up to 1.5 A** — a **12× difference** demanding different connector classes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Breakdown of Connectors in a Quadcopter Camera Assembly

A typical camera-to-quadcopter electrical chain is composed of the following connector stages, traversed in this order:

1. **Battery → Power Distribution Board (PDB) / BEC** — heavy-gauge XT60, XT30, or Deans (T-plug).
2. **BEC → 5V / 12V Camera Rail** — JST-SH, JST-GH, or 2-pin JST-PH.
3. **Camera → Flight Controller (OSD, Betaflight)** — 6-pin or 4-pin JST-SH (signal + power + ground).
4. **Camera → VTX (Video Transmitter)** — coax with **MMCX** or **U.FL (IPEX)** RF connector.
5. **VTX → Antenna** — **RP-SMA** or **SMA** threaded RF connector.
6. **Gimbal → Flight Controller** — multi-pin DF13 or JST-GH for PWM/SBus control.
7. **HD Action Camera Mount** — mechanical only (1/4-20 UNC thread or GoPro mount).

## 2.2 Why Each Connector Type Exists (The "Why")

| Function | Why a dedicated connector? |
|---|---|
| **XT60 / XT30** | High current (continuous 30–60 A) needs low resistance and secure locking. |
| **JST-SH (1.0 mm pitch)** | Compact size for FC-to-camera harness; standardized pinout across Betaflight. |
| **JST-PH (2.0 mm pitch)** | Slightly larger, used for VTX and camera power in mini-quads. |
| **U.FL (IPEX MHF)** | Tiny RF connector for low-power VTX; high-frequency mating is delicate. |
| **MMCX** | More robust than U.FL, supports higher power, used in long-range VTX. |
| **RP-SMA** | Threaded, weatherproof RF; mechanically stable during crashes. |
| **DF13** | Multi-pin (3–10 pin) for gimbal communication (S.Bus, PWM, telemetry). |
| **MIPI CSI (15–22 pin FPC)** | High-speed serial video for onboard HD processing (Jetson, RPi). |
| **FPC / FFC** | Flexible flat cable — required where the camera moves on a gimbal. |

## 2.3 The Core Engineering Trade-offs

When selecting connectors for a quadcopter camera assembly, the engineer must balance:

- **Current carrying capacity (A)** vs **weight (g)**
- **Mating cycle durability** vs **cost (INR / USD)**
- **Signal frequency (MHz–GHz)** vs **insertion loss (dB)**
- **Vibration resistance** vs **ease of assembly**
- **Pin pitch (mm)** vs **soldering difficulty**

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Rule of Thumb:** Connector current rating should be **at least 1.5× the steady-state load current** of the camera/VTX, and **2× the peak inrush current** (e.g., when the VTX switches from standby to full transmit power).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Critical LaTeX Notice:** Per the protocol, the vertical pipe `\|` is replaced with `\vert` in tables to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrical Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | Power consumed by camera | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | Voltage drop across connector | **V (Volts)** |
| 3 | Power Loss in Connector | $P_{\text{loss}} = I^{2} \cdot R_{\text{contact}}$ | Heat dissipated at the contact | **W (Watts)** |
| 4 | Voltage Drop | $\Delta V = I \cdot R_{\text{contact}}$ | Undesirable drop at the joint | **mV (millivolts)** |
| 5 | Insertion Loss (RF) | $IL = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | RF signal loss across connector | **dB (decibels)** |
| 6 | Return Loss | $RL = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left\vert \Gamma \right\vert$ | Reflection coefficient magnitude | **dB** |
| 7 | Bandwidth–Distance Limit | $t_{\text{rise}} \geq 0.35 / BW$ | Rise time vs bandwidth for video | **ns** |
| 8 | Energy in a Capacitor (VBAT filter) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in bulk capacitor | **J (Joules)** |
| 9 | Connector Resistance (rule) | $R_{\text{contact}} \leq \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{max}}}{I_{\text{load}}}$ | Max allowable contact resistance | **mΩ** |
| 10 | Camera Power Budget | $I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{cam}} + I_{\text{VTX}} + I_{\text{gimbal}}$ | Sum of all payload currents | **A (Amperes)** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defence:** Connector standards like **MIL-DTL-38999** ensure reliability in UAVs operating at high altitude and temperature extremes.
- **Cinematography Drones (DJI, Autel):** Use **custom proprietary connectors** that combine power, video, and gimbal control in a single quick-release mount.
- **FPV Racing:** Optimize for **lightweight, low-profile JST-SH and U.FL** to shave every gram and reduce cross-section drag.
- **Agricultural / Survey Drones:** **MIPI CSI** connectors link multi-spectral cameras to onboard NVIDIA Jetson processors for real-time NDVI computation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Camera Power Budget and Connector Current Rating

**Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** A quadcopter carries an FPV analog camera (5 V, 120 mA), a 5.8 GHz VTX (5 V, 600 mA at 25 mW transmit), and a 2-axis gimbal (5 V, 350 mA). Calculate the **total camera-payload current** and the **minimum current rating** of the connector feeding the BEC output. (Assume a 1.5× derating factor.)

### Step 1: Identify the load currents
The three subsystems are powered in parallel from a 5 V regulated rail.

$$
I_{\text{cam}} = 120\ \text{mA} = 0.120\ \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{\text{VTX}} = 600\ \text{mA} = 0.600\ \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{\text{gimbal}} = 350\ \text{mA} = 0.350\ \text{A}
$$

### Step 2: Sum the parallel currents

$$
I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{cam}} + I_{\text{VTX}} + I_{\text{gimbal}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 0.120 + 0.600 + 0.350
$$

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 1.070\ \text{A}
$$

The total camera-payload current is **1.07 A**.

### Step 3: Apply the KTU derating factor (1.5×)

$$
I_{\text{rated}} \geq 1.5 \times I_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{rated}} \geq 1.5 \times 1.070
$$

$$
I_{\text{rated}} \geq 1.605\ \text{A}
$$

The minimum connector current rating must be **≥ 1.605 A**.

### Step 4: Select a real connector

A **JST-PH 2-pin (5 V rail)** is rated for **2 A continuous**. Therefore, it satisfies the KTU derating rule (1.605 A < 2 A). ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Drop Across a Camera Power Connector

**Problem:** A 3-meter 22-AWG silicone wire harness terminated in a JST-SH connector feeds a 5 V / 0.5 A camera. The contact resistance of the JST-SH is **30 mΩ per pin**, and the wire resistance is **52.96 mΩ/m** (round trip = 2× the length for V+ and GND). Calculate the **voltage drop** at the camera input.

### Step 1: Compute the wire resistance for the round trip
Round trip length = $2 \times 3\ \text{m} = 6\ \text{m}$

$$
R_{\text{wire}} = 52.96\ \frac{\text{m}\Omega}{\text{m}} \times 6\ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_{\text{wire}} = 317.76\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

### Step 2: Compute the contact resistance
JST-SH has 2 contacts in series (V+ and GND):

$$
R_{\text{contact}} = 2 \times 30\ \text{m}\Omega = 60\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

### Step 3: Total series resistance

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{wire}} + R_{\text{contact}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 317.76 + 60
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 377.76\ \text{m}\Omega \approx 0.378\ \Omega
$$

### Step 4: Compute the voltage drop using Ohm's Law

$$
\Delta V = I \cdot R_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
\Delta V = 0.5 \times 0.378
$$

$$
\Delta V = 0.189\ \text{V}
$$

### Step 5: Voltage delivered to the camera

$$
V_{\text{cam}} = V_{\text{supply}} - \Delta V
$$

$$
V_{\text{cam}} = 5.000 - 0.189
$$

$$
V_{\text{cam}} = 4.811\ \text{V}
$$

This is **below the 5 V ± 5% tolerance** of most FPV cameras, and would cause **video noise / brownout**. The corrective action is to either (a) use a shorter harness, (b) use 18-AWG wire, or (c) switch to a lower-resistance connector (e.g., XT30 with **<5 mΩ** per pin).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Insertion Loss in a 5.8 GHz VTX RF Connector Chain

**Problem:** A 5.8 GHz VTX is connected to a cloverleaf antenna through **U.FL → pigtail → RP-SMA → antenna** chain. The insertion losses are: U.FL = **0.3 dB**, pigtail = **0.5 dB**, RP-SMA = **0.1 dB**. The VTX output is **25 dBm** (≈ 316 mW). What is the **Effective Radiated Power (ERP)** reaching the antenna feed-point?

### Step 1: Sum the insertion losses in the chain

$$
IL_{\text{total}} = IL_{\text{U.FL}} + IL_{\text{pigtail}} + IL_{\text{RP-SMA}}
$$

$$
IL_{\text{total}} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.1
$$

$$
IL_{\text{total}} = 0.9\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 2: Subtract from the source power (in dBm)

$$
P_{\text{ERP,dBm}} = P_{\text{VTX,dBm}} - IL_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{ERP,dBm}} = 25 - 0.9
$$

$$
P_{\text{ERP,dBm}} = 24.1\ \text{dBm}
$$

### Step 3: Convert back to milliwatts

$$
P_{\text{ERP,mW}} = 10^{\left(\frac{P_{\text{ERP,dBm}}}{10}\right)}
$$

$$
P_{\text{ERP,mW}} = 10^{\left(\frac{24.1}{10}\right)} = 10^{2.41}
$$

$$
P_{\text{ERP,mW}} \approx 257\ \text{mW}
$$

So the antenna is fed with **≈ 257 mW** instead of the full 316 mW — a **power loss of ~18.6%** purely due to connector-chain insertion loss. This is why **U.FL is replaced by MMCX** in long-range / high-power builds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Camera Connector

| Decision Criterion | JST-SH (1.0 mm) | JST-PH (2.0 mm) | U.FL (IPEX) | MMCX | MIPI CSI FPC | XT30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current rating (A)** | 1.0 | 2.0 | <0.5 RF | <0.5 RF | 0.3 (data) | 30 |
| **Frequency (Hz)** | DC | DC | 6 GHz | 6 GHz | 2.5 GHz | DC |
| **Weight (g)** | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.05 | 0.8 | 0.3 | 4.0 |
| **Mating cycles** | 30 | 30 | 30 | 500 | 20 | 1000 |
| **Vibration rating** | Medium | Medium | Poor | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| **Typical use** | FC↔Cam | VTX power | VTX RF | Long-range VTX | HD cam↔CPU | Battery |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quadcopter Camera Connection System

> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric. Labels contain no markdown formatting symbols that would break the parser.

```mermaid

    jetsonNode[ONBOARD COMPUTER JETSON NANO]
    hdCamNode[HD ACTION CAMERA 4K]
    gndNode[GROUND STATION GOGGLES OR MONITOR]

    battNode -->|XT60 CONNECTOR 60A| pdbNode
    pdbNode -->|JST SH 6 PIN| fcNode
    pdbNode -->|JST PH 2 PIN 5V| camAnalogNode

    pdbNode -->|JST PH 2 PIN 5V| gimbalNode

    camAnalogNode -->|ANALOG CVBS 75 OHM| vtxNode

    classDef power fill:#F9E79F,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000;
```
