
## 4.2 Connector Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[START NEW PAYLOAD]
    q1{WHAT IS THE FUNCTION?}
    q2{DC POWER LINE?}
    q3{ANALOG VIDEO?}
    q4{HD DIGITAL VIDEO?}
    q5{GIMBAL OR SERVO CONTROL?}
    q6{LOW POWER 25 MW?}
    q7{HIGH POWER ABOVE 200 MW?}

    a1[USE XT60 XT30 OR DEANS]
    a2[USE JST PH 2 PIN OR JST SH 4 PIN]
    a3[USE COAX WITH 75 OHM BNC OR DIRECT SOLDER]
    a4[USE MIPI CSI FFC 15 TO 22 PIN]
    a5[USE DF13 3 TO 10 PIN FOR SBUS]
    a6[USE U.FL IPEX CONNECTOR]
    a7[USE MMCX OR RP SMA]

    startA --> q1
    q1 -->|POWER| q2
    q1 -->|VIDEO| q3
    q1 -->|HD VIDEO| q4
    q1 -->|CONTROL| q5
    q2 -->|LT 10 AMP| a2
    q2 -->|GT 10 AMP| a1
    q3 --> a3
    q4 --> a4
    q5 --> a5
    q6 --> a6
    q7 --> a7
```

## 4.3 Physical Pinout Reference — JST-SH 6-Pin (Betaflight Cam Port)

```
 Pin 1  ── 5V (BEC OUTPUT)
 Pin 2  ── GND
 Pin 3  ── CAMERA VIDEO IN (CVBS)
 Pin 4  ── CAMERA VIDEO OUT (TO VTX / OSD)
 Pin 5  ── BATTERY VOLTAGE SENSE (VBAT, OPTIONAL)
 Pin 6  ── GROUND REFERENCE
```

> [!NOTE]
> Always verify the **pinout orientation** by consulting the specific flight controller's datasheet (e.g., SpeedyBee F7 V3). Reversing 5V and GND on a JST-SH cable will **permanently destroy** the camera sensor in under 1 second.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Module-2 contributes **20 marks** to the 100-mark end-semester exam. The "Camera Assembling – Connectors" topic typically appears in **Part A (3 marks)** for definitions and **Part B (14 marks)** for design/derivation questions.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: List and briefly explain any three types of connectors used to interface a camera module with a quadcopter flight controller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **JST-SH (1.0 mm pitch, 4–6 pin):** A compact, low-profile connector used to carry the 5 V camera power, ground, and analog video signal between the FPV camera and the FC's CAM-IN/OSD port. *(1 mark)*
2. **U.FL / IPEX MHF:** A micro-RF connector used to route the VTX's 5.8 GHz signal from the video transmitter to the antenna via a short coaxial pigtail. *(1 mark)*
3. **MIPI CSI FPC/FFC (15–22 pin):** A flexible flat-cable connector that carries high-speed serialized digital video from an HD camera to an onboard computer (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi). *(1 mark)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Why is the U.FL connector not recommended for high-power (≥ 600 mW) 5.8 GHz video transmitters in aerial robotics applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **U.FL connector** is rated for only **~30 mating cycles** and has poor mechanical retention — it can disconnect due to **vibration in flight**, causing total loss of video. *(1 mark)* Additionally, U.FL connectors exhibit **higher insertion loss (~0.3 dB at 5.8 GHz)** and **lower power handling** compared to MMCX (0.15 dB, 500 mating cycles) and RP-SMA (0.1 dB, threaded and vibration-proof). *(1 mark)* Therefore, for long-range or high-power VTX installations, the KTU-recommended practice is to use **MMCX (internal)** or **RP-SMA (external bulkhead)** connectors for mechanical and electrical reliability. *(1 mark)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **14 marks** (7 + 7).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** A quadcopter carries an FPV camera drawing **180 mA at 5 V**, a 5.8 GHz VTX drawing **700 mA at 5 V (peak 1.2 A during channel hop)**, and a 2-axis gimbal drawing **400 mA at 5 V**. **(7 marks)**
1. Calculate the total steady-state current on the 5 V BEC rail.
2. Determine the **minimum required current rating** of the camera/VTX power connector using a **1.5× derating factor**.
3. Justify the choice between **JST-SH** and **JST-PH** for this application.

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete connector signal path** from a 4S LiPo battery to a ground-station FPV monitor, identifying **every connector type** in the chain. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1: Steady-state current sum**
$$
I_{\text{ss}} = 0.180 + 0.700 + 0.400 = 1.280\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Apply 1.5× derating**
$$
I_{\text{rated}} \geq 1.5 \times 1.280 = 1.920\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 3: Connector selection justification**
A **JST-SH (1.0 mm pitch)** is rated for **~1 A continuous** — **insufficient** (1.92 A required).
A **JST-PH (2.0 mm pitch)** is rated for **~2 A continuous** — **sufficient** (1.92 A ≤ 2 A).

**Conclusion:** Use **JST-PH 2-pin** for the 5 V camera/VTX rail. *(7 marks distribution: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 3 for step 3 with justification.)*

---

**Part (b) — Signal path with connectors:**

The block diagram of the complete signal chain is:

> **Battery (4S LiPo, 14.8 V)** → **XT60 connector** → **PDB (with 5 V BEC)** → **JST-PH 2-pin (5 V to camera + VTX)** → **FPV Camera + VTX** → **U.FL connector + coax pigtail** → **RP-SMA bulkhead** → **5.8 GHz Antenna** → **Air RF link** → **Ground-station receiver with RP-SMA → HDMI to Goggles/Monitor**

Each stage is identified by its connector type, current/voltage rating, and function. *(7 marks for: 1 mark per connector identification + 2 marks for full signal-flow explanation + 1 mark for impedance matching (75 Ω video, 50 Ω RF) note.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** Derive the **voltage drop** across a 2-meter JST-SH camera harness (22-AWG, round trip) carrying 0.4 A at 5 V. Given: **wire resistance = 52.96 mΩ/m**, **JST-SH contact resistance = 30 mΩ per pin**. Comment on whether the camera will function correctly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **insertion loss**, **mechanical durability**, and **suitability for a long-range 5.8 GHz FPV build** of the following three RF connectors: **U.FL, MMCX, RP-SMA**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Round-trip length: $2 \times 2\ \text{m} = 4\ \text{m}$

Wire resistance:
$$
R_{\text{wire}} = 52.96 \times 4 = 211.84\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

Contact resistance (2 contacts in series):
$$
R_{\text{contact}} = 2 \times 30 = 60\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

Total resistance:
$$
R_{\text{total}} = 211.84 + 60 = 271.84\ \text{m}\Omega
$$

Voltage drop:
$$
\Delta V = I \times R_{\text{total}} = 0.4 \times 0.272 = 0.1087\ \text{V} \approx 109\ \text{mV}
$$

Voltage at camera:
$$
V_{\text{cam}} = 5.000 - 0.109 = 4.891\ \text{V}
$$

**Comment:** The drop is **~2.2%**, which is **within the 5% tolerance** of most FPV cameras — the camera will function, but with slight noise susceptibility. For safety, a 20-AWG harness or a JST-PH upgrade is recommended. *(7 marks: 2 for R_wire, 2 for R_contact, 1 for ΔV, 1 for V_cam, 1 for comment.)*

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | U.FL (IPEX) | MMCX | RP-SMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insertion loss @ 5.8 GHz** | ~0.3 dB | ~0.15 dB | ~0.10 dB |
| **Mating cycles** | 30 | 500 | 500+ |
| **Vibration resistance** | Poor (latches off) | Good (snap-on) | Excellent (threaded) |
| **Mount style** | Internal, board-side | Internal, board-side | External bulkhead |
| **Long-range suitability** | ❌ Not recommended | ⚠️ Acceptable | ✅ Industry standard |

**Conclusion:** For a **long-range FPV build** (≥ 1 km), the **RP-SMA bulkhead connector** is the only KTU-recommended option because of its **lowest insertion loss, highest mechanical durability, and threaded vibration-proof mating**. *(7 marks: 2 for table, 3 for explanation, 2 for conclusion.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Camera Connector Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pin pitch with current rating:** Students often write "JST-SH is rated 2 A" — this is **wrong**. JST-SH is rated **~1 A**; it is **JST-PH (2.0 mm pitch)** that is rated **2 A**. This single error costs **1 mark** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the round-trip factor in voltage-drop derivation:** Wire resistance is **per conductor**; you must multiply by 2 for V+ and GND. Examiners explicitly look for this.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong impedance for analog video:** FPV analog video uses **75 Ω** (CVBS standard), but RF (5.8 GHz) is **50 Ω**. Mixing these two values in a derivation is a guaranteed **−2 mark** penalty.
>
> 4. **Omitting the derating factor in connector selection:** A 1.5× derating is the **expected default** in KTU numericals. Stating the load current without applying the derating factor loses **1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Drawing connectors with reversed polarity in diagrams:** Always mark **Pin 1 = 5V (red wire)**, **Pin 2 = GND (black wire)** explicitly in any block diagram. Reversed pinout in a labeled diagram = **−1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring vibration:** In KTU design questions, you must explicitly mention **"vibration-resistant locking (RP-SMA threaded / XT60 snap-lock)"** as a design justification. A correct selection without this justification loses **1 mark**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connector ≠ Wire.** A connector is a *mating electro-mechanical interface*; it has its own current rating, contact resistance, and mating-cycle life.
- **Three functional categories:** **Power (XT60, JST)**, **Data/Video (JST-SH, MIPI CSI FFC)**, and **RF (U.FL, MMCX, RP-SMA)**.
- **Current rating derating rule:** **Rated ≥ 1.5 × I_load**; for VTX, **Rated ≥ 2 × I_peak** (to handle channel-hop transients).
- **Analog video is 75 Ω; RF is 50 Ω** — never mix these impedances.
- **Round-trip voltage drop** must be computed: $R_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot R_{\text{wire}} + n \cdot R_{\text{contact}}$ (where *n* = number of contacts in series).
- **U.FL is for low-power, internal short-range VTX**; **MMCX for medium-power; RP-SMA for high-power and long-range**.
- **MIPI CSI FPC/FFC** is the standard for HD digital video to onboard computers.
- **Pinout orientation matters:** Reversing 5 V and GND destroys the camera in **<1 second**.
- **Insertion loss** in dB is **subtracted** from the source power (in dBm) to get ERP at the antenna.
- **Mechanical locking:** Threaded (RP-SMA) > Snap (XT60) > Latch (U.FL).
- **KTU expects explicit justification** of every connector choice in a 14-mark question — stating the name alone is not enough.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mounting of Propellers and Powering Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mounting of Propellers and Powering Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Propeller Mounting** is the precise mechanical procedure of attaching rotating aerodynamic blades (propellers/props) to the output shafts of brushless DC (BLDC) motors on a multirotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV), ensuring correct rotational direction (CW/CCW), proper torque application, and dynamic balance.

**Powering Up** is the standardized, sequential electrical initialization procedure that supplies regulated DC power to the flight controller, ESCs (Electronic Speed Controllers), and onboard avionics, transitioning the aerial robot from a fully inert state to a flight-ready (armed) configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a fundamental **laboratory-grade operational competency**. Questions in ESE typically test your knowledge of safety protocols, identification of CW vs CCW propellers, the arming/disarming sequence, and the consequences of incorrect mounting (e.g., fly-aways, vibration, catastrophic failure).

## 1.2 Intuition / Real-World Analogy

Think of mounting propellers and powering up like **starting a twin-engine boat**:

- **Propeller Mounting = Fitting the Right Screw:** A boat has a left-side propeller and a right-side propeller. If you accidentally mount a *right-turning* prop on the *left* side, the boat will pull to one side uncontrollably. Similarly, a quadcopter has **Clockwise (CW)** and **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** propellers. Mixing them up means the drone will violently flip on takeoff (called a "Taco" or "tip-over").
- **Powering Up = The Ignition Key Sequence:** You don't just slam the throttle on a turbine engine. You must: insert the key, turn on the master switch, wait for avionics to boot, check the gauges, and then engage the starter. A drone's **Powering Up** follows a strict checklist: Connect battery → Wait for FC boot tones → Calibrate sensors → Arm motors.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Propeller Notation:** `Diameter × Pitch` in **inches** (e.g., **1045** = **10-inch** diameter, **4.5-inch** pitch).
> - **Standard Propeller Thread:** **M3, M5, M6, M8** depending on motor shaft diameter.
> - **Recommended Tightening Torque:** **0.6 – 1.2 Nm** for M3 threads (do not over-tighten; you risk stripping the prop hub).
> - **Standard LiPo Battery Voltage:** **11.1 V (3S)**, **14.8 V (4S)**, **22.2 V (6S)**.

## 1.4 Types of Propellers Used in Aerial Robotics

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Plastic (Polycarbonate)** | Cheap, flexible, breaks on impact | Beginner/training drones, indoor |
| **Carbon Fiber** | Stiff, expensive, brittle, high efficiency | Racing drones, heavy-lift UAVs |
| **Tri-blade** | More thrust, more drag, more battery drain | Cinewhoops, racing |
| **Bi-blade** | High efficiency, low drag | Long-endurance survey drones |
| **Pusher (Tractor)** | Pulls air from the front | Fixed-wing UAVs |
| **Puller** | Standard multirotor prop | Quadcopters, hexacopters |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theory of Propeller Mounting

### Step 1: Identification of CW vs CCW Propellers
- **Clockwise (CW) Prop:** Marked with an `R` (Right) on the hub. When you blow air through it (or spin it by hand), the leading edge cuts air in a clockwise direction.
- **Counter-Clockwise (CCW) Prop:** Marked with an `L` (Left) on the hub. Spins in a CCW direction.
- **Trick:** Look at the leading edge. The thinner, sharper edge always leads. If the prop is moving CW, the leading edge is the *left* face of the blade.

### Step 2: Motor Rotation Convention (Quadcopter X-Configuration)
- **Front-Right (FR) & Rear-Left (RL):** Rotate **Clockwise (CW)** → mount **CW (R)** props.
- **Front-Left (FL) & Rear-Right (RR):** Rotate **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** → mount **CCW (L)** props.
- *Why?* This counter-rotation cancels out the **net reactive torque** (gyroscopic precession) on the airframe, allowing stable hover.

### Step 3: Mounting Hardware
- **Prop Nut:** A central threaded nut tightened onto the motor shaft.
- **Prop Saver:** A rubber/silicone grommet system (no nut required) — common in micro drones.
- **Lock-nut / Nyloc Nut:** Self-locking nuts with a nylon insert to prevent loosening due to vibration.

### Step 4: Tightening Torque Logic
- Use a **torque wrench** or a **hex driver** set to the manufacturer's specified value.
- The general engineering rule:
  $$\tau = K \cdot d \cdot F$$
  where $\tau$ is the tightening torque, $K$ is the nut factor (≈ **0.2** for steel-on-steel), $d$ is the nominal bolt diameter, and $F$ is the clamp force.

### Step 5: Dynamic Balance
- An imbalanced prop causes **high-frequency vibration**, which degrades IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) sensor accuracy, blurs camera footage, and can cause premature bearing failure in motors.
- Imbalance is measured as **mg** (milligrams of mass offset) and corrected by adding small adhesive weights or sanding the heavier blade.

## 2.2 Powering Up Sequence (Standard Operating Procedure)

> [!NOTE]
> The powering-up sequence is governed by the **flight controller firmware** (e.g., PX4, ArduPilot, Betaflight, INAV). The typical boot order is:

1. **Pre-Power Safety Check:** Remove props. Verify wiring. Verify battery voltage.
2. **Battery Connection:** Connect the LiPo balance lead and main XT60/XT90 connector. The **Power Distribution Board (PDB)** energizes.
3. **BEC (Battery Elimination Circuit) Activation:** The PDB's 5V/12V regulator powers the flight controller.
4. **Boot Tones:** The FC emits a musical chime indicating healthy boot.
5. **Sensor Initialization:** Gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer, barometer, and GPS acquire lock.
6. **Transmitter Binding & Mode Verification:** Pilot verifies switch positions (Manual/Stabilize/Loiter).
7. **Arming:** Pilot commands ARM. ESCs beep. Motors begin to spin at idle RPM.
8. **Take-off Throttle Ramp:** Smooth throttle increase to hover RPM.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Propeller Tip Speed | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot n$ | m/s | $D$ = diameter, $n$ = rev/s |
| Thrust | $T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$ | N | $C_T$ = thrust coefficient, $\rho$ = air density (≈ 1.225 kg/m³) |
| Torque | $Q = C_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5$ | N·m | $C_Q$ = torque coefficient |
| Power Required | $P = 2 \pi \cdot n \cdot Q$ | W | |
| Motor KV × Voltage | $n_{max} \approx KV \cdot V_{nom}$ | RPM | No-load approximation |
| Tightening Torque | $\tau = K \cdot d \cdot F$ | N·m | $K \approx 0.2$ |
| Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot Ah$ | Wh | For flight time: $t \approx E / P_{hover}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Understanding these relationships is critical in UAV design. For example, doubling the prop diameter ($D$) at constant RPM **quadruples thrust** ($D^4$ scaling) but **multiplies torque by 32** ($D^5$ scaling), dramatically increasing motor and ESC current draw.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Doubling Propeller Size Quadruples Thrust

The momentum theory of propellers (Froude-Rankine Theorem) treats the propeller as an **actuator disk** that imparts a velocity increment $\Delta v$ to a mass flow of air $\dot{m}$.

**Step 1:** Define the mass flow rate through the propeller disk of area $A$:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_{ind}$$

**Step 2:** Express thrust as the rate of change of momentum:
$$T = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta v = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_{ind} \cdot \Delta v$$

**Step 3:** Using actuator disk theory, the induced velocity at the disk is half of the far-wake velocity:
$$v_{ind} = \frac{\Delta v}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:
$$T = \rho \cdot A \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta v}{2}\right) \cdot \Delta v = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot A \cdot (\Delta v)^2$$

**Step 5:** Express area in terms of diameter $D$:
$$A = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute to get the final form:
$$T = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot (\Delta v)^2 = \frac{\pi \rho (\Delta v)^2}{8} \cdot D^2$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** For a constant induced velocity $\Delta v$ (i.e., the same air acceleration), thrust scales as $D^2$. However, when we hold the **RPM constant** (as in real motors at a fixed throttle), the tip velocity scales linearly with $D$, leading to the well-known $D^4$ scaling used in empirical propeller tables.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tightening Torque for M3 Prop Nut

**Step 1:** Identify the standard nut factor for a steel nut on a steel motor shaft:
$$K = 0.20$$

**Step 2:** Convert nominal diameter to meters:
$$d = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Apply a target clamp force $F$. For a plastic hub, a safe value is $F \approx 500 \text{ N}$:
$$\tau = K \cdot d \cdot F = 0.20 \times 0.003 \times 500$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$\tau = 0.20 \times 1.5 = 0.30 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Add a safety margin of 2× to prevent loosening under vibration (final torque ≈ **0.6 N·m**), which matches the manufacturer specification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode → Consequence

| Failure Mode | Consequence | Detection Method |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| CW prop on CCW motor | Drone flips violently on takeoff | Visual inspection of prop markings |
| Loose prop nut | Prop flies off mid-flight | Audible clicking, vibration analysis |
| Over-tightened prop | Stripped threads, prop hub cracks | Torque wrench verification |
| Imbalanced prop | Camera shake, IMU drift | Vibration spectrum (FFT) analysis |
| Battery connected with reverse polarity | ESC/FC destruction | Smoke, magic smoke test |
| Arming without gyro calibration | Uncontrolled drift | FC warning LED or OSD message |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Propeller Mounting Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Motor Position] --> B{Motor Location?}
    B -->|Front Right or Rear Left| C[Install CW R Propeller]
    B -->|Front Left or Rear Right| D[Install CCW L Propeller]
    C --> E[Insert Prop on Motor Shaft]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Hand Tighten Prop Nut]
    F --> G[Apply Torque 0.6 to 1.2 Nm]
    G --> H[Spin Prop Manually]
    H --> I{Any Wobble or Grind?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Stop and Re-seat Prop]
    I -->|No| K[Mounting Complete]
    J --> E
    K --> L[Proceed to Powering Up]
```

## 4.2 Powering Up Sequence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Drone Off State] --> P1[Inspect Wiring and Frame]
    P1 --> P2[Connect LiPo Battery]
    P2 --> P3[PDB Energizes]
    P3 --> P4[BEC Powers Flight Controller]
    P4 --> P5[FC Emits Boot Tones]
    P5 --> P6{Sensors Healthy?}
    P6 -->|No| P7[Error LED / Disarm]
    P6 -->|Yes| P8[Bind Transmitter]
    P8 --> P9[Verify Flight Modes]
    P9 --> P10[Arm Command Issued]
    P10 --> P11[Motors Spin at Idle]
    P11 --> P12[Throttle Ramp to Hover]
    P12 --> P13[Flight Ready]
    P7 --> P0
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Power Distribution in a Quadcopter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[LiPo Battery 14.8V] --> PDB[Power Distribution Board]
    PDB --> ESC1[ESC 1]
    PDB --> ESC2[ESC 2]
    PDB --> ESC3[ESC 3]
    PDB --> ESC4[ESC 4]
    PDB --> BEC[BEC 5V Regulator]
    BEC --> FC[Flight Controller]
    FC --> ESC1
    FC --> ESC2
    FC --> ESC3
    FC --> ESC4
    FC --> GPS[GPS Module]
    FC --> RX[RC Receiver]
    ESC1 --> M1[Motor FL]
    ESC2 --> M2[Motor FR]
    ESC3 --> M3[Motor RR]
    ESC4 --> M4[Motor RL]
    M1 --> PROP1[CCW Prop L]
    M2 --> PROP2[CW Prop R]
    M3 --> PROP3[CW Prop R]
    M4 --> PROP4[CCW Prop L]
```

## 4.4 CW vs CCW Propeller Identification (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CW["Clockwise R Propeller"]
        CW1[Leading Edge] --> CW2[Trailing Edge]
        CW2 -.Spin Direction.-> CW3[Clockwise Rotation]
    end
    subgraph CCW["Counter-Clockwise L Propeller"]
        CCW1[Leading Edge] --> CCW2[Trailing Edge]
        CCW2 -.Spin Direction.-> CCW3[Counter-Clockwise Rotation]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between CW (R) and CCW (L) propellers. Why are both types used in a quadcopter?**

**Model Answer:**
CW (R) propellers rotate clockwise when viewed from above and are marked with an "R" on the hub. CCW (L) propellers rotate counter-clockwise and are marked with an "L". A quadcopter uses both types because the motors on diagonally opposite arms spin in opposite directions. This counter-rotation produces **equal and opposite reactive torques** that cancel out, preventing the airframe from spinning uncontrollably (Newton's Third Law applied to gyroscopic precession).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three safety precautions to be taken during the powering-up sequence of a multirotor UAV.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Always remove propellers** before connecting the battery during bench testing to prevent accidental injury from unintended motor spin-up.
2. **Verify battery polarity** (red to positive, black to negative) before connecting the XT60/XT90 connector to avoid damaging the ESCs.
3. **Maintain a safe distance** from the propellers after arming, as the motors can reach **several thousand RPM** within milliseconds of throttle input.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for mounting propellers on a quadcopter, including identification of CW/CCW props, mounting hardware, and the recommended tightening torque. Mention the engineering consequences of incorrect mounting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identification:**
A quadcopter in X-configuration has four motors. The **front-right (FR) and rear-left (RL)** motors rotate **clockwise (CW)**, and thus require **CW (R) propellers**. The **front-left (FL) and rear-right (RR)** motors rotate **counter-clockwise (CCW)**, requiring **CCW (L) propellers**. The "R" and "L" markings are stamped on the prop hub.

**Step 2 — Mounting Hardware:**
Each prop is secured using a **prop nut** (M2.5 / M3 / M5 depending on motor shaft diameter). High-vibration platforms should use **Nyloc nuts** or **thread-locking compound** (Loctite Blue 243).

**Step 3 — Tightening Torque:**
The tightening torque follows $\tau = K \cdot d \cdot F$. For a typical M3 prop on a plastic hub, the recommended torque is **0.6 N·m**. A hex driver or torque screwdriver is used to prevent over-tightening, which can strip the prop hub threads.

**Step 4 — Manual Spin Test:**
After tightening, the prop is spun manually. Any wobble, grinding noise, or uneven resistance indicates improper seating, requiring re-mounting.

**Engineering Consequences of Incorrect Mounting:**
- **CW prop on CCW motor** → drone flips violently on takeoff (catastrophic).
- **Loose prop** → prop flies off mid-air, causing total loss of aircraft.
- **Over-tightened prop** → cracked prop hub, vibration imbalance.
- **Imbalanced prop** → IMU sensor noise, blurred aerial imagery, reduced motor bearing life.

---

**(b)** Explain the complete powering-up sequence of a multirotor UAV, from battery connection to flight-ready state. Include the role of the PDB, BEC, and FC in this process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-Power Inspection:**
The pilot inspects wiring, frame integrity, and verifies battery state-of-charge (voltage ≥ 3.7 V/cell for LiPo).

**Step 2 — Battery Connection:**
The **LiPo battery** (e.g., 4S, 14.8 V) is connected via the main power connector. This energizes the **Power Distribution Board (PDB)**.

**Step 3 — BEC Activation:**
The PDB's **Battery Elimination Circuit (BEC)** steps down the high voltage to a regulated **5 V** (or 12 V) rail, which powers the **Flight Controller (FC)** and RC receiver.

**Step 4 — Flight Controller Boot:**
The FC boots its firmware (e.g., PX4, ArduPilot). It emits an audible **boot tone** through a piezo buzzer. The FC performs a **self-test (POST)** of its sensors.

**Step 5 — Sensor Initialization:**
The **gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer, barometer, and GPS module** acquire lock. The GPS typically takes 30–60 seconds for a first-time cold-start to acquire satellite fix.

**Step 6 — Transmitter Binding & Mode Check:**
The RC transmitter is bound. The pilot verifies the correct flight mode (Stabilize, AltHold, Loiter, etc.) is selected.

**Step 7 — Arming:**
The pilot issues the **ARM command** (via RC switch or stick gesture). The FC sends a PWM/DShot signal to each **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)**, which begins commutating the BLDC motors at idle RPM.

**Step 8 — Take-off Throttle Ramp:**
Smooth throttle increase transitions the drone from idle to hover thrust, achieving flight-ready state.

**Role Summary:**
- **PDB:** Distributes high-voltage battery power to all four ESCs and routes low-voltage power to the BEC.
- **BEC:** Provides regulated low voltage to the FC and avionics.
- **FC:** Acts as the central "brain" — reads sensor data, computes control loops, and commands ESCs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the identification, orientation, and mounting procedure of CW and CCW propellers on a quadcopter frame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.4. The mounting procedure follows the X-configuration convention: FR & RL motors take CW (R) props; FL & RR motors take CCW (L) props. The props are identified by the letter engraved on the hub. Mounting involves sliding the prop onto the motor shaft, hand-tightening the prop nut, and applying a calibrated torque of **0.6 N·m**. The CW prop's leading edge is its **left face** (when rotating CW), while the CCW prop's leading edge is its **right face**.)

---

**(b)** An aerial robot uses a 4S LiPo battery (14.8 V) and 2207 motors with a KV rating of 2450 RPM/V. Each propeller is 5 inches in diameter. Calculate the **(i)** theoretical no-load RPM, **(ii)** the prop tip speed at no-load, and **(iii)** the thrust produced assuming $C_T = 0.10$ at standard sea-level density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 14.8$ V
- $KV = 2450$ RPM/V
- $D = 5$ in $= 0.127$ m
- $C_T = 0.10$
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**(i) No-load RPM:**
$$n = KV \times V = 2450 \times 14.8 = 36,260 \text{ RPM}$$

Converting to rev/s:
$$n = \frac{36,260}{60} = 604.33 \text{ rev/s}$$

**(ii) Tip Speed:**
$$v_{tip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot n = \pi \times 0.127 \times 604.33$$
$$v_{tip} = 3.1416 \times 0.127 \times 604.33 = 241.13 \text{ m/s}$$

**Note:** This exceeds the speed of sound at sea level (343 m/s) only at ~70% — so subsonic. *(In practice, motor RPM is loaded down by ~70% to produce real thrust.)*

**(iii) Thrust:**
$$T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$$
$$T = 0.10 \times 1.225 \times (604.33)^2 \times (0.127)^4$$
$$T = 0.10 \times 1.225 \times 365,215 \times 2.60 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$T \approx 11.62 \text{ N per motor}$$

**Total Thrust (4 motors):**
$$T_{total} = 4 \times 11.62 = 46.48 \text{ N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** forget to convert RPM to rev/s when computing tip speed. A common mistake is using RPM directly in the $v_{tip} = \pi D n$ formula, leading to a 60× error.
> - **Do not** confuse no-load RPM with loaded RPM. The no-load calculation is a theoretical maximum; real hover RPM is ~50–70% of no-load.
> - **Do not** forget to state the **density of air** (1.225 kg/m³ at sea level) as per KTU standard assumptions.
> - **Unit consistency:** Mixing inches and SI units without conversion is the #1 cause of incorrect answers in KTU numericals.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CW (R) props go on FR & RL motors; CCW (L) props go on FL & RR motors** in a quadcopter X-configuration. *(High-yield KTU fact.)*
- Tightening torque for M3 prop nut ≈ **0.6 N·m**; do not over-tighten.
- Always **remove propellers** during bench testing and ESC calibration.
- Powering-up sequence: **Battery → PDB → BEC → FC boot tone → sensor init → ARM → motors idle → flight-ready.**
- Thrust scales as $D^4$ (diameter to the 4th power) at constant RPM; torque scales as $D^5$.
- Imbalanced propellers cause **IMU drift, vibration, and bearing wear** — always perform a manual spin test.
- **Reverse polarity** is the #1 cause of ESC and FC destruction during powering up.
- Standard air density: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level (KTU default).
- **Safety first:** Never arm a drone with people standing within the prop arc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crash Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crash Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crash Analysis** in Aerial Robotics is the systematic, multidisciplinary engineering study of the dynamic, structural, energetic, and operational consequences that occur when an Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) experiences an unintended loss of controlled flight, leading to impact with terrain, structures, other aircraft, or the ground. It integrates principles from **structural mechanics**, **impact dynamics**, **energy absorption theory**, **materials science**, and **flight data forensics** to evaluate, predict, and mitigate crash outcomes.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PERAT745, crash analysis encompasses the study of impact forces, energy dissipation, structural failure modes, and the post-event reconstruction of flight trajectories to determine root causes of UAV failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Crash Analysis is the bridge between theoretical flight dynamics and the unpredictable real-world operational environment. It answers three critical questions:
> 1. *Why* did the UAV crash? (Root cause analysis)
> 2. *How* did it crash? (Impact mechanics & trajectory)
> 3. *What* can be engineered to reduce severity? (Crashworthiness design)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car Crash Test:**
> Think of a UAV crash like a controlled car crash test. Engineers deliberately crash cars into walls to study crumple zones, airbag deployment timing, and deceleration profiles. Similarly, in aerial robotics, we analyze drone crashes by studying how the **kinetic energy** at the moment of impact gets distributed across the airframe, how the structure deforms or fractures, and how onboard sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes, GPS) recorded the final moments of flight.
>
> The key insight: **A crash is not just an event — it is a high-energy physical transaction** where energy must go somewhere (deformation, fragmentation, sound, heat). Crash analysis is the discipline of accounting for every joule of that energy.

## 1.3 Categories of UAV Crashes

| Crash Category | Description | Typical Energy Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT)** | UAV flies into obstacle due to navigation/control failure | High kinetic energy |
| **Loss of Link (LOL)** | Communication loss leading to uncontrolled descent | Variable |
| **Propulsion Failure** | Motor/battery failure causing autorotation or free-fall | High potential energy |
| **Structural Failure** | Wing/boom fracture mid-flight | Moderate to high |
| **Payload Separation** | Camera/sensor detachment affecting CG | Low to moderate |
| **Mid-Air Collision** | Impact with another aircraft or object | High localized impact |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravity acceleration:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Air density (sea level, ISA):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Speed of sound (sea level):** $a = 340.29 \text{ m/s}$
- **Key Crash Metric — Equivalent Crash Severity Index (ECSI):** measured in $g$-forces (multiples of $g$)
- **Energy unit:** Joule ($\text{J}$)
- **Impact force unit:** Newton ($\text{N}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of a UAV Crash — Step-by-Step Logic

A crash event can be decomposed into **four sequential phases**, each with distinct physics:

### Phase 1: Pre-Crash Flight (Steady-State Energy Accumulation)

The UAV accumulates kinetic energy $E_k$ and potential energy $E_p$ during normal flight.

$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgh$$

where $m$ is the UAV mass, $v$ is the true airspeed, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h$ is the altitude above the impact datum.

### Phase 2: Failure Onset & Uncontrolled Descent

The control system fails. The UAV transitions from controlled flight to an uncontrolled trajectory. Aerodynamic drag becomes a decelerating force:

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 S C_D$$

where $S$ is the reference wing/frontal area and $C_D$ is the drag coefficient.

### Phase 3: Impact Initiation (Contact Phase)

The instant the airframe touches the impact surface. This is characterized by an **impulsive force** with a very short duration (typically $\Delta t = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ seconds).

### Phase 4: Energy Dissipation (Post-Impact)

Energy is absorbed through:
- **Plastic deformation** of structural members
- **Fracture and fragmentation**
- **Frictional heat** at contact surfaces
- **Acoustic emission** (cracking sounds)
- **Elastic rebound** (minimal in composites)

## 2.2 Crashworthiness Design Philosophy

**Crashworthiness** is the ability of a structure to protect its payload (and occupants, in larger UAVs) during a crash by managing energy absorption and decelerations within survivable limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Crashworthiness:**
> *Energy must be dissipated progressively, not catastrophically.* The structure should crush in a controlled, predictable manner rather than shatter instantly.

### Crashworthiness Hierarchy (Applied to UAVs):

1. **Prevention** — Redundant flight control, geofencing, return-to-home (RTH)
2. **Mitigation** — Deployable parachutes, crumple zones, frangible booms
3. **Absorption** — Energy-absorbing landing gear, sacrificial skid plates
4. **Protection** — Shock-mounted avionics bays, foam-protected payloads
5. **Recovery** — Flight data recorder (black box), telemetry logs

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Translational kinetic energy | J |
| 2 | $E_p = mgh$ | Gravitational potential energy | J |
| 3 | $E_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Rotational kinetic energy (propellers, rotors) | J |
| 4 | $\bar{a} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | Mean deceleration during impact | m/s² |
| 5 | $F_{impact} = m \cdot \bar{a}$ | Average impact force (impulse-momentum) | N |
| 6 | $J = \int F \, dt = m \Delta v$ | Impulse-momentum theorem | N·s |
| 7 | $E_{abs} = \int F_{crush} \, dx$ | Energy absorbed by crushing deformation | J |
| 8 | $SEA = \frac{E_{abs}}{m_{structure}}$ | Specific Energy Absorption (crush efficiency) | J/kg |
| 9 | $C_{index} = \frac{\bar{a}_{max}}{g}$ | Crash severity index (in g's) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{stop} = \frac{v_{impact}}{\bar{a}}$ | Stopping time | s |
| 11 | $s_{crush} = \frac{v_{impact}^2}{2\bar{a}}$ | Crush distance (work-energy equivalence) | m |
| 12 | $\sigma_{dyn} = K_{dyn} \cdot \sigma_{static}$ | Dynamic stress amplification factor | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by UAV manufacturers (DJI, Parrot, Skydio), aerospace crash investigators (NTSB, AAIB equivalents), insurance assessors, and military UAV program offices to certify airworthiness under standards like **MIL-STD-810** (environmental testing including crash/shock) and **ASTM F3196** (UAV operational safety).

## 2.4 Impact Velocity Estimation (Free-Fall with Drag)

When a UAV loses power at altitude $h$ and enters uncontrolled descent, the terminal velocity is reached when drag equals weight:

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2mg}{\rho S C_D}}$$

The actual impact velocity $v_{impact}$ depends on whether $h$ is sufficient to reach terminal velocity. If $h \ll h_{terminal}$, then $v_{impact} \approx \sqrt{2gh}$ (free-fall approximation).

## 2.5 Energy Partition During Impact

At the moment of impact, the total kinetic energy must be partitioned:

$$E_{impact} = E_{deformation} + E_{fracture} + E_{friction} + E_{acoustic} + E_{rebound}$$

For a well-designed crashworthy UAV, $E_{deformation}$ should dominate (ideally $> 70\%$), indicating controlled energy absorption rather than catastrophic fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Impact Velocity from a Power Loss Event

**Problem Statement:** A quadcopter of mass $m = 2.5 \text{ kg}$ loses propulsion at altitude $h = 50 \text{ m}$. Assuming near free-fall conditions (low drag coefficient in uncontrolled tumble), determine the impact velocity.

**Step 1: State the governing energy conservation equation.**

Using conservation of mechanical energy from the moment of power loss to the moment of impact (assuming minimal aerodynamic drag for a tumbling airframe in the initial phase):

$$E_p = E_k$$

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv_{impact}^2$$

**Step 2: Cancel mass from both sides.**

Since $m \neq 0$, we divide both sides by $m$:

$$gh = \frac{1}{2}v_{impact}^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for $v_{impact}$.**

$$v_{impact}^2 = 2gh$$

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 50}$$

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{981}$$

$$\boxed{v_{impact} \approx 31.32 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the kinetic energy at impact.**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (31.32)^2$$

$$E_k = 1.25 \times 980.94$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 1226.2 \text{ J}}$$

This is the energy that must be dissipated upon impact — roughly equivalent to the kinetic energy of a 2.5 kg object dropped from a 5-story building.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Crash Severity Index & Average Deceleration

**Problem Statement:** For the quadcopter above, suppose the crashworthy landing gear compresses over a crush distance of $s_{crush} = 0.15 \text{ m}$. Determine the average deceleration experienced by the airframe and the equivalent g-force.

**Step 1: Apply the work-energy theorem.**

The kinetic energy at impact is fully absorbed by the crushing structure:

$$\frac{1}{2}mv_{impact}^2 = F_{avg} \cdot s_{crush}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the average impact force.**

$$F_{avg} = \frac{mv_{impact}^2}{2 \cdot s_{crush}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$F_{avg} = \frac{2.5 \times (31.32)^2}{2 \times 0.15}$$

$$F_{avg} = \frac{2.5 \times 980.94}{0.30}$$

$$F_{avg} = \frac{2452.35}{0.30}$$

$$\boxed{F_{avg} \approx 8174.5 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate average deceleration using Newton's second law.**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m} = \frac{8174.5}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{a} \approx 3269.8 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 5: Convert to g-force (crash severity index).**

$$C_{index} = \frac{\bar{a}}{g} = \frac{3269.8}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{C_{index} \approx 333.3 \, g}$$

**Step 6: Determine stopping time.**

$$t_{stop} = \frac{v_{impact}}{\bar{a}} = \frac{31.32}{3269.8}$$

$$\boxed{t_{stop} \approx 0.00958 \text{ s} \approx 9.58 \text{ ms}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 333 g deceleration lasting ~10 ms is survivable for electronic components rated to MIL-STD-810 (typically 40–100 g for operational shock, up to 300+ g for crash shock), but it explains why consumer-grade payloads (GoPro cameras, LiDAR sensors) often fail in such crashes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Terminal Velocity for a Tumbling UAV

**Problem Statement:** A fixed-wing UAV with mass $m = 8 \text{ kg}$, frontal area $S = 0.35 \text{ m}^2$, and drag coefficient $C_D = 1.2$ (tumbling configuration, $C_D$ is significantly higher than cruise) enters an uncontrolled descent. Calculate the terminal velocity.

**Step 1: Set up the force balance at terminal velocity.**

At terminal velocity, gravitational force equals aerodynamic drag:

$$mg = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{terminal}^2 S C_D$$

**Step 2: Solve for $v_{terminal}$.**

$$v_{terminal}^2 = \frac{2mg}{\rho S C_D}$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2mg}{\rho S C_D}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 0.35 \times 1.2}}$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{156.96}{0.5145}}$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{305.07}$$

$$\boxed{v_{terminal} \approx 17.47 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Determine the minimum altitude to reach terminal velocity.**

Using kinematic equations for the approach to terminal velocity:

$$h_{terminal} \approx \frac{v_{terminal}^2}{2g} = \frac{(17.47)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$\boxed{h_{terminal} \approx 15.55 \text{ m}}$$

**Interpretation:** If the UAV loses power above ~15.5 m, it will reach terminal velocity and impact at ~17.47 m/s regardless of additional altitude. This is a critical parameter for parachute deployment system design — the parachute must deploy and inflate before this terminal velocity is reached.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Crash Scenarios

| Scenario | $m$ (kg) | $v_{impact}$ (m/s) | $E_k$ (J) | $F_{avg}$ (N) | $C_{index}$ (g) | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quadcopter, 5 m drop | 2.5 | 9.90 | 122.5 | 817 | 33.3 | Low |
| Quadcopter, 50 m drop | 2.5 | 31.32 | 1226.2 | 8175 | 333.3 | Severe |
| Fixed-wing, powered crash | 8.0 | 45.00 | 8100.0 | 54000 | 687.1 | Catastrophic |
| Delivery drone, 20 m | 5.0 | 19.81 | 980.5 | 6537 | 133.3 | Moderate-Severe |
| Nano-drone, 3 m | 0.05 | 7.67 | 1.47 | 9.8 | 20.0 | Negligible |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Impulse-Momentum for Frangibility Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A UAV's wingtip strikes a rigid pole at $v = 20 \text{ m/s}$. The wingtip mass is $m_{tip} = 0.3 \text{ kg}$. If the impact duration is $\Delta t = 5 \text{ ms}$ and the wingtip is designed to detach (frangible design), determine the average force.

**Step 1: Apply the impulse-momentum theorem.**

$$J = F_{avg} \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$$

**Step 2: Determine change in velocity.**

Assuming the wingtip comes to a complete stop relative to the pole:

$$\Delta v = v_{final} - v_{initial} = 0 - 20 = -20 \text{ m/s}$$

Taking magnitudes:

$$\Delta v = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Solve for average force.**

$$F_{avg} = \frac{m \cdot \Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.3 \times 20}{0.005}$$

$$\boxed{F_{avg} = 1200 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate force per unit mass of wingtip structure.**

$$a_{wingtip} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m_{tip}} = \frac{1200}{0.3} = 4000 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a_{wingtip} \approx 407.7 \, g}$$

**Interpretation:** Frangible (breakaway) design intentionally allows structural separation at a predictable load threshold, protecting the central fuselage and payload from the full impact force. This is a fundamental principle in **blunt impact tolerance** and **bird-strike certification** for larger UAVs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Crash Analysis in the context of Aerial Robotics. List any four categories of UAV crashes.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Crash Analysis in Aerial Robotics is the systematic engineering study of the dynamic, structural, energetic, and operational consequences of an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) experiencing an unintended loss of controlled flight leading to impact. It encompasses the evaluation of impact forces, energy dissipation mechanisms, structural failure modes, and root-cause determination.

**Four Categories of UAV Crashes:**

1. **Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT)** — UAV impacts terrain/obstacle due to navigational error
2. **Loss of Link (LOL)** — Communication breakdown leading to uncontrolled descent
3. **Propulsion Failure** — Motor, ESC, or battery failure causing power loss
4. **Structural Failure** — Mid-flight fracture of airframe components

---

### Question 2: What is the Crash Severity Index? Explain its significance with a formula.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Crash Severity Index (CSI) is a dimensionless metric that quantifies the intensity of deceleration experienced by a UAV during impact, expressed in multiples of the standard gravitational acceleration $g$.

**Formula:**

$$C_{index} = \frac{\bar{a}_{max}}{g}$$

where $\bar{a}_{max}$ is the maximum average deceleration during the impact event (in m/s²) and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Significance:**
- It provides a normalized measure for comparing crashes across different UAV mass classes
- It is used to certify electronic components against shock survival ratings (e.g., MIL-STD-810G Method 516.7)
- It helps engineers select appropriate shock-mounting systems for sensitive payloads (cameras, LiDAR, IMUs)
- A CSI above ~100 g typically indicates a severe crash requiring complete airframe replacement

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks):

#### (a) Derive an expression for the impact velocity of a UAV undergoing free-fall from altitude $h$ after a complete propulsion failure. A quadcopter of mass $3 \text{ kg}$ loses all motor power at an altitude of $40 \text{ m}$. Calculate: (i) the impact velocity, and (ii) the kinetic energy at the moment of impact. (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Derivation**

Starting from conservation of mechanical energy (assuming negligible drag in the initial free-fall phase):

$$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv_{impact}^2$$

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 2: Numerical Calculation (i) — Impact Velocity**

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 40}$$

$$v_{impact} = \sqrt{784.8}$$

$$\boxed{v_{impact} \approx 28.01 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Calculation (ii) — Kinetic Energy**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times (28.01)^2$$

$$E_k = 1.5 \times 784.56$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 1176.84 \text{ J}}$$

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) in crashworthy UAV structures. A composite landing strut absorbs $850 \text{ J}$ of impact energy while undergoing $0.08 \text{ m}$ of crush displacement. The strut has a mass of $0.45 \text{ kg}$. Determine: (i) the average crushing force, and (ii) the Specific Energy Absorption. (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation:**

Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) is the energy absorbed per unit mass of the crushing structure. It is a key figure of merit for crashworthy materials and designs — a higher SEA means the structure can absorb more energy per gram, making it ideal for lightweight UAV applications.

$$SEA = \frac{E_{abs}}{m_{structure}}$$

**Step 1: Calculate Average Crushing Force (i)**

Using work-energy equivalence:

$$E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot s_{crush}$$

$$F_{avg} = \frac{E_{abs}}{s_{crush}} = \frac{850}{0.08}$$

$$\boxed{F_{avg} = 10625 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Specific Energy Absorption (ii)**

$$SEA = \frac{E_{abs}}{m_{structure}} = \frac{850}{0.45}$$

$$\boxed{SEA \approx 1888.9 \text{ J/kg}}$$

**Interpretation:** This SEA value is characteristic of moderately efficient composite crush structures (carbon fiber honeycomb can achieve 5,000–15,000 J/kg, while aluminum honeycomb can reach 20,000+ J/kg).

---

### Question B (14 Marks):

#### (a) With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the post-crash forensic analysis workflow for a UAV. Discuss the role of the Flight Data Recorder (FDR) in root cause identification. (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Flight Data Recorder] → [Telemetry Decoder]
[IMU Sensor Logs]    → [Telemetry Decoder]
[GPS Track Log]      → [Trajectory Reconstructor]
[Camera Footage]     → [Impact Angle Estimator]
                            ↓
              [Integrated Timeline Reconstruction]
                            ↓
                  [Root Cause Classification]
                            ↓
        [Design / Software / Operational Recommendations]
```

**Workflow Explanation:**

1. **Evidence Collection:** Wreckage is recovered from the crash site. Physical damage patterns are photographed and mapped. Electronic modules (flight controllers, ESCs) are recovered.

2. **Data Extraction:** The Flight Data Recorder (FDR) or flight controller flash memory is decoded. Telemetry logs are parsed using manufacturer-specific protocols (e.g., MAVLink for ArduPilot/PX4).

3. **Trajectory Reconstruction:** GPS coordinates, IMU readings, and attitude data from the final seconds of flight are plotted to reconstruct the 3D flight path.

4. **Impact Parameter Estimation:** The last-recorded accelerometer spike indicates impact direction and magnitude. Gyroscope data reveals any spin or tumble.

5. **Timeline Correlation:** Crash investigators correlate sensor timestamps with any ground-based observations, video footage, or witness reports.

**Role of FDR:**

The Flight Data Recorder serves as the **objective eyewitness** to the crash. Unlike human testimony, the FDR provides:
- **Quantitative data:** Exact speeds, altitudes, battery voltages, motor RPMs at millisecond resolution
- **Pre-crash anomalies:** Gradual battery voltage sag, GPS signal degradation, or sensor drift in the minutes/hours before impact
- **Impact signature:** The characteristic acceleration spike allows reconstruction of impact angle and severity
- **Causation chain:** By replaying the data, investigators can distinguish between *trigger events* (e.g., battery depletion), *contributing factors* (e.g., wind gust), and *proximal causes* (e.g., motor RPM drop to zero)

---

#### (b) A delivery drone of mass $5 \text{ kg}$ experiences a mid-air structural failure at $60 \text{ m}$ altitude. The estimated drag coefficient during the ensuing tumble is $C_D = 0.9$, and the equivalent flat-plate frontal area is $S = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$. Determine: (i) the terminal velocity of the tumbling drone, and (ii) whether the drone reaches terminal velocity before impact. Assume standard sea-level air density. (7 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formula Setup**

At terminal velocity, weight equals drag:

$$mg = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{terminal}^2 S C_D$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2mg}{\rho S C_D}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Terminal Velocity (i)**

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 0.25 \times 0.9}}$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{98.1}{0.2756}}$$

$$v_{terminal} = \sqrt{355.95}$$

$$\boxed{v_{terminal} \approx 18.87 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Determine Minimum Altitude for Terminal Velocity (ii)**

The altitude required to reach 95% of terminal velocity (engineering approximation) is approximately:

$$h_{95\%} \approx \frac{2 \cdot v_{terminal}}{g} \cdot v_{terminal} \approx \frac{2 v_{terminal}^2}{g}$$

$$h_{95\%} \approx \frac{2 \times (18.87)^2}{9.81}$$

$$h_{95\%} \approx \frac{712.15}{9.81}$$

$$h_{95\%} \approx 72.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Conclusion**

Since the actual altitude ($60 \text{ m}$) is less than the altitude required to reach terminal velocity ($72.6 \text{ m}$):

$$\boxed{60 \text{ m} < 72.6 \text{ m}}$$

**Conclusion:** The drone **does NOT reach terminal velocity** before impact. Therefore, the actual impact velocity will be lower than $v_{terminal}$ and must be calculated using the free-fall approximation:

$$v_{impact} \approx \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 60} \approx 34.31 \text{ m/s}$$

**Engineering Significance:** This result dictates that a parachute recovery system must be designed to deploy and fully inflate within the available altitude budget, otherwise it provides no benefit — a critical insight for emergency parachute system (EPS) certification per ASTM F3322.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by mixing units (e.g., km/h instead of m/s). Always convert to SI before substituting.
> 2. **Sign errors in impulse:** The impulse-momentum theorem requires careful sign handling. $\Delta v$ is the change in velocity, not the initial velocity alone.
> 3. **Free-fall vs. terminal velocity confusion:** Students often apply $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ even at high altitudes where drag is significant. Always check if $h > h_{terminal}$ before using the free-fall approximation.
> 4. **Crash Severity Index units:** The CSI must be expressed as a dimensionless ratio (in g's), not in m/s². Converting incorrectly is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Energy partition omission:** In crashworthiness questions, students often calculate impact energy but forget to discuss how it is partitioned among deformation, fracture, friction, etc.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crash Analysis**

- ✅ **Crash Analysis** = systematic study of UAV impact events: cause, mechanism, consequence
- ✅ **Four Phases:** Pre-crash flight → Failure/descent → Impact initiation → Energy dissipation
- ✅ **Core Energy Equation:** $E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgh$
- ✅ **Free-fall impact velocity:** $v_{impact} = \sqrt{2gh}$ (valid only when $h \ll h_{terminal}$)
- ✅ **Terminal velocity:** $v_{terminal} = \sqrt{\frac{2mg}{\rho S C_D}}$
- ✅ **Average impact force:** $F_{avg} = \frac{mv_{impact}^2}{2 s_{crush}}$
- ✅ **Crash Severity Index:** $C_{index} = \frac{\bar{a}_{max}}{g}$ (dimensionless, in g's)
- ✅ **Impulse-Momentum:** $F \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$
- ✅ **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA):** $SEA = \frac{E_{abs}}{m_{structure}}$ (J/kg)
- ✅ **Crashworthiness Hierarchy:** Prevention → Mitigation → Absorption → Protection → Recovery
- ✅ **Energy Partition Rule:** $E_{impact} = E_{deformation} + E_{fracture} + E_{friction} + E_{acoustic} + E_{rebound}$
- ✅ **Frangible design** intentionally allows controlled structural separation to protect critical components
- ✅ **Flight Data Recorder (FDR)** is the primary tool for post-crash forensic reconstruction
- ✅ **Standard testing protocols:** MIL-STD-810G (shock/vibration), ASTM F3322 (UAV parachute systems), ASTM F3196 (UAV operational safety)
- ✅ **Typical survivable CSI for electronics:** 40–100 g operational, 300+ g crash-shock rated
- ✅ **Key insight:** A well-designed crashworthy UAV should have $> 70\%$ of impact energy dissipated through controlled plastic deformation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Common Issues** in aerial robotics refer to the recurring set of operational, sensory, environmental, computational, and control-related challenges that degrade the performance, reliability, and autonomy of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). These issues manifest across all flight phases — take-off, hover, navigation, mission execution, and landing — and arise from imperfect hardware, uncertain environments, model inaccuracies, and integration limitations.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, this topic is classified under the broader umbrella of **State Estimation, Sensor Fusion, and Fault-Tolerant Operation**, where students must understand the *root cause*, *mathematical modelling*, and *mitigation strategies* for each dominant failure mode.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Analogy — "Driving a Car in Fog"**
> Imagine driving a car in dense fog. Your eyes cannot see far, your GPS signal bounces off buildings (multipath error), the steering feels slightly delayed, and a sudden gust of wind pushes you sideways. Aerial robotics faces a *simultaneous combination* of all these problems: poor or noisy sensors, wind gusts, signal loss, delayed control, and limited onboard "thinking" power. Just as a driver compensates by reducing speed and relying on instincts, a UAV must compensate using **sensor fusion, robust controllers, and fault detection algorithms**.

### 1.3 Classification of Common Issues
The common issues are broadly grouped into **six canonical categories** used in KTU board examinations:

| Category | Representative Issue | Typical Onset |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Sensory** | IMU bias, GPS dropout | Always present |
| **Environmental** | Wind gusts, turbulence | Outdoor flight |
| **Power** | Battery drain, voltage sag | Mid-mission |
| **Computational** | Latency, saturation | High-rate tasks |
| **Communication** | Link loss, latency | Beyond visual range |
| **Mechanical** | Vibration, actuator wear | Long endurance flights |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"List any four common issues in aerial robotic systems and briefly explain their impact on UAV performance."* This is a **3-mark definition-level question** under **CO3 (Apply fault-tolerant design principles)**.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
Key benchmark parameters students must memorise:

- **Earth's Gravitational Acceleration:** $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard Sea-Level Air Density:** $\rho \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Typical IMU Sampling Rate:** $\mathbf{100 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 1 \text{ kHz}}$
- **GPS Update Rate:** $\mathbf{1 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 10 \text{ Hz}}$ (Standard) / **20 Hz** (RTK)
- **Acceptable Hover Position Drift:** $< \mathbf{0.5 \text{ m}}$ (indoor), $< \mathbf{2 \text{ m}}$ (outdoor GPS)
- **Wind Resistance Threshold (Multirotor):** $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 12 \text{ m/s}}$ (Level 5 Beaufort)

> [!TIP] **Examiner Tip:** Always state the **units** explicitly. Writing "9.81" without "m/s²" costs a half-mark in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Common Issues

The "common issues" topic must be approached as a *cause → effect → mitigation* chain. Below is the structured analysis aligned with KTU's **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Understand → Apply → Analyse)**.

---

#### **ISSUE 1: Sensor Noise and Bias (IMU Drift)**
- **What:** MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers exhibit *bias instability* and *white noise*.
- **Why it happens:** Manufacturing imperfections, temperature variation, and high-frequency vibrations.
- **How it manifests:** Position estimate drifts by several meters per minute if uncorrected.
- **Mathematical model:** A noisy accelerometer reading is modelled as
  $$a_{measured} = a_{true} + b_a + n_a$$
  where $b_a$ is the bias and $n_a \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_a^2)$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise.

---

#### **ISSUE 2: GPS Signal Loss and Multipath Error**
- **What:** GPS becomes unavailable indoors, near tall buildings, or under heavy foliage.
- **Why it happens:** Line-of-sight (LoS) to satellites is obstructed; signals reflect off surfaces.
- **How it manifests:** Position estimate jumps discontinuously (multipath) or freezes (denial).
- **Mitigation:** Use **Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO)** or **LiDAR-based SLAM** as fallback.

---

#### **ISSUE 3: Wind Disturbances and Aerodynamic Effects**
- **What:** External wind forces the UAV off its planned trajectory.
- **Why it happens:** Atmospheric turbulence, ground effect, body-induced flow.
- **How it manifests:** Oscillations, position overshoot, increased battery consumption.
- **Model:** A constant wind $W$ modifies the velocity equation as
  $$v_{air} = v_{ground} + W$$

---

#### **ISSUE 4: Battery Depletion and Voltage Sag**
- **What:** As State of Charge (SoC) drops, the available thrust reduces.
- **Why it happens:** Chemical energy conversion is non-linear; voltage drops under load.
- **How it manifests:** Reduced maximum thrust → inability to hover → crash.
- **Critical relation:**
  $$m \cdot g \leq 4 \cdot T_{max}(SoC)$$

---

#### **ISSUE 5: Computational Latency and Onboard Limitations**
- **What:** Onboard processors (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano) have limited throughput.
- **Why it happens:** SLAM, path planning, and neural-network inference are computationally heavy.
- **How it manifests:** Control loop runs below the required $\mathbf{100 \text{ Hz}}$, leading to instability.

---

#### **ISSUE 6: Communication Link Loss and Latency**
- **What:** Telemetry/command link drops or experiences high latency.
- **Why it happens:** Range limits, interference, antenna misalignment.
- **How it manifests:** UAV executes last-known command or enters failsafe (RTL — Return to Launch).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory Equations to Memorise**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|----------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $a_{measured} = a_{true} + b_a + n_a$ | $b_a$: bias, $n_a$: noise | m/s² | IMU error model |
| 2 | $v_{air} = v_{ground} + W$ | $W$: wind vector | m/s | Aerodynamic disturbance |
| 3 | $T_{hover} = \frac{m \cdot g}{n_{motors}}$ | Hover thrust per motor | N | Thrust allocation |
| 4 | $P_{hover} = T_{hover} \cdot v_{i}$ | $v_i$: induced velocity | W | Power estimation |
| 5 | $v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}$ | $\rho$: air density, $A$: rotor area | m/s | Momentum theory |
| 6 | $\sigma_{pos} = \sigma_{GPS} \oplus \sigma_{IMU}$ | Sensor noise composition | m | Error propagation |
| 7 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | $\tau$: time constant, $R,C$: RC values | s | Control loop analysis |
| 8 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{signal}$ | Sampling theorem | Hz | Anti-aliasing |
| 9 | $E_{batt} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Battery energy | J | Endurance estimation |
| 10 | $m \cdot \ddot{x} = T - m g - D(v)$ | Longitudinal dynamics | N | Disturbance rejection |

> [!NOTE] **Note on Notation:** The symbol $\oplus$ in row 6 represents *sensor noise composition* (RSS — Root Sum of Squares) for uncorrelated sources, computed as $\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sigma_{GPS}^2 + \sigma_{IMU}^2}$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
These common issues dictate the **design of every commercial UAV**:
- **DJI Matrice series** uses RTK-GPS + Visual SLAM fusion to combat GPS loss in urban canyons.
- **Skydio 2+** relies on vision-only navigation to operate in **GPS-denied environments**.
- **Military UAVs (e.g., MQ-9 Reaper)** use *redundant IMUs* and *fault-tolerant flight control computers* to survive sensor failures.
- **Agricultural drones** compensate for wind using *adaptive feed-forward controllers*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: GPS + IMU Sensor Fusion Variance (Error Propagation)

**Problem Statement:**
A multirotor UAV uses a GPS receiver with horizontal position standard deviation $\sigma_{GPS} = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and an IMU-derived position estimate (after integration) with $\sigma_{IMU} = 2.5 \text{ m}$. Assuming the two sources are *independent and uncorrelated*, derive the **fused position uncertainty** $\sigma_{fused}$.

---

**Step 1 — State the Independence Assumption**

Because GPS and IMU errors arise from physically independent sources (satellite signal geometry vs. inertial integration drift), the cross-correlation term vanishes:
$$E[n_{GPS} \cdot n_{IMU}] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Write the Linear Combination Model**

The fused estimator is a weighted linear combination:
$$\hat{x}_{fused} = w_1 \hat{x}_{GPS} + w_2 \hat{x}_{IMU}$$
where $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ for an unbiased estimator.

**Step 3 — Compute the Variance of the Fused Estimate**

The variance of a linear combination with independent errors is:
$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_{GPS}^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_{IMU}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Optimal Weight (Minimum Variance Criterion)**

To minimise $\sigma_{fused}^2$, take the derivative with respect to $w_1$ and set to zero. Using Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ for the constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_1}\left[w_1^2 \sigma_{GPS}^2 + (1 - w_1)^2 \sigma_{IMU}^2 + \lambda (w_1 + w_2 - 1)\right] = 0$$

This gives:
$$2 w_1 \sigma_{GPS}^2 - 2(1 - w_1)\sigma_{IMU}^2 = 0$$

Solving:
$$w_1 \sigma_{GPS}^2 = (1 - w_1)\sigma_{IMU}^2$$

$$w_1 = \frac{\sigma_{IMU}^2}{\sigma_{GPS}^2 + \sigma_{IMU}^2}$$

Substituting values:
$$w_1 = \frac{(2.5)^2}{(1.5)^2 + (2.5)^2} = \frac{6.25}{2.25 + 6.25} = \frac{6.25}{8.50} \approx 0.735$$

And:
$$w_2 = 1 - 0.735 = 0.265$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Minimum Fused Variance**

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = (0.735)^2 (1.5)^2 + (0.265)^2 (2.5)^2$$

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = 0.540 \cdot 2.25 + 0.0702 \cdot 6.25$$

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = 1.215 + 0.439 = 1.654 \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_{fused} = \sqrt{1.654} \approx 1.286 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

> The fused estimate (1.286 m) is better than *both* individual sources (1.5 m and 2.5 m). This is the **fundamental value of sensor fusion** in aerial robotics — it reduces uncertainty below the noise floor of any single sensor.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Issue Severity vs. Frequency

| Issue | Severity (1-5) | Frequency (1-5) | Detectability (1-5) | Mitigation Cost (1-5) |
|-------|:-------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| IMU bias drift | 4 | 5 | 4 | 2 |
| GPS dropout | 5 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Wind gusts | 4 | 5 | 3 | 1 |
| Battery depletion | 5 | 5 | 5 | 1 |
| Computational lag | 3 | 3 | 2 | 4 |
| Link loss | 4 | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| Vibration | 3 | 4 | 2 | 2 |
| Actuator saturation | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |

> [!NOTE] **Scoring Key:** 1 = Low, 5 = High. This matrix is a common **14-mark analysis question** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Common Issues and Mitigation Pipeline

The diagram below maps how a typical aerial robot **detects → diagnoses → mitigates** common issues in real time. This block-level functional architecture is a safer alternative to physical drawings and is frequently used in KTU board answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UAV Mission Start] --> B[Sensor Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[IMU + GPS + Baro + Vision]
    C --> D{Data Validity Check}
    D -- Valid --> E[Extended Kalman Filter Fusion]
    D -- Invalid --> F[Fault Detection Module]
    F --> G{Fault Type Identification}
    G -- GPS Loss --> H[Switch to Visual Odometry]
    G -- IMU Drift --> I[Trigger Bias Re-estimation]
    G -- Wind Disturbance --> J[Activate Feed-forward Compensator]
    G -- Battery Low --> K[Engage Return to Launch]
    E --> L[State Estimate x, v, q]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M[Controller PD or PID]
    M --> N[Motor Command Allocation]
    N --> O[Actuator Output]
    O --> P[UAV Motion Update]
    P --> B
    J --> M
    K --> Q[Safe Landing Sequence]
```

**How to read this diagram (for KTU answer sheets):**
- The **outer loop** (B → C → D → E → L → M → N → O → P → B) is the **nominal control loop** running at **≥ 100 Hz**.
- The **inner branches** (D → F → G → H/I/J/K) form the **fault-handling layer** that runs in parallel.
- This is essentially a **hierarchical fault-tolerant control architecture**, a key concept tested in KTU Module 3.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Common Issues in Aerial Robotics] --> S1[Sensory Issues]
    Root --> S2[Environmental Issues]
    Root --> S3[System-Level Issues]
    S1 --> S1a[IMU Bias Drift]
    S1 --> S1b[GPS Multipath or Dropout]
    S1 --> S1c[Camera Motion Blur]
    S1 --> S1d[Magnetometer Interference]
    S2 --> S2a[Wind Gust Disturbance]
    S2 --> S2b[Temperature Variation]
    S2 --> S2c[Humidity and Rain]
    S3 --> S3a[Battery Depletion]
    S3 --> S3b[Computational Latency]
    S3 --> S3c[Communication Link Loss]
    S3 --> S3d[Actuator Saturation]
    S3 --> S3e[Mechanical Vibration]
```

**Reading guide:** The tree has 3 levels. Level 1 is the broad classification; Level 2 is the category; Level 3 is the specific issue. When answering KTU questions, always list at least **one issue from each of the 3 main branches** to demonstrate complete coverage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four common issues encountered in aerial robotic systems and state their impact on UAV performance.*

**Model Answer (4 issues, 3 marks):**

1. **IMU bias drift** — Causes position estimate to diverge over time, especially in GPS-denied environments.
2. **GPS signal loss / multipath** — Leads to sudden position jumps or complete loss of localisation, threatening flight safety.
3. **Wind disturbances** — Force the UAV off its planned trajectory, increasing control effort and battery consumption.
4. **Battery depletion / voltage sag** — Reduces maximum available thrust, potentially causing inability to maintain hover and leading to crash.

*(Examiner allocates ~0.75 mark per issue. A clear "issue → impact" structure fetches full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is GPS multipath error? How does it affect aerial robot navigation?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** GPS multipath error occurs when the satellite signal reaches the receiver via **two or more paths** — one direct (line-of-sight) and others reflected off buildings, ground, or water surfaces.
- **Effect on Navigation:** The reflected signal travels a longer path, causing a **pseudorange error** of several metres. This manifests as **position jumps**, **incorrect heading estimates**, and **localisation failure** in urban canyon environments.
- **Mitigation (1-line, bonus):** Use **dual-frequency GPS**, **antenna choke rings**, or **vision-based fallback**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by writing only the *symptom* of an issue (e.g., "the drone drifts") without explaining the **root cause** (e.g., "IMU integration drift due to bias instability").
> - Always pair the issue with a **mitigation strategy**. A question asking "common issues" *implicitly* requires solutions.
> - For 14-mark questions, ensure **at least 4 sub-points** per sub-part, with one **diagram or table**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a fault-tolerant aerial robotic system, highlighting the role of sensor fusion, fault detection, and failsafe mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UAV uses GPS (σ = 1.2 m) and a vision-based estimator (σ = 0.8 m) for horizontal position. Assuming uncorrelated errors, derive the **optimal fused uncertainty** and the **weights** to be assigned to each sensor. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A fault-tolerant aerial robotic system consists of three primary layers:

1. **Perception Layer:** Multiple redundant sensors — IMU, GPS, barometer, monocular/stereo camera, LiDAR, magnetometer.
2. **Processing Layer:** Sensor fusion (e.g., Extended Kalman Filter) produces a unified state estimate. A **Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)** module runs in parallel to identify anomalies.
3. **Control Layer:** A robust controller (PID / LQR / MPC) generates motor commands. A **failsafe logic block** triggers actions such as *Return-to-Launch*, *Hover-in-Place*, or *Safe Landing* upon detected faults.

**Key features:**
- **Redundancy:** At least two independent sources for each critical variable (position, attitude).
- **Health monitoring:** Continuous comparison of sensor outputs; if deviation exceeds a threshold, the faulty sensor is isolated.
- **Graceful degradation:** The system continues to operate with reduced capability instead of complete failure.

*(A neat block diagram earns an additional 1–2 marks. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a template.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
$$\sigma_{GPS} = 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_{vision} = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 1:** Compute optimal weights using the minimum-variance fusion rule:
$$w_{GPS} = \frac{\sigma_{vision}^2}{\sigma_{GPS}^2 + \sigma_{vision}^2} = \frac{0.64}{1.44 + 0.64} = \frac{0.64}{2.08} \approx 0.308$$

$$w_{vision} = 1 - 0.308 = 0.692$$

**Step 2:** Compute fused variance:
$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = (0.308)^2 (1.2)^2 + (0.692)^2 (0.8)^2$$

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = 0.0949 \cdot 1.44 + 0.4789 \cdot 0.64$$

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = 0.1366 + 0.3065 = 0.4431 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{fused} = \sqrt{0.4431} \approx 0.666 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Comment — The fused uncertainty (**0.666 m**) is significantly less than the better individual sensor (0.8 m), demonstrating the **~17% improvement** achievable through optimal sensor fusion. The vision sensor receives higher weight (69.2%) due to its lower variance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Classify the common issues in aerial robotics into sensory, environmental, and system-level categories. Provide **two examples** for each category with a one-line explanation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the issue of **wind disturbance** in multirotor UAVs. Derive the modified hover thrust equation in the presence of a constant horizontal wind $W$ and compute the additional thrust required for a 1.5 kg quadrotor experiencing a $5 \text{ m/s}$ wind. Assume $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and that the wind requires a tilt angle $\theta \approx \tan^{-1}(W / v_{h})$, where $v_{h} = 3 \text{ m/s}$ is the horizontal velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Category | Issue 1 | Issue 2 |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Sensory** | IMU bias drift — slow divergence of integrated position | GPS multipath — false position jumps near buildings |
| **Environmental** | Wind gusts — sudden lateral force | Temperature drift — affects barometer and battery |
| **System-Level** | Battery depletion — reduced thrust margin | Communication latency — delayed control response |

*(Use a table for full marks; 0.5 mark per correct entry, 0.5 mark per explanation.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

In the **no-wind** case, hover thrust equals weight:
$$T_{hover} = m g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

With a constant horizontal wind $W$, the multirotor must **tilt** by an angle $\theta$ to maintain its ground-track. The tilt angle is:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{W}{v_{h}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.667) \approx 59.04°$$

The total thrust now must counteract gravity *and* provide a horizontal component:
$$T_{total} = \frac{m g}{\cos \theta} = \frac{14.715}{\cos(59.04°)} = \frac{14.715}{0.515} \approx 28.57 \text{ N}$$

**Additional thrust required:**
$$\Delta T = T_{total} - T_{hover} = 28.57 - 14.715 \approx 13.86 \text{ N}$$

**Comment:** A 5 m/s wind nearly **doubles** the thrust demand, reducing endurance by approximately **50%** and potentially exceeding the motor's continuous rating.

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistake**
> Many students forget to divide by $\cos \theta$ and use the simple $T = mg$ formula, losing 3 marks. Always explicitly state that **tilt increases thrust demand by a factor of $1/\cos \theta$**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Common Issues)**

- ✅ Common issues in aerial robotics fall into **three primary categories**: Sensory, Environmental, and System-Level.
- ✅ The **most critical sensory issue** is *IMU bias drift*; mitigated by sensor fusion with GPS/vision.
- ✅ **GPS loss** is the most frequent outdoor failure mode; mitigated by **visual-inertial odometry (VIO)**.
- ✅ **Wind disturbance** requires the UAV to tilt; thrust increases by a factor of $\dfrac{1}{\cos \theta}$.
