# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Why is a quadcopter classified as an under-actuated system? What implication does this have on its control design?**

**Model Answer:**
A quadcopter possesses **six degrees of freedom** in motion — three translational $(x, y, z)$ and three rotational $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — but has only **four independent control inputs** (the angular velocities $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ of its four rotors). Since $4 < 6$, the system is **under-actuated**.

**Implication:** The controller cannot independently command all six states. Instead, it must exploit the *dynamic coupling* between axes — for example, generating a horizontal acceleration by *tilting* the drone (a rotation command). This leads naturally to a **two-loop cascade architecture**: an outer position loop produces desired roll/pitch angles, which the inner attitude loop then tracks. The mixing matrix mathematically resolves four motor commands from three torque requests plus one thrust request.

(3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the inner-loop and outer-loop controllers in a quadcopter's cascade architecture. Why must the inner loop be faster than the outer loop?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Outer Loop (Position) | Inner Loop (Attitude) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Goal | Track $(x_d, y_d, z_d)$ | Track $(\phi_d, \theta_d, \psi_d)$ |
| Output | Desired attitude | Desired torques |
| Typical rate | **50 Hz** | **250 – 1000 Hz** |
| Sensors used | GPS, vision, optical flow | IMU (gyro + accel) |
| Plant dynamics | Slow (large inertia) | Fast (small inertia) |

**Why inner loop must be faster:** The **bandwidth separation principle** in control theory states that if the inner loop's bandwidth is at least **5–10× greater** than the outer loop's, the two loops can be tuned *independently* and the closed-loop system remains stable. Practically, attitude dynamics respond in milliseconds, whereas position changes take hundreds of milliseconds; running the inner loop at the same slow rate would cause the drone to crash before attitude corrections could stabilize it.

(3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not skip the linearization step** — students lose up to **4 marks** for going directly from nonlinear to PD gains.
> - Always state the **equilibrium point** explicitly.
> - Always write the **closed-loop characteristic equation** before reading off $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.
> - For case-study questions, mention **sensors**, **controller type**, and **application context** — not just one of them.

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the necessary derivations, obtain the linearized vertical (altitude) dynamics of a quadcopter about its hover equilibrium and design a PD altitude controller for a critically damped response with a settling time of 2 seconds. Take the mass $m = 1.5$ kg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the cascade control architecture used in modern quadcopters with a neat functional block diagram. How is the under-actuation resolved through the mixing (control allocation) matrix? Write the control allocation matrix for an X-configuration quadcopter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Nonlinear vertical equation:**

$$
\ddot{z} = \frac{T\cos\phi\cos\theta}{m} - g
$$

**Step 2 — Hover trim condition:** $\phi = \theta = 0$, $T_0 = mg$, $\dot{z} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Small-angle linearization:** $\cos\phi \approx 1$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, $\delta T = T - mg$.

**Step 4 — Linearized equation:**

$$
\ddot{z} = \frac{\delta T}{m}
$$

**Step 5 — PD controller design:**

$$
\delta T = K_p(z_d - z) + K_d(\dot{z}_d - \dot{z})
$$

**Step 6 — Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
\frac{Z(s)}{Z_d(s)} = \frac{K_p / m}{s^2 + (K_d / m) s + K_p / m}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify $\omega_n, \zeta$:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{K_d}{2\sqrt{m K_p}}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute gains:**

For $t_s = 2$ s and $\zeta = 1$:

$$
\omega_n = \frac{4.6}{2} = 2.3 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
K_p = m \omega_n^2 = 1.5 \times 5.29 = 7.94 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
K_d = 2 m \omega_n = 2 \times 1.5 \times 2.3 = 6.9 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

(7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Cascade Architecture:**

The **outer loop** is a position controller (running at ~50 Hz) that converts position errors $(x_d - x), (y_d - y), (z_d - z)$ into **desired attitude** $(\phi_d, \theta_d, \psi_d)$ and total thrust $T_d$. The **inner loop** is an attitude controller (running at ~500 Hz) that converts attitude errors into **desired body torques** $(\tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi)$. The torques are then mapped to four motor RPMs via the **mixing matrix**.

**Resolution of Under-Actuation:**

Because the drone has only four motors but six controlled states, four of those states (e.g., $z, \phi, \theta, \psi$) are *directly* commanded, while $x$ and $y$ are *indirectly* commanded through tilt. The cascade exploits this: position errors → tilt commands → torque commands → motor RPMs.

**X-Configuration Mixing Matrix:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
T \\
\tau_\phi \\
\tau_\theta \\
\tau_\psi
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
k_f & k_f & k_f & k_f \\
-k_f L & -k_f L & k_f L & k_f L \\
k_f L & -k_f L & -k_f L & k_f L \\
k_m & -k_m & k_m & -k_m
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\omega_1^2 \\
\omega_2^2 \\
\omega_3^2 \\
\omega_4^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix is **invertible** because the four motors are arranged so that the four control vectors are linearly independent — a key requirement for full controllability.

(7 marks)

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the PID control strategy for the roll-axis of a quadcopter. Derive the closed-loop transfer function and discuss how $K_p, K_i, K_d$ each affect the response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any two case studies of commercially successful quadcopter systems (e.g., DJI Phantom, Parrot AR.Drone, Crazyflie, agricultural drones, delivery drones). For each, identify the application, sensors used, and control technique employed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Roll dynamics (linearized):**

$$
I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} = \tau_\phi
$$

**PID control law:**

$$
\tau_\phi = K_{p,\phi}(\phi_d - \phi) + K_{i,\phi}\int (\phi_d - \phi) dt + K_{d,\phi}(\dot{\phi}_d - \dot{\phi})
$$

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

Let $e_\phi = \phi_d - \phi$. Substituting:

$$
I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} = K_p e_\phi + K_i \int e_\phi\, dt + K_d \dot{e}_\phi
$$

Taking Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$
I_{xx} s^2 \Phi(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E_\phi(s)
$$

The open-loop transfer function is:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K_p s^2 + K_i + K_d s^3}{I_{xx} s^3} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{I_{xx} s^3}
$$

**Role of each gain:**

| Gain | Increases → | Trade-off |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| $K_p$ | Faster rise, smaller steady-state error | Overshoot, oscillation if too high |
| $K_i$ | Removes steady-state bias (e.g., unbalanced payload) | Slow response, integrator wind-up |
| $K_d$ | More damping, reduces overshoot | Amplifies high-frequency sensor noise |

A typical tuning sequence is: (i) set $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until the drone oscillates, (ii) increase $K_d$ until oscillation damps, (iii) add small $K_i$ to remove offset.

(7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Case Study 1: DJI Phantom 4 Pro (Consumer Mapping / Photography)**

- **Application:** Aerial photography, 3-D mapping, inspection.
- **Sensors:** GPS + GLONASS, dual IMU + compass barometer, **forward + downward stereo vision**, ultrasonic.
- **Control Technique:** Onboard **cascaded PID** with vision-based **optical-flow position hold**, sensor fusion via **extended Kalman filter (EKF)**. Includes *geo-fencing* and *return-to-home*.

**Case Study 2: DJI Agras T40 (Precision Agriculture)**

- **Application:** Variable-rate spraying of pesticides/fertilizers over farmland.
- **Sensors:** **RTK-GPS** for cm-level accuracy, terrain-following radar, flow-rate sensor, multispectral imaging.
- **Control Technique:** PID with **feed-forward** compensation for varying payload mass (spray tank emptying mid-flight), terrain-following altitude controller using radar altimeter, and **route planning** over pre-surveyed orchard maps.

*(Alternative valid case studies: Parrot AR.Drone — smartphone-controlled, ultrasound + camera; Crazyflie 2.1 — research-grade, LQR, motion-capture; Amazon Prime Air — MPC + sense-and-avoid; Skydio 2+ — vision-based SLAM.)*

(7 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 10 minutes before the exam):**

- A quadcopter has **6 DOF** but only **4 control inputs** → *under-actuated*.
- The plant transfer function for altitude and roll (linearized) is a **double integrator**: $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{m s^2}$ and $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{I_{xx} s^2}$ respectively.
- **Closed-loop form (PD)** is universally $\dfrac{K_p / I}{s^2 + (K_d / I) s + K_p / I}$.
- **Cascade architecture:** Outer position loop (~50 Hz) → Inner attitude loop (~500 Hz) → Mixing matrix → Motors.
- The **mixing matrix** for an X-config quadcopter is a $4 \times 4$ matrix mapping $(T, \tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi)$ to $(\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2, \omega_4^2)$.
- **PID tuning mnemonic:** *Proportional = Present, Integral = Past, Derivative = Future.*
- **Bandwidth separation rule:** inner-loop frequency $\geq 5\times$ outer-loop frequency.
- **Motor dynamics** introduce a first-order lag with time constant ~**20 ms**; the high-frequency inner loop must respect this.
- **Common case studies:** DJI Phantom (PID + vision), Parrot AR.Drone (PID + ultrasound), Crazyflie (LQR, research), Agras (PID + feed-forward + RTK), Prime Air (MPC + multi-sensor fusion).
- **Exam-safe constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², settling-time formula $t_s \approx 4.6 / (\zeta \omega_n)$.
- **Never confuse** thrust coefficient $k_f$ (N·s²/rad²) with moment coefficient $k_m$ (N·m·s²/rad²) — they differ by ~**two orders of magnitude**.
- Always declare the **hover trim point** before linearizing — this is where most students lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flight Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Flight Performance Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Flight Performance Analysis** is the rigorous aerospace engineering discipline that quantifies the operational capabilities of an aerial robotic platform (fixed-wing UAV, multirotor, or hybrid VTOL) under steady-state and quasi-steady flight conditions. It involves the derivation and application of aerodynamic, propulsion, and energy-based equations to predict the vehicle's **maximum range, endurance, ceiling, climb rate, glide ratio, and payload capacity** as functions of weight, altitude, configuration, and atmospheric properties. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, performance analysis is mapped directly to **Course Outcomes (CO4)** of PERAT745 — *"Analyse the flight performance characteristics of aerial robotic systems using first-principles aerodynamics."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Cyclist on a Flat Road** > > Imagine you are cycling on a flat road. To maintain a steady speed, your pedaling force must exactly balance air drag + rolling resistance. If you pedal harder than required, you accelerate; if less, you decelerate. **Flight performance analysis is the "engineering version" of this balance** — except now we have four forces (Lift, Weight, Drag, Thrust), and we ask: "At what speed is the *minimum pedaling power* needed?" or "What is the *steepest hill* this bike can climb at 15 km/h?" > > For a **drone**, replace the cyclist's legs with **battery-fed electric motors and propellers**; for a **fixed-wing UAV**, replace it with a **piston engine or brushless motor driving a propeller**. The math is identical — only the propulsion source changes. ### 1.3 Core Quantities and Standard Metrics The following symbols form the **canonical vocabulary** of flight performance for the KTU 2024 syllabus: | Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | |--------|----------|-----------------| | $W$ | Weight | **N** (newton) | | $T$ | Thrust | **N** | | $D$ | Drag force | **N** | | $L$ | Lift force | **N** | | $V$ | True airspeed | **m/s** | | $S$ | Wing reference area | **m²** | | $C_L$ | Lift coefficient | dimensionless | | $C_D$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless | | $C_{D_0}$ | Zero-lift drag coefficient | dimensionless | | $K$ | Induced drag factor | **1/rad²** | | $\rho$ | Air density | **kg/m³** (sea-level std: **1.225**) | | $q$ | Dynamic pressure $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | **Pa** | | $P$ | Power required | **W** | | $\eta_p$ | Propeller efficiency | dimensionless | | $\gamma$ | Flight-path angle | **rad** | | $R/C$ | Rate of climb | **m/s** | | $E$ | Endurance | **s** | | $R$ | Range | **m** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Pointer:** The drag polar $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ is the **single most important equation** in flight performance. Every performance metric (range, endurance, ceiling, climb) is derived from it. Master it first. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Drag Polar — Foundation of Performance For a fixed-wing UAV in the **linear (low-AoA) regime**, total drag decomposes into two physically distinct parts: $$ C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 $$ - **Parasitic drag** ($C_{D_0} \cdot q S$): Skin friction + form drag + interference. Independent of lift. - **Induced drag** ($K C_L^2 \cdot q S$): Byproduct of generating lift via wingtip vortices. Grows with $C_L^2$ and decays with speed. ### 2.2 Steady Level Flight — The Equilibrium Condition In **straight-and-level unaccelerated flight**, Newton's second law gives: $$ \sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T = D $$ $$ \sum F_z = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L = W $$ Substituting the drag polar and the lift equation $L = q S C_L = W$: $$ \boxed{\;T = q S \, C_{D_0} + \frac{K W^2}{q S}\;} $$ This is the **Thrust Required equation** — a U-shaped curve in $V$ (or $V^2$). ### 2.3 Minimum Thrust Speed ($V_{T_{\min}}$) Setting $\dfrac{dT}{dV} = 0$ yields the airspeed at which thrust required is minimum: $$ V_{T_{\min}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}} \left(\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At this speed, the **parasitic drag equals the induced drag** — a fundamental aerodynamic identity used in nearly every KTU numerical problem. ### 2.4 Minimum Power Speed ($V_{P_{\min}}$) Since $P = T \cdot V$, minimising power gives: $$ V_{P_{\min}} = \frac{V_{T_{\min}}}{3^{1/4}} \approx 0.76 \, V_{T_{\min}} $$ > [!NOTE] > The fastest *cruise* is at $V_{P_{\min}}$; the most *efficient* (longest endurance) speed for propeller UAVs is also near $V_{P_{\min}}$, not at minimum drag. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick-Reference) | # | Performance Metric | Formula | Conditions / Best Use | |---|-------------------|---------|------------------------| | 1 | Thrust required | $T_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} + \dfrac{K W^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$ | Steady level flight | | 2 | Power required | $P_R = T_R \cdot V$ | Propeller-driven level flight | | 3 | Min thrust speed | $V_{T_{\min}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{\rho S}}\,(K/C_{D_0})^{1/4}$ | Min fuel flow rate (jet) | | 4 | Min power speed | $V_{P_{\min}} = V_{T_{\min}} \cdot 3^{-1/4}$ | Min fuel flow (prop), endurance | | 5 | Best $L/D$ (max glide) | $(L/D)_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}$ at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$ | Glide, jet range | | 6 | Best glide speed | $V_{gd} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2W}{\rho S}}\,(K/C_{D_0})^{1/4}$ | Same as $V_{T_{\min}}$! | | 7 | Climb angle | $\sin\gamma = (T_A - T_R)/W$ | Climbing flight | | 8 | Rate of climb | $R/C = \dfrac{V (T_A - T_R)}{W}$ | Excess-power metric | | 9 | Absolute ceiling | $V_{climb} = 0 \Rightarrow T_A = T_R$ | Max altitude | | 10 | Service ceiling | $R/C = 100 \text{ \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$ | Ideal hover, $T = W$ | | 17 | Multirotor climb power | $P_{climb} = T \cdot V_{climb} + P_{hover}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{3 V_{climb}^2}{V_i^2}\right)$ approx | Vertical climb | > **Real-World Utility:** These equations are the **design DNA** of every commercial UAV — from the DJI Mavic's endurance specs to military UAV cruise profiles. Performance analysis drives the *trade-off triangle* of **payload ↔ range ↔ endurance** that defines the entire unmanned aerospace industry. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No skip phrases. Read line-by-line to build derivational fluency for KTU ESE. ### 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Thrust Speed **Step 1 — Start with the thrust-required equation in terms of $V$.** Using $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$, the thrust required becomes: $$ T_R = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} + \frac{K W^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} $$ **Step 2 — Rewrite with a common denominator to simplify differentiation.** $$ T_R = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0} + \frac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $T_R$ with respect to $V$ and set to zero for the extremum.** $$ \frac{dT_R}{dV} = \rho V S C_{D_0} - \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho V^3 S} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange the equality.** $$ \rho V S C_{D_0} = \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho V^3 S} $$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $\rho V^3 S$.** $$ \rho^2 V^4 S^2 C_{D_0} = 4 K W^2 $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $V^4$ and then for $V$.** $$ V^4 = \frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D_0}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;V_{T_{\min}} = \left(\frac{4 K W^2}{\rho^2 S^2 C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4}\;} $$ **Step 7 — Substitute back to confirm the parasitic = induced identity.** At $V_{T_{\min}}$, the two drag components are equal: $$ D_{parasitic} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_{D_0}, \quad D_{induced} = \tfrac{2 K W^2}{\rho V^2 S} $$ Setting them equal and inserting $V = V_{T_{\min}}$ verifies consistency — this is a **frequently asked KTU 3-mark question**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Best Glide Ratio and Best Glide Speed **Step 1 — In an unpowered glide, $T = 0$. The aircraft descends at flight-path angle $\gamma_{gd} < 0$.** Force balance along and perpendicular to the flight path gives: $$ L = W \cos\gamma_{gd} \approx W \quad (\text{small } \gamma) $$ $$ D = W \sin\gamma_{gd} \approx W \gamma_{gd} $$ **Step 2 — Form the glide ratio (range / height lost).** $$ \frac{R}{h} = \frac{1}{\tan\gamma_{gd}} = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} $$ **Step 3 — Maximise $C_L/C_D$ using the drag polar.** Let $f(C_L) = \dfrac{C_L}{C_{D_0} + K C_L^2}$. Differentiate and set to zero: $$ \frac{df}{dC_L} = \frac{(C_{D_0}+K C_L^2) - C_L (2 K C_L)}{(C_{D_0}+K C_L^2)^2} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Numerator condition.** $$ C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 - 2 K C_L^2 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{D_0} - K C_L^2 = 0 $$ $$ C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute back to get the maximum lift-to-drag ratio.** $$ \left(\frac{C_L}{C_D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}}{C_{D_0} + K (C_{D_0}/K)} = \frac{\sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}}{2 C_{D_0}} = \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{K C_{D_0}}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}}\;} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to best glide speed using $C_L = W/(qS)$.** $$ q^* = \frac{W}{S C_L^*} = \frac{W}{S}\sqrt{\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{gd} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4} $$ This is **identical** to $V_{T_{\min}}$ — a beautiful KTU-favourite exam insight. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Breguet Range Equation (Propeller UAV) **Step 1 — Range as integral of velocity over time.** $$ R = \int V \, dt $$ **Step 2 — Rate of fuel/energy consumption (electric UAV: $W$ itself decays as battery discharges).** Specific fuel/energy consumption: $c_T = \dfrac{1}{W}\dfrac{dW}{dt}$ (per second, 1/s). **Step 3 — Replace $dt = -dW / (c_T W)$.** $$ R = \int_{W_i}^{W_f} \frac{V}{c_T} \frac{dW}{W} \cdot (-1) = \frac{1}{c_T} \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \frac{V}{W} dW $$ **Step 4 — For propeller: thrust power $TV = \eta_p P_{engine}$, so $V = \eta_p P_{engine}/T$.** In level flight $T = D$: $$ V = \frac{\eta_p P_{engine}}{D} $$ **Step 5 — But $P_{engine}/W = -c_T \cdot W / W$...** instead use the standard Breguet assumption $c_P = c_T \cdot V$ (power-specific consumption) with $\eta_p$: $$ R = \frac{\eta_p}{c_T} \frac{C_L}{C_D} \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) $$ > **KTU Insight:** $C_L/C_D$ is **evaluated at the chosen cruise speed**, not at $(L/D)_{\max}$ for *range* — although for *jet* range the optimal is $(L/D)_{\max}$. For propeller UAVs (the dominant case in KTU PERAT745), the optimal $C_L$ for maximum range is *different* from best $L/D$. --- ### 3.4 Multirotor Hover Power (Momentum Theory) **Step 1 — Actuator disk model.** A rotor of area $A$ induces a uniform velocity $v_i$ through it. By conservation of mass and momentum, the thrust equals the rate of change of momentum of the air: $$ T = \dot{m} (V_{wake} - V_{\infty}) = \rho A v_i (V_{wake} - 0) = \rho A v_i^2 \quad (\text{hover}, V_\infty = 0) $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $v_i$ and compute the power imparted to the air.** $$ v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} $$ **Step 3 — Apply energy conservation: power required = thrust × induced velocity (for ideal case).** $$ \boxed{\;P_{hover,\text{ideal}} = T \cdot v_i = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Real systems multiply by a Figure-of-Merit $FM$ (typically 0.6–0.8):** $$ P_{hover,\text{actual}} = \frac{P_{hover,\text{ideal}}}{FM} $$ > This is the **fundamental performance limit** of multirotor UAVs and the basis of multirotor endurance estimation in the KTU syllabus. ## 4. In KTU 14-mark problems, identifying the correct drag polar term to manipulate is **70% of the marks**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` Define **best glide ratio** for a fixed-wing UAV. Derive the expression for the airspeed at which the maximum glide ratio is achieved. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Best Glide Ratio** is the maximum value of $L/D$, representing the *flattest* unpowered descent — i.e., the greatest horizontal distance covered per unit of altitude lost. **Derivation (sketch):** From $L/D = C_L/C_D$, maximising gives: $$ C_L^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_{D_0}}{K}} \quad\text{and}\quad \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)_{\max} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{K C_{D_0}}} $$ The corresponding best glide speed follows from $L = qSC_L = W$: $$ V_{gd} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4} $$ > ✅ *Examiner's note (1 mark reserved for final numerical form).* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` Distinguish between **$V_{T_{\min}}$** and **$V_{P_{\min}}$**. State which is preferred for (a) jet endurance, and (b) propeller-driven endurance. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Speed | Definition | Preferred For | |-------|------------|---------------| | $V_{T_{\min}}$ | Speed at which **thrust required** is minimum | **Jet endurance** (fuel ∝ thrust) | | $V_{P_{\min}}$ | Speed at which **power required** is minimum | **Propeller endurance** (fuel ∝ power) | Relation: $V_{P_{\min}} = V_{T_{\min}} / 3^{1/4} \approx 0.76\,V_{T_{\min}}$ (1 mark for the numerical relation). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4 Comprehensive]` A small electric fixed-wing surveillance UAV has the following specifications: - Weight: $W = 150$ N - Wing area: $S = 0.5$ m² - Zero-lift drag coefficient: $C_{D_0} = 0.02$ - Induced drag factor: $K = 0.05$ - Propeller efficiency: $\eta_p = 0.75$ - Air density at mission altitude: $\rho = 1.05$ kg/m³ - Battery specific energy consumption: $c_T = 0.0002$ s⁻¹ - Initial weight: $W_i = 150$ N, Final weight: $W_f = 130$ N (after mission) **Answer the following:** #### **(a)** Compute the **minimum thrust speed** $V_{T_{\min}}$ and the **minimum power speed** $V_{P_{\min}}$. Also determine the corresponding values of $C_L$, $C_D$, and $L/D$ at $V_{P_{\min}}$. (7 marks) #### **(b)** Calculate the **range** of the UAV when cruising at $V_{P_{\min}}$, and the **endurance** if the UAV loiters (cruises in level flight) at the same speed. Discuss which flight regime gives a larger value. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Question A:** ##### Part (a) — Critical Speeds and Aerodynamic Coefficients (7 marks) **Step 1 — Minimum thrust speed** (using the derived formula): $$ V_{T_{\min}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{K}{C_{D_0}}\right)^{1/4} $$ $$ V_{T_{\min}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 150}{1.05 \times 0.5}}\left(\frac{0.05}{0.02}\right)^{1/4} = \sqrt{571.43} \times (2.5)^{0.25} $$ $$ V_{T_{\min}} = 23.90 \times 1.2574 = 30.06 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Minimum power speed:** $$ V_{P_{\min}} = \frac{V_{T_{\min}}}{3^{1/4}} = \frac{30.06}{1.3161} = 22.84 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3 — Lift coefficient at $V_{P_{\min}}$** (from $L = W = qSC_L$): $$ C_L = \frac{W}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S} = \frac{2 \times 150}{1.05 \times 22.84^2 \times 0.5} = \frac{300}{272.79} = 1.100 $$ **Step 4 — Drag coefficient at $V_{P_{\min}}$:** $$ C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2 = 0.02 + 0.05 \times 1.100^2 = 0.02 + 0.0605 = 0.0805 $$ **Step 5 — Lift-to-drag ratio:** $$ \frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{1.100}{0.0805} = 13.66 $$ > *(Note: best $L/D$ would be at $C_L^* = \sqrt{0.02/0.05} = 0.6325$, giving $(L/D)_{\max} = 1/(2\sqrt{0.05 \times 0.02}) = 15.81$ — slightly higher but at much higher speed and weight-fraction cost.)* --- ##### Part (b) — Range and Endurance (7 marks) **Step 1 — Breguet range for propeller (electric) UAV at $V_{P_{\min}}$:** $$ R = \frac{\eta_p}{c_T} \frac{C_L}{C_D} \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) $$ $$ R = \frac{0.75}{0.0002} \times 13.66 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{150}{130}\right) $$ $$ R = 3750 \times 13.66 \times \ln(1.1538) = 3750 \times 13.66 \times 0.1431 $$ $$ \boxed{\;R = 7330 \text{ m} \approx 7.33 \text{ km}\;} $$ **Step 2 — Endurance (loiter) at $V_{P_{\min}}$:** Endurance at $V_{P_{\min}}$ is found from the propeller-endurance Breguet form, or equivalently from fuel-fraction logic: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_T} \left(\frac{C_L}{C_D}\right)\frac{1}{V_{P_{\min}}}\ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) \quad\text{(simplified form)} $$ Wait — let us use the **energy-based form** more rigorously. The standard loiter-endurance relation for propeller aircraft is: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_T}\frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}}\left(W_i^{-1/2}-W_f^{-1/2}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Compute $C_L^{3/2}/C_D$:** $$ C_L^{3/2} = 1.100^{1.5} = 1.153 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D} = \frac{1.153}{0.0805} = 14.32 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the weight-difference term:** $$ W_i^{-1/2} - W_f^{-1/2} = 150^{-0.5} - 130^{-0.5} = 0.08165 - 0.08771 = -0.00606 $$ Hmm — sign error. The formula uses $W_f < W_i$, so the absolute value: $$ |W_i^{-1/2} - W_f^{-1/2}| = 0.00606 $$ Wait, I need to recheck — actually the endurance form is usually written as: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_T}\frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{W_f}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{W_i}}\right) $$ Let me re-derive this for clarity. The standard propeller-endurance Breguet (from Anderson's *Introduction to Flight*) is: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P}\frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{W_f}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{W_i}}\right) $$ where $c_P$ is the **power-specific fuel consumption** (per unit of power per unit weight, 1/s). For electric UAVs, $c_P = c_T \cdot V$ in some formulations, but let me use a cleaner approach: For a propeller electric UAV, the **Breguet loiter endurance** simplifies to: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p \cdot E_{bat}}{g \cdot P_{specific}} $$ where $E_{bat}$ is battery energy. To keep this KTU-friendly, we'll use the **standard Anderson form** assuming constant $C_P$: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P}\frac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho S}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{W_f}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{W_i}}\right) $$ **Given the problem statement specifies only $c_T$**, we adapt by recognising that for many KTU problems the **range** formula is the primary deliverable, and endurance is often given by the **first-order approximation**: $$ E \approx \frac{W_f - W_i}{-c_T W_{avg}} \quad \text{or simply}\quad E = -\frac{1}{c_T}\ln(W_f/W_i) $$ Using the simpler integrated form (valid when cruise speed changes negligibly with $W$): $$ E = \frac{1}{c_T}\ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right) = \frac{1}{0.0002}\ln(1.1538) = 5000 \times 0.1431 = 715.5 \text{ s} $$ $$ \boxed{\;E \approx 715.5 \text{ s} \approx 11.9 \text{ minutes}\;} $$ **Step 5 — Comparison and discussion:** - **Range = 7.33 km**, **Endurance ≈ 11.9 min** - The endurance is *longer in time* but range is *shorter in distance* than typical cruise at higher altitude (best $L/D$). - For a **surveillance UAV**, **endurance** is usually more important than raw range — so loitering at $V_{P_{\min}}$ is the correct operational choice. > ✅ *(Full marks: 7 marks — 1 for choosing formula, 3 for substitution, 1 for final numeric, 2 for discussion.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` A quadcopter hovers at sea level with the following data: - Total weight: $W = 20$ N (4 rotors combined) - Total rotor disk area: $A = 0.10$ m² - Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - Figure of merit: $FM = 0.70$ - Specific energy of battery: $E_{bat} = 50$ Wh = 180,000 J - Motor + ESC efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.85$ **Answer the following:** #### **(a)** Calculate the **ideal hover power** (momentum theory) and the **actual electrical power drawn** from the battery in hover. (7 marks) #### **(b)** Estimate the **hover endurance** in minutes. Compare with a fixed-wing UAV of the same weight that loiters at $(L/D) = 15$ with $c_P = 0.0003$ s⁻¹. Comment on the relative endurance. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Question B:** ##### Part (a) — Hover Power (7 marks) **Step 1 — Induced velocity at hover** (from $T = \rho A v_i^2$): $$ v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.10}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{0.245}} = \sqrt{81.63} = 9.035 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Ideal hover power** (momentum theory): $$ P_{hover,\text{ideal}} = T \cdot v_i = 20 \times 9.035 = 180.7 \text{ W} $$ > ✅ *Alternatively using the compact form:* $P_{ideal} = T^{3/2}/\sqrt{2\rho A} = 20^{1.5}/\sqrt{0.245} = 89.44/0.495 = 180.7$ W — same result. **Step 3 — Actual aerodynamic power** (Figure of Merit correction): $$ P_{aero} = \frac{P_{hover,\text{ideal}}}{FM} = \frac{180.7}{0.70} = 258.1 \text{ W} $$ **Step 4 — Electrical power drawn from battery** (motor + ESC efficiency): $$ P_{elec} = \frac{P_{aero}}{\eta_m} = \frac{258.1}{0.85} = 303.6 \text{ W} $$ $$ \boxed{\;P_{hover,\text{ideal}} = 180.7 \text{ W}, \quad P_{battery} \approx 304 \text{ W}\;} $$ --- ##### Part (b) — Hover Endurance and Comparison (7 marks) **Step 1 — Multirotor hover endurance:** $$ E_{hover} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{elec}} = \frac{180{,}000}{303.6} = 593.0 \text{ s} \approx 9.88 \text{ min} $$ $$ \boxed{\;E_{hover} \approx 9.9 \text{ minutes}\;} $$ **Step 2 — Fixed-wing loiter endurance (Breguet propeller form):** Using the simplified $E = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_P}\dfrac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho S}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{W_f}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{W_i}}\right)$ form is over-engineered for this question. The simpler form, valid when $\eta_p \approx 1$ and using the **fuel-fraction approximation**: $$ E = \frac{\eta_p}{c_P}\left(\frac{L}{D}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{V_{P_{\min}}} $$ But the cleanest KTU-style approach: use the **first-principle Breguet for propeller** as: $$ E = \frac{1}{c_P} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln(W_i/W_f) $$ For a loiter with say 10% fuel burn, $W_f = 0.9 W_i$, giving $\ln(1/0.9) = 0.1054$: $$ E = \frac{1}{0.0003} \times 15 \times 0.1054 = 5268 \text{ s} \approx 87.8 \text{ min} $$ > *(For full KTU credit, present the general formula and state that typical fixed-wing endurance of 1–2 hours is **5–10× higher** than quadrotor hover for the same weight class.)* **Step 3 — Comparison and engineering insight:** - **Quadrotor hover:** $\sim 10$ minutes, dominated by $T^{3/2}$ scaling. - **Fixed-wing loiter:** $\sim 1.5$ hours, dominated by efficient $L/D$. - **Ratio:** Fixed-wing ≈ 9× longer endurance. - **Engineering trade-off:** Quadrotor wins on **hover capability and VTOL**; fixed-wing wins on **endurance and range**. This is precisely why **hybrid VTOL UAVs** (PERAT745 Module 5 onwards) are an active research frontier. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $V_{T_{\min}}$ and $V_{P_{\min}}$** — Students often use the *wrong* speed for range/endurance problems. **Remember:** jet → $V_{T_{\min}}$; propeller → $V_{P_{\min}}$. > 2. **Forgetting the $\eta_p$ factor** in the propeller Breguet equation — it is a *guaranteed* 1-mark deduction if missed. > 3. **Sign errors in $1/\sqrt{W_f} - 1/\sqrt{W_i}$** — always verify that $W_f < W_i$ before substitution. > 4. **Mixing SI and imperial** — KTU strictly uses SI (N, m, kg, s). 0.5-mark deduction per unit slip. > 5. **Not specifying flight regime** — every performance number must state the flight condition (level, climb, glide, hover). Vague answers = -1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ The **drag polar** $C_D = C_{D_0} + K C_L^2$ is the **single root** of all performance equations. - ✅ In **steady level flight**: $L = W$ and $T = D$ — always start here. - ✅ $V_{T_{\min}}$ and $V_{P_{\min}}$ are **not equal**; $V_{P_{\min}} = V_{T_{\min}}/3^{1/4} \approx 0.76\,V_{T_{\min}}$. - ✅ At $V_{T_{\min}}$: **parasitic drag = induced drag** — a classic KTU identity test. - ✅ **Best glide speed** = $V_{T_{\min}}$; **best $L/D$** occurs at $C_L^* = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$. - ✅ **Breguet range** (propeller) requires $\eta_p$; **jet range** does not. Use the correct one. - ✅ **Endurance ≠ range**: fixed-wing maximises range at *one* $C_L$ and endurance at *another* (often $V_{P_{\min}}$). - ✅ **Climb performance** depends on *excess thrust*; **ceiling** is where excess thrust/power vanishes. - ✅ **Multirotor hover** scales as $T^{3/2}$ — a hard physical limit set by **momentum theory**. - ✅ **Figure of Merit (FM)** of a real multirotor is typically **0.6–0.8** — include it in numerical problems. - ✅ **Hybrid VTOL** combines multirotor hover (low endurance) + fixed-wing cruise (high endurance). - ✅ Always quote the **flight regime** (level, climb, glide, hover) before quoting a performance number. - ✅ **Unit discipline**: $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, $S$ in m², $W$ in N — yields $T$ in N and $P$ in W. - ✅ KTU 14-mark problems expect **derivation + substitution + final number + discussion** — 4-element answer structure. - ✅ For **battery-powered UAVs**: range/endurance *both* decrease as $W$ decreases (because $V$ changes too).

topics.title: Dynamics and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamics and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — DYNAMICS AND DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerial Robot Dynamics & Design (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The branch of aerial robotics concerned with the **rigorous mathematical modelling of motion** (translational + rotational) of an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) under the action of aerodynamic, propulsive, and gravitational forces, together with the **systematic sizing and configuration design** of the vehicle (frame, motors, propellers, ESCs, batteries) such that mission requirements (payload, endurance, agility, stability) are satisfied.

In KTU's 2024 scheme, this topic sits at the intersection of **classical mechanics**, **aerodynamics**, and **mechatronic design**. It forms the foundation upon which **control algorithms** (PID, LQR, MPC, geometric control) of Module 5 operate.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hand-Powered Quadrotor"

Imagine you are holding a square cardboard frame at its four corners by four small electric fans. To make it rise, all four fans push air down together. To tilt it forward (move forward), the **rear fans** must spin faster than the **front fans**, so the back lifts and the nose dips — exactly how a quadrotor works.

- The **collective thrust** = "lift" (all fans together).
- The **roll/pitch** motion = differential thrust across opposite arms.
- The **yaw** motion = reaction torque (Newton's third law on counter-rotating propellers).
- The **dynamic model** tells us *how fast* and *how far* the cardboard moves for any given fan-speed command.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - Air density at sea level: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard hover condition: **Total Thrust = m·g**
> - Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR) for stable hover: **≥ 1.5** (typical design value)
> - Disk loading: **DL = m / (π·D²/4)** in N/m²
> - Power loading: **PL = W_total / P_available** in N/W

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Reference Frames (Why We Need Them)

A quadrotor is described using **two coordinate frames**:

| Frame | Symbol | Origin | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inertial (Earth) Frame** | $E \;(x_E, y_E, z_E)$ | Fixed to ground | Tracking absolute position, GPS |
| **Body Frame** | $B \;(x_B, y_B, z_B)$ | Fixed to UAV centre of mass | Motor commands, sensor readings |

The rotation between them is described by the **rotation matrix** $R \in SO(3)$, parameterised by **Euler angles** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ for **roll, pitch, yaw**.

### 2.2 Why a Quadrotor? — Under-Actuated Insight

A quadrotor has **4 motors** but **6 DOF** (3 position + 3 orientation). Hence it is **under-actuated**: we cannot command position and orientation independently. We control **4 control inputs**:
- $U_1$ → Total thrust (collective lift)
- $U_2$ → Roll torque
- $U_3$ → Pitch torque
- $U_4$ → Yaw torque

### 2.3 Rotor Thrust & Reaction Torque

Each rotor $i$ produces:

$$
T_i = k_t \, \omega_i^2
$$

$$
\tau_i = k_m \, \omega_i^2 \quad (\text{CW rotation gives negative torque, CCW positive})
$$

where:
- $k_t$ = **thrust coefficient** (N·s²/rad²)
- $k_m$ = **moment (drag) coefficient** (N·m·s²/rad²)
- $\omega_i$ = rotor speed (rad/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixing — The Heart of a Quadrotor's Allocator:**
> $$
> \begin{bmatrix}
> U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4
> \end{bmatrix}
> =
> \begin{bmatrix}
> k_t & k_t & k_t & k_t \\
> 0 & -k_t\, l & 0 & k_t\, l \\
> -k_t\, l & 0 & k_t\, l & 0 \\
> k_m & -k_m & k_m & -k_m
> \end{bmatrix}
> \begin{bmatrix}
> \omega_1^2 \\ \omega_2^2 \\ \omega_3^2 \\ \omega_4^2
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
> This matrix is called the **Control Allocation Matrix** — it appears in **almost every KTU 14-mark question**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Are Repeatedly Tested.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hover thrust | $T = m g$ | N | All 4 motors combined |
| 2 | Hover motor speed | $\omega_{hov} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{4 k_t}}$ | rad/s | Per motor |
| 3 | Single rotor thrust | $T_i = k_t \omega_i^2$ | N | Quadratic in ω |
| 4 | Rotor reaction torque | $\tau_i = k_m \omega_i^2$ | N·m | Newton–3rd law |
| 5 | Newton-Euler translational | $m \ddot{p} = R \, T_{B} \, \hat{z}_B - m g \, \hat{z}_E + F_{aero}$ | N | $p$ = position in $E$ frame |
| 6 | Newton-Euler rotational | $I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega) = \tau$ | N·m | $\Omega$ = body angular rate |
| 7 | Thrust-to-Weight Ratio | $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{m g}$ | — | $\geq 1.5$ for hover agility |
| 8 | Disk loading | $\text{DL} = \dfrac{m g}{A_{disk}}$ | N/m² | $A_{disk} = \pi D^2 / 4$ |
| 9 | Propeller thrust coefficient | $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$ | N | Momentum-theory based |
| 10 | Propeller power coefficient | $P = C_P \rho n^3 D^5$ | W | Used in endurance sizing |
| 11 | Figure of Merit | $\text{FoM} = \dfrac{P_{ideal}}{P_{actual}}$ | — | $0 < \text{FoM} \leq 1$ |
| 12 | Motor 1st-order model | $\tau_m \dot{\omega} + \omega = k_u \, u$ | — | $\tau_m$ = time constant |
| 13 | Hover power | $P_{hov} = \dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho A_{disk}}}}$ | W | Momentum-theory limit |
| 14 | Battery endurance | $t_{end} = \dfrac{C_{bat} V_{bat} \,\eta}{P_{hov}}$ | s | $C_{bat}$ in Ah |
| 15 | Stability margin (pitch) | $\text{SM} \propto \dfrac{\partial \tau_\theta}{\partial \theta}$ | — | Open-loop sign check |

**Real-World Utility:**
- **Surveillance drones** (military/agriculture): need high endurance → low disk loading → large propellers.
- **Racing drones** (3″-5″): need high TWR (≥ 5) → high KV motors, small props.
- **Delivery drones** (logistics): need stable hover with payload → high TWR margin, redundant rotors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Newton-Euler Equations of Motion of a Quadrotor

We treat the quadrotor as a **rigid body of mass $m$** and **inertia tensor $I \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3}$**.

#### Step 1 — Force balance in the Inertial Frame (Translational Dynamics)

By **Newton's second law**, the sum of external forces equals mass times acceleration of the centre of mass:

$$
\sum F_E = m \ddot{p}
$$

The three external forces are:
1. **Gravity** (in $E$ frame): $F_g = -m g \, \hat{z}_E$
2. **Total thrust** (in $B$ frame, magnitude $U_1$): $F_T = R \, U_1 \, \hat{z}_B$
3. **Aerodynamic disturbances** (drag, wind): $F_{aero}$

Therefore, in the inertial frame:

$$
m \ddot{p} = R \, U_1 \, \hat{z}_B - m g \, \hat{z}_E + F_{aero}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Thrust is generated along the body's $z_B$ axis, so we project it into the inertial frame using $R$.

#### Step 2 — Torque balance in the Body Frame (Rotational Dynamics)

By **Euler's equation for a rigid body**:

$$
I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega) = \tau
$$

where:
- $\Omega = [p, q, r]^T$ = body angular velocity (roll, pitch, yaw rates)
- $\tau = [U_2, U_3, U_4]^T$ = control torques (roll, pitch, yaw)
- $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$ for symmetric quadrotor

**Conversion logic:** The term $\Omega \times (I\Omega)$ is the **gyroscopic coupling** — it is *not* zero because the body rotates about its own axes (Coriolis-like term).

#### Step 3 — The Complete 6-DOF Model

Combining translation and rotation, and assuming small Euler-angle approximation $\phi, \theta$ are small and $\psi$ varies slowly:

$$
\ddot{x} = -\frac{U_1}{m} (\sin\phi \sin\psi + \cos\phi \cos\psi \sin\theta)
$$

$$
\ddot{y} = -\frac{U_1}{m} (\cos\phi \sin\psi \sin\theta - \sin\phi \cos\psi)
$$

$$
\ddot{z} = g - \frac{U_1}{m} (\cos\phi \cos\theta)
$$

$$
\ddot{\phi} = \dot{\theta} \dot{\psi} \left(\frac{I_y - I_z}{I_x}\right) + \frac{U_2}{I_x}
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta} = \dot{\phi} \dot{\psi} \left(\frac{I_z - I_x}{I_y}\right) + \frac{U_3}{I_y}
$$

$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dot{\phi} \dot{\theta} \left(\frac{I_x - I_y}{I_z}\right) + \frac{U_4}{I_z}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **non-linear coupling terms** $\dot\theta \dot\psi \left(\tfrac{I_y-I_z}{I_x}\right)$ etc. are critical for KTU 14-mark problems — they justify the use of **non-linear controllers** like feedback linearisation in Module 5.

#### Step 4 — Conversion to Vertical Channel (KTU Favourite 7-Mark Sub-Question)

Hover at equilibrium: $\phi = \theta = 0$, $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta} = \dot{\psi} = 0$.

From the $z$-equation:

$$
\ddot{z} = g - \frac{U_1}{m}
$$

**Hover condition** $\Rightarrow \ddot z = 0$:

$$
U_1^{hov} = m g
$$

Per-motor hover speed:

$$
\omega_{hov} = \sqrt{\frac{U_1^{hov}}{4 \, k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{4 k_t}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some students write $U_1^{hov} = m g$ but forget to **divide by 4** when computing per-motor speed — losing 1 mark.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Motor + Propeller Dynamics (Bounded 1st-Order System)

A brushless DC motor with propeller load is modelled as:

$$
\tau_m \, \dot{\omega}_i + \omega_i = k_u \, u_i
$$

where $u_i \in [0, 1]$ is the **PWM command** to the ESC.

**Solution for a step input** $u_i(t) = u_0$ with $\omega_i(0) = 0$:

$$
\omega_i(t) = k_u \, u_0 \, (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})
$$

**Steady-state speed:**

$$
\omega_i^{ss} = k_u \, u_0
$$

**Steady-state thrust:**

$$
T_i^{ss} = k_t \, (k_u \, u_0)^2 = k_t \, k_u^2 \, u_0^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for design:** A **large $\tau_m$** (slow motor response) limits the **bandwidth** of the attitude controller. KTU examiners often ask: *"How do you choose the motor to satisfy a closed-loop bandwidth of, say, 20 rad/s?"*

**Design rule of thumb:**

$$
\tau_m \leq \frac{1}{5 \, \omega_{bandwidth}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Sizing — Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Given:**
- Quadrotor mass $m = 1.5$ kg
- Required TWR = 2.0 (with safety margin)
- $k_t = 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s²/rad²
- $k_m = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$ N·m·s²/rad²
- Arm length $l = 0.225$ m

**Find:**
(a) Required hover thrust and per-motor thrust.
(b) Required per-motor hover speed.
(c) Maximum roll torque available at hover.

#### Solution (a):

$$
T_{max} = \text{TWR} \cdot m g = 2.0 \times 1.5 \times 9.81 = 29.43 \text{ N}
$$

Per motor:

$$
T_i = \frac{T_{max}}{4} = \frac{29.43}{4} = 7.36 \text{ N}
$$

#### Solution (b):

$$
\omega_{hov} = \sqrt{\frac{T_i}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{7.36}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{5.66 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2379.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{2379.5 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 22{,}725 \text{ rpm}
$$

#### Solution (c):

Roll torque is generated by differential thrust between motors 2 and 4 (front/back pair):

$$
U_2 = l \cdot (T_4 - T_2)
$$

Maximum value occurs when one motor is at maximum and the opposite at minimum. Assuming full saturation:

$$
U_2^{max} = l \cdot T_{max} = 0.225 \times 29.43 = 6.62 \text{ N·m}
$$

(Each motor gives $T_{max}/2 = 14.715$ N differential contribution.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Quadrotor Dynamics & Control Allocation Chain

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **complete signal flow** from a pilot/command input down to motor RPMs and back up to position feedback. This is the type of figure KTU examiners expect when the question says *"Draw the block diagram of a quadrotor control system."*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmdA["Desired Position p_des"] --> ctlPos["Position Controller Kp"]
    cmdB["Desired Velocity v_des"] --> ctlPos
    ctlPos --> attDes["Desired Attitude phi_des, theta_des, psi_des"]
    cmdC["Desired Yaw psi_des"] --> attDes
    attDes --> ctlAtt["Attitude Controller Ka"]
    ctlAtt --> uVec["Control Vector U1, U2, U3, U4"]
    uVec --> alloc["Control Allocation Matrix B"]
    alloc --> rpm1["Motor 1 RPM w1"]
    alloc --> rpm2["Motor 2 RPM w2"]
    alloc --> rpm3["Motor 3 RPM w3"]
    alloc --> rpm4["Motor 4 RPM w4"]
    rpm1 --> dynT["Thrust and Reaction Torque"]
    rpm2 --> dynT
    rpm3 --> dynT
    rpm4 --> dynT
    dynT --> fbd["Newton-Euler 6-DOF Rigid Body"]
    fbd --> sens["IMU and GPS Sensors"]
    fbd --> aerodyn["Aero Drag Disturbances"]
    fbd --> grav["Gravity mg"]
    sens --> fbAtt["Attitude Feedback phi, theta, psi"]
    sens --> fbPos["Position Feedback p"]
    fbAtt --> ctlAtt
    fbPos --> ctlPos
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Design Sizing Loop

The following diagram captures the **iterative design trade-off** between mission requirements, propulsion performance, and structural sizing. This is useful for the KTU 14-mark design question: *"Design a quadrotor for a 1 kg payload with 20 min endurance."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req["Mission Requirements: Payload, Endurance, Range, Cost"]
    req --> mTotal["Total Mass m = m_frame + m_payload + m_bat + m_motor"]
    mTotal --> twr["Hover Thrust T_hov = m g, TWR greater than 1.5"]
    twr --> motorSel["Motor Selection: KV, max current, max thrust"]
    motorSel --> propSel["Propeller Selection: diameter D, pitch P"]
    propSel --> diskLoad["Disk Loading DL = m g over A_disk"]
    diskLoad --> perfCheck{"Performance Met?"}
    perfCheck -- No --> motorSel
    perfCheck -- Yes --> bat["Battery Sizing: energy = P_hov times endurance"]
    bat --> struct["Structural Frame: arm length l, stiffness"]
    struct --> ctrl["Controller Bandwidth: greater than 5 over tau_m"]
    ctrl --> final["Final Design Verification via Simulation"]
```

### 4.3 Force & Torque Allocation Diagram (X-Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "X-Configuration Quadrotor Top View"
        dir1["Front: Motor 1 CCW"]
        dir2["Right: Motor 2 CW"]
        dir3["Rear: Motor 3 CCW"]
        dir4["Left: Motor 4 CW"]
    end
    dir1 --> U1["U1 = Total Thrust"]
    dir2 --> U1
    dir3 --> U1
    dir4 --> U1
    dir1 --> U2["U2 = Roll Torque via Motor 2 minus Motor 4"]
    dir4 --> U2
    dir2 --> U2
    dir1 --> U3["U3 = Pitch Torque via Motor 1 minus Motor 3"]
    dir3 --> U3
    dir1 --> U4["U4 = Yaw Torque via CW motors minus CCW motors"]
    dir2 --> U4
    dir3 --> U4
    dir4 --> U4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the **rotation direction** (CW/CCW) of motors. Opposite-diagonal motors spin the same way (1 & 3 = CCW, 2 & 4 = CW, or vice versa). This balances the **net yaw torque** at hover.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the *Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR)* of a quadrotor. Why is a TWR greater than 1 essential for stable agile flight, and what is the typical design value?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** $\text{TWR} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{m g}$ — the ratio of maximum total thrust producible by all motors to the weight of the UAV.
- (1 mark) **Why > 1:** If TWR ≤ 1, the vehicle cannot even hover, let alone climb, accelerate, or reject disturbances.
- (1 mark) **Typical value:** For an **agile multirotor**, $\text{TWR} \geq 1.5$ (commonly 2.0–5.0 for racing); for **endurance optimised**, $\text{TWR} \approx 1.5$–$1.8$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is meant by the term *control allocation* in a quadrotor? Write the control allocation matrix for an X-configuration quadrotor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** Control allocation is the mapping from the desired control inputs $\left[U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4\right]^T$ (collective thrust + 3 torques) to individual motor speed-squared commands $\left[\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2, \omega_4^2\right]^T$.
- (2 marks) **Matrix:**

$$
B =
\begin{bmatrix}
k_t & k_t & k_t & k_t \\
0 & -k_t l & 0 & k_t l \\
-k_t l & 0 & k_t l & 0 \\
k_m & -k_m & k_m & -k_m
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Newton-Euler equations of motion** of a quadrotor in 3D space. Clearly define the body and inertial frames, the rotation matrix, and the inertia tensor. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadrotor has the following parameters: mass $m = 2.0$ kg, thrust coefficient $k_t = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s²/rad², moment coefficient $k_m = 1.4 \times 10^{-8}$ N·m·s²/rad², and arm length $l = 0.25$ m. Compute the **(i)** per-motor hover speed, **(ii)** hover RPM, and **(iii)** maximum roll torque. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Frame definitions (1 mark):**
- Inertial frame $E$: $x_E$ (North), $y_E$ (East), $z_E$ (Down or Up — KTU convention uses **NED**).
- Body frame $B$: $x_B$ (front), $y_B$ (right), $z_B$ (down).
- Rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ parameterised by ZYX Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$:

$$
R = R_z(\psi) \, R_y(\theta) \, R_x(\phi)
$$

(1 mark for stating the frames and Euler sequence.)

**Step 2 — Translational equation (3 marks):**

The total external force in $E$ frame:

$$
m \ddot{p} = R \, U_1 \, \hat{z}_B - m g \, \hat{z}_E
$$

Expanding using the explicit form of $R$:

$$
\ddot{x} = -\frac{U_1}{m} (\sin\phi \sin\psi + \cos\phi \cos\psi \sin\theta)
$$

$$
\ddot{y} = -\frac{U_1}{m} (\cos\phi \sin\psi \sin\theta - \sin\phi \cos\psi)
$$

$$
\ddot{z} = g - \frac{U_1}{m} (\cos\phi \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Rotational equation (3 marks):**

Euler's equation for rigid body in body frame:

$$
I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega) = \tau
$$

With $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$ and $\Omega = [p, q, r]^T$, expanding component-wise:

$$
I_{xx} \dot{p} = (I_{yy} - I_{zz}) \, q r + U_2
$$

$$
I_{yy} \dot{q} = (I_{zz} - I_{xx}) \, r p + U_3
$$

$$
I_{zz} \dot{r} = (I_{xx} - I_{yy}) \, p q + U_4
$$

The terms $(I_{yy} - I_{zz}) q r$ etc. are **gyroscopic coupling terms**.

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Per-motor hover speed (3 marks):**

$$
U_1^{hov} = m g = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_i^{hov} = \frac{U_1^{hov}}{4} = \frac{19.62}{4} = 4.905 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\omega_{hov} = \sqrt{\frac{T_i^{hov}}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.905}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{4.0875 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2021.7 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Hover RPM (2 marks):**

$$
\text{RPM}_{hov} = \frac{\omega_{hov} \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{2021.7 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 19{,}308 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Maximum roll torque (2 marks):**

For X-configuration, roll torque is generated by motors 2 (right) and 4 (left):

$$
U_2^{max} = l \cdot T_{max}^{single}
$$

Assuming one motor at maximum hover thrust differential (≈ $T_i$):

$$
U_2^{max} = 0.25 \times 4.905 \times 2 = 2.4525 \text{ N·m}
$$

(Or, if assuming full saturation such that one motor produces 2× hover thrust: $U_2^{max} = l \cdot (2 T_i) = 0.25 \times 9.81 = 2.45$ N·m — both forms accepted depending on convention.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the **control allocation matrix** of an X-configuration quadrotor. Show how collective thrust, roll, pitch, and yaw torques map to motor speed-squared commands. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadrotor is required to hover at $m = 1.2$ kg and reach a forward velocity of $5$ m/s in **2 seconds** from rest. Compute the **(i)** required **forward thrust**, **(ii)** required **pitch angle** for steady cruise, and **(iii)** required **total thrust** at the start of acceleration. Use $C_D A = 0.05$ kg/m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Control allocation (7 marks):**

For an X-configuration quadrotor with arm length $l$ and motor numbering as 1=front, 2=right, 3=rear, 4=left:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
k_t & k_t & k_t & k_t \\
0 & -k_t l & 0 & k_t l \\
-k_t l & 0 & k_t l & 0 \\
k_m & -k_m & k_m & -k_m
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\omega_1^2 \\ \omega_2^2 \\ \omega_3^2 \\ \omega_4^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

- $U_1$: all motors contribute equally → **collective lift**.
- $U_2$: motors 2 and 4 contribute with opposite signs → **roll torque**.
- $U_3$: motors 1 and 3 contribute with opposite signs → **pitch torque**.
- $U_4$: depends on motor spin direction → **yaw reaction torque**.

(5 marks for matrix + 2 marks for explanation with diagram — see SECTION 4.3 above.)

---

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Forward thrust to overcome drag at $v = 5$ m/s (2 marks):**

$$
F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 (C_D A) = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 25 \times 0.05 = 0.766 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Required pitch angle for steady cruise at $v = 5$ m/s (3 marks):**

In steady cruise, horizontal force balance:

$$
U_1 \sin\theta = F_{drag}
$$

Assuming $U_1 \approx m g$ (small angle):

$$
\sin\theta \approx \theta = \frac{F_{drag}}{m g} = \frac{0.766}{1.2 \times 9.81} = 0.0651 \text{ rad} \approx 3.73^\circ
$$

**(iii) Required total thrust at the start of acceleration (2 marks):**

At $t=0$, $v = 0$, so no drag. Required horizontal acceleration:

$$
a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{5 - 0}{2} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

Forward force needed:

$$
F_x = m a = 1.2 \times 2.5 = 3.0 \text{ N}
$$

Total thrust (vector sum):

$$
U_1^{req} = \sqrt{(m g)^2 + F_x^2} = \sqrt{(11.772)^2 + (3.0)^2} = \sqrt{138.58 + 9} = \sqrt{147.58} \approx 12.15 \text{ N}
$$

(This is a 3.2% increase over hover thrust — well within the TWR margin.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in the rotation matrix** — getting $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$ swapped costs full marks on the translational sub-question.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$ in $\ddot{z}$ equation** — many write $\ddot{z} = -\tfrac{U_1}{m} \cos\phi \cos\theta$ and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up yaw motor signs** — yaw torque depends on **direction of rotation** (CW vs CCW); just stating "$U_4 = k_m(\omega_2^2 - \omega_4^2)$" without specifying CW/CCW convention costs 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Inertia tensor confusion** — students write $I_{xy}$ terms for a symmetric quadrotor (which is **diagonal**); wrong.
> 5. **Unit mismatch** — mixing N (thrust) and kg·m/s² without stating equivalence; losing 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Dynamics & Design" — KTU Module 4:**

- ✅ Quadrotor is a **6-DOF, 4-input under-actuated** rigid body.
- ✅ Two reference frames: **Inertial ($E$)** and **Body ($B$)**; rotation $R \in SO(3)$.
- ✅ Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ parameterise $R$ — convention is **ZYX intrinsic**.
- ✅ **Hover condition:** $U_1^{hov} = m g$ → per-motor speed $\omega_{hov} = \sqrt{m g / (4 k_t)}$.
- ✅ **Control allocation matrix** $B$ is $4\times 4$, maps $\omega_i^2 \rightarrow [U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4]^T$.
- ✅ **Newton-Euler translational:** $m\ddot{p} = R U_1 \hat{z}_B - m g \hat{z}_E + F_{aero}$.
- ✅ **Newton-Euler rotational:** $I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega) = \tau$ → expands to **3 coupled equations**.
- ✅ **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio:** $\text{TWR} = T_{max} / (mg)$; design for **≥ 1.5**; aggressive flight **≥ 3.0**.
- ✅ **Motor model:** 1st-order LPF $\tau_m \dot\omega + \omega = k_u u$; bandwidth limit.
- ✅ **Propeller:** $T = C_T \rho n^2 D^4$, $P = C_P \rho n^3 D^5$.
- ✅ **Disk loading:** $\text{DL} = m g / (\pi D^2 / 4)$ — low DL = high efficiency.
- ✅ **Figure of Merit (FoM):** ideal vs actual power; max FoM = 1.
- ✅ **Endurance:** $t_{end} = C_{bat} V_{bat} \eta / P_{hov}$.
- ✅ Gyroscopic coupling terms $(I_{yy} - I_{zz}) q r$ etc. **must not be omitted** in the rotational equation.
- ✅ Symmetric quadrotor ⇒ $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$ — **off-diagonal terms vanish**.
- ✅ Motor directions: **diagonally opposite motors spin the same way** (CCW + CW).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark design problems: always end with a **TWR check** and **endurance estimate**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Challenge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Challenge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Design Challenge" in Aerial Robotics?

A **Design Challenge** in Aerial Robotics is a structured, end-to-end engineering problem that compels a student to translate a high-level mission statement (e.g., "deliver a 500 g medical payload over 5 km") into a fully realized, quantitatively justified **Unmanned Aerial System (UAS)** architecture. It is the synthesis-level exercise where aerodynamics, propulsion, structures, control, and power-systems engineering converge into a single optimized platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (PERAT745, Module 4):**
> *A Design Challenge is an integrated, constraint-driven design exercise in which a student must (i) define a mission profile, (ii) perform weight & power budgeting, (iii) select airframe, propulsion, and avionics, (iv) size the energy source, and (v) verify the design against performance, stability, and regulatory constraints (DGCA / FAA Part 107 equivalents).*

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this maps to the highest cognitive levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** — *Analyze, Evaluate, and Create* — and tests Course Outcomes **CO4 (Design)** and **CO5 (Modern tool usage and engineering judgment)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Real-World Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Delivery Tricycle"**
>
> Imagine you are asked to build a delivery tricycle for a small-town courier. You wouldn't just buy a heavy steel frame and a weak motor. You would:
> 1. **Define the mission:** "Carry 5 kg of parcels across 3 km in 30 minutes."
> 2. **Budget the weight:** Frame ≈ 8 kg, rider ≈ 60 kg, payload ≈ 5 kg. Total = 73 kg.
> 3. **Size the power:** Slopes + friction × speed ⇒ pick a 250 W motor.
> 4. **Size the battery:** 250 W × 0.5 h = **125 Wh**, plus a 20 % margin ⇒ **150 Wh**.
> 5. **Verify:** Will the brakes hold? Will it fit through gates? Will it tip on turns?
>
> A **UAV Design Challenge is exactly this**, but with a *third dimension* — gravity, drag, and energy density become your new "friction," and your "slopes" become hover-induced power loss. The rules of physics, not the rider's leg, set the limits.

The student is judged on **engineering justification**, not just numbers. Every selection — a 6S 10000 mAh LiPo over a 4S — must be backed by a quantitative reason tied to the mission.

---

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of an Aerial Robotics Design Challenge

| # | Pillar | Core Question Answered | Typical Output |
|---|--------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Mission Definition** | What must the UAV *do*? | Mission Profile Diagram |
| 2 | **Weight Budget** | How heavy is everything? | Weight Breakdown Table |
| 3 | **Power Budget** | How much energy per mission? | Endurance & Range Plots |
| 4 | **Propulsion Sizing** | Which motor, prop, ESC, battery? | Trade-off Curves |
| 5 | **Stability & Control** | Will it fly straight & recover? | Control Loop / Sensor Suite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** A well-designed multirotor for a 5 km survey mission should achieve a **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR) ≥ 2.0** for hover agility and a **Hover Disk Loading (HDL) ≤ 80 N/m²** for propulsive efficiency. Energy density of a modern Li-Po is ≈ **150–200 Wh/kg** at the cell level (≈ **120 Wh/kg** at the pack level — use this in KTU calculations).

---

## 1.4 Why Is This Module Crucial?

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, Module 4 shifts the student from *understanding* a drone (Modules 1–3) to *creating* one. The Design Challenge is typically evaluated as a **14-mark Part-B question** in the End Semester Exam and a major component of the **Continuous Evaluation (CE) project**. It is also the foundation for final-year capstone projects and national-level competitions such as **Drone Olympics** and **ABU Robocon (aerial variants)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Design Spiral" — Iterative Workflow

A UAV design is **never linear**. It is a spiral that converges only when all constraints are simultaneously satisfied. Below is the step-by-step logic that examiners expect a student to articulate.

### Step 1 — Mission Profile Definition
Identify the **regime sequence** of the flight:
> Takeoff → Climb → Cruise → Loiter → Descent → Landing.

Each regime has different power demands. KTU questions often supply a profile like *"hover 5 min, climb 2 min, cruise 10 min, return 5 min"* — the student must compute energy for each leg *separately*, then sum.

### Step 2 — Weight Estimation (Statistical Build-up)
Begin with an **empty-weight fraction estimate** using historical data. For a 2–5 kg multirotor:
- Frame: 20–25 % of MTOW
- Motors: 8–12 %
- ESCs: 3–5 %
- Propellers: 2–4 %
- Battery: 25–35 % (this is the key sizing variable)
- Avionics (FC, GPS, TX/RX, sensors): 10–15 %
- Payload: 15–25 %

### Step 3 — Propulsion Sizing
Choose TWR first. For **agricultural/survey** drones, TWR ≈ 1.5–1.8; for **racing**, TWR ≥ 4.0; for **delivery**, TWR ≈ 2.0–2.5.

### Step 4 — Energy Sizing
Total energy is the time-integral of power. For a **constant-power** mission approximation:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_i$$

For a **variable-power** mission, integrate using Breguet's equations (see §3).

### Step 5 — Verification & Margin
KTU mandates a **20 % safety margin** on energy and a **2.0× TWR** for hover. Failure to include these loses marks.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet — Print This.** Every formula below has appeared (in equivalent form) in KTU question papers for PERAT745 and its predecessor codes.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Assumptions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Hover Power (Momentum Theory)** | $P_{\text{hover}} = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$ | W (T in N, A in m²) | Ideal, single rotor |
| 2 | **Hover Power (Realistic, with Figure of Merit)** | $P_{\text{hover}} = \dfrac{T \cdot v_h}{FM}, \quad v_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{2 \rho A}}$ | W | $FM \in [0.6, 0.8]$ for small UAVs |
| 3 | **Disk Loading** | $\delta = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W}{n A_{\text{prop}}}$ | N/m² | n = number of rotors |
| 4 | **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio** | $TWR = \dfrac{n T_{\max}}{W_{\text{MTOW}}}$ | — | Must be ≥ 2.0 for hover |
| 5 | **Endurance (Multirotor, Hover)** | $E = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot C_b}{P_{\text{hover}}}$ | hours | $C_b$ in Wh, $P$ in W |
| 6 | **Breguet Range (Fixed-wing)** | $R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$ | m | $\eta_p$ = prop efficiency |
| 7 | **Breguet Endurance (Fixed-wing)** | $E = \dfrac{1}{c_P} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$ | s | $c_P$ specific power (1/s) |
| 8 | **Battery Energy** | $E_{\text{bat}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{\text{dis}}$ | Wh | $V_{\text{nom}}$ = 3.7 V/cell (LiPo) |
| 9 | **Power Loading** | $PL = \dfrac{W}{P_{\text{avail}}}$ | N/W | Inverse of specific power |
| 10 | **Climb Power** | $P_{\text{climb}} = T \cdot v_{\text{climb}}$ | W | $v_{\text{climb}} \in [2, 5]$ m/s typical |
| 11 | **Required Thrust (Climb)** | $T_{\text{req}} = W + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D + W \cdot \dfrac{v_{\text{climb}}}{V}$ | N | Fixed-wing climb |
| 12 | **Battery Mass (from Energy)** | $m_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\rho_E \cdot \eta_{\text{tot}} \cdot DoD}$ | kg | $\rho_E \approx 120$ Wh/kg pack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Alert (KTU 2024):** Use $W$ for weight (N) and $m$ for mass (kg). Never confuse $W$ (weight) with $W$ (work) — KTU examiners will dock a mark for ambiguity. Always state units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the **lingua franca** of the UAV industry. Sizing spreadsheets used by DJI, Skydio, and ideaForge all start from this exact foundation. A student who masters this set can:
- **Evaluate** a vendor's spec sheet ("Does this drone really have 30 min hover endurance?").
- **Estimate** the battery swap interval for a fleet of delivery drones (Swiggy/Zomato use-case).
- **Reverse-engineer** a competitor's design choices for academic comparison.
- **Fail fast** in capstone design reviews — catching an under-powered battery *before* a $5,000 crash.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Momentum Theory of Hover Power

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 4. A KTU Part-B question (14 marks) will commonly allocate **5 marks** to this derivation alone.

### Step 1: Control Volume Setup
Consider a single rotor in steady hover, with control volume $CV$ enclosing the rotor disk of area $A$ and the far-wake. Air enters with zero velocity and leaves with velocity $v_h$ (the *induced velocity* at the disk).

**Conversion logic:** Mass flow rate through the disk equals density $\times$ velocity $\times$ area.

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

### Step 2: Thrust Equation (Momentum)
Thrust equals the rate of change of momentum in the wake. Since the far-wake velocity is $2 v_h$ (continuity requires this), thrust $T$ is:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot (2 v_h) = \rho A v_h \cdot 2 v_h = 2 \rho A v_h^2$$

**Conversion logic:** Velocity doubles from disk to far-wake due to incompressibility (Bramwell's argument).

### Step 3: Solve for $v_h$ in Terms of $T$

$$v_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{2 \rho A}}$$

### Step 4: Power Equation
Power is thrust $\times$ induced velocity at the disk (this is *not* the far-wake velocity):

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = T \cdot v_h$$

Substituting $v_h$:

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = T \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result — Boxed for KTU:**
> $$P_{\text{hover, ideal}} = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$$
> This is the **minimum theoretical power** to hover. No real rotor can do better, and most achieve only $60\text{–}80\,\%$ of this. The ratio is the **Figure of Merit (FM)**.

### Step 5: Apply Figure of Merit
Real-world (with profile, tip, and parasitic losses):

$$P_{\text{hover, real}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{FM} = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{FM \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho A}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Breguet Range Equation (Fixed-Wing)

This derivation is the **canonical exam question** for the "Design Challenge of a fixed-wing survey drone."

### Step 1: Range Definition
Range is the integral of velocity over time. For a steady cruise at $V$, the aircraft's mass (and thus weight) decreases as fuel is burned.

$$R = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{total}}} V \cdot dt$$

**Conversion logic:** $W = mg$, and $dt = -\dfrac{dW}{g \cdot \dot{m}_f} = -\dfrac{dW}{c_T \cdot V \cdot \eta_p}$ where $c_T$ is the thrust-specific fuel consumption (kg fuel / N thrust / s). For batteries, $c_T$ is replaced by the equivalent electrical burn rate.

### Step 2: Substitute

$$R = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{V}{-dW / (c_T \eta_p V)} = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} -\dfrac{c_T \eta_p V^2}{g \cdot dW} \cdot g = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot dW$$

Wait — using $L = W$ in cruise, $L/D = W/D$, and $D = T$ in unaccelerated flight, with $\dot{W} = -c_T T V / g$. Let us redo cleanly:

$$R = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} V \, dt = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{V}{-\dot{W}} dW = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{V}{c_T \, T \, V / g} dW = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{g}{c_T \, T} dW$$

For level flight, $T = D = W / (L/D)$, so:

$$R = \int_{W_f}^{W_i} \dfrac{g}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{1}{W} dW = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boxed Result — KTU Kite:**
> $$R = \dfrac{\eta_p}{c_T} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$$
> This says: *better lift-to-drag, more efficient propeller, or higher fuel fraction ⇒ more range.* It is the *design lever* a student should pull first when asked to "extend range."

### Step 3: Numerical Example
Suppose a fixed-wing UAV with $L/D = 15$, $\eta_p = 0.7$, $W_i = 50$ N, $W_f = 35$ N, $c_T = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ N$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ (Li-Po equivalent).

$$R = \dfrac{0.7}{1.1 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot 15 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{50}{35}\right) = 9.55 \times 10^{5} \cdot 0.357 = 3.41 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} = 341 \text{ km}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Design Trade-offs

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Examiner's Tool:** When asked to *evaluate* a design choice, refer to this 2-axis matrix.

| Design Lever | ↑ Increases… | ↓ Decreases… | Trade-off |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Propeller diameter** | Hover efficiency | Agility / responsiveness | Long blades = efficient hover, but sluggish pitch response |
| **Battery capacity** | Endurance | TWR & payload | Heavy battery ⇒ less room for sensors |
| **Motor KV** | Speed / RPM | Torque per amp | High-KV = fast, low-torque; use with small props |
| **Number of rotors (n)** | Redundancy & safety | Disk loading penalty | 6 > 4 > 3 in efficiency, 8 starts losing due to inter-rotor interference |
| **Wing area (fixed-wing)** | $L/D_{\max}$ | Cruise speed | Larger wing = lower stall speed, but more drag at cruise |
| **TWR** | Agility & wind margin | Endurance | "Sport mode" robs ~30 % endurance |
| **Frame stiffness** | Vibration isolation (indirectly) | Weight | Carbon weave > GFRP but 2× cost |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — "The KTU 5 kg Survey Drone"

**Problem (typical 14-mark style):**
> Design a quadrotor to carry a 1.5 kg camera payload over a 2 km survey line in still air. Required hover time 15 min, climb rate 3 m/s, return-to-home margin 25 %.

**Solution (skeleton — full marks requires complete table):**

**Step 1: Weight Build-up (iteration 1)**

$$W_{\text{MTOW}} = m_{\text{frame}} + m_{\text{bat}} + 4 m_{\text{motor}} + 4 m_{\text{ESC}} + m_{\text{avionics}} + m_{\text{payload}}$$

Assume initial guess $W_{\text{MTOW}} = 5$ kg. From statistical fractions, battery $\approx 1.5$ kg, frame $1.0$ kg, motors $4 \times 0.15 = 0.6$ kg, ESCs $4 \times 0.05 = 0.2$ kg, avionics $0.3$ kg, payload $1.5$ kg ⇒ sum = 5.1 kg. **Converged in one iteration.**

**Step 2: Hover Power**

Using 9-inch props ($A = \pi (0.1145)^2 / 2 \approx 0.0206$ m² per prop, 4 props ⇒ total $A = 0.0824$ m²) and $T = 5.1 \times 9.81 = 50$ N:

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{50^{1.5}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.0824}} = \dfrac{353.6}{0.45} = 786 \text{ W}$$

With $FM = 0.7$, $P_{\text{hover}} = 786 / 0.7 = 1123$ W. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** This is in the right ballpark. A real 5 kg quad drawing ~1.1 kW in hover is normal.

**Step 3: Climb Power**

$$P_{\text{climb}} = T \cdot v_{\text{climb}} = 50 \cdot 3 = 150 \text{ W (additional)}$$

So total in climb = $1123 + 150 = 1273$ W. Time to climb 50 m = 16.7 s ≈ 0.28 min.

**Step 4: Energy Budget**

| Leg | Duration (min) | Power (W) | Energy (Wh) |
|-----|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| Hover (survey) | 15 | 1123 | 280.8 |
| Climb (out) | 0.3 | 1273 | 6.4 |
| Cruise transit (out 1 km) | 1.5 | 800 (est.) | 20.0 |
| Cruise transit (back 1 km) | 1.5 | 800 | 20.0 |
| RTH margin (25 %) | — | — | 81.8 |
| **Total** | **18.3** | — | **409 Wh** |

**Step 5: Battery Sizing**
With 6S LiPo, $V_{\text{nom}} = 22.2$ V, $DoD = 0.8$, $\eta = 0.95$:

$$m_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{409}{120 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.95} = 4.48 \text{ kg}$$

**Uh-oh — this exceeds our 1.5 kg budget by 3×.** The design has *failed the iteration* and must be re-sized (e.g., higher $L/D$ fixed-wing, or 8S, or a hybrid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This contradiction is **expected** and **graded favorably**. The student who *identifies* the infeasibility and proposes a corrective redesign (larger props, higher L/D, reduced payload, or fixed-wing) gets full marks. The student who *just plows through the numbers* loses 4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Challenge "V-Model" Workflow

The V-Model is the industry-standard framework for systems engineering in UAV design. KTU examiners expect a student to be able to **sketch and explain this V** in any Module-4 Part-B question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Definition] --> B[Requirement Analysis]
    B --> C[Conceptual Design]
    C --> D[Preliminary Sizing]
    D --> E[Detailed Design Airframe]
    D --> F[Detailed Design Propulsion]
    D --> G[Detailed Design Avionics]
    E --> H[Integration and Test Airframe]
    F --> I[Integration and Test Propulsion]
    G --> J[Integration and Test Avionics]
    H --> K[System Integration and Flight Test]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Mission Verification]
    L --> M[Validation Against Requirements]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the V:**
> The **left leg** of the V is *decomposition* (problem → sub-problems). The **bottom** is *integration*. The **right leg** is *verification* (sub-solutions → system solution). The key KTU takeaway: **testing** (right leg) must mirror **design** (left leg) at every level.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Multirotor Design Challenge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Mission Profile and Constraints]
        A2[Payload Specs]
        A3[Regulatory Limits]
    end

    subgraph processLayer["PROCESS LAYER"]
        B1[Weight Estimation]
        B2[Power and Endurance Sizing]
        B3[Propulsion Selection]
        B4[Stability and Control Design]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1[Airframe Geometry]
        C2[Motor and Prop Selection]
        C3[Battery Pack Specification]
        C4[Flight Controller Tuning Parameters]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B1 --> C4
    A3 --> B4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mapping:** This is the *block-level functional architecture* of any "Design a drone to do X" exam answer. Each block is a **3–4 mark subsection** in a 14-mark answer. Filling all 5 blocks (Input, Weight, Power, Propulsion, Control) is the formula for full marks.

---

## 4.3 Decision-Flow for Component Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Mission Defined] --> q1{Is it a hover intensive mission?}
    q1 -- Yes, e.g. inspection --> multi[Multirotor Platform]
    q1 -- No, endurance critical --> fixed[Fixed Wing Platform]
    multi --> q2{Payload less than 2 kg?}
    q2 -- Yes --> quad[Quadrotor]
    q2 -- No, heavy lift --> hexa[Hexa or Octa]
    quad --> motor[Select Motor from KV and Thrust Chart]
    hexa --> motor
    motor --> prop[Match Prop Diameter to Motor and ESC]
    prop --> batt[Size Battery from Energy Budget]
    batt --> verify{TWR greater than 2 and Endurance Meets Spec?}
    verify -- No --> redesign[Return to Step 1 and Iterate]
    verify -- Yes --> lock[Lock Design and Document]
    fixed --> wing[Sizing Wing for Required L over D]
    wing --> bat2[Size Battery with Breguet Equation]
    bat2 --> verify
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners often ask *"Why a quadrotor and not a hexacopter?"* The answer must reference **disk loading, redundancy, and payload-to-weight ratio**, not just personal preference. Memorize one quantitative justification.

---

## 4.4 Power-Budget Stacked-Bar Concept

A canonical KTU-style diagram to show power consumption across mission phases. While Mermaid cannot draw real axis-aligned bars, the *data flow* of a power-budget analysis is shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hover Phase Power 1123 W] --> sum[Total Energy Wh]
    P2[Climb Phase Power 1273 W] --> sum
    P3[Cruise Phase Power 800 W] --> sum
    P4[Landing Phase Power 600 W] --> sum
    sum --> margin[Add 20 percent Margin and 25 percent RTH]
    margin --> result[Final Battery Sizing in Wh]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A student who presents *only* the final battery size (e.g., "5000 mAh 6S") without a power-budget table loses **3 marks** for missing the *design rationale*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)
