<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define NDVI. Explain how a drone-mounted multispectral sensor uses NDVI to detect crop stress.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) is a dimensionless spectral index defined as
> $$\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{Red}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{Red}}}$$
> where $R_{\text{NIR}}$ and $R_{\text{Red}}$ are reflectance values in the near-infrared (~840 nm) and red (~670 nm) bands.
>
> **Mechanism (1 mark):** Healthy chlorophyll strongly absorbs red light and the spongy mesophyll reflects NIR, giving NDVI values close to +1. Under stress (water, disease, nutrient deficiency), chlorophyll degrades, $R_{\text{Red}}$ rises, and NDVI drops below 0.4.
>
> **Drone workflow (1 mark):** The drone captures georeferenced multispectral images over the field; software computes pixel-wise NDVI, generating a false-color map where red/yellow zones indicate stressed patches requiring intervention.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three applications of drones in agriculture and briefly explain the role of LiDAR in precision farming.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Crop health monitoring** using multispectral imaging and NDVI (1 mark).
> 2. **Pesticide/fertilizer spraying** using variable-rate spray drones (1 mark).
> 3. **Field mapping and topographic survey** using LiDAR or photogrammetry (1 mark).
>
> **Role of LiDAR:** LiDAR emits laser pulses and measures return time to construct a high-density 3D point cloud of the terrain, providing Digital Elevation Models (DEM) with vertical accuracy of < 5 cm. This is essential for designing drainage in terrace farming (e.g., Wayanad cardamom plantations) and for canopy height measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module 4 Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for confusing NDVI formula signs (writing $\frac{R_{\text{Red}} - R_{\text{NIR}}}{...}$).
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for **not converting units** in GSD/coverage problems (cm vs m, ha vs m²).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not labelling arrows in flow diagrams.
> - Vague statements like "drones help farmers" get **zero marks** — always quantify (e.g., "reduces pesticide use by 30–50%").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**Explain in detail the role of UAVs in precision agriculture. Discuss crop monitoring, soil and field analysis, and spraying applications with suitable diagrams. Derive the formula for Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) of a drone camera.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the applications of UAVs in (i) crop monitoring and (ii) soil and field analysis. Include a description of the relevant sensors and vegetation indices used.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Crop Monitoring (3.5 marks):**
UAVs fitted with **multispectral cameras** (e.g., MicaSense RedEdge) capture images in five bands: Blue, Green, Red, Red-Edge, and NIR. These are processed using **Structure-from-Motion (SfM)** photogrammetry to generate an **orthomosaic**. From this, **NDVI** and **GNDVI** maps are computed to identify:
- Chlorophyll content (vigour)
- Water stress (using Red-Edge or thermal bands)
- Pest/disease hotspots (NDVI drop zones)

**(ii) Soil and Field Analysis (3.5 marks):**
- **Pre-sowing soil survey** — RGB + LiDAR generates high-resolution **Digital Terrain Model (DTM)** to plan row spacing, terrace alignment, and drainage.
- **Soil moisture mapping** — thermal infrared sensors capture surface temperature; wet soil stays cooler during daytime, producing differential thermal signatures.
- **Topography-based VRA (Variable Rate Application)** — Slopes and aspect maps derived from DEM determine where fertilizer is needed (e.g., leached slopes need more nitrogen).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe pesticide/fertilizer spraying using agricultural drones. Derive the formula for GSD and compute it for a camera with $p = 3.0 \,\mu\text{m}$, $f = 12 \,\text{mm}$, flying at $H = 80 \,\text{m}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Spraying Application (3 marks):**
Spray drones (e.g., DJI Agras T40) carry a 10–40 L tank and use **centrifugal or hydraulic nozzles** that produce droplets of VMD **100–300 µm**. They fly **1–3 m above the crop canopy** at **3–7 m/s** to ensure canopy penetration. **Variable-rate spraying** uses real-time NDVI data to modulate flow rate via **PWM-controlled solenoid valves** per nozzle.

**Derivation of GSD (2 marks):**
From similar-triangle camera geometry:
$$\frac{\text{GSD}}{p} = \frac{H}{f} \implies \text{GSD} = \frac{p \cdot H}{f}$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
Given $p = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}$, $f = 12 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}$, $H = 80 \,\text{m}$:
$$\text{GSD} = \dfrac{(3.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times 80}{0.012} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.012} = 0.02 \,\text{m/pixel} = 2 \,\text{cm/pixel}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**With neat diagrams, explain the components and workflow of an agricultural drone system. Discuss the significance of vegetation indices, and derive the coverage rate formula for a spray drone. A spray drone has swath width 8 m and flies at 6 m/s. Compute its coverage rate in hectares/hour.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw a block diagram of an agricultural drone system and explain the function of each block. Discuss the importance of NDVI and GNDVI in crop health assessment.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks)** — see SECTION 4.1; the student should redraw the key blocks:
- **Aerial Platform** (multirotor frame, motors, ESCs, battery)
- **Flight Controller** (Pixhawk) + GPS/IMU
- **Payload** (camera/sprayer)
- **GCS Link** (telemetry radio)
- **Ground Station Software** (Mission Planner / Pix4D)
- **Data Analytics Module** (NDVI processor)

**Function of each block (2 marks):** The platform provides lift; the flight controller maintains stability and executes waypoint navigation; the payload acquires data; the GCS link transmits telemetry; the ground station plans missions; the analytics module generates prescription maps.

**NDVI vs GNDVI (3 marks):**

$$
\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{Red}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{Red}}} \quad ; \quad \text{GNDVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{Green}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{Green}}}
$$

- NDVI saturates in dense canopies (values plateau at 0.8–0.9).
- GNDVI uses the green band, which penetrates deeper into the canopy, giving better discrimination in mature crops (e.g., paddy at booting stage).
- For **Kerala's paddy and coconut**, GNDVI is often preferred during the mid-growth stages.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the formula for the area coverage rate of a spray drone. Given $W_s = 8 \,\text{m}$, $V = 6 \,\text{m/s}$, compute the coverage rate in ha/hr.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

In time $T$, the drone covers a strip of length $L = V \cdot T$ and width $W_s$.

$$
A_{\text{pass}} = W_s \cdot V \cdot T \quad [\text{m}^2]
$$

Since 1 hectare = $10{,}000 \,\text{m}^2$ and 1 hour = 3600 s:

$$
R_c = \dfrac{W_s \cdot V \cdot 3600}{10{,}000} = 0.36 \cdot W_s \cdot V \quad [\text{ha/hr}]
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

$$
R_c = 0.36 \times 8 \times 6 = 0.36 \times 48 = 17.28 \,\text{ha/hr}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The drone can spray approximately **17 hectares per hour** — sufficient to cover a mid-sized Kerala paddy field (typical 5–20 ha) in under an hour. Battery swaps every 15–20 min must be factored into total mission time $T_f = A / R_c$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4: Agricultural Drone Applications**

- [x] **Three categories of agricultural drone applications:** Sensing/Mapping, Actuation, Decision Support.
- [x] **NDVI formula (must memorize):** $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{Red}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{Red}}}$
- [x] **GNDVI formula:** $\text{GNDVI} = \dfrac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{Green}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{Green}}}$
- [x] **GSD formula:** $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{p \cdot H}{f}$ — units: m/pixel.
- [x] **Coverage rate formula:** $R_c = 0.36 \cdot W_s \cdot V$ in ha/hr.
- [x] **Image overlap:** Front-lap 80%, Side-lap 70% for photogrammetric stitching.
- [x] **Typical flight altitude:** 50–120 m AGL for sensing; 1–3 m for spraying.
- [x] **Spray droplet VMD:** 100–300 µm (coarse — less drift, better canopy deposition).
- [x] **Spectral band uses:** Red = chlorophyll absorption, NIR = cell structure, Red-Edge = early stress, Thermal = water stress.
- [x] **Photogrammetry pipeline:** Images → SfM → Point Cloud → Orthomosaic + DSM → NDVI map.
- [x] **Variable Rate Application (VRA):** Adjusts spray rate per zone based on NDVI prescription map.
- [x] **Kerala-specific crops where drones are deployed:** Paddy, coconut, cardamom, tea, rubber, banana, pepper.
- [x] **Benefits to memorize for essays:** 30–50% pesticide reduction, 90% water saving in survey, 5× faster than manual scouting.
- [x] **Challenges to mention in disadvantages section:** Battery life, payload limit (< 25 kg for multirotors), regulatory DGCA No-Permission-No-Takeoff (NPNT), weather dependency, initial cost.
- [x] **Indian regulation context:** Drone Rules 2021 (replaced 2018 draft), Digital Sky Platform, NPNT compliance, airspace color codes (red/yellow/green zones).
- [x] **Examiner tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** for full-marks questions — it demonstrates systems-level understanding.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Don't write "UAV" and "drone" interchangeably in formal answers — KTU prefers **UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle)** as the technical term.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Cities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smart Cities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart City (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Smart City** is an urban ecosystem that integrates **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)**, **Internet of Things (IoT)** sensors, **Aerial Robotics (UAVs/UAS)**, and **data-driven intelligence** into the management of city infrastructure, transportation, public safety, utilities, and citizen services to improve operational efficiency, sustainability, and quality of life.

In the context of **Aerial Robotics (PERAT745)**, a smart city is the *operational theatre* where Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) act as **aerial data collectors, mobile sensor platforms, and autonomous logistics agents** integrated into a city-wide cyber-physical system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Components of a Smart City (KTU Perspective):**
> - **Smart Infrastructure** → IoT-enabled roads, bridges, buildings.
> - **Smart Transportation** → UAV-based traffic monitoring, last-mile delivery.
> - **Smart Energy Grid** → UAV inspection of power lines, solar farms.
> - **Smart Governance** → UAV surveillance, emergency response.
> - **Smart Environment** → Air quality monitoring using aerial swarms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🍎 The "Nervous System of a City" Analogy:**
> Imagine a human body. Your **nerves** sense pain, temperature, and movement. Your **brain** processes these signals, and your **hands and legs** act on them. Now think of a city:
> - The **UAVs/Drones** are the **eyes and ears** flying above, sensing traffic, pollution, and emergencies.
> - The **IoT Sensors** on the ground are the **nerve endings**, transmitting real-time data.
> - The **Cloud/Edge Servers** are the **brain**, processing the data using AI.
> - The **Response Teams / Automated Drones** are the **hands**, executing decisions like delivering medicine or rerouting traffic.
>
> A **Smart City** is essentially a city that *thinks, senses, and responds* — just like a living organism. Aerial Robotics gives this organism **3D situational awareness**, which ground-only systems cannot achieve.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Smart City Performance Indicators (KPIs):**
> - **Sustainability Index:** Target **30% reduction in CO₂ emissions** by 2030 (UN-Habitat).
> - **UAV Coverage Density:** Typically **1 drone per 1 km²** for urban monitoring.
> - **Response Time (UAV-mediated emergency):** **< 5 minutes** in dense urban zones.
> - **Data Throughput:** **4G/5G** enabled, with **latency < 20 ms** for control loops.
> - **Airspace Altitude Ceiling:** UAVs operate below **120 m (400 ft)** per DGCA (India) regulations.

## 1.4 Why Aerial Robotics is a Force Multiplier in Smart Cities

| Feature | Ground-Based Systems | Aerial Robotics (UAV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Field of View (FOV)** | Limited, occluded | 360° bird's-eye view |
| **Deployment Speed** | Slow (vehicle-based) | **Rapid (<60s launch)** |
| **Mobility** | 2D (constrained by roads) | **3D (free airspace)** |
| **Cost per Mission Hour** | $50–$200 | **$5–$30** |
| **Risk to Human Operatives** | High (in disasters) | **Zero (autonomous)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a UAV-Enabled Smart City

A smart city ecosystem with aerial robotics follows a **5-Layer Reference Architecture**:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensing)** → UAV-mounted LiDAR, RGB cameras, multispectral sensors, gas detectors.
2. **Communication Layer (Transmission)** → 4G/5G, LoRa, Wi-Fi 6, M2M (Machine-to-Machine) protocols.
3. **Data Processing Layer (Edge/Fog Computing)** → Onboard AI inference, real-time object detection.
4. **Decision Layer (Cloud Intelligence)** → Big-data analytics, predictive models, digital twin.
5. **Actuation Layer (Response)** → Autonomous UAV swarm re-tasking, alerts to emergency services.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Flow

- **Step 1: Mission Assignment** → A central Urban Air Mobility (UAM) controller assigns UAV tasks based on event triggers (e.g., traffic accident detected).
- **Step 2: Path Planning** → The UAV computes a 3D path avoiding **No-Fly Zones (NFZ)**, buildings, and other drones using algorithms like **A\***, **RRT\***, or **D\* Lite**.
- **Step 3: Take-off & Navigation** → Autonomous flight using **GPS + Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO)** fusion.
- **Step 4: Data Acquisition** → LiDAR/camera captures data; onboard processor performs **edge AI inference** (e.g., YOLOv8 for object detection).
- **Step 5: Transmission & Handoff** → Data streamed to edge server via 5G; hand-off to next drone in the swarm if out of range.
- **Step 6: Return-to-Home (RTH)** → UAV returns to a **droneport / vertiport** for automated battery swap or wireless charging.

## 2.3 Key UAV Applications in Smart Cities (KTU High-Yield)

- **🚓 Traffic Monitoring & Management:** Real-time congestion analysis using computer vision.
- **📦 Last-Mile Delivery:** Amazon Prime Air, Zipline medical delivery (range **> 80 km**, payload **> 2 kg**).
- **🚒 Disaster Response & Search-and-Rescue (SAR):** Thermal imaging in post-earthquake scenarios.
- **🌳 Environmental Monitoring:** Air Quality Index (AQI) mapping, noise pollution heat-maps.
- **🏗️ Infrastructure Inspection:** Bridge crack detection, high-rise façade audit using **Structure-from-Motion (SfM)**.
- **👮 Public Safety & Surveillance:** Crowd density estimation, perimeter security.
- **🌾 Precision Agriculture (peri-urban):** Crop health NDVI mapping.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU 2024 examination (Smart Cities & UAV integration):**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UAV Flight Endurance** | $T_{flight} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{hover} + P_{payload}}$ | Battery capacity divided by total power draw | minutes (min) |
| **Coverage Area per Hour** | $A_{cov} = v_{cr} \cdot w_{swath} \cdot T_{flight}$ | Speed × swath width × endurance | km²/hr |
| **Coverage Area (Circular)** | $A_{circ} = \pi \cdot R_{range}^{2}$ | Disk of radius $R_{range}$ | m² |
| **Number of Drones Required** | $N_{drone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{city}}{A_{cov} \cdot \eta_{eff}} \right\rceil$ | Ceiling of (city area / effective coverage) | integer |
| **Line-of-Sight (LoS) Range** | $d_{LoS} = \sqrt{2 \cdot R_e \cdot h_{tx}} + \sqrt{2 \cdot R_e \cdot h_{rx}}$ | Earth curvature-limited radio range | km |
| **Communication Latency (5G)** | $\tau_{5G} \leq 20$ ms | URLLC requirement | ms |
| **Swarm Separation (Safety)** | $d_{safe} \geq k \cdot v_{max}$ | $k$ = reaction time (typically 1 s) | m |
| **Energy per Mission** | $E_{mission} = \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt$ | Total energy consumed | Wh (Watt-hour) |
| **Data Generated per Flight** | $D_{gen} = R_{sensor} \cdot T_{flight}$ | Sensor bitrate × time | GB |
| **Payload Capacity Bound** | $m_{payload} \leq m_{TOW} - m_{frame} - m_{batt}$ | Max usable payload | kg |

**Legend:** $C_{batt}$ = Battery capacity (Ah), $V_{batt}$ = Battery voltage (V), $P_{hover}$ = Hover power (W), $v_{cr}$ = Cruise speed (m/s), $w_{swath}$ = Sensor swath width (m), $R_e$ = Earth radius ($\approx 6371$ km), $h_{tx}, h_{rx}$ = Antenna heights (m), $R_{range}$ = Operational radius (m), $\eta_{eff}$ = Operational efficiency (0.6–0.8).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, these formulas are used by:
- **Urban planners** to estimate the **fleet size** for city-wide drone coverage.
- **Telecom engineers** to position **5G base stations** ensuring UAV LoS connectivity.
- **Logistics companies** to compute **delivery SLA** and **battery swap frequency**.
- **Disaster managers** to deploy **SAR swarms** within the **golden hour** of rescue.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition and numerical evaluation step is explicitly written out below. No shortcuts, no truncation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Drones Required for Full City Coverage

**Problem Statement:** A smart city has a total area $A_{city} = 100$ km². Each UAV has a coverage swath $w_{swath} = 0.5$ km, cruise speed $v_{cr} = 60$ km/hr, endurance $T_{flight} = 30$ min, and operational efficiency $\eta_{eff} = 0.7$. Find the **minimum number of drones** required for full city coverage.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Express the coverage area per UAV per hour:
$$A_{cov} = v_{cr} \cdot w_{swath} \cdot T_{flight}$$

**Step 2:** Convert endurance to hours:
$$T_{flight} = 30 \text{ min} = \dfrac{30}{60} = 0.5 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:
$$A_{cov} = 60 \text{ km/hr} \cdot 0.5 \text{ km} \cdot 0.5 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 4:** Perform multiplication:
$$A_{cov} = 60 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 15 \text{ km}^2 \text{ per flight}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the efficiency factor (accounts for recharging, weather, transit):
$$A_{cov,eff} = A_{cov} \cdot \eta_{eff} = 15 \cdot 0.7 = 10.5 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the fleet-size formula:
$$N_{drone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{city}}{A_{cov,eff}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 7:** Substitute:
$$N_{drone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{100}{10.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.524 \rceil$$

**Step 8:** Apply ceiling function:
$$N_{drone} = 10 \text{ drones}$$

**✅ Final Answer:** A minimum of **10 UAVs** is required for 100% city coverage under the given parameters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Line-of-Sight (LoS) Range for UAV–5G Communication

**Problem Statement:** A UAV flying at altitude $h_{tx} = 200$ m communicates with a 5G tower at height $h_{rx} = 50$ m. Compute the maximum LoS distance, assuming a smooth-earth model with $R_e = 6371$ km.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the LoS formula:
$$d_{LoS} = \sqrt{2 \cdot R_e \cdot h_{tx}} + \sqrt{2 \cdot R_e \cdot h_{rx}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert altitudes to kilometers for unit consistency:
$$h_{tx} = 0.2 \text{ km}, \quad h_{rx} = 0.05 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the first term — UAV-to-horizon:
$$d_1 = \sqrt{2 \cdot 6371 \cdot 0.2} = \sqrt{2548.4}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the square root:
$$d_1 \approx 50.48 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the second term — tower-to-horizon:
$$d_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot 6371 \cdot 0.05} = \sqrt{637.1}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the square root:
$$d_2 \approx 25.24 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7:** Sum the two distances:
$$d_{LoS} = d_1 + d_2 = 50.48 + 25.24 = 75.72 \text{ km}$$

**✅ Final Answer:** The maximum line-of-sight communication range is approximately **$75.72$ km**. In practice, urban obstructions (buildings) reduce this to **10–15 km** in dense smart-city environments.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: UAV Flight Endurance Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A quadrotor uses a **6S 10,000 mAh LiPo battery** ($V_{batt} = 22.2$ V) with hover power $P_{hover} = 350$ W and payload power $P_{payload} = 50$ W. Compute the flight endurance.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Express the endurance formula:
$$T_{flight} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{hover} + P_{payload}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert battery capacity to Ah:
$$C_{batt} = 10{,}000 \text{ mAh} = 10 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 3:** Compute total power draw:
$$P_{total} = 350 + 50 = 400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Compute total battery energy:
$$E_{batt} = C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt} = 10 \cdot 22.2 = 222 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 5:** Compute endurance in hours:
$$T_{flight} = \dfrac{222 \text{ Wh}}{400 \text{ W}} = 0.555 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to minutes:
$$T_{flight} = 0.555 \cdot 60 = 33.3 \text{ min}$$

**✅ Final Answer:** The UAV can fly for **~33 minutes** on a full charge. For 24×7 city operations, **3 battery cycles/day per drone** are required, with automated **battery-swap droneports**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Smart City UAV Use-Case Comparison

| **Use Case** | **Altitude (m)** | **Payload (kg)** | **Endurance (min)** | **Sensors** | **Regulatory Class** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Traffic Monitoring | 80–120 | 0.5 | 25–30 | RGB, IR | Micro/ Mini UAV |
| Last-Mile Delivery | 60–100 | 2–5 | 20–35 | GPS, Camera | Small UAV |
| Disaster SAR | 50–150 | 1–3 | 30–45 | Thermal, LiDAR | Small UAV |
| Infrastructure Inspection | 30–80 | 1–2 | 20–30 | LiDAR, Hyperspectral | Small UAV |
| Air Quality Mapping | 100–200 | 0.5–1 | 30–40 | Gas sensors, PM sensor | Mini UAV |
| Perimeter Security | 60–150 | 1 | 40–60 | Thermal, RGB + PTZ | Mini UAV |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Swarm Separation Distance for Collision Avoidance

**Step 1:** State the safety separation rule:
$$d_{safe} \geq k_{react} \cdot v_{max}$$

**Step 2:** Define variables — $k_{react}$ = reaction time, $v_{max}$ = maximum cruise speed.

**Step 3:** Assume $k_{react} = 1$ s and $v_{max} = 20$ m/s:
$$d_{safe} \geq 1 \cdot 20 = 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** In dense urban swarms, a **3σ safety buffer** is added:
$$d_{safe,final} = 3 \cdot d_{safe} = 60 \text{ m}$$

**✅ Interpretation:** Each UAV in a smart-city swarm must maintain a **minimum 60 m bubble** in 3D airspace, orchestrated by a **UTM (UAS Traffic Management)** system.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT745]**
**Define a Smart City. List any FOUR roles of aerial robotics in building a smart city.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Smart City** is an urban area that uses ICT, IoT, and data analytics to enhance the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of city services. (1 mark)
>
> **Four roles of aerial robotics:**
> 1. **Traffic monitoring & congestion analysis** using real-time aerial video. (0.5 mark)
> 2. **Disaster response and search-and-rescue** with thermal imaging. (0.5 mark)
> 3. **Last-mile delivery** of medical supplies and packages. (0.5 mark)
> 4. **Infrastructure inspection** of bridges, buildings, and power lines. (0.5 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT745]**
**What is UTM? Why is it essential in UAV-based smart city operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **UTM (UAS Traffic Management)** is a digital ecosystem that manages low-altitude airspace for safe, efficient, and scalable UAV operations. (1 mark)
>
> **Why essential:**
> 1. **Deconfliction** — prevents mid-air collisions in dense UAV swarms. (1 mark)
> 2. **Airspace authorization** — dynamic No-Fly Zone (NFZ) management. (0.5 mark)
> 3. **Regulatory compliance** — ensures DGCA/ICAO airspace rules are followed. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT745]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **layered architecture of a UAV-enabled smart city**. Draw a neat block diagram and describe the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A UAV-enabled smart city architecture is organized into **five functional layers**, each with a distinct role:

1. **Perception Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Comprises UAV-mounted **LiDAR, RGB cameras, thermal sensors, gas detectors, and multispectral cameras**.
   - Performs **raw data acquisition** — point clouds, videos, gas concentrations.
   - Example: A traffic-monitoring UAV streams 4K video at 30 fps.

2. **Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Uses **5G, 4G LTE, LoRa, Wi-Fi 6, or satellite** for UAV–cloud data exchange.
   - Must guarantee **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)** for control loops.
   - Latency target: **< 20 ms** in 5G networks.

3. **Data Processing Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Performs **onboard edge AI inference** (e.g., YOLOv8 for object detection, CNN for crack segmentation).
   - Filters noise and **pre-aggregates** data before transmission.
   - Reduces bandwidth by **> 60%** through edge analytics.

4. **Decision Layer (1 mark):**
   - Cloud-based **digital twin** and **predictive analytics** for city-wide planning.
   - Example: Predicts traffic jam 30 min in advance using LSTM models.

5. **Actuation Layer (1.5 marks):**
   - Triggers **autonomous UAV re-tasking**, alerts emergency services, or dispatches delivery drones.
   - Closes the **sense–decide–act loop** in real time.

**✅ Block Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1.

---

**(b)** A quadrotor UAV with **hover power 400 W**, **payload power 50 W**, and a **22.2 V / 10 Ah LiPo battery** is used for a smart-city surveillance mission.
(i) Compute its **flight endurance** in minutes. **(3 marks)**
(ii) If the cruise speed is **15 m/s** and the camera swath is **200 m**, compute the **area covered per flight**. **(4 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Flight Endurance (3 marks):**

$$T_{flight} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{hover} + P_{payload}} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 22.2}{400 + 50} = \dfrac{222}{450}$$

$$T_{flight} = 0.4933 \text{ hr} = 0.4933 \cdot 60 = 29.6 \text{ min}$$

**✅ Endurance ≈ 29.6 minutes.** (3 marks)

**(ii) Coverage Area per Flight (4 marks):**

$$A_{cov} = v_{cr} \cdot w_{swath} \cdot T_{flight}$$

**Step 1:** Convert endurance to seconds: $T_{flight} = 29.6 \cdot 60 = 1776$ s.

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$A_{cov} = 15 \text{ m/s} \cdot 200 \text{ m} \cdot 1776 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$A_{cov} = 5{,}328{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 5.328 \text{ km}^2$$

**✅ Coverage Area ≈ 5.33 km² per flight.** (4 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT745]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **UAVs in smart-city traffic management and disaster response**, with real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Traffic Management (3.5 marks):**
- UAVs provide **bird's-eye, real-time video** of intersections and arterial roads.
- **Computer vision algorithms** (YOLO, DeepSORT) detect **vehicle counts, average speed, and congestion levels**.
- Data is fed to **Adaptive Traffic Signal Control Systems (ATSCS)** to dynamically adjust green-light durations.
- **Example:** Hangzhou, China, uses UAV-based traffic monitoring on highways to reduce congestion by **15%**.
- UAVs also detect **illegal parking, accidents, and pedestrian violations** in real time.

**Disaster Response (3.5 marks):**
- In **post-earthquake or flood scenarios**, UAVs deploy within **minutes**, faster than ground teams.
- Equipped with **thermal cameras**, they detect **trapped survivors** through debris.
- **LiDAR payloads** create **3D maps of collapsed buildings** for first responders.
- **Example:** After the 2023 Turkey earthquake, UAV swarms mapped **> 50 km²** of rubble in 24 hours.
- UAVs also **deliver medical supplies** (insulin, blood) in cut-off zones — e.g., **Zipline drones in Rwanda** delivered **> 200,000 units** of blood by 2023.
- Integration with the city's **Emergency Operations Center (EOC)** enables **coordinated, multi-drone SAR missions**.

---

**(b)** Compute the **minimum number of drones** required for continuous monitoring of a **50 km²** smart-city zone, given that each UAV has an effective coverage of **2.5 km² per flight** and an **efficiency factor of 0.6**. Also explain what a **droneport** is and its role. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Effective coverage per drone per flight:
$$A_{cov,eff} = 2.5 \cdot 0.6 = 1.5 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the fleet-size formula:
$$N_{drone} = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{city}}{A_{cov,eff}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{1.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 33.33 \rceil$$

**Step 3:** Apply ceiling:
$$N_{drone} = 34 \text{ drones}$$

**✅ Minimum number of drones required = 34.** (4 marks)

**Droneport — Definition and Role (3 marks):**
- A **droneport (or vertiport)** is a **dedicated ground infrastructure** where UAVs **take off, land, recharge, swap batteries, and upload/download payloads**.
- It acts as the **"gas station" of the urban air mobility network**.
- Modern droneports feature **automated battery-swap in < 90 seconds**, weather shielding, and **secure command-and-control links**.
- For continuous 24×7 monitoring, droneports enable **seamless fleet rotation**, allowing a returning UAV to immediately hand off its mission to a freshly charged replacement.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where students lose marks in Smart City / Aerial Robotics questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always write `km`, `m`, `Wh`, `min` explicitly. The examiner allocates 0.5–1 mark for correct units.
> 2. **Confusing UTM with ATC** — ATC manages manned aircraft (above 500 ft); UTM manages UAVs (below 400 ft / 120 m). Mixing them up costs full marks on UTM questions.
> 3. **Not mentioning the efficiency factor (η)** — In real-world coverage problems, ignoring battery-swap and weather time leads to an **over-estimation of coverage** and a wrong fleet-size answer.
> 4. **Skipping the digital twin layer** — Many students forget to include the **decision/analytics layer** when drawing the architecture diagram. Always include all 5 layers.
> 5. **Omitting regulatory context** — In India, DGCA mandates **120 m altitude ceiling**, **Visual Line of Sight (VLOS)** unless BVLOS is approved, and **no flights over people/night without permits**. Failing to cite this loses marks on regulatory sub-questions.
> 6. **Wrong formula for LoS** — Mixing up **flat-earth** and **curved-earth** LoS models. Use $d_{LoS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h}$ per endpoint.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Smart Cities & Aerial Robotics:**

- **Definition:** Smart City = ICT + IoT + Aerial Robotics + Data Intelligence for urban management.
- **Aerial Robotics enables 3D situational awareness** that ground systems cannot match.
- **5-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Communication → Processing → Decision → Actuation.
- **Standard Altitude Ceiling:** **120 m (400 ft)** under DGCA rules.
- **Communication:** 5G URLLC is the backbone, with latency **< 20 ms**.
- **Key Performance Metrics:** Flight endurance (T), coverage area (A), fleet size (N), LoS range (d), safety separation (d_safe).
- **Endurance formula:** $T_{flight} = C_{batt} V_{batt} / P_{total}$ — always in **minutes** for exam.
- **Coverage formula:** $A_{cov} = v_{cr} \cdot w_{swath} \cdot T_{flight}$ — watch the **unit consistency** (m/s × m × s = m²).
- **Fleet size:** $N_{drone} = \lceil A_{city} / (A_{cov} \cdot \eta) \rceil$ — always use **ceiling function**.
- **LoS formula:** $d_{LoS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h_{tx}} + \sqrt{2 R_e h_{rx}}$ — with $R_e = 6371$ km.
- **UTM** = UAS Traffic Management = mandatory for safe multi-drone urban operations.
- **Droneport** = automated UAV home for charging, battery swap, and payload handling.
- **Major Applications:** Traffic, SAR, delivery, inspection, environment, security.
- **Real-world examples:** Zipline (Rwanda), Amazon Prime Air, Hangzhou traffic drones, Turkey earthquake SAR swarms.
- **Challenges to mention in exams:** Airspace congestion, privacy laws, BVLOS regulations, cybersecurity, weather resilience, public acceptance.
- **KPIs to memorize:** $T_{flight} \approx 30$ min, $v_{cr} \approx 10$–$20$ m/s, $\eta_{eff} \approx 0.6$–$0.8$, $A_{cov} \approx 1$–$10$ km² per flight.
- **Regulatory bodies (India):** DGCA — Digital Sky Platform, NPNT (No Permission No Takeoff) protocol.
- **Keyword triggers for full marks:** *Digital Twin*, *Edge AI*, *Swarm Intelligence*, *URLLC*, *NPNT*, *BVLOS*, *Vertiport/Droneport*, *Urban Air Mobility (UAM)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📦 Delivery Drones — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delivery Drones** are *Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)* specifically engineered, configured, and integrated with mission-critical subsystems (cargo bay, winch/drop mechanism, payload securing system, autonomous navigation stack, and Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) communication) to perform the **transportation of physical goods** from a dispatch node to a designated recipient node, optimizing the *last-mile delivery* supply chain segment.

In the KTU **Aerial Robotics** framework, delivery drones represent the **convergence of aerospace engineering, mechatronics, control theory, and logistics optimization** — a fully autonomous cyber-physical system executing a flight plan under the *sense–plan–act* paradigm.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Delivery Drone as a "Flying Letter Box on Autopilot."**
> Imagine a small, self-piloting helicopter that:
> - Receives an instruction: *"Take this 2 kg parcel from Point A to Point B (5 km away)"*
> - Computes its own flight path
> - Avoids trees, buildings, birds, and other aircraft
> - Delivers the package (either by hovering-low-release, winch-lowering, or full landing)
> - Returns to base — **all without a human pilot in the loop**.

Just as a courier on a motorcycle navigates traffic using maps and signals, a delivery drone navigates airspace using **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) waypoints, inertial sensors, and onboard obstacle avoidance algorithms**.

---

## 📋 Classification of Delivery Drones (KTU Standard)

| Configuration | Typical Payload | Range | Use Case |
|---------------|----------------|-------|----------|
| **Multi-rotor (Quad/Hexa/Octa)** | $0.5 - 5$ kg | $5 - 15$ km | Urban last-mile |
| **Fixed-wing** | $5 - 25$ kg | $50 - 150$ km | Rural/inter-city |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | $2 - 10$ kg | $20 - 80$ km | Versatile BVLOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlighted Metric:** The global delivery drone market is projected to cross **USD 8 billion by 2030**, with **Zipline, Wing (Alphabet), Amazon Prime Air, Matternet, and DJI FlyCart** being the industry leaders. The **DGCA (India)** released the *Drone Rules 2021* and *Digital Sky Platform* to formalize BVLOS operations.

---

## 🏗️ Core Subsystems of a Delivery Drone

1. **Airframe & Propulsion** — Multirotor/fixed-wing frame with BLDC motors and propellers.
2. **Avionics Stack** — Flight controller (Pixhawk, Cube Orange), GPS, IMU, barometer, magnetometer.
3. **Cargo Mechanism** — Winch system, parcel release servo, magnetic lock, or VTOL landing skid.
4. **Power Subsystem** — Li-Po/Li-ion battery pack with Battery Management System (BMS).
5. **Communication Link** — 4G/5G telemetry, RC backup, command & control (C2) link.
6. **Sense-and-Avoid Suite** — LiDAR, mmWave radar, optical flow, computer vision.
7. **Mission Computer** — Onboard companion computer (NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi 4) running ROS 2 / PX4.

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The autonomous delivery mission lifecycle (aligned with PX4-ROS 2 mission framework) executes in **six distinct phases**:

### **Phase 1 — Mission Initialization & Pre-Flight Check**
- Operator uploads waypoints via Ground Control Station (GCS).
- Drone performs **system health check**: battery voltage, GPS lock ($> 10$ satellites), sensor calibration, motor continuity.
- Pre-arm safety: propellers disengage until ARM command.

### **Phase 2 — Takeoff & Climb to Cruise Altitude**
- Multirotor transitions vertically to **cruise altitude** (typically **$80 - 120$ m AGL** to remain above obstacles yet below manned aircraft).
- Hybrid VTOL uses multirotor mode for takeoff, then rotates nacelles to fixed-wing cruise.

### **Phase 3 — Forward Transit (Payload-Bearing Cruise)**
- Multirotor tilts to **multi-rotor forward flight** (limited to $\sim 60$ km/h).
- Fixed-wing achieves $\sim 100 - 150$ km/h cruise (energy-efficient).
- Controller maintains altitude via **PID cascade** (outer position $\rightarrow$ inner velocity $\rightarrow$ attitude $\rightarrow$ rate).

### **Phase 4 — Terminal Approach & Delivery**
- Drone descends to delivery altitude ($2 - 10$ m).
- Three delivery strategies:
   1. **Hover-and-Release** — Parachute or low-impact drop.
   2. **Winch Lowering** — Package lowered on a tether (preserves drone hover stability).
   3. **Full Landing** — Drone touches down, opens cargo bay, then relaunches.

### **Phase 5 — Return-to-Home (RTH)**
- Autonomous reverse waypoint traversal.
- Triggered automatically if:
   - Battery falls below **$20\%$ State of Charge (SoC)**.
   - GPS signal lost for $> 10$ s.
   - Communication link severed.

### **Phase 6 — Recovery & Post-Flight Analysis**
- Battery telemetry logged.
- Flight data (black box) analyzed for next mission optimization.

---

## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** from first principles of rotorcraft aerodynamics and energy conservation.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Hover Power (Ideal) | $P_{hover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(m_{total} \cdot g)^3}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}$ | W | Momentum theory |
| 2 | Hover Power (Induced + Profile) | $P_{hover} = T \cdot v_i + P_{profile}$ | W | $v_i$ = induced velocity |
| 3 | Endurance | $E = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | s (or h) | $C_{bat}$ in Ah, $V_{bat}$ in V |
| 4 | Range | $R = v_{cruise} \cdot E$ | m | For fixed-wing cruise |
| 5 | Power Loading | $PL = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{P_{total}}$ | N/W | Efficiency indicator |
| 6 | Disc Loading | $DL = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{n \cdot A_{prop}}$ | N/m² | $n$ = number of rotors |
| 7 | Energy per Delivery | $E_{delivery} = P_{avg} \cdot t_{mission}$ | Wh | Mission energy |
| 8 | Effective Payload Fraction | $\mu_{eff} = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{total}}$ | dimensionless | $\mu_{eff} < 0.5$ typical |
| 9 | Battery DoD (Depth of Discharge) | $DoD = 1 - SoC$ | dimensionless | Max safe $DoD = 0.8$ for Li-Po |
| 10 | Mission Time | $t_{mission} = t_{climb} + t_{cruise} + t_{descend} + t_{delivery} + t_{return}$ | s | Sum of all phases |

> **Constants Reference (Kerala Exam-Ready):**
> - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea-level, ISA)
> - $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.85$, $\eta_{ESC} \approx 0.95$, $\eta_{prop} \approx 0.75$

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

Delivery drones solve the **"last-mile problem"** in logistics — the most expensive and least efficient segment of the supply chain. Applications include:
- **Medical supply delivery** (Zipline in Rwanda/Ghana — blood & vaccines to remote clinics).
- **E-commerce** (Amazon Prime Air, Wing's partnership with Walmart).
- **Disaster relief** (post-flood/tsunami in Kerala, drone corridors for medicine).
- **Agriculture** (seed/pesticide delivery to terraced farms in Wayanad/Munnar).
- **Inspection logistics** (delivering tools to wind turbine technicians mid-tower).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The *last-mile delivery* accounts for **41% of total supply chain cost**. Drones can reduce this by **$40 - 70\%$** for sub-urban/rural routes.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📊 Derivation 1: Hover Power via Momentum Theory

We derive the **ideal power required to hover** for a multirotor delivery drone carrying a payload. This is the foundation of all energy-budget calculations in delivery drone mission planning.

### **Given**
- Total mass $m_{total} = m_{frame} + m_{payload} + m_{battery}$
- Number of rotors $n$
- Propeller disc area $A_{prop} = \pi r_{prop}^{2}$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### **Step 1: Total Thrust Required**

For a delivery drone to hover, total thrust must equal weight:

$$
T_{total} = m_{total} \cdot g
$$

### **Step 2: Thrust Per Rotor**

Assuming equal loading across $n$ rotors:

$$
T_{rotor} = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{n}
$$

### **Step 3: Induced Velocity at Hover Disc**

From **momentum theory** (Rankine-Froude), the air velocity induced through a propeller disc creating thrust $T$ is:

$$
v_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{rotor}}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}
$$

### **Step 4: Ideal Power Per Rotor**

Power is thrust times induced velocity (kinetic energy rate imparted to air):

$$
P_{rotor,ideal} = T_{rotor} \cdot v_i = T_{rotor} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{rotor}}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}
$$

### **Step 5: Total Hover Power**

$$
P_{hover,ideal} = n \cdot T_{rotor} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{rotor}}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}
$$

Substituting $T_{rotor} = \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{n}$:

$$
P_{hover,ideal} = n \cdot \dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{n} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{2 \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}
$$

$$
P_{hover,ideal} = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{total} \cdot g}{2 \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hover,ideal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(m_{total} \cdot g)^{3}}{2 \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop}}}}
$$

This is the **classic KTU-exam-ready** hover power formula.

---

## 📊 Derivation 2: Mission Endurance from Battery Capacity

### **Given**
- Battery capacity $C_{bat}$ in Ah (Ampere-hours)
- Battery nominal voltage $V_{bat}$ in V
- Average mission power $P_{avg}$ in W
- Maximum safe Depth of Discharge $DoD_{max} = 0.8$

### **Step 1: Total Battery Energy**

Energy stored in a Li-Po battery pack:

$$
E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \cdot 3600 \quad \text{[Joules]}
$$

Or in Watt-hours:

$$
E_{bat,Wh} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \quad \text{[Wh]}
$$

### **Step 2: Usable Energy**

Applying the $80\%$ DoD safety margin (prevents over-discharge, extends cycle life):

$$
E_{usable} = DoD_{max} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat}
$$

### **Step 3: Endurance (Flight Time)**

Endurance is usable energy divided by average power draw:

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{DoD_{max} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat}}{P_{avg}}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
\boxed{t_{flight} = \dfrac{60 \cdot DoD_{max} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat}}{P_{avg}} \quad \text{[minutes]}}
$$

---

## 📊 Derivation 3: Effective Range of a Delivery Drone

### **Step 1: Cruise Speed for Multirotor**

Multirotor forward flight speed is empirically limited by **blade tip speed**:

$$
v_{cruise,max} = k_{tip} \cdot v_{tip}
$$

where $k_{tip} \approx 0.3$ to $0.4$ (advance ratio limit) and $v_{tip} = \omega \cdot r_{prop}$.

### **Step 2: Power in Forward Flight**

For multirotor forward flight, power increases moderately:

$$
P_{forward} \approx P_{hover} \cdot \left(1 + k_f \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{v_{tip}^{2}}\right)
$$

where $k_f \approx 0.4 - 0.6$ (empirical).

### **Step 3: Total Range**

$$
\boxed{R = v_{cruise} \cdot t_{flight} = v_{cruise} \cdot \dfrac{DoD_{max} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat}}{P_{forward}}}
$$

---

## 📊 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A delivery drone has the following specifications:
> - $m_{frame} = 4$ kg, $m_{payload} = 2$ kg, $m_{battery} = 2$ kg
> - $n = 4$ rotors, $r_{prop} = 0.25$ m
> - Battery: $6S \, 10000$ mAh ($V_{bat} = 22.2$ V)
> - Average cruise power $P_{avg} = 500$ W, $DoD_{max} = 0.8$
>
> **Find:** (a) Hover power, (b) Endurance, (c) Maximum range at $v_{cruise} = 15$ m/s.

### **Solution:**

**(a) Hover Power:**

$$
m_{total} = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
A_{prop} = \pi \cdot (0.25)^{2} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{hover,ideal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(8 \cdot 9.81)^{3}}{2 \cdot 4 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.1963}}
$$

$$
m_{total} \cdot g = 78.48 \text{ N}
$$

$$
(m_{total} \cdot g)^{3} = (78.48)^{3} = 4.832 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}^{3}
$$

$$
2 \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{prop} = 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.1963 = 1.923
$$

$$
P_{hover,ideal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.832 \times 10^{5}}{1.923}} = \sqrt{2.513 \times 10^{5}} \approx 501 \text{ W}
$$

**(b) Endurance:**

$$
E_{bat,Wh} = 22.2 \cdot 10 = 222 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 22.2 \cdot 10}{500} = \dfrac{177.6}{500} = 0.3552 \text{ h} = 21.3 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(c) Range:**

$$
R = 15 \cdot 0.3552 \cdot 3600 = 19{,}176 \text{ m} \approx 19.2 \text{ km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **$1.5 \times$ factor** between hover power ($501$ W ideal) and actual flight power ($\sim 750$ W real) due to motor/ESC/propeller inefficiencies and parasitic drag. Use a **Figure of Merit $FM \approx 0.6 - 0.7$** in real calculations.

---

## 📊 Payload–Range Trade-Off (Analytical Matrix)

| Payload (kg) | Total Mass (kg) | Hover Power (W) | Endurance (min) | Range (km) |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| $0.5$ | $6.5$ | $382$ | $27.9$ | $25.1$ |
| $1.0$ | $7.0$ | $421$ | $25.3$ | $22.8$ |
| $2.0$ | $8.0$ | $501$ | $21.3$ | $19.2$ |
| $3.0$ | $9.0$ | $586$ | $18.2$ | $16.4$ |
| $5.0$ | $11.0$ | $770$ | $13.8$ | $12.5$ |

> **Key Takeaway:** Range scales **inversely with cube root of mass** — doubling payload reduces range by only $\sim 26\%$, not $50\%$.

## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Choose this OR Question B)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **major subsystems of an autonomous delivery drone** and describe the function of each. Highlight how the **sense-and-avoid module** contributes to safe BVLOS operation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A multirotor delivery drone has the following parameters: total mass $m = 10$ kg, $n = 6$ rotors, propeller radius $r = 0.2$ m, battery $V_{bat} = 25.2$ V, $C_{bat} = 16$ Ah, $DoD = 0.8$, average cruise power $P_{avg} = 700$ W, $v_{cruise} = 18$ m/s. Compute:
   - (i) Ideal hover power
   - (ii) Flight endurance in minutes
   - (iii) Total range in km *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The subsystems of a delivery drone are:

1. **Airframe & Propulsion (2 marks):** Carbon-fiber hexacopter frame with $6$ BLDC motors ($920$ KV), $13$-inch propellers, ESCs rated $40$ A continuous. Provides lift and structural mounting for all other subsystems.

2. **Avionics Stack (2 marks):** Pixhawk Cube Orange flight controller, HERE 3+ GPS (RTK-capable for cm-level accuracy), IMU (3-axis gyro + accelerometer), barometer (altitude), magnetometer (heading). Runs PX4 autopilot firmware with mission mode.

3. **Cargo Delivery Mechanism (1.5 marks):** Electromagnetic parcel lock with servo-actuated release. A winch can lower packages on a $5$ m tether for ground delivery without full landing.

4. **Sense-and-Avoid Module (1.5 marks):** 360° 2D LiDAR (e.g., RPLIDAR A3) + downward mmWave radar (TI IWR6843) for obstacle detection up to $40$ m. Software uses **VOXL ESC** obstacle-avoidance stack running on **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano**, executing **DWA (Dynamic Window Approach)** for real-time path re-planning.

> **Sense-and-Avoid Contribution:** Enables **safe BVLOS operations** as mandated by DGCA. Detects static obstacles (buildings, trees) and dynamic threats (birds, other drones), triggering **reactive avoidance maneuvers** within $< 100$ ms response time, ensuring regulatory compliance and mission safety.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Ideal Hover Power:**

$$
A_{prop} = \pi (0.2)^{2} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
m_{total} \cdot g = 10 \cdot 9.81 = 98.1 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_{hover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(98.1)^{3}}{2 \cdot 6 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.1257}}
$$

$$
(98.1)^{3} = 9.442 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.847
$$

$$
P_{hover} = \sqrt{5.112 \times 10^{5}} = 715.0 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Endurance:**

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 25.2 \cdot 16}{700} = \dfrac{19{,}353.6}{700}
$$

$$
t_{flight} = 27.65 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Range:**

$$
R = v_{cruise} \cdot t_{flight} = 18 \cdot (27.65 \cdot 60) = 18 \cdot 1659 = 29{,}862 \text{ m} \approx 29.9 \text{ km}
$$

> **Verification:** Since $P_{hover} \approx P_{avg}$, the drone is operating near optimal efficiency. Real-world range will be $\sim 22$ km after accounting for $30\%$ system losses.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **regulatory framework for delivery drones in India** under the *Drone Rules 2021* and the role of the **Digital Sky Platform**. Mention **UIN, UAOP, and PLIA** in your answer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **three primary delivery mechanisms** (hover-release, winch lowering, and VTOL landing) with their respective **trade-offs** in terms of energy consumption, accuracy, and parcel safety. Use suitable equations to compute the **extra energy required to hover for an additional 30 seconds** at the delivery point, given $P_{hover} = 600$ W. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The **Drone Rules 2021** (notified by the Ministry of Civil Aviation, GoI on 25 Aug 2021) liberalized the Indian drone ecosystem. Key points:

- **UIN (Unique Identification Number):** Every drone must be registered on the **Digital Sky Platform** to receive a UIN, akin to a license plate. Issued for a fee of ₹100 (small) to ₹25,000 (large category).

- **UAOP (Unmanned Aircraft Operator Permit):** Required for **commercial operations** including delivery. Valid for $5$ years. Mandatory for drones $> 2$ kg or operating BVLOS.

- **PLIA (Pilot License for Remote Pilots):** Required for any commercial drone operation. Candidates must be $\geq 18$ years, Class X passed, and clear a DGCA-approved training program from an **RPTO (Remote Pilot Training Organisation)**.

- **Digital Sky Platform:** A unified online portal for:
   - Drone registration
   - Airspace management (red/yellow/green zones)
   - Real-time flight permissions via **NPNT (No Permission No Take-off)** protocol
   - Pilot verification

- **Airspace Categorization:**
   - **Red Zone:** Restricted (near airports, military bases). Permission required.
   - **Yellow Zone:** Controlled airspace.
   - **Green Zone:** Permitted up to $400$ ft AGL (Above Ground Level) without prior approval.

> **Current Status (2024-2026):** DGCA has been conducting **BVLOS drone trials** since 2021. Companies like **Anra Tech, Skye Air, Marut Drones, and Throttle Aerospace** are actively testing medicine/e-commerce delivery corridors in Telangana, Karnataka, and Delhi NCR.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

| Mechanism | Energy Cost | Accuracy | Parcel Safety |
|-----------|-------------|----------|---------------|
| **Hover-Release** | Low (parachute drop) | $\pm 2$ m GPS error | Moderate (impact damage) |
| **Winch Lowering** | Medium (hover held) | $\pm 0.5$ m | High (controlled descent) |
| **VTOL Landing** | High (full descent + relaunch) | $\pm 0.1$ m | Highest (no drop) |

**Energy for Extra 30-Second Hover:**

$$
E_{extra} = P_{hover} \cdot \Delta t = 600 \text{ W} \cdot 30 \text{ s} = 18{,}000 \text{ J} = 5 \text{ Wh}
$$

In terms of battery percentage for a $222$ Wh pack:

$$
\% \text{ extra battery} = \dfrac{5}{222} \times 100 \approx 2.25\%
$$

> **Trade-Off Insight:** The winch system is the **most balanced**, consuming only $5$ Wh extra (versus $\sim 30$ Wh for a full landing cycle) while providing sub-meter delivery accuracy — preferred for fragile medical supplies.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Hover Power formulas** — Using $P = T \cdot v$ without the induced velocity square root is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the Depth of Discharge (DoD) factor** — Always multiply battery energy by $0.8$ for Li-Po. Skipping this loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing disc area and blade area** — Use $A = \pi r^{2}$ (full disc), not blade swept area.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert Wh to Joules** when units are required in SI — $1$ Wh $= 3600$ J.
> 5. **Listing Zipline / Wing as startups** — They are **commercial operators**; stating "Google" instead of "Alphabet's Wing" loses partial credit.
> 6. **Ignoring the no-payload range vs. payload range distinction** — Examiners often ask this. Always compute the **effective range** with payload mass included.
> 7. **Omitting regulatory abbreviations (UIN, UAOP, PLIA, NPNT)** in the India-specific regulation question — these are **must-mention** terms.
> 8. **Using the disc loading formula incorrectly** — Number of rotors $n$ must appear in the denominator.
