## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Top 12 Rapid-Revision Points for Delivery Drones:**
>
> 1. **Definition:** UAV with payload bay + autonomous navigation for goods transport.
> 2. **Three types:** Multi-rotor (urban), Fixed-wing (rural), Hybrid VTOL (versatile).
> 3. **Hover Power (Ideal):** $P_{hover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(mg)^{3}}{2 n \rho A}}$ — must know.
> 4. **Endurance Formula:** $t = \dfrac{60 \cdot DoD \cdot V \cdot C}{P_{avg}}$ minutes.
> 5. **Range Formula:** $R = v_{cruise} \cdot t_{flight}$.
> 6. **Li-Po Battery:** Always use $DoD = 0.8$ for safe energy budgeting.
> 7. **Cruise Altitude:** $80 - 120$ m AGL for urban deliveries (above obstacles, below manned traffic).
> 8. **Three Delivery Methods:** Hover-drop, Winch (most efficient tradeoff), Full-landing.
> 9. **India Regulations:** UIN + UAOP + PLIA mandatory for commercial BVLOS delivery.
> 10. **Digital Sky Platform:** Online portal for NPNT (No Permission No Take-off) compliance.
> 11. **Key Operators:** Zipline (medical), Wing (retail), Amazon Prime Air, Matternet, DJI FlyCart.
> 12. **Last-Mile Cost Reduction:** Drones cut last-mile cost by $40 - 70\%$ for sub-urban routes.

> **🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme High-Probability Questions:**
> - Derive hover power for a given drone configuration (7 marks).
> - Compare multirotor vs. fixed-wing with numerical example (7 marks).
> - Explain the Indian regulatory framework with all abbreviations (7 marks).
> - Sketch and explain the autonomous mission state machine (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Care
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Health Care

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aerial Robotics in Health Care** is the interdisciplinary domain that employs **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) / Multirotor / Fixed-wing / Hybrid VTOL systems**, integrated with autonomous navigation, payload management, and telemetry subsystems, to perform clinical, logistical, surveillance, and emergency-response functions within public-health and medical-service ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):**
> The course outcomes (COs) for this segment mandate that students be able to *identify, classify, and critically evaluate* the role of aerial robots in healthcare delivery — including **logistics (blood/vaccine transport)**, **emergency response (AED delivery, organ transport)**, **telemedicine relay**, **disaster triage**, and **epidemiological surveillance**.

In the formal taxonomy of UAV operations, healthcare missions are categorized as **critical-time-sensitive (CTS)** missions, governed by:

$$\text{Mission Class} = f(\text{Payload Integrity},\ \text{Time-Criticality},\ \text{Regulatory Tier})$$

Where:

- **Payload Integrity**: Maintenance of cold-chain, sterility, vibration < **0.5 g RMS** (for biologics).
- **Time-Criticality**: Target delivery windows of **< 60 minutes** for emergency payloads.
- **Regulatory Tier**: BVLOS operations under **medical waiver (FAA Part 107)** or **DGCA RPAS regulations**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flying White Coat"

Imagine a hospital's white-coat courier — the person who sprints between blood banks, ICUs, and operation theatres carrying plasma bags, tissue samples, or urgent prescriptions. Now remove the human limitations (traffic, fatigue, single-thread delivery), strap a **rotor to the courier**, give it a **GPS address of the destination**, and let it fly **autonomously above the city's chaos** at **60–120 km/h**.

That is precisely the role of a healthcare UAV — a *flying white coat* that ignores ground congestion, operates 24/7, and reaches rural clinics, accident sites, or rooftop helipads in a fraction of the time a ground ambulance would.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchor:** Rwanda's national drone network by *Zipline International* has delivered **> 500,000 medical products** (blood, vaccines, antivenom) to **> 2,500 hospitals** since 2016 — reducing maternal-haemorrhage mortality by **> 51%** in covered districts.

## 1.3 Standard Healthcare UAV Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | Medical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload Mass** | **0.5 – 25 kg** | Determines blood/vaccine capacity |
| **Cruise Speed** | **60 – 120 km/h** | Time-critical delivery |
| **Endurance** | **30 – 240 min** | Round-trip feasibility |
| **Operational Radius** | **50 – 160 km** | Regional hospital network |
| **Specific Energy (Battery)** | **150 – 260 Wh/kg** | Mission range ceiling |
| **Cold-Chain Window** | **2 – 8 °C** | Vaccine / plasma integrity |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Healthcare UAV Applications

The KTU 2024 scheme partitions healthcare aerial robotics into **five functional pillars**:

1. **Logistics & Supply Chain Delivery** — Blood, vaccines, pharmaceuticals, lab samples.
2. **Emergency Medical Response (EMR)** — Automated External Defibrillator (AED) drop, cardiac-arrest intervention.
3. **Organ & Biologic Transport** — Time-critical organ delivery (kidney, heart, cornea).
4. **Disaster Response & Triage** — Aerial reconnaissance, supply airdrop, comms relay.
5. **Public-Health Surveillance** — Vector control (mosquito mapping), air-quality monitoring, epidemic screening.

## 2.2 Operational Sub-Systems Architecture

Every healthcare UAV integrates the following **functional stack**:

- **Airframe Layer:** Multirotor (hover), Fixed-wing (range), Hybrid VTOL (combined).
- **Propulsion Layer:** Li-Po / Li-S / Hydrogen-Fuel-Cell (specific energy **> 600 Wh/kg** for long range).
- **Guidance Layer:** GNSS-RTK (cm-level), INS fusion, visual SLAM (GPS-denied indoor).
- **Payload Layer:** Insulated cold-box, biometric lock, anti-vibration cradle, real-time telemetry.
- **Mission Layer:** Mission Management Software (MMS) with redundancy and fail-safe return-to-home (RTH).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical** mathematical toolkit. Memorize the forms and physical units — KTU board valuations penalize missing units by **0.5 – 1 mark** per equation.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{batt}} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Battery energy capacity | Wh |
| 2 | $m_{\text{mission}} = m_{\text{airframe}} + m_{\text{payload}} + m_{\text{batt}}$ | Gross Take-Off Mass (GTOM) | kg |
| 3 | $P_{\text{hover}} = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$ | Rotor hover power | W |
| 4 | $t_{\text{endurance}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{meh}}}{P_{\text{hover}} + P_{\text{avionics}}}$ | Mission endurance | s |
| 5 | $R = \eta_{\text{prop}} \cdot \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{W} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Breguet range equation | m |
| 6 | $T_{\text{cold}}(t) = T_{\text{amb}} + (T_0 - T_{\text{amb}}) e^{-kt}$ | Newton's cooling (vaccine integrity) | °C |
| 7 | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \left( \sum_i \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i} \right)^{-1}$ | Series-reliability for redundant sub-systems | hours |
| 8 | $\text{OnTime}\% = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Late Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100$ | Medical logistics SLA | % |
| 9 | $v_{\text{cruise}} = \dfrac{R}{t_{\text{mission}}/2}$ | Average cruise velocity requirement | m/s |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\text{vib}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(a_i - \bar{a})^2}$ | Payload vibration RMS (must be **< 0.5 g**) | g |

> **Symbols:** $T$ = thrust (N); $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³); $A$ = rotor disc area (m²); $W$ = weight (N); $L/D$ = lift-to-drag ratio; $\eta_{\text{meh}}$ = mechanical efficiency; $k$ = thermal time-constant (s⁻¹); $\eta_{\text{prop}}$ = propulsive efficiency.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Equation

- **Equations 1–2** anchor **mission-mass accounting** — examiners frequently ask students to verify GTOM before declaring a flight feasible.
- **Equations 3–5** define the **fundamental flight-envelope**. Endurance scales linearly with battery energy but **inversely with the 3/2 power of thrust** — meaning doubling payload is **catastrophic** for hover endurance.
- **Equation 6** is unique to **biological payloads**. Insulin, vaccines (mRNA), and red blood cells must remain within **2 – 8 °C**. Passive insulated containers use Newton's law of cooling; KTU loves asking the *thermal time constant* $k$.
- **Equation 7** quantifies **redundancy engineering** — for medical drones, dual-GNSS, dual-IMU, and dual-battery are mandatory because **single-point failure can cost a life**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In Indian KTU industry context, aerial robotics for healthcare aligns with the **"Drone Shakti" initiative (Ministry of Civil Aviation, 2021)** and **ICMR's drone-based vaccine delivery pilot (Manipur, 2023)** — where 1,500+ doses of COVID-19 vaccines were delivered across 31 km in **< 25 minutes** versus **2.5 hours** by road.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mission Range for a Medical Delivery UAV

**Problem Statement:**
A multirotor UAV is tasked with delivering a **2 kg** vaccine payload over a **40 km** range. Given:
- Battery capacity: $E_{\text{batt}} = 500$ Wh
- Propulsive efficiency: $\eta_{\text{prop}} = 0.65$
- Lift-to-drag ratio: $L/D = 12$
- Total weight: $W = 25$ N (≈ 2.55 kg GTOM)

Determine if the mission is feasible.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (Breguet's approximation for electric UAV):**

$$R = \eta_{\text{prop}} \cdot \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{W} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.65 \times \frac{500 \ \text{Wh}}{25 \ \text{N}} \times 12 \\
  &= 0.65 \times 20 \ \text{Wh/N} \times 12 \\
  &= 0.65 \times 240 \ \text{Wh/N}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Unit Conversion Note:** 1 Wh = 3600 J, and 1 N·m = 1 J. So Wh/N has units of meters (since J/N = m). Thus:

$$R = 156 \ \text{km}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with required mission radius:**

Required one-way distance = **40 km**. Total mission range (out-and-back, factoring headwind reserve) ≈ $40 \times 2 + \text{reserve}$.

Assuming a **20% reserve** (per DGCA medical-drone guidelines):

$$R_{\text{required}} = 80 \times 1.2 = 96 \ \text{km}$$

**Step 4 — Feasibility Verdict:**

Since $R_{\text{available}} = 156 \ \text{km} > R_{\text{required}} = 96 \ \text{km}$, the mission is **feasible with 38% safety margin**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Cold-Chain Integrity for Vaccine Payload

**Problem Statement:**
A UAV carries mRNA vaccines initially at $T_0 = 4$ °C into ambient temperature $T_{\text{amb}} = 35$ °C. The insulated container has a thermal time constant $k = 1/1800$ s⁻¹ (i.e., 30 minutes). Find the temperature after **45 minutes** of flight.

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Law of Cooling:**

$$T(t) = T_{\text{amb}} + (T_0 - T_{\text{amb}}) e^{-kt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(2700) &= 35 + (4 - 35) \cdot e^{-(1/1800) \times 2700} \\
        &= 35 + (-31) \cdot e^{-1.5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

**Step 4 — Final temperature:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(2700) &= 35 + (-31)(0.2231) \\
        &= 35 - 6.916 \\
        &= 28.08 \ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Clinical Decision:**

Since $T = 28.08$ °C **violates** the **2 – 8 °C** cold-chain envelope, the mission requires either:
- A shorter delivery window (< 30 min), or
- Active **phase-change-material (PCM) cooling packs** with effective $k \le 1/3600$ s⁻¹.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Series-Reliability for a Redundant Medical UAV

**Problem Statement:**
Compute the system MTBF for a healthcare UAV with the following sub-system MTBFs:

- Flight Controller: **8,000 h**
- Dual GNSS Receivers (parallel-redundant): each **12,000 h**
- Dual IMU (parallel-redundant): each **10,000 h**
- Battery Pack: **2,000 h**

**Step 1 — Compute redundant block MTBF:**

For two components in parallel: $\text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = \text{MTBF}_1 + \text{MTBF}_2 - \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_1 \cdot \text{MTBF}_2}{\text{MTBF}_1 + \text{MTBF}_2}$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{GNSS}} = 12000 + 12000 - \frac{12000^2}{24000} = 24000 - 6000 = 18000 \ \text{h}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{IMU}} = 10000 + 10000 - \frac{10000^2}{20000} = 20000 - 5000 = 15000 \ \text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply series MTBF (series failure of all blocks):**

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \left( \frac{1}{8000} + \frac{1}{18000} + \frac{1}{15000} + \frac{1}{2000} \right)^{-1}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \left( 0.000125 + 0.0000556 + 0.0000667 + 0.0005 \right)^{-1} \\
&= \left( 0.0007473 \right)^{-1} \\
&\approx 1338 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The **battery pack is the dominant failure driver** (MTBF = 2,000 h vs. system MTBF = 1,338 h). For medical-grade certification, KTU examiners will mark the **identification of the weakest link** and its **mitigation strategy** (e.g., hot-swappable dual battery).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — UAV Class vs. Healthcare Application

| UAV Class | Payload (kg) | Range (km) | Best-Fit Healthcare Mission | Why Selected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Micro Multirotor** | 0.5 – 2 | 5 – 15 | AED drop, lab samples | Hover precision, low inertia |
| **Heavy Multirotor** | 5 – 25 | 30 – 50 | Blood/plasma delivery | High payload, vertical take-off |
| **Fixed-wing** | 3 – 15 | 100 – 160 | Inter-hospital vaccine chain | Long range, high speed |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | 5 – 20 | 80 – 120 | Rural clinic supply | Combines VTOL + range |
| **eVTOL Air-Ambulance** | 200 – 500 | 100 – 250 | Patient transport | FAA / EASA certified |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Healthcare UAV Mission Architecture — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a medical UAV delivery system, mapping interactions from order placement to clinical hand-off.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Hospital Order Portal"] --> B["Mission Management Server"]
    B --> C["Flight Planner Module"]
    C --> D["Airspace Authorization API"]
    D --> E["UAV Launchpad"]
    E --> F["Healthcare UAV"]
    F --> G["GNSS RTK + Visual SLAM"]
    F --> H["Cold Chain Payload Bay"]
    F --> I["Telemetry Downlink 4G 5G"]
    I --> J["Ground Control Station"]
    J --> K["Recipient Clinic"]
    H --> K
    K --> L["Biometric Payload Unlock"]
    L --> M["Confirmation + Return to Home"]
    F -.->|"Fail Safe"| N["Parachute + RTH"]
    N --> E
```

**Node-Description Table:**

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| A | Clinical order entry (web/mobile dashboard) |
| B | Central mission dispatcher |
| C | Route, weather, NOTAM integration |
| D | DGCA / FAA airspace deconfliction |
| E | Autonomous takeoff + winch launch |
| F | Multirotor / Hybrid VTOL airframe |
| G | Dual-redundant navigation stack |
| H | Insulated PCM-cooled payload |
| I | Live telemetry with biometric seal status |
| J | Remote pilot-supervisor monitoring |
| K | Destination hospital/clinic rooftop |
| L | Authorized medical officer unlocks payload |
| M | Mission close + autonomous return |
| N | Parachute deployment if critical failure |

## 4.2 Cold-Chain Payload Subsystem Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Vaccine Vial 4 C"] --> P2["PCM Cooling Pack"]
    P2 --> P3["Vacuum Insulated Enclosure"]
    P3 --> P4["Anti-Vibration Cradle"]
    P4 --> P5["UAV Cargo Hold"]
    P5 --> P6["Temperature Telemetry"]
    P6 --> P7["Cloud Dashboard"]
    P7 --> P8["Cold Chain Compliance Log"]
```

## 4.3 Mission Failure-Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["Mission Start"] --> T1{"GPS Lock Acquired"}
    T1 -->|Yes| T2{"Battery SOC > 60%"}
    T1 -->|No| F1["Hover + Visual SLAM"]
    T2 -->|Yes| T3{"Wind < 12 m/s"}
    T2 -->|No| F2["Return to Home"]
    T3 -->|Yes| T4["Cruise to Target"]
    T3 -->|No| F3["Hold + Reroute"]
    T4 --> T5{"Cold Chain OK"}
    T5 -->|Yes| T6["Payload Release"]
    T5 -->|No| F4["Abort + Notify Clinic"]
    T6 --> T7["Mission End"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**Q1.** List any **three** distinct applications of aerial robots in healthcare delivery and specify the dominant UAV class (multirotor / fixed-wing / hybrid VTOL) best suited for each. Justify in one line per application.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Application | UAV Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AED delivery for cardiac arrest within urban 5 km radius | Multirotor | VTOL precision landing, low-speed hover over bystanders |
| 2 | Inter-hospital vaccine transfer over 80 km terrain | Hybrid VTOL | Combines VTOL from clinic pad with efficient cruise |
| 3 | Aerial spraying for mosquito/vector control | Fixed-wing | Large area coverage, high endurance, lower per-km cost |

> **Examiner's Note:** Marks awarded only for **class-justification pairing**, not generic listing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Q2.** What is the **cold-chain integrity window** for mRNA COVID-19 vaccines? State **two engineering measures** a UAV must incorporate to preserve this window during aerial transit.

**Model Answer:**

- **Cold-Chain Window:** **2 – 8 °C** (per WHO PQS E006 standard).
- **Engineering Measures:**
  1. **Vacuum-insulated payload enclosure** with phase-change-material (PCM) cooling packs to buffer ambient heat ingress.
  2. **Real-time temperature telemetry** with abort-and-return logic if the envelope is breached.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE: 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often omit **units** in equations like $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$. Deduct **1 mark** for missing Wh/J.
> - Confusing **endurance** (time) with **range** (distance) costs **2 marks** in derivation questions.
> - For cold-chain questions, failing to mention the **2 – 8 °C** specific range results in loss of **1 mark**.
> - Drawing block diagrams without arrow direction labels loses **0.5–1 mark** under KTU strict marking.

### Question A — 14 Marks [Design / Analysis Type — KTU Bloom Level L4]

**Q.A (a)** A healthcare multirotor UAV is to deliver a **3 kg** medical payload (insulin + blood samples) over a one-way distance of **25 km**. The airframe mass is **8 kg**, battery mass **6 kg**, and avionics **1 kg**.

Given:
- Battery specific energy: **180 Wh/kg**
- Propulsive efficiency: **0.62**
- Lift-to-drag ratio: **10**
- Reserve fraction: **25%**

Compute:
1. The **available range** using the Breguet equation. **[7 marks]**
2. Whether the mission is feasible. **[1 mark]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Total weight:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= m_{\text{total}} \cdot g \\
  &= (m_{\text{airframe}} + m_{\text{payload}} + m_{\text{batt}} + m_{\text{avio}}) \cdot g \\
  &= (8 + 3 + 6 + 1) \times 9.81 \\
  &= 176.58 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Available battery energy:**

$$E_{\text{batt}} = 180 \ \text{Wh/kg} \times 6 \ \text{kg} = 1080 \ \text{Wh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Breguet range equation:**

$$R = \eta_{\text{prop}} \cdot \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{W} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.62 \times \frac{1080}{176.58} \times 10 \\
  &= 0.62 \times 6.116 \times 10 \\
  &= 37.92 \ \text{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for 25% reserve (out-and-back):**

$$R_{\text{required}} = 25 \times 2 \times 1.25 = 62.5 \ \text{km}$$

**Step 5 — Feasibility verdict:**

Since $R_{\text{available}} = 37.92 \ \text{km} < R_{\text{required}} = 62.5 \ \text{km}$, the mission is **NOT feasible** with current battery configuration.

**Recommendation:** Increase battery mass to **≥ 10 kg** OR switch to a **hybrid VTOL with Li-S cells (specific energy 350 Wh/kg)**.

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain with a labeled block diagram the **functional architecture** of a UAV-based medical cold-chain delivery system, identifying **at least 6 blocks** and the flow of **temperature telemetry**. **[6 marks]**

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to the **Cold-Chain Payload Subsystem Flow** in Section 4.2 of these notes. Expected student diagram must include:

1. **Vaccine/Plasma Source Block** — pharmacy dispatch.
2. **Insulated Payload Bay with PCM** — passive cooling.
3. **Temperature Sensor Array** — DS18B20 / PT100 RTD.
4. **Onboard Microcontroller** — Arduino / STM32 logging.
5. **4G/5G Telemetry Module** — live cloud upload.
6. **Ground Control / Cloud Dashboard** — receiving station.

> Students must mention the **closed-loop** nature: if $T > 8$ °C, the **abort logic** triggers RTH.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [Analytical / Numerical Type — KTU Bloom Level L4/L5]

**Q.B (a)** A drone delivers a vaccine payload initially at **5 °C** through ambient air at **38 °C**. The insulated container has a thermal time constant $k = 1/2400$ s⁻¹. Determine the **time at which the cold-chain ceiling (8 °C)** is breached. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Newton's Law of Cooling:**

$$T(t) = T_{\text{amb}} + (T_0 - T_{\text{amb}}) e^{-kt}$$

**Step 2 — Set $T(t_c) = 8$ °C and solve for $t_c$:**

$$8 = 38 + (5 - 38) e^{-k t_c}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
8 - 38 &= -33 \cdot e^{-k t_c} \\
-30 &= -33 \cdot e^{-k t_c} \\
e^{-k t_c} &= \frac{30}{33} = 0.9091
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$-k t_c = \ln(0.9091) = -0.0953$$

$$t_c = \frac{0.0953}{1/2400} = 0.0953 \times 2400 \approx 228.7 \ \text{s} \approx 3.81 \ \text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The cold chain is breached in **~ 3.8 minutes**. Hence the UAV must complete the active cooling phase well before this window — typically using a **larger PCM mass** to push $k$ below $1/3600$ s⁻¹, extending the safe window to **> 15 minutes**.

---

**Q.B (b)** Compare and contrast the use of **multirotor**, **fixed-wing**, and **hybrid VTOL** UAVs for **organ transport** missions. Use a tabular format with at least **four parameters**: endurance, payload, range, landing precision, and regulatory complexity. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (b):**

| Parameter | Multirotor | Fixed-Wing | Hybrid VTOL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Endurance** | 25 – 45 min | 90 – 240 min | 60 – 180 min |
| **Payload Capacity** | 5 – 15 kg | 3 – 10 kg | 5 – 20 kg |
| **Operational Range** | 15 – 40 km | 100 – 250 km | 80 – 160 km |
| **Landing Precision** | Excellent (cm-level hover) | Poor (needs runway) | Excellent (VTOL hover) |
| **Regulatory Complexity** | Low (visual line-of-sight) | High (BVLOS waiver) | High (BVLOS + VTOL certification) |
| **Vibration Level (RMS)** | 0.3 – 0.6 g | 0.1 – 0.3 g | 0.2 – 0.4 g |
| **Best-Fit for Organ Transport?** | Short-range urban | Long-range inter-city | **Yes — optimal balance** |

> **Examiner's Insight:** Hybrid VTOL is the **gold standard** for organ transport because:
> - VTOL enables **rooftop hospital landing** (no runway).
> - Cruise efficiency preserves **> 60 min endurance**.
> - Lower vibration than pure multirotor preserves **biologic integrity**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Health Care**

- ✅ **Five Pillars** of healthcare UAVs: Logistics, EMR, Organ Transport, Disaster Response, Surveillance.
- ✅ **Cold-Chain Window** for most biologics = **2 – 8 °C**; insulin/vaccines strict.
- ✅ **Breguet Range Equation** $R = \eta_{\text{prop}} \cdot (E_{\text{batt}} / W) \cdot (L/D)$ is the exam favourite.
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling** governs payload thermal integrity; master the exponential form.
- ✅ **Series-reliability** decreases as more sub-systems are added — battery is typically the weakest link.
- ✅ **Vibration RMS** must stay **< 0.5 g** for sensitive biologics — multirotors are noisier than fixed-wing.
- ✅ **Redundancy Engineering** (dual GNSS, dual IMU, dual battery) is mandatory for medical certification.
- ✅ **Time-critical SLA**: emergency deliveries target **< 60 minutes** door-to-door.
- ✅ **Regulatory framework**: DGCA RPAS rules (India), FAA Part 107 (USA), EASA SC-VTOL (EU).
- ✅ **Hybrid VTOL** is the emerging gold standard — combines hover precision with cruise efficiency.
- ✅ **Real-world exemplars** for case-study answers: Zipline (Rwanda/Ghana), Matternet (Switzerland), Wingcopter (Germany), ICMR Manipur pilot (India).
- ✅ **Specific Energy** of Li-Po ≈ **150 – 220 Wh/kg**; Li-S ≈ **350 – 500 Wh/kg**; Hydrogen fuel cell > **600 Wh/kg**.
- ✅ **MTBF formulation** for parallel-redundant blocks must use the inverse-of-sum rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defense Surveillance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT745 - AERIAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Defense Surveillance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Defense Surveillance** using Aerial Robotics refers to the systematic deployment of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** for **Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance (ISR)** and **Target Acquisition (TA)** missions in military and paramilitary operational environments. It encompasses the autonomous or semi-autonomous acquisition, processing, transmission, and analysis of battlefield data through onboard sensor payloads, in compliance with doctrines of **Network-Centric Warfare (NCW)** and **Multi-Domain Operations (MDO)**.

The functional architecture integrates three primary ISR loops:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block**
> **SENSE → PROCESS → RELAY → ANALYZE → ACT**
> A closed-loop ISR pipeline where the UAV acts as a persistent, low-risk *aerial node* within a larger **Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (C4ISR)** grid.

**Surveillance** is defined as the *systematic observation of an area*, while **Reconnaissance** is a *mission-oriented information-gathering operation*. For KTU, both fall under the umbrella of persistent battlefield awareness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a defense surveillance drone as a **silent night-watchman with superhuman senses floating above a fortress**:

- The fortress is the **Area of Interest (AOI)** — say, a 50 km stretch of hostile border.
- The watchman is the **UAV**, hovering 5,000 ft above, invisible to ground forces.
- His senses are the **onboard sensor suite** (cameras, radar, SIGINT antennas) that "see" heat, motion, radio chatter, and even footprints.
- The lantern he carries is the **datalink**, beaming back what he senses to the **Ground Control Station (GCS)**.
- His patrol pattern is the **search trajectory** (lawn-mower, spiral, perimeter).
- The other watchmen in the sky are the **swarm partners**, sharing the load and covering blind spots.

Just as a single watchman cannot guard a vast perimeter alone, defense surveillance often uses **multiple coordinated UAVs** that hand off targets to each other — the essence of *cooperative ISR*.

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Terms)

The following terms appear in nearly every KTU 2024 scheme question on this topic. Memorize them as **bolded constants/metrics**:

- **Area Coverage Rate (ACR)** — km²/min.
- **Sensor Footprint / Ground Sample Distance (GSD)** — m/pixel.
- **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** — maximum geometric visibility distance.
- **Standoff Distance (SD)** — safe lateral separation from threat axis.
- **Time-on-Station (TOS)** — endurance over the AOI.
- **Probability of Detection (P_d)** and **Probability of False Alarm (P_fa)**.
- **Combat Radius (R_c)** — max distance from base for a single sortie.
- **NATO STANAG 4586** — standard for UAV control interoperability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERAT745 Module 4):**
> Defense surveillance blends *mission planning*, *payload selection*, *trajectory design*, *autonomous target detection*, and *secure datalinks*. Expect theory questions on sensor trade-offs, swarm coordination, and coverage algorithms.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Defense Surveillance Mission

The mission decomposes into seven logically sequential phases. Each phase is a separate KTU exam sub-topic:

1. **Mission Definition (M-D)**
   - Define AOI polygon, threat axis, friendly force locations.
   - Specify *surveillance objective*: wide-area search vs. point target tracking.
   - Output: Mission Requirements Document (MRD).

2. **Platform & Payload Selection (PPS)**
   - Match UAV class to mission:
     * **MALE UAV** (Medium Altitude Long Endurance) e.g., Heron, Predator — for 24 h persistent ISR at < 25,000 ft.
     * **HALE UAV** (High Altitude Long Endurance) e.g., Global Hawk — for 30+ h at > 60,000 ft.
     * **Tactical Mini-UAV** e.g., RQ-11 Raven — for platoon-level ISR at < 1,000 ft.
   - Match payload to threat:
     * **Electro-Optical (EO)** — daylight identification.
     * **Infrared (IR)** — night/target-signature detection.
     * **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** — all-weather penetration.
     * **SIGINT (SIGnals INTelligence)** — RF emitter geolocation.
     * **LIDAR** — 3D terrain mapping.
     * **Hyperspectral Imager (HSI)** — camouflage detection.

3. **Coverage & Trajectory Planning (CTP)**
   - **Boustrophedon (lawn-mower) pattern** for area search.
   - **Spiral / Expanding Square** for point-target cueing.
   - **Perimeter / Orbits** for convoy escort.
   - **Persistent stare** via loitering munitions (loiter at 200–500 m).

4. **Sensor Modeling (SM)**
   - Camera: GSD = (H × pixel_size) / focal_length.
   - Radar: Range resolution = c / (2 × B), where B = chirp bandwidth.
   - SIGINT: Angle of Arrival (AoA) for emitter geolocation.

5. **Target Detection, Tracking & Classification (TDTC)**
   - Onboard: CNN/Edge-AI for real-time detection (YOLOv8 on Jetson Orin).
   - Tracking: Kalman Filter / Particle Filter for kinematic state estimation.
   - Classification: ATR (Automatic Target Recognition) algorithms.

6. **Secure Datalink & Relay (SDR)**
   - LOS via C-band/Ku-band SATCOM.
   - BLOS (Beyond Line of Sight) via INMARSAT, Iridium.
   - Anti-jam: Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS), MEO-LEO constellations.

7. **Battle Damage Assessment (BDA) & Dissemination (BDA-D)**
   - Post-strike image comparison, change detection.
   - Push to **Common Operating Picture (COP)** via STANAG 4586 datalinks.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (KTU "How" and "Why")

- *Why* **GSD** matters: NATO requires GSD ≤ 0.3 m for *positive identification* of a tank.
- *How* a **boustrophedon** path achieves full coverage: parallel tracks spaced ≤ 2 × W, where W = swath width.
- *Why* **swarm surveillance** is preferred: A single UAV has a single point-of-failure and limited payload. A swarm of 10 small UAVs covers 10× the area and offers redundancy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Ground Sample Distance (GSD) | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | m/pixel | Image resolution design |
| 2 | Swath Width (Camera) | $W = H \cdot \tan(\theta_{FOV}/2) \cdot 2$ | m | Coverage planning |
| 3 | Area Coverage Rate | $ACR = V_g \cdot W$ | m²/s | Mission duration |
| 4 | Radar Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | m | SAR design |
| 5 | Maximum LOS (geometric) | $d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2Rh + h^2} + \sqrt{2Rh_r + h_r^2}$ | km | Datalink planning |
| 6 | Endurance (Breguet-like) | $E = \dfrac{\eta_{prop} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)}{V \cdot g}$ | h | Loiter time |
| 7 | Time-on-Station | $TOS = E - 2t_{transit}$ | h | Persistence |
| 8 | Standoff Distance | $SD = V \cdot t_{REACTION}$ | m | Survivability |
| 9 | Track Spacing | $s = 2W$ | m | No gap coverage |
| 10 | Probability of Cumulative Detection | $P_D = 1 - (1 - p_d)^N$ | — | Multi-pass search |
| 11 | SNR (sensor) | $SNR = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 k T B F}$ | dB | Radar detection |
| 12 | Swath overlap factor | $K = 1 - \dfrac{s}{W}$ | — | Coverage redundancy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For derivations, the examiner typically selects **GSD**, **Swath Width**, and **Endurance**. Master units, $\log$ properties, and **why** every variable appears.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Border Surveillance**: India uses DRDO's **TAPAS-BH-201** for 24-h border ISR.
- **Counter-Insurgency**: Indian Army deploys **SWITCH UAV** in J&K.
- **Maritime Patrol**: Navy's **P-8I + MQ-9B SeaGuardian** joint ops.
- **Swarm ISR**: US **LOCUST** program, Chinese **Swarm-of-100s** demos.
- **Civil Spin-offs**: Forest fire watch, disaster response, anti-poaching.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Camera GSD and Swath Width

### 3.1.1 Setup
A UAV flies at altitude $H$ (meters) carrying a camera with focal length $f$ (mm) and pixel pitch $p$ (µm). The camera's instantaneous Field of View is $\theta_{FOV}$ (radians).

**Step 1: Pin-hole projection of one pixel onto the ground.**

A single pixel of size $p$ (µm) maps onto the ground over a distance $GSD$ (m/pixel). By similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pixel angle} &= \frac{p}{f} \quad [\text{radians}] \\
\text{Ground projection} &= H \cdot \frac{p}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting $p$ from µm to m ($p_{m} = p \times 10^{-6}$) and $f$ from mm to m ($f_{m} = f \times 10^{-3}$):

$$
GSD = \frac{H \cdot p_{m}}{f_{m}} = \frac{H \cdot p \cdot 10^{-6}}{f \cdot 10^{-3}} = \frac{H \cdot p}{1000 \cdot f} \;\;[\text{m}]
$$

**Step 2: Compute swath width using $\theta_{FOV}$.**

The full FOV spans $W$ on the ground:

$$
W = 2 \cdot H \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_{FOV}}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Sample numerical evaluation.**

> $H = 3000$ m, $f = 50$ mm, $p = 5$ µm, $\theta_{FOV} = 60°$.

$$
GSD = \frac{3000 \cdot 5}{1000 \cdot 50} = \frac{15000}{50000} = 0.30 \text{ m/pixel}
$$

$$
W = 2 \cdot 3000 \cdot \tan(30°) = 6000 \cdot 0.5774 \approx 3464 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 0.3 m GSD allows NATO-standard *positive vehicle identification*; a 3.46 km swath covers ~3.5 km wide strips per pass.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Area Coverage Rate (ACR) and Time to Cover a Polygon

**Step 1: Definition.**

The ACR is the ground area swept per second by the sensor footprint:

$$
ACR = V_g \cdot W \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 2: Include overlap factor $K$** (if $K = 20\%$ overlap, only $0.8W$ is new area):

$$
ACR_{eff} = V_g \cdot W \cdot (1 - K)
$$

**Step 3: Time to cover polygon of area $A$ (m²):**

$$
T_{cover} = \frac{A}{ACR_{eff}} = \frac{A}{V_g \cdot W \cdot (1 - K)}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**

> $V_g = 50$ m/s, $W = 3464$ m, $K = 0.2$, $A = 100$ km² = $10^8$ m².

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR_{eff} &= 50 \cdot 3464 \cdot 0.8 = 138560 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \\
T_{cover} &= \frac{10^8}{138560} \approx 721.6 \text{ s} \approx 12.0 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single tactical UAV can cover a 100 km² AOI in ~12 min at 3 km altitude — useful for KTU mission-planning questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Endurance and Time-on-Station

**Step 1: Breguet-like endurance for a propeller-driven UAV** (level flight, loiter):

$$
E = \frac{\eta_{prop} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \ln(W_i / W_f)}{V \cdot g}
$$

**Step 2: Insert symbols.**

- $\eta_{prop}$ = propulsive efficiency (≈ 0.7–0.85).
- $L/D$ = lift-to-drag ratio (≈ 18 for tactical, 30+ for HALE).
- $W_i, W_f$ = initial and final weight (N).
- $V$ = loiter speed (m/s).
- $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 3: Time-on-Station** subtracts round-trip transit:

$$
TOS = E - 2 t_{transit}, \quad t_{transit} = \frac{R_c}{V_{cruise}}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**

> $\eta = 0.8$, $L/D = 20$, $W_i/W_f = 2.0$, $V = 35$ m/s, $R_c = 75$ km, $V_{cruise} = 45$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{0.8 \cdot 20 \cdot \ln(2)}{35 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{16 \cdot 0.6931}{343.4} = \frac{11.09}{343.4} \approx 0.0323 \text{ h} \\
&\approx 116 \text{ s} \;\; \text{(not realistic — values chosen to show algebra)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above is algebra only. In real KTU problems, plug the values in **step order** and present *every* numerical substitution.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Radar / SAR Range Resolution

**Step 1: Two scatterers separated by $\Delta R$ are resolved if their echoes differ by one pulse width $\tau$.**

**Step 2: For chirp bandwidth $B$, $\tau \cdot B \approx 1$, so $\tau = 1/B$.**

**Step 3: Round-trip time difference:**

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2 \Delta R}{c}
$$

**Step 4: Setting $\Delta t = \tau$:**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

> $B = 100$ MHz.

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 100 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 MHz SAR resolves 1.5 m features — sufficient for vehicle detection, not for component identification.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Probability of Detection in Multi-Pass Search

**Step 1: Single-pass detection probability** = $p_d$ (sensor-dependent, typically 0.5–0.9).

**Step 2: Probability of *miss* in one pass** = $1 - p_d$.

**Step 3: Probability of *miss* in N independent passes** = $(1 - p_d)^N$.

**Step 4: Cumulative detection probability:**

$$
P_D = 1 - (1 - p_d)^N
$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

> $p_d = 0.4$, $N = 5$.

$$
P_D = 1 - (0.6)^5 = 1 - 0.07776 = 0.9222
$$

**Conclusion:** 5 passes raise detection to 92.2% — the *why* behind persistent ISR.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mission ↔ Payload ↔ Trajectory

| Mission Type | Altitude | Best Payload | Trajectory | KTU-Relevant Trade-off |
|--------------|----------|--------------|------------|------------------------|
| Wide-area search | 3–8 km | EO/IR + SAR | Boustrophedon | Coverage vs. GSD |
| Point target track | 1–3 km | EO + LiDAR | Loiter circle | Resolution vs. endurance |
| Border patrol | 5–10 km | SAR + SIGINT | Perimeter / fence | All-weather vs. cost |
| Convoy escort | 200–500 m | EO/IR | Follow-me | Survivability vs. clarity |
| BDA | 3–5 km | EO + HSI | Repeat pass | Pre/post comparison |
| Urban ISR | < 300 m | EO + acoustic | Spiral-in | Stealth vs. resolution |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERAT745]**
*Define the term "Standoff Distance" in the context of a defense surveillance UAV. Why is it operationally important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Standoff distance $SD$ is the lateral separation maintained between the UAV and the threat axis to keep the platform outside the envelope of short-range air defenses. It is calculated as $SD = V \cdot t_{REACTION}$, where $V$ is the ground speed and $t_{REACTION}$ is the time for the enemy to detect, identify, and engage. Operationally, it preserves the UAV (a high-value asset) while still allowing EO/IR/SAR sensors to image the target at angles ≤ 30° depression, ensuring acceptable GSD.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT745]**
*Distinguish between MALE and HALE UAVs. Give one operational example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MALE (Medium Altitude Long Endurance) UAVs operate at 10,000–25,000 ft for 24 h — example: IAI Heron, MQ-9 Reaper. HALE (High Altitude Long Endurance) UAVs operate above 60,000 ft for 30+ h — example: RQ-4 Global Hawk. The difference lies in altitude ceiling, payload mass, and *strategic vs. tactical* role.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sensor & Coverage Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PERAT745 Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** A tactical UAV is fitted with an EO camera having focal length $f = 35$ mm and pixel pitch $p = 4$ µm. The FOV is $50°$. The UAV flies at $H = 2500$ m with ground speed $V_g = 45$ m/s. **Compute (i) the GSD in m/pixel, (ii) the swath width in metres, and (iii) the effective area coverage rate assuming 25% overlap.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) GSD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GSD &= \frac{H \cdot p}{1000 \cdot f} \\
&= \frac{2500 \cdot 4}{1000 \cdot 35} \\
&= \frac{10000}{35000} \\
&\approx 0.286 \text{ m/pixel}
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies NATO's ≤ 0.3 m/pixel standard for vehicle ID.

**(ii) Swath width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 2 \cdot H \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_{FOV}}{2}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot 2500 \cdot \tan(25°) \\
&= 5000 \cdot 0.4663 \\
&\approx 2331.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Effective ACR (K = 0.25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR_{eff} &= V_g \cdot W \cdot (1 - K) \\
&= 45 \cdot 2331.5 \cdot 0.75 \\
&\approx 78689 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \\
&\approx 4.72 \text{ km}^2/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** The AOI is a rectangular strip 6 km wide × 80 km long. **Estimate the time to achieve 90% cumulative detection probability given single-pass $p_d = 0.45$, and the total flight time required to cover the strip.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Number of passes N for P_D = 0.9:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1 - p_d)^N &\ge 0.9 \\
(0.55)^N &\le 0.1 \\
N \ln(0.55) &\le \ln(0.1) \\
N &\ge \frac{\ln(0.1)}{\ln(0.55)} = \frac{-2.3026}{-0.5978} \approx 3.85 \\
N &= 4 \text{ passes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Coverage time (single pass, 25% overlap):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area} &= 6 \times 80 = 480 \text{ km}^2 = 4.8 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^2 \\
T_{cover} &= \frac{4.8 \times 10^8}{78689} \approx 6100 \text{ s} \approx 101.7 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total mission time (4 passes):**

$$
T_{total} = 4 \times 101.7 = 406.8 \text{ min} \approx 6.78 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plus 10% turn-around reserve → **≈ 7.5 h on station**. Requires a MALE-class UAV.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Surveillance Strategy & Swarm

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT745]**

**(a) Discuss the role of a swarm of UAVs in defense surveillance, comparing it with a single high-altitude platform. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A swarm of N small UAVs offers three advantages over a single HALE platform:
1. **Coverage Redundancy** — N × ACR, with graceful degradation if one fails.
2. **Multi-perspective fusion** — multiple look angles improve ATR and reduce occlusion.
3. **Cost & survivability** — small UAVs are expendable; HALE platforms are high-value.

Disadvantages include datalink congestion, deconfliction complexity, and regulatory issues. In KTU 2024 context, expect a table of swarms vs. single-platform HALE on parameters: *Coverage, Survivability, Cost, Persistence, Sensor Resolution*.

**(b) A swarm of 6 UAVs is tasked to monitor a 12 km × 12 km square. Each UAV covers a 4 km × 6 km sub-area per sortie at 1.5 km altitude using EO sensors. The single-pass probability of detection is 0.6. Compute the total number of sorties required for a 95% cumulative detection probability. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Per-sortie coverage by swarm:**

$$
6 \times (4 \times 6) = 144 \text{ km}^2
$$

**AOI = 144 km²** — exactly one parallel sortie covers the AOI.

**Cumulative detection per sorty pass:**

$$
P_D^{(1)} = 1 - (0.4)^6 = 1 - 0.004096 = 0.9959
$$

A single sortie already exceeds 95%, so the answer is **N = 1 sortie**. The student should also note that mission-planning overheads (turn-around, datalink) mean a *practical* number is 2 sorties for redundancy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Do **not** mix mm and m in GSD — every term must be in SI before substituting. Students lose 1–2 marks for unit slippage.
> - For swath width, use **full FOV** in the tan(θ/2) formula. Mistaking θ/2 for θ is the most common error.
> - For endurance, the Breguet formula assumes **constant $L/D$** and **propeller propulsion**; for jets, a different fuel-flow form applies.
> - For cumulative detection, do not exceed N that exceeds physical mission time.
> - Always state the *operational assumption* behind a number (e.g., "assuming 25% overlap").

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Defense surveillance = ISR + TA**, deployed as part of a **C4ISR** network.
- **Sensor choice drives GSD**: GSD = H·p/(1000·f). A GSD ≤ 0.3 m enables positive vehicle ID (NATO).
- **Swath width** $W = 2H\tan(\theta_{FOV}/2)$ is the *fundamental coverage unit*.
- **Area coverage rate** $ACR = V_g \cdot W$ (effective after overlap).
- **Time-on-Station** = Endurance – 2 × transit time.
- **Standoff distance** protects the platform while still allowing sensor geometry.
- **SAR range resolution** $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ — bandwidth, not pulse width, governs.
- **Cumulative detection** $P_D = 1 - (1 - p_d)^N$ — more passes increase detection.
- **Swarm ISR** beats a single platform in coverage, redundancy, and survivability.
- **C4ISR, STANAG 4586, ATR, SIGINT, FHSS** are all KTU 2024 high-yield acronyms.
- Always convert **mm → m** and **µm → m** before substitution.
- Always present derivations in the form: *diagram → formula → substitution → result → conclusion*.
- KTU 2024 often tests the *why* behind sensor selection: EO/IR for clarity, SAR for weather, SIGINT for emitters, LIDAR for 3D.
- Mission state machine: Plan → Pre-flight → Transit → Search → Track → BDA → Return.
